topics.title: Single Sided (Single Layer)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Sided (Single Layer)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single-Sided PCB** (also called a **Single-Layer PCB** or **Single-Sided Board**) is a type of **Printed Circuit Board** in which the entire copper conductive pattern — including component pads, signal traces, and ground/power planes — is fabricated on **only one side** of the dielectric substrate. The opposite side of the substrate is left un-clad (bare laminate) and is typically used solely for mounting through-hole components, mechanical support, or silkscreen marking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Concept):**
> *"A single-sided PCB has all its conductive copper tracks, pads, and components confined to a single face of the insulating base material, while the reverse face remains a non-conductive dielectric, used only for component anchoring and labelling."*

**Key Terminology (High-Yield for KTU Board Exam):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Substrate** | The base insulating material (commonly **FR-2, FR-4, CEM-1, CEM-3**) |
| **Copper Clad Laminate (CCL)** | The starting raw material — dielectric + bonded copper foil |
| **Trace** | A continuous conductive copper path carrying signal or power |
| **Pad** | The exposed copper area where a component lead is soldered |
| **Silkscreen** | Top-side legend layer (component designators, logos) |
| **Solder Mask** | Green/blue polymer layer protecting copper except at pads |
| **Through-Hole (THT)** | Component leads inserted through drilled holes |
| **V-cut / Mouse Bites** | Methods to depanelize multiple PCBs from a panel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Copper Weight:** The most common copper foil thickness in commercial single-sided PCB fabrication is **1 oz/ft² (ounce per square foot)**, which is electrically equivalent to **35 µm (micrometers)** of copper thickness.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (First-Time Learner Friendly)

Imagine a **single-lane, one-way road** running through a small town:

- The **road surface** = the copper layer (where the "traffic" — i.e., electricity — flows)
- The **earth/soil beneath the road** = the insulating substrate (FR-4 fiberglass)
- The **opposite side of the road (the ditch / unpaved shoulder)** = the bare dielectric side with no copper

In a **single-sided PCB**, all the cars (electrons) travel on **only one face** of the board. There is no highway on the back side. If the engineer needs the current to "cross" over another trace, they must either:
1. Route it around the other trace (longer path), or
2. Use a **zero-ohm resistor / jumper wire** (a physical wire acting like a flyover bridge).

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Mnemonic:** *SINGLE = S ingle S ide, N o G round plane, G round, L ayers, E asy.*
> Single-sided boards are the **easiest, cheapest, and oldest** form of PCB technology, dating back to **Paul Eisler (1936)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Single-Sided PCBs Still Matter in 2024+ Engineering

Even in the era of HDI (High-Density Interconnect) and 12-layer motherboards, **single-sided PCBs dominate the global electronics market by volume**. Reasons include:

- **Cost-sensitive consumer products** (toys, calculators, remote controls, LED bulbs)
- **High-volume disposable electronics** (cigarette lighters, greeting cards with sound)
- **Educational kits and lab prototypes** (Arduino UNO clone single-sided variants)
- **Power electronics** (relay drivers, AC-DC converters below 100 W)
- **Replacement / spare boards** for legacy industrial equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic (For Context):** Single-sided PCBs still represent approximately **30–40% of global PCB production volume** in the consumer-grade segment, because they require **only 1 photolithographic exposure step** and **1 etching step**, halving fabrication cost vs double-sided boards.

---

## 1.4 Comparison Snapshot (Quick Reference for KTU)

| Parameter | Single-Sided | Double-Sided | Multi-Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Copper Layers | **1** | 2 | 4 to 20+ |
| Plated Through Holes (PTH) | Not required | Required | Required |
| Component Density | Low | Medium | High |
| Fabrication Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Application | Toys, LEDs | Consumer | Smartphones, CPUs |
| EMI Performance | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (with ground planes) |
| Design Complexity | **Simple** | Medium | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer Stack-Up of a Single-Sided PCB

A single-sided PCB, when sliced vertically, has the following structure from top to bottom:

> **L1 — Silkscreen (component legends, top side, optional)**
> **L2 — Solder Mask (green lacquer, ~25 µm)**
> **L3 — Copper Conductive Layer (1 oz/ft² = 35 µm)**
> **L4 — Adhesive / Bonding Layer (only for paper-based CCL)**
> **L5 — Dielectric Substrate (FR-4 = 0.8 mm to 1.6 mm typical)**
> **L6 — Bare Un-clad Bottom Side (no copper)**

The absence of an internal ground plane (L4–L6 in multilayer boards) is the **single most defining feature** of single-sided boards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why do single-sided PCBs have poor high-frequency performance?"* The answer is: **the lack of a continuous ground plane** means there is no return path for high-frequency signals, leading to uncontrolled **characteristic impedance**, **crosstalk**, and **radiated EMI**.

---

## 2.2 Materials Used in Single-Sided CCL

| Material Code | Base Composition | Typical Use Case | Tg (Glass Transition Temp) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FR-2** | Phenolic resin + paper | Toys, calculators (low cost) | ~105 °C |
| **FR-3** | Epoxy + paper | Mid-range consumer | ~115 °C |
| **FR-4** | Epoxy + woven glass | **Most common — industrial grade** | **130–180 °C** |
| **CEM-1** | Epoxy + paper core + glass surface | Low-cost power supplies | ~120 °C |
| **CEM-3** | Epoxy + glass (cheaper FR-4 substitute) | Consumer electronics | ~125 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FR-4 is the de-facto industry standard** for single-sided PCBs in 2024 due to its excellent **mechanical strength**, **moisture resistance**, **flame retardancy (UL94-V0 rated)**, and **stable dielectric constant** (Dk ≈ **4.2 to 4.6** at 1 MHz).

---

## 2.3 Design Rules for Single-Sided PCBs (KTU Practical Exam Essentials)

### 2.3.1 Minimum Trace Width & Spacing

| Parameter | Standard Value (1 oz Cu) | Minimum Achievable (Advanced) |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum trace width | **0.2 mm (8 mil)** | 0.1 mm (4 mil) |
| Minimum trace spacing | **0.2 mm (8 mil)** | 0.1 mm (4 mil) |
| Minimum pad diameter | **1.0 mm** | 0.5 mm |
| Minimum drill hole | **0.3 mm** | 0.2 mm |
| Annular ring | **0.15 mm** | 0.1 mm |
| Edge clearance | **0.5 mm** | 0.25 mm |

### 2.3.2 Trace Current Carrying Capacity (IPC-2152 Modified)

For a single-sided PCB trace with **external layer** and **1 oz copper (35 µm)**, the IPC-2152 empirical equation is:

$$ I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$

Where:
- $I$ = allowable current in **Amperes**
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise above ambient in **°C** (typical: **10 °C, 20 °C, or 30 °C**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of trace in **mil²** ( = thickness × width in mils)
- $k$ = constant (for external traces, $k \approx 0.048$)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Resistance of a copper trace | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law for trace voltage drop | V, A, $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | IPC-2152 trace current capacity | A, °C, mil² |
| 4 | $A_{trace} = t_{cu} \times w_{trace}$ | Cross-sectional area of trace | mil² |
| 5 | $t_{cu} = 1\,\text{oz/ft}^2 = 35\,\mu m = 1.378\,\text{mil}$ | Standard copper thickness | mil |
| 6 | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98h}{0.8w + t}\right)$ | Microstrip impedance (only valid for double-sided) | Ohms |
| 7 | $R_{cu} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot L}{A}$ | Copper resistivity = $1.724 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot m$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $C_{trace} \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{d}$ | Capacitance of trace-to-trace (parallel plate approx) | Farads |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** use the microstrip impedance formula (Z₀) for single-sided PCBs. That formula requires a **ground plane on the opposite side**. For a single-sided trace over free air, the characteristic impedance is **uncontrolled and very high** (>200 Ω typical).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-sided PCBs are the **backbone of low-cost, high-volume, and power-electronics hardware**. The most common industrial use cases in 2024 are:

1. **LED Driver Boards** — AC-DC step-down converters in LED bulbs (constant current ICs like BP2832A on a single-sided FR-4).
2. **Appliance Control Boards** — Washing machines, microwave ovens, induction cooktops (under 200 W logic sections).
3. **Automotive Sub-circuits** — Dashboard indicator lamps, relay drivers, 12 V fan controllers.
4. **Educational Boards** — Soldering practice kits, basic amplifier boards (e.g., LM386 audio amp on single-sided FR-4).
5. **Toy & Hobby Electronics** — Musical greeting cards, talking dolls, remote-controlled toys.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Companies like **TP-Link, Havells, Philips (low-end SKUs), and Syska** manufacture **millions of single-sided PCBs per month** because the per-unit cost difference vs double-sided is roughly **30–50% cheaper**, which at scale is enormous.

---

## 2.6 Key Engineering Limitations of Single-Sided PCBs

1. **No ground plane** → poor EMC, high radiated emissions.
2. **No power plane** → voltage drop issues, IR losses on long traces.
3. **Routing congestion** → components must be spaced out to avoid trace crossings.
4. **Cannot use SMD components on both sides** (only one side is metallized).
5. **Slower signal rise times** → limited to **~50 MHz** digital or **~10 MHz** analog bandwidth.
6. **Mechanical fragility** — paper-based FR-2 is brittle, prone to cracking under vibration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance of a Copper Trace

### Statement
Derive the DC resistance of a single-sided PCB copper trace given its length, width, and standard copper thickness.

### Given Data
- Trace length, $L$ = **0.2 m** (200 mm)
- Trace width, $w$ = **0.4 mm** = 0.0004 m
- Copper thickness, $t$ = **35 µm** = $35 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Resistivity of copper, $\rho$ = **$1.724 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot m$**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The fundamental resistance of a uniform conductor is given by:

$$ R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} $$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to current flow.

**Step 2 — Express the cross-sectional area of a PCB trace.**

A PCB trace is a thin rectangular strip. Its cross-section (viewed end-on) has:

$$ A = w \times t_{cu} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (0.0004\,\text{m}) \times (35 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}) \\
A &= 1.4 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the resistance formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times (0.2)}{1.4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
R &= \frac{0.3448 \times 10^{-8}}{1.4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
R &= 0.2463\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Result and Engineering Interpretation.**

The trace has a resistance of approximately **0.25 Ω**. At a current of 1 A, this would cause a voltage drop of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I \times R \\
V_{drop} &= 1 \times 0.2463 = 0.2463\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **8.2% of a 3.3 V supply**, which is significant. Hence, **power traces on single-sided PCBs must be made wider** to minimize IR drop.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Trace Width for a Given Current (IPC-2152)

### Statement
Calculate the minimum required trace width on a 1 oz/ft² single-sided PCB to safely carry 3 A with a maximum temperature rise of 20 °C above ambient.

### Given Data
- Current, $I$ = **3 A**
- Copper thickness, $t$ = **1.378 mil** (1 oz/ft² = 35 µm = 1.378 mil)
- Allowable $\Delta T$ = **20 °C**
- External layer constant, $k$ = **0.048**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Rearrange the IPC-2152 equation to solve for cross-sectional area $A$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} \\
A^{0.725} &= \frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} \\
A &= \left(\frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}}\right)^{1/0.725}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T^{0.44}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T^{0.44} &= 20^{0.44} \\
&= e^{0.44 \times \ln(20)} \\
&= e^{0.44 \times 2.9957} \\
&= e^{1.3181} \\
&\approx 3.7355
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} &= 0.048 \times 3.7355 \\
&= 0.1793
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio I / (k · ΔT^0.44).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} &= \frac{3}{0.1793} \\
&= 16.732
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Raise to the power of 1/0.725 = 1.3793.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (16.732)^{1.3793} \\
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.3793 \times \log_{10}(16.732) \\
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.3793 \times 1.2236 \\
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.6877 \\
A &= 10^{1.6877} \\
A &\approx 48.71\,\text{mil}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required trace width.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{A}{t_{cu}} \\
w &= \frac{48.71}{1.378} \\
w &\approx 35.35\,\text{mil}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= 35.35 \times 0.0254\,\text{mm} \\
w &\approx 0.898\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Conclusion.**

The minimum required trace width is approximately **0.9 mm** (~36 mil). Designers typically round up to **1.0 mm** for safety margin, especially for single-sided boards which lack a heat-spreading ground plane.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | KTU Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trace width | ↓ Resistance, ↑ Current capacity, ↑ Cu usage | ↑ Resistance, ↑ Voltage drop | Use wide traces for power, narrow for signal |
| Copper thickness | ↑ Current capacity, ↑ Cost | ↓ Cost, ↓ Capacity | 1 oz is standard; 2 oz used for high current |
| Substrate thickness | ↑ Mechanical strength, ↓ Flex | ↑ Flex, thinner board | 1.6 mm standard for FR-4 |
| Solder mask | ↑ Protection, ↑ Cost | ↓ Protection, ↓ Cost | Always use in production |
| Component spacing | ↓ Crosstalk, ↓ Density | ↑ Density, ↑ Crosstalk | 0.5 mm minimum for hand soldering |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Voltage Drop in a Power Trace

A single-sided PCB routes a 12 V, 2 A load from a barrel jack to a regulator. The trace is 0.3 mm wide, 0.15 m long, on 1 oz copper. Find the voltage drop and power dissipation.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= w \times t_{cu} \\
A &= (0.3 \times 10^{-3}) \times (35 \times 10^{-6}) \\
A &= 1.05 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Resistance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\rho L}{A} \\
R &= \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 0.15}{1.05 \times 10^{-8}} \\
R &= 0.2463\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I \times R \\
V_{drop} &= 2 \times 0.2463 = 0.4926\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation in trace.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{loss} &= I^2 \times R \\
P_{loss} &= (2)^2 \times 0.2463 \\
P_{loss} &= 0.9852\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Insight:** A **0.49 V drop on a 12 V line is ~4.1% loss**. For a regulator, this may be acceptable, but for battery-powered devices, it is **too high**. **Solution: widen the trace to 1 mm or use 2 oz copper.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vertical Cross-Section of a Single-Sided PCB (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional layer architecture** of a single-sided PCB in a top-down block-flow representation, since physical cross-sections cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1: SILKSCREEN TOP - Component Designators, Logos, Reference IDs"]
    L2["LAYER 2: SOLDER MASK - Green or Blue Polymer Lacquer - Thickness 25 to 35 microns"]
    L3["LAYER 3: COPPER CONDUCTIVE LAYER - 1 oz per sq ft equals 35 microns - All Traces Pads Vias"]
    L4["LAYER 4: ADHESIVE BOND - For Paper Based CCL like FR-2 and CEM-1"]
    L5["LAYER 5: DIELECTRIC SUBSTRATE - FR-4 Epoxy Woven Glass - Thickness 0.8 to 1.6 mm"]
    L6["LAYER 6: BARE UNCLAD BOTTOM - No Copper - Used for Component Mounting Only"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    style L1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style L3 fill:#daa520,stroke:#5c3317,stroke-width:2px
    style L4 fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L5 fill:#f5deb3,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px
    style L6 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 Manufacturing Process Flow for Single-Sided PCBs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["START: Copper Clad Laminate CCL"] --> B["STEP 1: CUTTING AND CLEANING"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: DRILLING HOLES for THT Components"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: PHOTORESIST APPLICATION Dry Film or Liquid"]
    D --> E["STEP 4: UV EXPOSURE through Photographic Film"]
    E --> F["STEP 5: DEVELOPING Removes Uncured Resist"]
    F --> G["STEP 6: ETCHING Ferric Chloride or Cupric Chloride Removes Unwanted Copper"]
    G --> H["STEP 7: STRIPPING RESIST Removes Remaining Photoresist"]
    H --> I["STEP 8: SOLDER MASK APPLICATION by Screen Printing"]
    I --> J["STEP 9: SILKSCREEN PRINTING Component Legends"]
    J --> K["STEP 10: HOT AIR LEVELING HAL or Lead Free HASL"]
    K --> L["STEP 11: ROUTING AND V CUT for Depanelization"]
    L --> M["STEP 12: ELECTRICAL TESTING Flying Probe or Bed of Nails"]
    M --> N["FINISHED SINGLE SIDED PCB READY FOR ASSEMBLY"]

    style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Component Side vs Solder Side — Single-Sided PCB

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP_SIDE["TOP SIDE OF PCB - Component Side"]
        R1["Resistor R1 - THT Axial"]
        C1["Capacitor C1 - Electrolytic"]
        IC1["IC1 - DIP Package"]
        LED1["LED1 - Indicator"]
    end

    subgraph BOTTOM_SIDE["BOTTOM SIDE OF PCB - Solder Side - Copper Layer"]
        T1["Trace 1: Power Rail +12V"]
        T2["Trace 2: Signal Path"]
        T3["Trace 3: Ground Return"]
        PAD1["Solder Pads for THT Leads"]
    end

    R1 -. "Lead through hole" .-> PAD1
    C1 -. "Lead through hole" .-> PAD1
    IC1 -. "Lead through hole" .-> PAD1
    LED1 -. "Lead through hole" .-> PAD1

    T1 --- PAD1
    T2 --- PAD1
    T3 --- PAD1

    style TOP_SIDE fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style BOTTOM_SIDE fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Routing Strategy — Avoiding Trace Crossings on Single-Sided PCBs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Need to cross two traces on a single sided PCB"] --> B{"Is there a ground plane?"}
    B -->|"NO - Single Sided"| C["Option 1: Use 0 Ohm Resistor Jumper"]
    B -->|"NO - Single Sided"| D["Option 2: Use a Wire Jumper"]
    B -->|"NO - Single Sided"| E["Option 3: Reroute one trace around the other"]

    C --> F["Recommended for production"]
    D --> G["Use only for prototypes"]
    E --> H["Use only if board space allows"]

    style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define a single-sided PCB. List any four applications where it is preferred over a double-sided PCB.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Single-Sided PCB** is a printed circuit board in which the entire conductive copper pattern is fabricated on **only one side** of the dielectric substrate, with the opposite side being a bare, non-conductive laminate.

**Four applications where it is preferred:**

1. **Low-cost consumer electronics** (e.g., remote controls, calculators, toys) — due to **lowest fabrication cost**.
2. **LED driver boards** (constant-current IC-based AC-DC converters) — due to **simple topology** and **low component count**.
3. **Educational and training kits** — due to **ease of fabrication, drilling, soldering, and debugging** for students.
4. **High-volume disposable electronics** (musical greeting cards, talking toys) — due to **single-step photolithography** and **low per-unit cost at scale**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for applications = **3 marks total.**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Why is FR-4 the most commonly used substrate material for single-sided PCBs? Mention any two key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**FR-4 (Flame Retardant-4)** is a composite of **woven fiberglass cloth impregnated with epoxy resin**, and is the most widely used substrate for single-sided PCBs because:

1. **Excellent mechanical strength** — the woven glass reinforcement provides high tensile and flexural strength (~**200–300 MPa**), making the board resistant to vibration and handling damage.

2. **High glass transition temperature (Tg)** — typically **130–180 °C**, which allows the board to survive the high temperatures of **reflow soldering (250 °C)** and **hot air solder leveling (HASL)** processes.

3. **Flame retardancy** — rated **UL94-V0**, meaning it self-extinguishes within 10 seconds after flame removal, crucial for **safety compliance** in consumer electronics.

4. **Stable dielectric constant (Dk ≈ 4.2–4.6)** and low loss tangent, ensuring **predictable electrical behavior** even at moderate frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for naming FR-4 composition + 2 marks for any two properties with values = **3 marks total.**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step **fabrication process of a single-sided PCB** using the **photo-etching (subtractive) method**. Include the role of photoresist, etchant, and solder mask in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a single-sided PCB, a power trace is **0.6 mm wide**, **120 mm long**, and uses **1 oz/ft² (35 µm)** copper. Calculate:
- (i) The DC resistance of the trace.
- (ii) The voltage drop and power dissipation when carrying **2.5 A**.
- (iii) The minimum trace width needed to keep the voltage drop **below 0.1 V** for the same current.

**Constants:** $\rho_{cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot m$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

### **Part (a) — Fabrication Process [7 Marks]**

The **subtractive photo-etching process** is the most common method for single-sided PCB fabrication. The steps are:

**Step 1 — Material Preparation [1 Mark]**
The process begins with a **Copper Clad Laminate (CCL)**, which is a dielectric substrate (e.g., FR-4) with **35 µm copper foil** bonded to one side. The board is cut to size and **mechanically cleaned** with pumice to remove oxidation and improve photoresist adhesion.

**Step 2 — Drilling [0.5 Mark]**
A **CNC drilling machine** creates holes for through-hole component leads. On a single-sided board, these holes do **not** need to be plated (no PTH process).

**Step 3 — Photoresist Application [1 Mark]**
A **dry film photoresist (DFR)** or **liquid photoresist** is laminated onto the copper surface. This light-sensitive polymer will harden upon UV exposure in selected regions, protecting the copper beneath from the etchant.

**Step 4 — UV Exposure and Developing [1.5 Marks]**
A **photographic film (photomask)** containing the desired trace pattern is placed over the photoresist, and the board is exposed to **UV light (365 nm)**. The exposed areas of DFR **polymerize (harden)**. In developing, the unexposed resist is **washed away with sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) solution**, exposing the unwanted copper.

**Step 5 — Etching [1.5 Marks]**
The board is immersed in an **etchant** — either **Ferric Chloride (FeCl₃)** for hobby use or **Cupric Chloride (CuCl₂)** for industrial use. The etchant **chemically dissolves the exposed (unwanted) copper**, leaving only the protected trace pattern. Reaction:
$$ \text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2 $$

**Step 6 — Resist Stripping [0.5 Mark]**
The remaining hardened photoresist is stripped using **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** solution, exposing the shiny copper trace pattern.

**Step 7 — Solder Mask and Silkscreen [0.5 Mark]**
A **green solder mask** (UV-curable epoxy or liquid photoimageable) is screen-printed or laminated onto the board, with **windows opened at the pads** for soldering. A **silkscreen layer** prints component designators (R1, C2, U3) using white epoxy ink.

**Step 8 — Surface Finish and Depanelization [0.5 Mark]**
The exposed copper pads are coated with **HASL (Hot Air Solder Leveling)** or **ENIG (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold)** for solderability and oxidation protection. Finally, the panel is **routed, V-cut, or mouse-bitten** to separate individual PCBs.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $w = 0.6\,\text{mm} = 0.0006\,\text{m}$, $L = 120\,\text{mm} = 0.12\,\text{m}$, $t = 35\,\mu m = 35 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $I = 2.5\,\text{A}$, $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot m$.

**(i) DC Resistance [2 Marks]**

Cross-sectional area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= w \times t \\
A &= (0.0006) \times (35 \times 10^{-6}) \\
A &= 2.1 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Resistance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\rho L}{A} \\
R &= \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 0.12}{2.1 \times 10^{-8}} \\
R &= \frac{0.2069 \times 10^{-8}}{2.1 \times 10^{-8}} \\
R &= 0.0985\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Voltage Drop and Power Dissipation [2 Marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I \times R = 2.5 \times 0.0985 = 0.2463\,\text{V} \\
P_{loss} &= I^2 \times R = (2.5)^2 \times 0.0985 = 0.6156\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Trace Width for V_drop < 0.1 V [3 Marks]**

For $V_{drop} < 0.1\,\text{V}$ at $I = 2.5\,\text{A}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{V_{drop}}{I} = \frac{0.1}{2.5} = 0.04\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Required cross-sectional area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{min} &= \frac{\rho L}{R_{max}} \\
A_{min} &= \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 0.12}{0.04} \\
A_{min} &= 5.172 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Required width:
$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{min} &= \frac{A_{min}}{t} \\
w_{min} &= \frac{5.172 \times 10^{-8}}{35 \times 10^{-6}} \\
w_{min} &= 1.478 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \\
w_{min} &= 1.48\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The trace width must be **at least 1.5 mm** (round up for design margin) to keep the voltage drop below 0.1 V. Hence, the original 0.6 mm width is **inadequate** for 2.5 A current over 120 mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **single-sided PCBs** with **double-sided PCBs** in terms of:
- (i) Layer count and copper utilization
- (ii) Cost and fabrication complexity
- (iii) EMI/EMC performance
- (iv) Plated through-hole (PTH) requirement
- (v) Typical applications
- (vi) Routing density
- (vii) Suitability for high-speed signals

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-sided PCB uses **2 oz/ft² copper (70 µm)** for a power trace carrying **5 A continuous** with a maximum temperature rise of **30 °C**. Using the **IPC-2152 equation**, calculate the required trace width in mm.

**Constants:** $k = 0.048$ (external layer), $1\,\text{oz/ft}^2 = 1.378\,\text{mil}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

### **Part (a) — Comparative Study [7 Marks — 1 Mark Each Point]**

| Parameter | Single-Sided PCB | Double-Sided PCB |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Layer count** | **1 copper layer** on one side only | **2 copper layers**, one on each side |
| **(ii) Cost & complexity** | **Lowest cost**, **simplest** process — 1 exposure, 1 etch step | **~30–50% higher cost** — 2 exposures, possible PTH, alignment critical |
| **(iii) EMI/EMC** | **Poor** — no ground plane, high radiated emissions | **Better** — bottom layer often used as ground plane |
| **(iv) PTH required?** | **No** — through-holes are not plated (solder flows by capillary action) | **Yes** — PHT process adds cost and step |
| **(v) Applications** | Toys, LED bulbs, calculators, simple power supplies | Consumer electronics, automotive ECUs, industrial controls |
| **(vi) Routing density** | **Low** — only one routing layer limits component density | **Higher** — 2 layers allow better routing and vias |
| **(vii) High-speed signals** | **Not suitable** — uncontrolled impedance, no return path | **Suitable up to ~100 MHz** with proper ground plane |

---

### **Part (b) — Trace Width Using IPC-2152 [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $I = 5\,\text{A}$, $t = 2\,\text{oz/ft}^2 = 2 \times 1.378 = 2.756\,\text{mil}$, $\Delta T = 30\,°C$, $k = 0.048$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta T^{0.44}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
30^{0.44} &= e^{0.44 \times \ln(30)} \\
&= e^{0.44 \times 3.4012} \\
&= e^{1.4965} \\
&\approx 4.4668
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange IPC-2152 to solve for A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{0.725} &= \frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} \\
A &= \left(\frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}}\right)^{1/0.725}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} &= 0.048 \times 4.4668 \\
&= 0.2144
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} &= \frac{5}{0.2144} \\
&= 23.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute A by raising to the power 1/0.725 = 1.3793.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.3793 \times \log_{10}(23.32) \\
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.3793 \times 1.3678 \\
\log_{10}(A) &= 1.8864 \\
A &= 10^{1.8864} \\
A &\approx 76.99\,\text{mil}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required width in mils.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{A}{t} = \frac{76.99}{2.756} \\
w &\approx 27.94\,\text{mil}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= 27.94 \times 0.0254 \\
w &\approx 0.71\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The required trace width is approximately **0.71 mm**, which is feasible in standard single-sided PCB fabrication. Designers typically round up to **0.8 mm or 1.0 mm** for safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert copper thickness** between oz/ft² and mil/µm — this is a **1-mark loss** if not done.
> 2. **Using $k = 0.024$ (internal layer constant)** instead of **0.048 (external layer)** — single-sided traces are always external, so $k = 0.048$ is correct.
> 3. **Rounding errors** in logarithmic computation — keep at least 4 significant figures until the final step.
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions** of the IPC-2152 equation (external trace, no nearby heat sinks, ambient 25 °C).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- A **single-sided PCB** has **one copper layer** on one side of a dielectric substrate.
- The other side is **bare laminate** — no copper, no ground plane.
- Most common substrate = **FR-4 (Tg 130–180 °C)**.
- Standard copper thickness = **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm = 1.378 mil**.

### 🔹 Key Formulas
- Trace resistance: $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ where $\rho_{cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot m$
- Voltage drop: $V_{drop} = I \cdot R$
- IPC-2152: $I = 0.048 \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ (external)
- Power loss: $P = I^2 \cdot R$

### 🔹 Design Rules (Must-Know)
- Min trace width: **0.2 mm (8 mil)**
- Min trace spacing: **0.2 mm (8 mil)**
- Min drill hole: **0.3 mm**
- Edge clearance: **0.5 mm**
- Annular ring: **0.15 mm**

### 🔹 Fabrication Steps (in order)
1. Cut CCL → 2. Drill → 3. Apply photoresist → 4. UV expose → 5. Develop → 6. Etch (FeCl₃) → 7. Strip resist → 8. Solder mask → 9. Silkscreen → 10. HASL → 11. Route/V-cut → 12. Electrical test

### 🔹 Etching Chemistry
$$ \text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2 $$

### 🔹 Advantages
- ✅ Lowest cost
- ✅ Simplest fabrication
- ✅ Fastest prototyping (DIY-friendly)
- ✅ Easy to debug and repair

### 🔹 Disadvantages
- ❌ No ground plane → poor EMI
- ❌ No power plane → IR drop
- ❌ Routing congestion
- ❌ Cannot use SMD on both sides
- ❌ Limited to ~50 MHz digital

### 🔹 Applications
- Toys, calculators, LED bulbs, remote controls, low-cost power supplies, educational kits.

### 🔹 One-Line Exam Mantra
> *"Single-sided = one copper side, one ground-less design, one cost-effective choice."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Layer (Double Layer)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multi-Layer (Double Layer) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Multi-Layer Printed Circuit Board (PCB)**, specifically the **Double-Layer (Double-Sided) PCB** form, is a flat, rigid (or flexible) laminated substrate used to mechanically support and electrically interconnect electronic components, where conductive copper patterns are etched on **both the top and bottom sides** of a dielectric base material. When additional internal signal or plane layers are sandwiched between the outer surfaces using bonding plies, the board is termed a **Multi-Layer PCB** (commonly 4, 6, 8, or up to 30+ layers). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEEVT612 - Module 1):** A *Double-Layer PCB* is the foundational step up from a Single-Sided PCB, employing conductive tracks on both faces of an insulating substrate, joined electrically through **plated through-holes (PTH)**. The Multi-Layer extension uses alternating prepreg (pre-impregnated) and core laminates fused under heat and pressure. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Floor Building":** > Imagine a **single-floor house** (Single-Sided PCB): all electrical wires (copper traces) run on the ground floor only, and crossing wires requires awkward bridges (jumpers). > A **Double-Sided PCB is like a two-story house** — wires run on the **ground floor AND first floor**, connected by **staircases (vias/plated through-holes)**. Traffic can flow in two dimensions freely, eliminating most crossings. > A **Multi-Layer PCB is a high-rise building** (4, 6, 8+ floors). The bottom floors become dedicated power and ground (the "infrastructure"), and the upper floors carry signals (the "residents"). Dedicated infrastructure floors give clean power and low noise — the hallmark of high-speed digital design. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Substrate Thickness:** **1.6 mm** (typical FR-4 board) - **Copper Foil Weight:** **1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm** (outer layers), **0.5 oz/ft² ≈ 17 µm** (inner layers) - **Dielectric Constant (Dk) of FR-4:** **4.2 – 4.6** at 1 MHz (drops at GHz) - **Dielectric Breakdown Voltage:** **>40 kV/mm** - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** **130 °C – 180 °C** for standard/high-Tg FR-4 - **Standard Board Sizes:** Eurocard (100 × 160 mm), ATX (305 × 244 mm) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction Stack-Up of a Double-Layer PCB The Double-Layer PCB is the **simplest multi-conductor structure** in PCB engineering. Its stack-up consists of: 1. **Substrate (Dielectric Base):** FR-4 (woven glass-fibre + epoxy resin) — provides mechanical rigidity and electrical insulation. 2. **Copper Foil (Top Layer):** Laminated/clad on the upper face, then photolithographically patterned. 3. **Copper Foil (Bottom Layer):** Laminated on the lower face, also patterned. 4. **Soldermask (Top & Bottom):** Green (or other colour) epoxy layer protecting exposed copper, leaving only pads exposed. 5. **Silkscreen (Legend):** White epoxy-ink layer for component reference designators. 6. **Plated Through-Holes (PTH):** Holes drilled through the entire board, walls electroplated with copper to connect top and bottom layers electrically. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Plated Through-Holes (PTH) Matter:** Without PTH, the two copper layers are *electrically isolated islands*. PTHs are the **only electrical bridge** in a double-layer board, making the drilling and plating processes mission-critical. ## 2.2 Construction Stack-Up of a 4-Layer Multi-Layer PCB A standard 4-layer board extends the double-layer concept by adding two **internal plane layers**: 1. **Top Layer (L1) — Signal** 2. **Prepreg (Bonding Ply)** — semi-cured B-stage epoxy/glass 3. **Inner Layer 2 (L2) — Ground Plane (continuous copper)** 4. **Core (Fully Cured C-Stage Laminate with copper on both sides)** 5. **Inner Layer 3 (L3) — Power Plane** 6. **Prepreg** 7. **Bottom Layer (L4) — Signal** The lamination cycle (heat ≈ 185 °C, pressure ≈ 200–400 psi, vacuum) fuses these into a monolithic structure. ## 2.3 Via Technology (KTU High-Yield Topic) A **via** is a conductive pathway connecting two or more copper layers. In multi-layer boards, vias take several forms: | Via Type | Description | KTU Application | |----------|-------------|-----------------| | **Through-Hole Via** | Drilled completely through all layers; plated | Default in Double-Layer PCBs | | **Blind Via** | Connects outer layer to one or more inner layers only | High-density BGA breakouts | | **Buried Via** | Connects two inner layers only, invisible from outside | 6+ layer boards, dense routing | | **Microvia (μVia)** | Laser-drilled, < 150 µm diameter, typically 1-2 layer span | HDI (High-Density Interconnect) boards | | **Via-in-Pad** | Via placed directly under a component pad | BGA, QFN packages, thermal management | ## 2.4 Why Multi-Layer > Double-Layer > Single-Sided > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-offs:** > - **Routing Density:** Multi-layer offers ~3–5× the routing channels of double-layer. > - **EMI/EMC Performance:** Dedicated ground & power planes act as **return-path references** and provide inherent **decoupling capacitance** (~1–10 pF/cm²) between adjacent plane layers. > - **Signal Integrity:** Controlled impedance stripline/microstrip routing is possible only with reference planes (so practically impossible in 2-layer boards above ~50 MHz). > - **Cost:** 2-layer is ~2× the cost of single-sided; 4-layer is ~2–3× the cost of 2-layer; cost grows nonlinearly with layer count. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > All quantities use SI units unless otherwise stated. The symbol `∈` denotes the dielectric constant (Dk). Use `\vert` instead of `|` in KTU answer scripts to avoid markdown issues. | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Use | |---|--------------------|------------|-----------------| | 1 | Microstrip Impedance (outer trace) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \ln\left(\dfrac{5.98h}{0.8w + t}\right) \; \Omega$ | Controlled-impedance signal lines | | 2 | Stripline Impedance (inner trace) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\left(\dfrac{1.9b}{0.8w + t}\right) \; \Omega$ | Embedded traces between planes | | 3 | Plane Capacitance (per unit area) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Decoupling between power & ground | | 4 | Propagation Delay (microstrip) | $t_{pd} \approx 3.33 \sqrt{0.475 \varepsilon_r + 0.67} \; \text{ps/mm}$ | Timing budget calculations | | 5 | Via Inductance (approximation) | $L_{via} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\left[h \ln\left(\dfrac{4h}{d}\right) + \dots\right] \;\text{H}$ | High-speed via stub analysis | | 6 | Skin Depth (copper, σ = 5.8×10⁷ S/m) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu_0 \sigma}}$ | AC current distribution in traces | | 7 | Track Current Carrying Capacity (IPC-2152) | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ (empirical) | Thermal design of power traces | **Where:** - $h$ = dielectric thickness to reference plane (m) - $w$ = trace width (m) - $t$ = copper thickness (m) - $b$ = total dielectric between reference planes (m) - $A$ = plane overlap area (m²) - $d$ = dielectric thickness between planes (m) - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones use **8–12 layer HDI boards** with microvias and via-in-pad. - **Automotive ECUs:** 4–6 layer boards with dedicated power/ground planes for CAN, LIN, FlexRay buses. - **Aerospace & Defence:** 14–30 layer rigid-flex boards for radar, avionics, satellite payloads. - **Medical Devices:** 4–8 layer boards with controlled impedance for MRI, ultrasound signal chains. - **IoT Edge Nodes:** 2-layer boards (cost-optimized) for ESP32, STM32 sensor hubs. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Plane-to-Plane Capacitance of an Inner Pair > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Multi-layer boards derive intrinsic decoupling capacitance from parallel plane pairs. A 4-layer board has one such pair; an 8-layer board has three. The total bulk capacitance helps suppress power-rail noise at mid-frequencies (1 MHz – 1 GHz). **Given:** A power plane (Layer 3) and ground plane (Layer 2) of a 4-layer PCB, separated by a core dielectric of thickness $d$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. The two planes form a parallel-plate capacitor. **Find:** The capacitance per unit area, $C'$ (F/m²), and the total bulk capacitance for a board of area $A$. ### Step 1 — Start with the Parallel-Plate Capacitor Equation The classical parallel-plate capacitor is: $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} $$ where: - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the vacuum permittivity - $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative dielectric constant of the core (≈ 4.4 for FR-4 at 1 MHz) - $A$ is the effective overlapping area in m² - $d$ is the dielectric thickness in m ### Step 2 — Normalize to Obtain Capacitance Per Unit Area Dividing both sides by $A$, we get the areal capacitance density: $$ C' = \frac{C}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d} \;\; \left[ \frac{\text{F}}{\text{m}^2} \right] $$ **Interpretation:** $C'$ depends only on the dielectric properties and spacing, not on the size of the planes. This is why designers speak of "pF per square inch" as a board constant. ### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values for a Typical 4-Layer Stack-Up For a typical 4-layer board, the core between L2 (GND) and L3 (PWR) is **0.36 mm (8 mil)** thick FR-4 with $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$. $$ C' = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4.4)}{0.36 \times 10^{-3}} $$ Breaking the multiplication in the numerator: $$ \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4.4 = 3.8958 \times 10^{-11} \;\; \text{F/m} $$ Dividing by $d$: $$ C' = \frac{3.8958 \times 10^{-11}}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.082 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{F/m}^2 $$ Converting to more intuitive units: $$ C' = 108.2 \;\; \text{nF/m}^2 = 108.2 \times 10^{-9} \times (0.0254)^2 \;\; \text{F/in}^2 \cdot \text{per m}^2\ \text{conversion} $$ Step-by-step conversion: 1 m² = (39.37 in)² ≈ 1550 in² $$ C' = \frac{108.2 \;\text{nF}}{1550 \;\text{in}^2} \approx 0.0698 \;\text{nF/in}^2 \approx 70 \;\text{pF/in}^2 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Total Bulk Capacitance for a 100 × 80 mm Board Board area: $$ A = 0.100 \times 0.080 = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2 $$ Total capacitance: $$ C = C' \times A = (1.082 \times 10^{-7}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 8.66 \times 10^{-10} \;\text{F} $$ Converting: $$ C \approx 866 \;\text{pF} $$ ### Step 3.X — Engineering Insight > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** 866 pF is *not enough* to supply decoupling for a modern digital IC (which may need 1–10 µF of bulk capacitance per rail). The intrinsic plane capacitance is therefore a **mid-frequency supplement (1 MHz – 1 GHz)**, not a replacement for discrete decoupling capacitors (typically 100 nF X7R + 10 µF tantalum/ceramic per IC). ### Step 4.X — Summary Table of Plane Capacitance vs. Core Thickness | Core Thickness $d$ (mm) | $C'$ (pF/in²) | $C$ for 100×80 mm board | Application | |------------------------|----------------|--------------------------|-------------| | 0.20 (8 mil) | 124 | 1568 pF | 4-layer, thin core | | 0.36 (14 mil) | 70 | 866 pF | Standard 4-layer | | 0.71 (28 mil) | 35 | 445 pF | 2-layer with wide dielectric | | 1.55 (62 mil) | 16 | 203 pF | Single-sided / power boards | --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Trace Resistance and Voltage Drop **Given:** A copper trace of length $L$, width $W$, thickness $t$, resistivity $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega \cdot \text{m}$ at 25 °C. **Find:** DC resistance $R_{DC}$ and the voltage drop for a 1 A load. ### Step 1 — DC Resistance Formula $$ R_{DC} = \frac{\rho L}{W t} $$ ### Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation for a Typical Power Trace Let $L = 50$ mm, $W = 1$ mm, $t = 35 \; \mu\text{m}$ (1 oz copper). Computing cross-sectional area: $$ A_{cs} = W \times t = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (35 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.5 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^2 $$ Computing resistance: $$ R_{DC} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times (50 \times 10^{-3})}{3.5 \times 10^{-8}} $$ Numerator: $$ (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times (50 \times 10^{-3}) = 8.62 \times 10^{-10} $$ Dividing: $$ R_{DC} = \frac{8.62 \times 10^{-10}}{3.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.46 \times 10^{-2} \;\Omega = 24.6 \;\text{m}\Omega $$ ### Step 3 — Voltage Drop at 1 A $$ V_{drop} = I \times R_{DC} = 1 \times 0.0246 = 0.0246 \;\text{V} = 24.6 \;\text{mV} $$ ### Step 4 — Temperature Correction (KTU Bonus Point) Copper has a temperature coefficient $\alpha = 3.93 \times 10^{-3} \; /^{\circ}\text{C}$. At 70 °C (ΔT = 45 °C): $$ R_{hot} = R_{25} \left[ 1 + \alpha \Delta T \right] = 0.0246 \times \left[ 1 + (3.93 \times 10^{-3})(45) \right] $$ $$ R_{hot} = 0.0246 \times [1 + 0.1769] = 0.0246 \times 1.1769 = 0.02896 \;\Omega \approx 29 \;\text{m}\Omega $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Double-Layer PCB Cross-Sectional View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["TOP COPPER LAYER (L1)"] T1["Component Pad TOP"] T2["Top-side Signal Trace"] T3["Soldermask Opening (Pad)"] end subgraph SUB["DIELECTRIC SUBSTRATE (FR-4)"] D1["Woven Glass + Epoxy Resin"] D2["Thickness 1.6 mm typical"] D3["Dk 4.2 to 4.6"] end subgraph L2["BOTTOM COPPER LAYER (L2)"] B1["Component Pad BOTTOM"] B2["Bottom-side Signal Trace"] B3["Ground Pour / Polygon"] end subgraph CONN["INTERLAYER CONNECTIONS"] V1["Plated Through-Hole PTH"] V2["Drill Diameter 0.3 to 0.8 mm"] V3["Copper Plating 25 micron min"] end L1 --> SUB SUB --> L2 V1 -. connects .-> T1 V1 -. connects .-> B1 V2 --- V1 V3 --- V1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The two copper layers (L1, L2) are mechanically and electrically separated by the FR-4 dielectric. Electrical continuity between them is achieved *only* through the plated through-hole (PTH), shown as a vertical copper barrel. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: 4-Layer Multi-Layer PCB Stack-Up ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Layer 1: TOP SIGNAL
1 oz copper 35 micron
Exposed to components"] B["Prepreg 1080 or 2113
Thickness 0.10 to 0.21 mm
Semi-cured B-stage"] C["Layer 2: GROUND PLANE
0.5 to 1 oz copper
Continuous pour"] D["CORE LAMINATE
Thickness 0.36 to 1.55 mm
Fully cured C-stage FR-4"] E["Layer 3: POWER PLANE
0.5 to 1 oz copper
Continuous pour"] F["Prepreg 1080 or 2113
Symmetric to top"] G["Layer 4: BOTTOM SIGNAL
1 oz copper 35 micron
Exposed to components"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetry Rule (KTU favourite):** A good 4-layer stack-up is **symmetric about its centre plane**. The reason is **warpage control** during reflow soldering — asymmetric stacks curl due to mismatched thermal expansion. ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: PCB Design-to-Fabrication Workflow (Double & Multi-Layer) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Schematic Capture
Eagle / KiCad / Altium"] S2["Component Footprint
Library Mapping"] S3["PCB Layout
Routing Rules"] S4["Design Rule Check
DRC Verification"] S5["Gerber File Generation
RS-274X format"] S6["CAM Verification
at PCB Vendor"] S7["Inner Layer Imaging
Photolithography"] S8["Lamination Cycle
Heat 185 C Pressure 300 psi"] S9["Drilling and Plating
PTH Formation"] S10["Outer Layer Patterning
and Soldermask"] S11["Surface Finish
ENIG / HASL / OSP"] S12["Electrical Test
Continuity and Isolation"] S13["Fabricated PCB Ready
for Assembly"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12 --> S13 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Via Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB VIA["VIA TYPES IN MULTI-LAYER PCB"] VIA --> TH["Through-Hole Via
Spans ALL layers
Lowest cost"] VIA --> BV["Blind Via
Outer to Inner only
Laser drilled"] VIA --> URV["Buried Via
Inner to Inner
Hidden from outside"] VIA --> MV["Microvia
Diameter under 150 micron
1 to 2 layer span"] VIA --> VIP["Via-in-Pad
Via under component pad
BGA QFN thermal use"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a single-sided, double-sided, and multi-layer PCB. List two advantages of multi-layer PCBs over double-sided PCBs.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Single-Sided | Double-Sided | Multi-Layer | |---------|--------------|--------------|-------------| | Conductive Layers | 1 (top only) | 2 (top + bottom) | 4, 6, 8, 12+ | | Interlayer Connection | None (jumpers/wires) | Plated Through-Holes (PTH) | PTH + Blind + Buried + Microvias | | Routing Density | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | EMI/EMC Performance | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (with planes) | | Cost | Lowest | ~2× of single | ~2–3× of double (4-layer) | **Two advantages of multi-layer over double-sided:** 1. **Dedicated Power and Ground Planes** → provide low-impedance return paths, intrinsic decoupling capacitance, and superior EMI suppression. 2. **Higher Routing Density and Controlled Impedance** → enables high-speed signals (>100 MHz), BGA fan-out, and HDI designs impractical on double-layer boards. *(Full 3 marks: 1 for clear definition, 1 for table comparison, 1 for advantages)* ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is a Plated Through-Hole (PTH)? Explain its role in a double-layer PCB.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Plated Through-Hole (PTH)** is a hole drilled through the entire thickness of a PCB, whose inner wall is electroplated with a thin layer of copper (~25 µm), forming an electrically conductive barrel that connects copper pads and traces on the top and bottom layers. **Role in a double-layer PCB:** 1. **Electrical Interconnection:** It is the *only* mechanism that electrically connects the top (L1) and bottom (L2) copper layers in a double-layer board. 2. **Component Mounting:** It serves as the mechanical and electrical anchor for through-hole component leads (e.g., electrolytic capacitors, connectors). 3. **Via Function:** Acts as a "via" enabling signal routing between the two layers, replacing the cumbersome jumper wires used in single-sided boards. *(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for electrical role, 1 for component-mounting role)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) format under the **2024 Scheme** provides an *internal choice* — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both options below are written to full 14-mark depth. --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction of a **4-layer multi-layer PCB**. Discuss the role of prepreg and core laminate in the lamination process. #### Model Solution (Part a): **Construction of a 4-Layer PCB (cross-section, top to bottom):** 1. **Layer 1 (Top Signal Layer):** 1 oz/ft² (35 µm) copper foil; component-side signal traces and pads. 2. **Prepreg (Bonding Sheet 1):** Woven glass cloth pre-impregnated with B-stage (semi-cured) epoxy resin; thickness 0.10–0.21 mm. 3. **Layer 2 (Ground Plane):** 0.5–1 oz/ft² copper; preferably a continuous pour with minimum splits to act as a return-path reference. 4. **Core Laminate:** Fully cured (C-stage) FR-4 sheet with copper clad on both sides; thickness 0.36–1.55 mm; provides the structural backbone. 5. **Layer 3 (Power Plane):** Similar to L2, copper pour for one or more supply rails (e.g., +3.3 V, +5 V). 6. **Prepreg (Bonding Sheet 2):** Mirrors the top prepreg for **symmetric stack-up**. 7. **Layer 4 (Bottom Signal Layer):** 1 oz/ft² copper; second component-side signal layer. **Role of Prepreg:** - Acts as the **adhesive layer** between copper foils and the core during lamination. - Melts and flows at ~135 °C (B-stage cure), then fully polymerises at ~185 °C (C-stage), bonding all layers into a single rigid monolithic board. - Determines the **dielectric thickness** between adjacent conductive layers, directly affecting trace impedance and plane capacitance. **Role of Core Laminate:** - The **mechanical backbone** of the board; provides rigidity, dimensional stability, and the bulk of the dielectric thickness. - Pre-clad with copper on both sides, which is **subtractive-etched** to form the inner plane layers (L2 and L3) *before* final lamination. - Sets the **Tg rating** of the board (130 °C standard, 150–180 °C for high-Tg). **Lamination Process:** The stack is placed in a hydraulic press with heated platens, ramped to ~185 °C at 200–400 psi under vacuum for ~1–2 hours. Vacuum removes trapped air and volatile gases, preventing voids and delamination. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for stack-up description, 1 for prepreg, 1 for core, 1 for lamination process)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the following via technologies with applications: **(i)** Through-hole via, **(ii)** Blind via, **(iii)** Buried via, **(iv)** Microvia. State two advantages of using microvias in HDI designs. #### Model Solution (Part b): | Via Type | Construction | Layer Span | Application / Use Case | |----------|-------------|------------|------------------------| | **(i) Through-hole** | Mechanical drill (0.2–0.8 mm) through entire board; plated with copper | All layers (L1 → Ln) | Default in 2-layer boards; through-hole component leads; power connections | | **(ii) Blind via** | Laser or controlled-depth mechanical drill; plated on one side only | Outer layer → one or more inner layers | BGA fan-out; outer-layer component pads to internal ground | | **(iii) Buried via** | Drilled and plated *before* final lamination; ends are covered by subsequent layers | Inner layer → inner layer | High-density 6+ layer boards; routing relief in complex IC breakouts | | **(iv) Microvia** | CO₂ or UV laser-drilled; diameter < 150 µm; aspect ratio ≤ 1:1; copper-filled or plated | 1–2 adjacent layers only | Smartphones, wearables, HDI motherboards, fine-pitch BGA (≤ 0.4 mm pitch) | **Two advantages of microvias in HDI designs:** 1. **Space Efficiency:** Allow escape routing from fine-pitch BGAs (0.4 mm and below) that is impossible with standard through-holes. 2. **Improved Electrical Performance:** Shorter stub length and lower parasitic inductance (~1 nH vs. 5–10 nH for through-hole) make them suitable for signals > 5 GHz (e.g., USB 3.0, HDMI, 5G RF front-ends). *(7 marks: 4 for table entries with 1 mark each, 2 for microvia advantages, 1 for engineering relevance)* --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the design rules for **double-layer PCBs**, covering: minimum track width, minimum track spacing, annular ring, drill-to-copper clearance, and soldermask expansion. #### Model Solution (Part a): | Parameter | Standard Value (Double-Layer) | Reasoning / Consequence | |-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------| | **Minimum Track Width** | 0.20 mm (8 mil) for 1 oz copper | Lower widths increase resistance and risk of etching defects | | **Minimum Track Spacing** | 0.20 mm (8 mil) for ≤ 50 V signals | Prevents short circuits and arcing; must be increased for high-voltage (>200 V) | | **Annular Ring** | 0.15 mm (6 mil) minimum | The copper pad that "wraps around" the drilled hole; too small causes " breakout" and broken connections | | **Drill-to-Copper Clearance** | 0.30 mm (12 mil) | Prevents drill-bit wander from damaging adjacent traces | | **Soldermask Expansion (Dam)** | 0.075–0.10 mm (3–4 mil) | The soldermask opening is *larger* than the pad by this amount to ensure complete pad exposure and reliable soldering | | **Hole-to-Hole Spacing** | 0.50 mm (20 mil) | Prevents hole-to-hole shorts and drill-bit mechanical interference | | **Edge Clearance (Trace to Board Edge)** | 0.30 mm (12 mil) | Prevents trace lifting during depanelization | | **Silkscreen-to-Pad Clearance** | 0.20 mm (8 mil) | Prevents ink contamination of solderable pads | **Design Rule Hierarchy:** Rules are enforced in the **Design Rule Check (DRC)** of EDA tools (KiCad, Altium, Eagle). Violations block fabrication until resolved. **IPC Standard Reference:** Most double-layer board houses follow **IPC-2221 (Generic Standard on Printed Board Design)** and **IPC-2222 (Sectional Design Standard for Rigid Organic PCBs)**. *(7 marks: 1 per rule (5 rules × 1 = 5), 1 for DRC context, 1 for IPC reference)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **DC resistance** of a copper trace of length 80 mm, width 0.5 mm, and thickness 35 µm at 25 °C. If the trace carries 2 A, find the **(i)** voltage drop and **(ii)** power dissipation. If the operating temperature rises to 85 °C, recalculate the resistance (using $\alpha = 3.93 \times 10^{-3} /^{\circ}\text{C}$ for copper). #### Model Solution (Part b): **Given:** - Length $L = 80 \;\text{mm} = 0.080 \;\text{m}$ - Width $W = 0.5 \;\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$ - Thickness $t = 35 \;\mu\text{m} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}$ - Resistivity of copper $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \;\Omega \cdot \text{m}$ at 25 °C - Current $I = 2 \;\text{A}$ - $\alpha = 3.93 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{per } ^{\circ}\text{C}$ **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:** $$ A_{cs} = W \times t = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.75 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — DC Resistance at 25 °C:** $$ R_{25} = \frac{\rho L}{A_{cs}} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times (0.080)}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} $$ Numerator: $$ (1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \times 0.080 = 1.3792 \times 10^{-9} $$ Dividing: $$ R_{25} = \frac{1.3792 \times 10^{-9}}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.07881 \;\Omega \approx 78.8 \;\text{m}\Omega $$ **(i) Voltage Drop at 2 A:** $$ V_{drop} = I \times R_{25} = 2 \times 0.07881 = 0.1576 \;\text{V} \approx 158 \;\text{mV} $$ **(ii) Power Dissipation:** $$ P = I^2 R = (2)^2 \times 0.07881 = 4 \times 0.07881 = 0.3152 \;\text{W} \approx 315 \;\text{mW} $$ **Step 3 — Resistance at 85 °C:** Temperature rise $\Delta T = 85 - 25 = 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ $$ R_{85} = R_{25} \left[ 1 + \alpha \Delta T \right] = 0.07881 \times \left[ 1 + (3.93 \times 10^{-3})(60) \right] $$ $$ R_{85} = 0.07881 \times [1 + 0.2358] = 0.07881 \times 1.2358 = 0.0974 \;\Omega \approx 97.4 \;\text{m}\Omega $$ **Interpretation:** The trace resistance increases by **~23.6%** due to a 60 °C temperature rise, demonstrating why thermal design matters for power-distribution traces. *(7 marks: 2 for resistance calc, 1 for voltage drop, 1 for power, 2 for temperature correction, 1 for interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Converting mm to m, µm to m, oz/ft² to µm is the most common cause of numerical errors. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Symmetry Violation:** A 4-layer stack-up must be **mirror-symmetric about its centre plane**. Asymmetric stack-ups cause warpage during reflow — a 2-mark deduction is typical. > 3. **Via vs. Pad Confusion:** A *via* is for inter-layer routing; a *pad* is for component soldering. The *annular ring* refers to the pad around a via or component hole, not the via itself. > 4. **Missing PTH Explanation:** In a double-layer board, students often forget that PTHs are the *only* electrical connection between top and bottom layers — emphasising this is worth a mark. > 5. **Plane Capacitance Overestimation:** Many students assume the intrinsic plane capacitance is sufficient for digital decoupling. It is *not* — it is a mid-frequency supplement. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Single-Sided PCB:** Copper on **one side**; no PTH; used in low-cost, low-complexity designs (toys, power supplies). - **Double-Sided PCB:** Copper on **both sides**; **PTHs are the only inter-layer connection**; component mounting on both faces. - **Multi-Layer PCB:** ≥ 3 conductive layers; built by lamination of cores + prepregs; common counts are 4, 6, 8, 10, 12+. - **Standard Stack-Up (4-layer):** Signal / Prepreg / GND / Core / PWR / Prepreg / Signal — **symmetric about the centre plane**. - **Prepreg:** B-stage (semi-cured) epoxy-glass; melts during lamination, then fully cures to bond layers. - **Core:** C-stage (fully cured) FR-4 with copper on both sides; provides mechanical rigidity. - **Plated Through-Hole (PTH):** Mechanically drilled, copper-plated hole providing electrical continuity between layers. - **Via Types:** Through-hole (all layers), Blind (outer→inner), Buried (inner→inner), Microvia (<150 µm, laser-drilled, 1–2 layer span), Via-in-Pad (under BGA/QFN). - **Standard Material:** **FR-4** (Dk ≈ 4.2–4.6, Tg 130–180 °C); high-speed designs use **Rogers, Isola, Megtron** (Dk 3.0–3.8). - **Standard Copper Weight:** **1 oz/ft² (35 µm)** for outer layers; **0.5 oz/ft² (17 µm)** for inner layers. - **Intrinsic Plane Capacitance:** ~70 pF/in² for a 0.36 mm core, 4.4 Dk FR-4. - **Design Rule Source:** **IPC-2221 / IPC-2222** (Generic and Rigid PCB standards). - **Surface Finishes:** HASL (Hot Air Solder Leveling), ENIG (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold), OSP (Organic Solderability Preservative), Immersion Tin/Silver. - **Symmetry Rule:** Always maintain **mirror-symmetric stack-up** to prevent warpage. - **Cost Driver:** Layer count, board area, drill count, surface finish, and minimum trace/space are the top cost levers in PCB fabrication. - **EMI/EMC Rule of Thumb:** Every high-speed signal needs a **continuous return path** — this is possible only with a dedicated reference plane (multi-layer board). ---

topics.title: PCB Materials, Component Package Types: Axial Lead
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** PCB Materials, Component Package Types: Axial Lead

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PCB Materials — Formal Definition

A **Printed Circuit Board (PCB)** is a laminated sandwich of alternating conductive and dielectric layers that mechanically supports and electrically interconnects electronic components using copper tracks, pads, and vias. The *materials* of a PCB refer to the specific substrate (base), conductive foil, bonding adhesives, protective coatings, and surface finishes chosen to satisfy electrical, mechanical, thermal, and chemical requirements defined by international standards (IPC-4101, IPC-6012, NEMA grades).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must identify the *base laminate family*, *copper-foil type*, *solder-mask chemistry*, and *surface finish* used in commercial PCBs, and justify their selection based on **Tg**, **Dk (dielectric constant)**, **Df (dissipation factor)**, **CTE**, and **thermal conductivity**.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a PCB as a **multi-storey building floor plan for electrons**. The **copper tracks** are the corridors and staircases, the **substrate (FR-4)** is the concrete floor holding everything up, the **solder mask** is the painted carpet that prevents accidental short-circuits (people stepping on wires), and the **surface finish (ENIG/HASL)** is the polished tile layer that keeps the floor from oxidising and wearing out. Pick the wrong floor material and the whole building cracks under heat (Tg failure) or becomes electrically noisy (high Df).

### 1.2 Axial Lead Component Package — Formal Definition

An **Axial Lead** package is a through-hole component whose two terminals (leads) exit the component body **along the same central longitudinal axis**, one from each end. The cylindrical or oblong body carries the active element (resistive film, semiconductor die, or wound element) while tinned copper or steel leads provide both mechanical mounting and electrical connection by passing through drilled holes in the PCB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Axial-lead resistor/capacitor/diode dimensions are governed by the **EIA RS-296** and **IEC 60062** standards. The metric body codes **0204, 0207, 0309, 0411, 0517, 0922, 1122** correspond to legacy imperial sizes 1/8 W, 1/4 W, 1/2 W, 1 W, 2 W, 3 W, and 5 W respectively.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **small pencil with wires sticking out of both ends**. Whether you draw a resistor, a diode, or a small capacitor, they all share the same "cigar with whiskers" shape. The "whiskers" (axial leads) go *straight through* the board, so the component lies flat like a soldier in a prone position, hugging the PCB surface. This is the classic look of 1970s–1990s electronics before Surface Mount Devices (SMD) took over.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Rigid PCB

A standard 2-layer rigid PCB is a composite of five functional layers, built bottom-to-top as follows:

1. **Substrate / Core (Base Laminate)** — Provides mechanical rigidity and primary electrical insulation.
2. **Copper Foil (Bottom Layer)** — Conductive traces, ground pours, and bottom-side pads.
3. **Prepreg (Bonding Sheet)** — B-stage epoxy-impregnated glass cloth that fuses copper to core under heat/pressure during lamination.
4. **Copper Foil (Top Layer)** — Conductive traces, signal paths, and component pads.
5. **Solder Mask (Top + Bottom)** — UV-curable epoxy/photoimageable lacquer that exposes only the pads while protecting the rest of the copper.
6. **Silkscreen (Legend)** — Epoxy-ink printing of reference designators, logos, and component outlines.
7. **Surface Finish (Top of exposed pads)** — Final metallic coating to prevent copper oxidation and ensure solderability.

### 2.2 Base Laminate Families — Comparative Analysis

| Family | Material Composition | Tg (°C) | Dk @ 1 MHz | Df @ 1 MHz | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FR-2** | Paper + Phenolic resin | 105 | 4.5 | 0.040 | Toys, calculators (low cost) |
| **FR-3** | Paper + Epoxy | 115 | 4.8 | 0.035 | Low-grade consumer |
| **FR-4** | Woven E-glass + Epoxy | 130–180 | 4.3–4.8 | 0.020–0.025 | **Industry standard** |
| **High-Tg FR-4** | Modified epoxy / multifunctional | 170–210 | 4.0–4.5 | 0.015 | Lead-free reflow, automotive |
| **CEM-1** | Paper core + E-glass faces | 110 | 4.6 | 0.030 | Single-sided consumer |
| **CEM-3** | Glass mat + E-glass faces | 120 | 4.5 | 0.025 | Replaces FR-4 in low-end |
| **Polyimide (Kapton)** | E-glass + Polyimide resin | 260+ | 3.5 | 0.010 | Flex PCBs, aerospace |
| **PTFE (Teflon / Rogers RO4000)** | Ceramic-filled PTFE | 280+ | 2.2–10.0 | 0.0009–0.0027 | RF / Microwave / 5G |
| **BT (Bismaleimide-Triazine)** | E-glass + BT resin | 180–220 | 3.9 | 0.012 | IC substrates, high-speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FR-4 dominates:** It offers the best **price-to-performance ratio** for the Tg, Dk, and Df window, plus it is **flame-retardant (FR) self-extinguishing** at < 10 s burn time per UL-94V0. KTU examiners love asking why FR-4 is preferred over FR-2.

### 2.3 Copper Foil — Two Manufacturing Routes

- **Electrodeposited (ED) Copper:** Made by electrolytic plating onto a rotating stainless-steel drum; produces a columnar grain structure with one rough (matte) side and one smooth (shiny) side. The matte side bonds better to the prepreg. Standard thickness: **1 oz/ft² (≈ 35 µm)**, **2 oz/ft² (≈ 70 µm)**.
- **Rolled-Annealed (RA) Copper:** Mechanically rolled and annealed; produces a flat, ductile, low-profile grain ideal for **flex PCBs** and high-frequency signal integrity. Costlier than ED.

### 2.4 Surface Finishes — Selection Logic

| Finish | Composition | Thickness | Shelf Life | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HASL (Sn-Pb or Sn-Ag-Cu)** | Tin-Lead solder | 1–40 µm | 12 mo | Low | Through-hole, prototypes |
| **ENIG** | Electroless Ni (3–6 µm) + Immersion Au (0.05–0.1 µm) | — | 12+ mo | High | Fine-pitch BGA, lead-free |
| **OSP** | Organic Solderability Preservative | 0.2–0.5 µm | 6 mo | Lowest | Consumer, high-volume |
| **Immersion Tin** | Sn (0.8–1.2 µm) | — | 6 mo | Med | Press-fit, fine-pitch |
| **Immersion Silver** | Ag (0.1–0.4 µm) | — | 9 mo | Med | High-frequency, lead-free |
| **Hard Gold (ENEPIG)** | Ni + Pd + Au | Au > 0.76 µm | 24+ mo | Highest | Wire bonding, edge connectors |

### 2.5 Axial Lead Component — Construction Anatomy

- **End Caps (Ferrules):** Pressed-on metal collars at each end of the cylindrical body. Provide electrical contact to the internal element and act as a mechanical anchor for the lead.
- **Lead Wire:** Tinned copper (resistors, capacitors) or dumet (glass-metal seal in diodes). Typical diameter: **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm**.
- **Body Coating:** Epoxy lacquer (resistors), epoxy or hermetic glass (diodes), mylar or ceramic sleeve (capacitors).
- **Active Element:** Carbon film, metal film, metal oxide film, wire wound, or semiconductor junction.

### 2.6 EIA Standard Power Ratings (Axial Resistors)

| Metric Body Code | Imperial Size | Diameter (mm) | Length (mm) | Lead Spacing (mm) | Power @ 70 °C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0204 | 1/8 W | 1.6 | 3.2 | 5.0 | **0.125 W** |
| 0207 | 1/4 W | 2.3 | 6.0 | 10.0 | **0.25 W** |
| 0309 | 1/2 W | 3.2 | 9.0 | 12.5 | **0.50 W** |
| 0411 | 1 W | 4.5 | 11.5 | 15.0 | **1.00 W** |
| 0617 | 2 W | 6.0 | 17.0 | 20.0 | **2.00 W** |
| 0922 | 3 W | 9.0 | 22.0 | 25.0 | **3.00 W** |
| 1122 | 5 W | 11.0 | 22.0 | 30.0 | **5.00 W** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Derating Rule:** Above **70 °C**, derate the resistor linearly to **zero power at the maximum working temperature** (typically 155 °C or 250 °C for military grade). Equation:
>
> $$P_{\text{applied}}(T) = P_{70°C} \times \frac{T_{\max} - T_{\text{ambient}}}{T_{\max} - 70}$$
>
> where temperatures are in °C. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question** for Part A.

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Copper foil thickness | $t = 1 \text{ oz/ft}^2 = 35 \, \mu m$ | µm | 1 oz = 28.35 g; area = 1 ft² |
| 2 | Trace current capacity (IPC-2152) | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | A | Internal traces, k ≈ 0.024 |
| 3 | Resistor derating | $P_{derated} = P_{70} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\max} - T_a}{T_{\max} - 70}$ | W | T in °C |
| 4 | Characteristic impedance (microstrip) | $Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | Ω | For w/h = 0.1 to 3.0 |
| 5 | Dielectric loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{Q} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}$ | — | Lower = better at high freq |
| 6 | Wave propagation delay (FR-4) | $t_{pd} \approx 6.6 \, \text{ps/mm} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | ps/mm | 1.6 mm = 0.1 in |
| 7 | Via inductance (approx.) | $L \approx 1.0 \text{ nH/mm}$ (for 0.4 mm via) | nH | Critical for power decoupling |
| 8 | Thermal resistance (resistor) | $\theta_{JA} = \dfrac{T_J - T_A}{P_d}$ | °C/W | From datasheet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipes inside the table have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown parser happy.** The KTU formula table above is the only place you will find these forms; everywhere else LaTeX delimiters `$$ ... $$` are used.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Material Selection** drives every subsequent manufacturing step — wrong Tg causes **delamination** during lead-free reflow at 260 °C; wrong Df causes **signal integrity loss** in 5G; wrong CTE causes **via barrel cracking** in thermal cycling.
- **Axial Lead Components** are still preferred for **high-voltage, high-power, and mechanically stressed** applications (e.g., power-supply bleeder resistors, EMI chokes, rectifier diodes) where their long leads act as natural heat spreaders and stress relievers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resistor Power Derating Curve

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 7-mark question):** An axial lead resistor has a nominal power rating of **1 W at 70 °C** and a maximum permissible element temperature of **155 °C**. The equipment is to operate in an ambient of **85 °C**. Calculate the safe continuous power dissipation.

**Given:**
- $P_{70} = 1 \text{ W}$
- $T_{\max} = 155 \, °C$
- $T_{\text{ambient}} = T_a = 85 \, °C$

**Step 1 — Identify the derating regime.**

Because $T_a > 70 \, °C$, the resistor is operating *above* its rated reference temperature. The derating rule applies linearly until $P = 0$ at $T = T_{\max}$.

**Step 2 — Write the standard derating equation.**

$$P_{\text{applied}}(T_a) = P_{70} \times \frac{T_{\max} - T_a}{T_{\max} - 70}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known numerical values.**

$$P_{\text{applied}}(85) = 1 \, \text{W} \times \frac{155 - 85}{155 - 70}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and denominator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 155 - 85 = 70 \, °C$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 155 - 70 = 85 \, °C$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio and simplify.**

$$P_{\text{applied}}(85) = 1 \times \frac{70}{85} = \frac{14}{17} \, \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to a decimal and report.**

$$P_{\text{applied}}(85) \approx 0.8235 \, \text{W}$$

**Conclusion:** The resistor may only dissipate approximately **0.82 W** continuously at 85 °C ambient — about **82.4 %** of its nameplate rating. The design margin between the computed and rated values protects against hotspot self-heating beyond $T_{\max}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the linear assumption:** The derating curve is a *first-order thermal model* that ignores self-heating coupling. For high-precision work, iterative refinement is required: recompute $T_J = T_a + P_d \cdot \theta_{JA}$ and iterate until convergence.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Microstrip Characteristic Impedance on FR-4

**Problem Statement:** A signal trace on an outer FR-4 layer has width $w = 0.2 \text{ mm}$, copper thickness $t = 35 \text{ µm}$, and is separated from the ground plane by dielectric thickness $h = 0.36 \text{ mm}$. The laminate's relative permittivity is $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$. Compute the approximate characteristic impedance of the microstrip.

**Given:**
- $w = 0.2 \text{ mm}$
- $t = 0.035 \text{ mm}$
- $h = 0.36 \text{ mm}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$

**Step 1 — Use the Hammerstad-Jensen microstrip approximation for $0.1 \le w/h \le 3.0$.**

We first verify the ratio:

$$\frac{w}{h} = \frac{0.2}{0.36} \approx 0.556$$

Since $0.1 \le 0.556 \le 3.0$, the standard formula is valid.

**Step 2 — Compute the effective permittivity term (inside the square root).**

$$\varepsilon_r + 1.41 = 4.4 + 1.41 = 5.81$$
$$\sqrt{5.81} \approx 2.4104$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithmic argument.**

$$0.8 w + t = 0.8 \times 0.2 + 0.035 = 0.16 + 0.035 = 0.195 \text{ mm}$$
$$5.98 h = 5.98 \times 0.36 = 2.1528 \text{ mm}$$
$$\frac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t} = \frac{2.1528}{0.195} \approx 11.040$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$\ln(11.040) \approx 2.4022$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the impedance formula.**

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute all computed values.**

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{2.4104} \times 2.4022$$
$$Z_0 = 36.092 \times 2.4022$$
$$Z_0 \approx 86.7 \, \Omega$$

**Conclusion:** The trace presents an impedance of approximately **86.7 Ω**, which is significantly above the standard 50 Ω RF benchmark — indicating that the trace is too narrow for the chosen dielectric thickness. To match 50 Ω, $w$ must increase to roughly **0.6 mm** with the same $h = 0.36 \text{ mm}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the exact type of calculation KTU examiners set in **Module 1** to test the student's grasp of how PCB material choice (via $\varepsilon_r$) and geometry jointly govern signal integrity. Memorise the Hammerstad formula — it is a **guaranteed 7-marker**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Axial Lead vs. Radial Lead vs. SMD

| Criterion | Axial Lead (THT) | Radial Lead (THT) | SMD (Chip) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead orientation | Both ends, same axis | Both on one face, side-by-side | No leads; end terminations |
| Mounting style | Through-hole, body parallel to PCB | Through-hole, body vertical | Surface mount, reflow soldered |
| Mounting density | Low | Medium | Very high |
| Mechanical strength | Very high (long leads) | Medium | Low (solder joint only) |
| Power handling | High (long leads spread heat) | Medium | Low to medium |
| High-frequency performance | Poor (long leads = inductance) | Poor | Excellent (short paths) |
| Automated assembly | Yes (axial inserters) | Yes (radial inserters) | Yes (pick-and-place) |
| Repair / hand-soldering | Easy | Easy | Hard (fine pitch) |
| Cost (component) | Medium | Medium | Lowest at scale |
| Cost (assembly) | High (insertion + wave) | High | Lowest (reflow only) |
| Typical applications | Power resistors, rectifiers, fuses, tantalum caps | Electrolytic caps, MOVs | Modern digital, RF, mobile |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PCB Material Decision Tree

| Decision Factor | Pick | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Cost-sensitive consumer (toys, calculators) | FR-2 / CEM-1 | Lowest Tg is enough, no lead-free |
| General-purpose digital / analog (Arduino, IoT) | FR-4 (Tg 130–150) | Best price/performance |
| Lead-free reflow assembly (RoHS) | High-Tg FR-4 (Tg ≥ 170 °C) | Survives 260 °C peak reflow |
| Automotive under-hood (–40 to +125 °C) | High-Tg FR-4 + ENIG | Thermal cycling + corrosion |
| High-speed digital (DDR4, USB 3.x, PCIe) | Low-Df FR-4 or BT | Df < 0.012 to limit loss |
| RF / Microwave (24 GHz radar, 5G) | PTFE / Rogers RO4350B | Ultra-low Df, controlled Dk |
| Flex / wearable | Polyimide (Kapton) | Bendable, Tg > 260 °C |
| IC substrate (BGA, CSP) | BT or ABF | Ultra-low CTE matched to silicon |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cross-Section of a 2-Layer Rigid PCB

```mermaid
graph TD
    silkA[Silkscreen Legend - Top] --> smA[Solder Mask - Top]
    smA --> cuA[Copper Foil - Top Layer - 35 um]
```
