# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Board Footprint of an N-pin DIP

**Problem Statement:**
A 16-pin PDIP IC is to be mounted on a PCB. The pin pitch is 2.54 mm, the lead radius at the pad is 0.5 mm, and the row spacing is 7.62 mm. Calculate the total rectangular footprint (length × width) of the IC on the PCB.

**Given Data:**
- Pin pitch: $e = 2.54$ mm
- Number of pins: $N = 16$
- Pins per row: $N/2 = 8$
- Lead pad radius: $r_p = 0.5$ mm
- Row spacing: $D_1 = 7.62$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the formula for X-axis (horizontal) footprint.**
The horizontal span is determined by the number of pitches between the first and last pin in a single row, plus the pad radius on each end.

$$W_x = (N_{row} - 1) \cdot e + 2 r_p$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_x &= (8 - 1) \cdot 2.54 + 2 \cdot 0.5 \\
    &= 7 \cdot 2.54 + 1.0 \\
    &= 17.78 + 1.0 \\
    &= 18.78 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the formula for Y-axis (vertical) footprint.**
The vertical span equals the row-to-row spacing plus the pad radius on each end.

$$W_y = D_1 + 2 r_p$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_y &= 7.62 + 2 \cdot 0.5 \\
    &= 7.62 + 1.0 \\
    &= 8.62 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Present the final footprint.**

$$\boxed{W_x \times W_y = 18.78 \text{ mm} \times 8.62 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to a more intuitive unit.**

$$\text{Footprint area} = W_x \cdot W_y = 18.78 \times 8.62 = 161.88 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 1.62 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Conversion Logic (Verbal):** A 16-pin PDIP occupies roughly 1.62 cm² of PCB area. This is about 4× larger than an equivalent 16-pin SOIC package (~0.4 cm²), illustrating the space penalty of DIP in modern designs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Power Dissipation of a DIP IC

**Problem Statement:**
A 7400-series logic IC in a PDIP-14 package is specified with a maximum junction temperature $T_J^{max} = 150$ °C. The thermal resistance from junction to ambient is $\theta_{JA} = 90$ °C/W. The IC operates in an enclosure where the ambient temperature is $T_A = 45$ °C. Determine the maximum continuous power the IC can dissipate.

**Given Data:**
- Maximum junction temperature: $T_J^{max} = 150$ °C
- Thermal resistance: $\theta_{JA} = 90$ °C/W
- Ambient temperature: $T_A = 45$ °C

**Step 1 — Recall the thermal Ohm's law for semiconductors.**

$$P_{max} = \frac{T_J^{max} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \frac{150 - 45}{90} \\
        &= \frac{105}{90} \\
        &= 1.1667 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Round off to engineering significance.**

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 1.17 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation.**
The 7400 TTL gate IC in PDIP-14 typically dissipates only 10 mW per gate. With 4 gates per IC, total dissipation is ~40 mW, which is **far below** the 1.17 W limit. This means **no heatsink is required** for this specific device in this application.

**Conversion Logic (Verbal):** The DIP's high thermal resistance (90 °C/W) actually limits high-power applications, so DIP is rarely used for power ICs (which use TO-220, TO-247, or DPAK packages instead).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pin Count for a Given Board Area Constraint

**Problem Statement:**
A PCB designer has allocated a rectangular area of $25 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ for a DIP IC. The pin pitch is 2.54 mm and the row spacing is 7.62 mm, with $r_p = 0.5$ mm. Determine the maximum number of pins the DIP can have.

**Given Data:**
- Available X-axis length: $L_{avail} = 25$ mm
- Available Y-axis width: $W_{avail} = 10$ mm
- Pin pitch: $e = 2.54$ mm
- Row spacing: $D_1 = 7.62$ mm

**Step 1 — Check if the Y-axis dimension can accommodate the row spacing.**

$$D_1 + 2 r_p = 7.62 + 1.0 = 8.62 \text{ mm} \le 10 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the maximum number of pins per row using the X-axis constraint.**

$$L_{avail} = (N_{row} - 1) \cdot e + 2 r_p$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N_{row}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(N_{row} - 1) \cdot e &= L_{avail} - 2 r_p \\
(N_{row} - 1) \cdot 2.54 &= 25 - 1.0 = 24 \\
N_{row} - 1 &= \frac{24}{2.54} = 9.4488 \\
N_{row} &= 10.4488
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Take the floor (integer) since pin count must be an integer, and use even number for symmetric DIP.**

$$\boxed{N = 2 \times N_{row} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ pins (max)}}$$

**Conversion Logic (Verbal):** A 25 mm × 10 mm PCB area can accommodate at most a **PDIP-20**. This is exactly why classic logic ICs (e.g., 74LS245, 74LS374) are commonly available in PDIP-20 packages.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DIP vs. Competing Packages

| Parameter | DIP | SOIC | QFP | QFN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pin pitch (mm) | 2.54 | 1.27 | 0.80 | 0.65 |
| Max pin count (typical) | 64 | 28 | 304 | 84 |
| Mount type | Through-hole | SMT | SMT | SMT |
| Lead inductance (nH) | 8–12 | 4–7 | 2–4 | 0.5–1 |
| Lead capacitance (pF) | 0.5–1.0 | 0.3–0.6 | 0.2–0.4 | < 0.2 |
| Thermal resistance (°C/W) | 70–100 | 60–90 | 30–50 | 15–35 |
| Standoff height (mm) | 3.0–4.0 | 1.75 | 1.0–1.5 | 0.5 |
| Pin density (pins/cm²) | ~2.5 | ~10 | ~25 | ~50 |
| Board area for 16-pin | 1.62 cm² | 0.40 cm² | 0.20 cm² | 0.10 cm² |
| Compatible with sockets | Yes | No (clamshell) | No | No |
| Hand-solderable by hobbyist | Easy | Moderate | Hard | Very hard |
| Cost per pin | Low | Low | Moderate | High |

$$P_{left} \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N/2\}, \quad P_{right} \in \{N, N-1, \ldots, N/2+1\}$$

For a 16-pin DIP: Left row has Pins 1–8 (top to bottom), Right row has Pins 16–9 (bottom to top), when viewed from the **top side** of the IC.

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the layered internal construction of a Plastic DIP, showing how the silicon die connects to the external leads through bond wires and a lead frame.

**Engineering Note (KTU Board Expectation):**
The cross-section shows the **signal flow path**: PCB trace → solder joint → external lead → lead frame → bond wire → silicon die pad. Each transition contributes parasitic resistance, inductance, and capacitance. For a 2.54 mm DIP, the **total lead inductance is approximately 8–12 nH**, which limits high-frequency performance above 50 MHz.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Dual Inline Package (DIP). List any four salient features of DIP used in electronic hardware design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The Dual Inline Package (DIP) is a through-hole mounting package for integrated circuits in which two parallel rows of metallic leads extend perpendicularly from a rectangular plastic or ceramic body. The leads are inserted through holes drilled in a printed circuit board and soldered on the opposite side.

**Four Salient Features:**

1. **Standardised pin pitch:** The pin pitch is fixed at **2.54 mm (100 mil)** as per JEDEC standards, ensuring worldwide compatibility with breadboards, IC sockets, and PCBs.
2. **Symmetric dual-row lead configuration:** All pins lie in a single plane along two parallel rows, simplifying PCB layout and manual assembly.
3. **Through-hole mechanical strength:** The solder joints formed in plated through-holes provide superior mechanical anchoring compared to surface mount pads, making DIP robust against vibration and thermal cycling.
4. **Socket compatibility:** DIP packages can be inserted into machined IC sockets, allowing the IC to be easily removed or replaced — a key advantage in prototyping, programming, and repair.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the pin numbering convention of a 14-pin DIP with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The pin numbering of a DIP follows a **counter-clockwise** convention when the package is viewed from the top (component side), with Pin 1 identified by a notch, dot, or beveled edge on the package.

**Rules for a 14-pin DIP (N = 14, N/2 = 7):**

- **Left row (top to bottom):** Pin 1, Pin 2, Pin 3, Pin 4, Pin 5, Pin 6, Pin 7.
- **Right row (bottom to top):** Pin 8, Pin 9, Pin 10, Pin 11, Pin 12, Pin 13, Pin 14.

**Neat Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
       Notch (Pin 1 indicator)
            |
        [1] [14]
        [2] [13]
        [3] [12]
        [4] [11]
        [5] [10]
        [6] [ 9]
        [7] [ 8]
            |
   (Top view of the package)
```

**Note for KTU Examiners:** Students often incorrectly number the right row as continuing 8, 9, 10 ... from top to bottom. The correct convention is **counter-clockwise**, meaning the right row is numbered **bottom to top**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — 14 Marks]

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the internal construction of a Plastic Dual Inline Package (PDIP). Discuss the role of the lead frame and bond wires in signal transmission. [7 Marks]**

**(b) List any six advantages and four disadvantages of DIP packages. Why is DIP being replaced by surface mount packages in modern electronic hardware? [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The Plastic DIP is constructed in four distinct layers as shown in the cross-sectional diagram below:

```
   External Lead (Tin-plated Cu)
        |
   =====Lead Frame===== ==== (Alloy 42, 0.25 mm thick)
        |              |
   [Bond Wire]     [Bond Wire]   (25 µm Au wire)
        |              |
   ====================
   |   Silicon Die    |   (0.25-0.50 mm Si, Al metallization)
   ====================
   | Die Attach Pad  |   (Conductive epoxy / Ag-glass)
   ====================
   |   Mold Compound |   (Epoxy novolac, black)
   ====================
```

**Role of the Lead Frame:**
The lead frame is a stamped or etched **copper-alloy sheet** (typically Alloy 42 or C194) that serves three critical functions:
1. **Mechanical support** for the silicon die via the die attach pad.
2. **Electrical routing** from the die pads to the external leads through the lead-frame fingers.
3. **Heat spreading** from the die to the external leads and PCB, providing a thermal pathway.

**Role of Bond Wires:**
Bond wires (typically **25 µm diameter gold or aluminum wires**) provide the **electrical interconnection** between the aluminium bonding pads on the silicon die and the inner ends of the lead-frame fingers. They are welded using **thermosonic or ultrasonic bonding**. Each bond wire contributes approximately 1 nH of series inductance and ~0.1 pF of shunt capacitance, which limits the high-frequency performance of the DIP package.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Six Advantages of DIP:**

1. **Mechanical robustness:** Through-hole solder joints provide high pull strength (typically > 10 N per pin).
2. **Breadboard and protoboard compatibility:** DIPs can be plugged directly into solderless breadboards.
3. **IC socket compatibility:** Easy insertion and removal for programming, testing, and repair.
4. **Hand-solderable:** Even amateur technicians can solder DIPs without specialized tools.
5. **Visual inspection:** Solder joints are clearly visible on the underside of the PCB.
6. **High current per pin:** DIP leads can carry 1 A continuously, suitable for power applications.

**Four Disadvantages of DIP:**

1. **Large PCB area:** Pin pitch of 2.54 mm consumes 5–10× more board area than equivalent SMT packages.
2. **Limited pin count:** Practical maximum is ~64 pins; higher I/O devices cannot be packaged as DIPs.
3. **High lead inductance (8–12 nH):** Restricts use in high-frequency (> 50 MHz) and high-speed digital designs.
4. **Not compatible with automated pick-and-place:** Slower assembly speed and higher labour cost.

**Why DIP is Being Replaced by SMT:**
Modern electronic products (smartphones, laptops, IoT devices) demand **high component density, miniaturization, and high-speed signal integrity**. Surface mount packages (SOIC, QFP, QFN, BGA) offer:
- Pin pitches as small as 0.4 mm (vs. 2.54 mm for DIP).
- Lead inductances below 1 nH (vs. 8–12 nH for DIP).
- Compatibility with fully automated assembly lines.
- Reduced PCB area and weight.

Therefore, DIP has been relegated primarily to **prototyping, educational kits, IC sockets, and legacy industrial systems** where ease of handling outweighs miniaturization.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Compare DIP with SOIC and QFP packages under the following heads: pin pitch, lead inductance, thermal resistance, pin density, and assembly method. [7 Marks]**

**(b) A 24-pin PDIP IC is mounted on a PCB. The pin pitch is 2.54 mm, row spacing is 15.24 mm (wide-body), and pad radius is 0.5 mm. Calculate (i) the X-axis footprint, (ii) the Y-axis footprint, and (iii) the total board area. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

| Parameter | DIP | SOIC | QFP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pin pitch** | 2.54 mm | 1.27 mm | 0.50–0.80 mm |
| **Lead inductance** | 8–12 nH | 4–7 nH | 2–4 nH |
| **Thermal resistance (θJA)** | 70–100 °C/W | 60–90 °C/W | 30–50 °C/W |
| **Pin density** | ~2.5 pins/cm² | ~10 pins/cm² | ~25 pins/cm² |
| **Assembly method** | Through-hole | Surface mount (gull-wing) | Surface mount (gull-wing) |
| **Max pin count (typical)** | 64 | 28 | 304 |
| **Socket compatible** | Yes | No (clamshell only) | No |

**Conclusion:** The progression DIP → SOIC → QFP represents an evolution toward **finer pitch, lower parasitic effects, better thermal performance, and higher pin density** at the cost of increased assembly complexity.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Number of pins: $N = 24$, so $N/2 = 12$ pins per row.
- Pin pitch: $e = 2.54$ mm.
- Row spacing (wide-body): $D_1 = 15.24$ mm.
- Pad radius: $r_p = 0.5$ mm.

**Step (i): X-axis footprint.**

$$W_x = (N_{row} - 1) \cdot e + 2 r_p$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_x &= (12 - 1) \cdot 2.54 + 2 \cdot 0.5 \\
    &= 11 \cdot 2.54 + 1.0 \\
    &= 27.94 + 1.0 \\
    &= 28.94 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii): Y-axis footprint.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_y &= D_1 + 2 r_p \\
    &= 15.24 + 1.0 \\
    &= 16.24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii): Total board area.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= W_x \times W_y \\
  &= 28.94 \times 16.24 \\
  &= 470.0 \text{ mm}^2 \\
  &= 4.70 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** A 24-pin wide-body PDIP occupies **28.94 mm × 16.24 mm = 4.70 cm²** of PCB area.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Pin numbering direction:** Students often number the right row of a DIP as continuing 8, 9, 10 ... from top to bottom. The correct convention is **counter-clockwise**, so the right row goes **bottom to top** (Pin 8 at bottom, Pin 14 at top for a 14-pin DIP). This error alone can cost **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting pad radius in footprint calculation:** Many students compute footprint as $(N-1) \cdot e$ only, forgetting the $+ 2 r_p$ term. This results in a footprint that is **1 mm too small**, leading to an incorrect board layout. **Always include the pad radii.**
>
> 3. **Confusing narrow-body and wide-body DIPs:** A 24-pin DIP is typically a **wide-body** package with $D_1 = 15.24$ mm. Using the narrow-body value (7.62 mm) will give an incorrect Y-axis footprint.
>
> 4. **Ignoring counter-clockwise rule:** The DIP pin numbering is counter-clockwise when viewed from the **TOP** of the IC. Reversing this rule will cause students to miswire power and ground pins in practical exam problems.
>
> 5. **Using wrong unit for thermal resistance:** When computing $P_{max}$, ensure that $T_J$ and $T_A$ are both in °C (or both in K). Mixing scales will give a wildly wrong answer.
>
> 6. **Confusing DIP with SIP (Single Inline Package):** SIPs have only one row of pins; DIPs always have two parallel rows. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- DIP stands for **Dual Inline Package** — a through-hole IC package with two parallel rows of leads.
- **Standard pin pitch:** $\mathbf{2.54 \text{ mm}}$ (100 mil) — JEDEC standard.
- **Narrow-body row spacing:** $\mathbf{7.62 \text{ mm}}$; **Wide-body row spacing:** $\mathbf{15.24 \text{ mm}}$.
- **Pin numbering:** Counter-clockwise, starting from the notch indicator, viewed from the top.
- **Variants:** PDIP (plastic), CERDIP (ceramic), SDIP (shrunk pitch).
- **Footprint formula:** $W_x = (N/2 - 1) \cdot e + 2 r_p$ and $W_y = D_1 + 2 r_p$.
- **Thermal formula:** $P_{max} = (T_J - T_A) / \theta_{JA}$; for PDIP, $\theta_{JA} \approx 70$–$100$ °C/W.
- **Typical pin counts:** 4, 6, 8, 14, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 40, 48, 64.
- **Advantages:** Through-hole strength, breadboard compatibility, socket-friendly, hand-solderable.
- **Disadvantages:** Large footprint, limited pin count, high lead inductance, not SMT-compatible.
- **Mounting methods:** Through-hole, wave soldering, IC socket insertion.
- **Key applications:** Educational kits, prototyping, legacy industrial electronics, EPROM/BIOS sockets, hobbyist projects (Arduino shields often host DIP-compatible headers).
- **Comparison anchors:** DIP < SOIC < QFP < QFN in pin density and high-frequency performance.
- **Plated through-hole diameter:** 0.9–1.0 mm for standard 0.25 mm leads.
- **Standoff height:** 3.0–4.0 mm body above PCB for flux cleaning and inspection.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain DIP with a diagram," always include: (1) the **counter-clockwise pin numbering rule**, (2) the **2.54 mm pin pitch**, (3) **at least three advantages and two disadvantages**, and (4) **one comparison** with SOIC or QFP. This four-point structure covers 90% of KTU marking schemes for this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Outline (TO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transistor Outline (TO) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Transistor Outline (TO) package** is a standardized semiconductor device mounting configuration defined and registered by the **Joint Electron Device Engineering Council (JEDEC)** under the **JESD standards** series. The TO designation specifies the physical envelope, lead arrangement, mechanical dimensions, and thermal interface characteristics of a hermetically sealed (or plastic encapsulated) discrete semiconductor device such as a bipolar junction transistor (BJT), power MOSFET, voltage regulator, or thyristor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The TO numbering is **NOT** a sequence of performance ranking. Numbers (e.g., TO-92, TO-220, TO-3) refer to a **registration index in the JEDEC outline registry**, not to power rating, size progression, or technology generation. A higher number does **NOT** imply a better or larger package. The nomenclature follows the pattern: $$\text{TO} - \text{[Registration Index Number]}$$ For example: - **TO-18** → small metal-can package, originally for low-power signal transistors - **TO-92** → economical plastic package for low-power devices - **TO-220** → medium-power plastic package with mounting tab - **TO-3** → high-power metal package with flange mounting ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Uniform" Concept** > > Think of TO packages like **standardized school uniforms** in Kerala schools. The color, cut, and badge placement are fixed by a central authority (JEDEC) so that any tailor (manufacturer) anywhere in the world can produce the same garment (package), and any student (circuit designer) can expect the shirt to fit the same way regardless of who stitched it. > > In electronics, a transistor built in Bangalore, Munich, or Shenzhen with a "TO-220 outline" will have **identical pin spacing, hole diameter, and heatsink mounting pattern**. This allows **interchangeability, second-sourcing, and automated PCB design** without re-engineering the mechanical layout for every new vendor. ## 1.3 Why TO Packages Matter in Engineering Standardization via the TO outline family delivers four engineering benefits: 1. **Interchangeability** — Drop-in replacement across manufacturers. 2. **Automated Assembly** — Pick-and-place machines recognize standard lead pitches. 3. **Thermal Predictability** — Standardized thermal resistance ($\theta_{JA}$, $\theta_{JC}$) for heatsink design. 4. **Design Library Reuse** — PCB footprints (land patterns) can be pre-built in CAD libraries. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics Always Referenced in TO Package Datasheets** > - **Lead Pitch:** typically **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** — the legacy through-hole standard. > - **Lead Diameter:** **0.45 mm – 0.80 mm** depending on current rating. > - **Operating Junction Temperature ($T_J$):** typically **−55 °C to +150 °C** (military) or **0 °C to +150 °C** (commercial). > - **Thermal Resistance Junction-to-Case ($\theta_{JC}$):** ranges from **0.5 °C/W (TO-264)** to **83.3 °C/W (TO-92)**. > - **Soldering Temperature:** peak **260 °C** for **≤ 10 seconds** (JEDEC J-STD-020 for SMD variants). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a TO Package — Structural Breakdown Every TO outline package consists of **four mandatory structural elements**: 1. **The Die (Semiconductor Chip)** — The actual silicon die containing the transistor junctions. 2. **The Lead Frame / Post Pins** — Metallic conductors (Kovar, Alloy 42, or copper) that carry signals and power between the die and the external circuit. 3. **The Header / Mounting Base** — A metallic platform (or flange) that provides mechanical rigidity and the primary thermal conduction path to an external heatsink. 4. **The Encapsulant** — Either a **metal can** (Kovar/steel, hermetic) or an **epoxy molding compound** (plastic, non-hermetic) that protects the die from moisture, contamination, and mechanical stress. > [!NOTE] > **Hermetic vs. Non-Hermetic Distinction** > - **Hermetic packages** (TO-3, TO-18, TO-39): Metal can with glass-to-metal seal — leak rate < **10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s He** (MIL-STD-883). > - **Non-hermetic packages** (TO-92, TO-220, TO-126): Plastic epoxy — moisture sensitivity level (MSL) classified per J-STD-033. ## 2.2 Classification of TO Packages by Power Dissipation The TO outline family is conventionally grouped into three thermal classes. Understanding this classification is **high-yield for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions**. ### 2.2.1 Low-Power TO Packages ($P_D \le 1\,\text{W}$) These packages rely on **free-air convection** plus minimal lead conduction for cooling. The lead frame is the only meaningful heat path. | Outline | Encapsulation | Lead Count | Typical Pin Pitch | Thermal Class | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **TO-18** | Metal can | 3 | 2.54 mm | Low-power signal | | **TO-39** | Metal can | 3 / 6 / 8 | 2.54 mm | Low-power signal | | **TO-92** | Plastic epoxy | 3 | 1.27 mm | Low-power consumer | | **TO-226 (TO-92 var.)** | Plastic | 3 | 1.27 mm | Low-power alternate | ### 2.2.2 Medium-Power TO Packages ($1\,\text{W} < P_D \le 50\,\text{W}$) These packages introduce a **dedicated metal tab or heatsink slug** that bolts to an external heatsink. Thermal conduction shifts from the leads to the **case-to-heatsink path**. | Outline | Encapsulation | Mounting Feature | Typical Use | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **TO-126** | Plastic | Center tab with hole | Voltage regulators, audio | | **TO-220** | Plastic | Metal tab with hole | Power MOSFETs, regulators | | **TO-218 (TO-3P)** | Plastic | Larger tab | Higher-current MOSFETs | | **TO-225 (TO-126 var.)** | Plastic | Center tab | Discrete transistors | ### 2.2.3 High-Power TO Packages ($P_D > 50\,\text{W}$) These packages feature a **massive metallic flange** that forms the body of the device, designed to be bolted directly to a chassis or large heatsink. | Outline | Encapsulation | Mounting Feature | Typical Use | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **TO-3** | Metal can | Flange with 2 holes | Power transistors, audio amps | | **TO-66** | Metal can | Smaller flange | Older power transistors | | **TO-247** | Plastic | Wide tab with 2 holes | High-current MOSFETs/IGBTs | | **TO-264** | Plastic | Extra-wide tab | Very high power IGBTs | ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **exam-critical**. Memorize each variable definition. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $P_D = \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$ | Maximum power dissipation in free air | W | | 2 | $P_D = \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_C}{\theta_{JC}}$ | Maximum power dissipation with case temperature controlled | W | | 3 | $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | Junction temperature under free-air operation | °C | | 4 | $T_J = T_C + P_D \cdot \theta_{JC}$ | Junction temperature with heatsink | °C | | 5 | $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$ | Total junction-to-ambient thermal resistance | °C/W | | 6 | $T_J(\text{safety}) = T_J(\max) - \text{Derating Margin}$ | Recommended design junction temperature (typically 25 °C below max) | °C | > [!NOTE] > **Variable Legend (must memorize for KTU):** > - $T_J$ = Junction temperature > - $T_A$ = Ambient temperature > - $T_C$ = Case temperature > - $\theta_{JA}$ = Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance > - $\theta_{JC}$ = Junction-to-case thermal resistance > - $\theta_{CS}$ = Case-to-heatsink thermal resistance (≈ 0.5 °C/W with thermal compound) > - $\theta_{SA}$ = Heatsink-to-ambient thermal resistance > - $P_D$ = Power dissipation ## 2.4 Thermal Derating — The Engineering Reality > [!IMPORTANT] > Datasheet power ratings assume $T_A = 25\,°C$. At higher ambient temperatures, the allowable $P_D$ **decreases linearly** until it reaches zero at $T_J(\max)$. $$\boxed{P_D(\text{derated}) = P_D(\max) \times \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{T_J(\max) - 25\,°C}}$$ This is the linear derating law used universally in KTU power-electronics problems. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Typical TO Package Used | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---| | **Linear Power Supplies (LM7812)** | TO-220 | Easy bolt-on heatsink, ~15 W handling | | **Audio Amplifier Output Stages** | TO-3, TO-264 | High dissipation, rugged flange | | **3-Phase Motor Drive IGBTs** | TO-247, TO-264 | High current, screw-mountable tab | | **Microcontroller Reset Supervisors** | TO-92 | Low cost, 3-lead simplicity | | **RF Small-Signal Amplifiers (2N2222)** | TO-18, TO-92 | Short leads, low parasitic inductance | | **Automotive ECU Drivers** | TO-220, TO-263 | Vibration-tolerant, AEC-Q101 qualified | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Thermal Ohm's Law to Heatsink Sizing The TO package thermal model is the **direct electrical analog** of Ohm's law. We can derive every relation by recognizing the formal substitution: $$\boxed{V \;\longleftrightarrow\; \Delta T, \quad I \;\longleftrightarrow\; P_D, \quad R \;\longleftrightarrow\; \theta}$$ ### Step 1 — Define the Thermal Circuit A TO-220 mounted on a heatsink forms a **series thermal path**: $$\text{Junction} \xrightarrow{\theta_{JC}} \text{Case} \xrightarrow{\theta_{CS}} \text{Sink} \xrightarrow{\theta_{SA}} \text{Air}$$ Applying the thermal analog of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law: $$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange for Junction Temperature $$T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$$ This is the **master equation** for any heatsink design problem in KTU exams. ### Step 3 — Solve for Required Heatsink Resistance Given a target junction temperature $T_J^{\text{target}}$, a known power dissipation $P_D$, a known ambient $T_A$, and a chosen TO package (which fixes $\theta_{JC}$): $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{SA}^{\text{required}} &= \dfrac{T_J^{\text{target}} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} \\[4pt] \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Substitution Logic:** We subtract the two known internal resistances ($\theta_{JC}$, $\theta_{CS}$) from the total allowable thermal budget to isolate the only remaining unknown — the heatsink's own resistance $\theta_{SA}$. ### Step 4 — Apply Numerical Example (KTU Typical) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Numerical Problem — TO-220 Thermal Design** **Given:** - Package: TO-220 with $\theta_{JC} = 3.0\,°C/W$ (datasheet) - Insulator pad + thermal grease: $\theta_{CS} = 1.0\,°C/W$ - Power dissipation: $P_D = 8\,\text{W}$ - Ambient temperature: $T_A = 50\,°C$ - Maximum junction temperature: $T_J(\max) = 150\,°C$ - Design target: keep $T_J \le 125\,°C$ (25 °C derating margin) **Step 4.1 — Compute the total allowable thermal resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{JA}^{\max} &= \dfrac{T_J^{\text{target}} - T_A}{P_D} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{125\,°C - 50\,°C}{8\,\text{W}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{75\,°C}{8\,\text{W}} = 9.375\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.2 — Subtract the internal thermal resistances:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{SA}^{\text{required}} &= \theta_{JA}^{\max} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} \\[4pt] &= 9.375 - 3.0 - 1.0 \\[4pt] &= 5.375\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.3 — Select a heatsink:** The student selects a standard extruded aluminum heatsink rated at $\theta_{SA} = 5.0\,°C/W$ (slightly better than required for safety). **Step 4.4 — Verification — Recompute $T_J$:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}) \\[4pt] &= 50 + 8 \cdot (3.0 + 1.0 + 5.0) \\[4pt] &= 50 + 8 \cdot 9.0 \\[4pt] &= 50 + 72 \\[4pt] &= 122\,°C \;\;\le 125\,°C \;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** A 5 °C/W heatsink keeps the junction safely below the 125 °C target. ## 3.2 Derivation: Linear Power Derating Curve > [!NOTE] > **Derivation of the derating equation from first principles.** A silicon device is rated for maximum power $P_D(\max)$ at $T_A = 25\,°C$. The maximum junction temperature $T_J(\max)$ is a fixed material property. By thermal Ohm's law: $$ \begin{aligned} P_D(\max) &= \dfrac{T_J(\max) - 25\,°C}{\theta_{JA}} \end{aligned} $$ At an arbitrary ambient $T_A$, the derated power becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} P_D(T_A) &= \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{\theta_{JA}} \end{aligned} $$ Dividing the two: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_D(T_A)}{P_D(\max)} &= \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{T_J(\max) - 25\,°C} \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying both sides by $P_D(\max)$: $$\boxed{P_D(T_A) = P_D(\max) \times \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{T_J(\max) - 25\,°C}}$$ This is the **derating law** that must be applied to every TO package above 25 °C. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right TO Package | Design Constraint | Recommended Outline | Rationale | |:---|:---:|:---| | $P_D < 0.5\,\text{W}$, no heatsink | **TO-92 / TO-18** | Free-air cooling is sufficient | | $P_D \approx 1\text{–}15\,\text{W}$, bolt-on heatsink | **TO-220** | Industry default, easy sourcing | | $P_D \approx 15\text{–}50\,\text{W}$, high current | **TO-247 / TO-218** | Larger tab, lower $\theta_{JC}$ | | $P_D > 50\,\text{W}$, rugged chassis mount | **TO-3 / TO-264** | Flange-style for maximum heat transfer | | Hermetic / military / aerospace | **TO-18 / TO-39** | Metal-can, glass-sealed | | Surface-mount equivalent needed | **TO-252 (DPAK), TO-263 (D²PAK)** | SMD counterparts of TO-220 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The TO Package Family Classification The following Mermaid block diagram maps the TO outline family into a functional taxonomy based on **power class and encapsulation style**. ```mermaid graph TD A[TO OUTLINE PACKAGE FAMILY]:::root A --> B[LOW POWER PD less than 1W]:::low A --> C[MEDIUM POWER 1W to 50W]:::med A --> D[HIGH POWER greater than 50W]:::high B --> B1[TO-18 Metal Can]:::node1 B --> B2[TO-39 Metal Can]:::node2 B --> B3[TO-92 Plastic]:::node3 B --> B4[TO-226 Plastic]:::node4 C --> C1[TO-126 Plastic Tab]:::node5 C --> C2[TO-220 Plastic Tab]:::node6 C --> C3[TO-218 Plastic Tab]:::node7 C --> C4[TO-225 Plastic Tab]:::node8 D --> D1[TO-3 Metal Flange]:::node9 D --> D2[TO-66 Metal Flange]:::node10 D --> D3[TO-247 Plastic Tab]:::node11 D --> D4[TO-264 Plastic Tab]:::node12 B1 --> E1[Small Signal BJTs]:::app1 B2 --> E2[RF Amplifiers]:::app2 B3 --> E3[Consumer Voltage Regs]:::app3 B4 --> E3 C1 --> E4[Audio Drivers]:::app4 C2 --> E5[Power MOSFETs]:::app5 C3 --> E5 C4 --> E4 D1 --> E6[High Power Audio]:::app6 D2 --> E6 D3 --> E7[Motor Drive IGBTs]:::app7 D4 --> E7 classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef low fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#1a4d1a,color:#000 classDef med fill:#f7d774,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000 classDef high fill:#e57373,stroke:#5a0000,color:#000 classDef node1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node5 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node6 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node7 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node8 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node9 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node10 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node11 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef node12 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333 classDef app1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app2 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app3 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app4 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app5 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app6 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 classDef app7 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — TO-220 Internal Cross-Section (Block Form) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **internal layered structure** of a TO-220 package as a stacked functional architecture, since the physical cross-section cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid. ```mermaid graph TD L1[LAYER 1 EXTERNAL LEADS
Tin plated copper alloy]:::layer1 L2[LAYER 2 LEAD FRAME
Copper or Alloy 42 strip]:::layer2 L3[LAYER 3 DIE ATTACH
Solder or conductive epoxy]:::layer3 L4[LAYER 4 SILICON DIE
Transistor junctions active area]:::layer4 L5[LAYER 5 BOND WIRES
Aluminum or gold 25um diameter]:::layer5 L6[LAYER 6 METAL HEADER TAB
Copper slug with mounting hole]:::layer6 L7[LAYER 7 ENCAPSULANT
Black epoxy molding compound]:::layer7 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 L6 -. heat flow path .-> HF[EXTERNAL HEATSINK
Bolt on tab with thermal grease]:::heat HF -. thermal resistance theta SA .-> AIR[AMBIENT AIR]:::ambient classDef layer1 fill:#fbbc04,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000 classDef layer2 fill:#f6993f,stroke:#7a4500,color:#000 classDef layer3 fill:#c69214,stroke:#5a3d00,color:#000 classDef layer4 fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#1a3d7a,color:#000 classDef layer5 fill:#a64d79,stroke:#5a0033,color:#fff classDef layer6 fill:#999999,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef layer7 fill:#274e13,stroke:#0a1a04,color:#fff classDef heat fill:#cc0000,stroke:#5a0000,color:#fff classDef ambient fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Block — TO Package Thermal Equivalent Circuit The thermal model of a TO package is the direct electrical analog. The following block diagram maps **each thermal resistance to a resistor symbol**, and **each temperature to a voltage node**. ```mermaid graph LR TJ[JUNCTION TEMP TJ
Voltage Node]:::nodeT VJC[theta JC
Internal Junction to Case]:::resistor TC[CASE TEMP TC
Voltage Node]:::nodeT VCS[theta CS
Interface Material]:::resistor TS[SINK TEMP TS
Voltage Node]:::nodeT VSA[theta SA
External Heatsink]:::resistor TA[AMBIENT TEMP TA
Voltage Node]:::nodeT PD[POWER DISSIPATION PD
Equivalent to Current Source]:::source TJ --> VJC --> TC --> VCS --> TS --> VSA --> TA PD -. injects at junction .-> TJ classDef nodeT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef source fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1a3d7a,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **thermal equivalent circuit** used universally in KTU power-electronics and electronics-instrumentation problems. Current $P_D$ flows from the junction node (highest potential) to the ambient node (lowest potential), and the voltage drops across each resistor represent the temperature drops across each thermal interface. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q: Define a "Transistor Outline (TO) package" and explain the role of JEDEC in its standardization.** **Model Answer:** A Transistor Outline (TO) package is a standardized mechanical and thermal envelope used to house a discrete semiconductor device such as a BJT, MOSFET, or voltage regulator. It defines the physical dimensions, lead configuration, and mounting hole pattern of the device. **JEDEC** (Joint Electron Device Engineering Council) is the semiconductor industry standards body under the **Electronic Industries Alliance (EIA)** that registers and publishes these outline numbers. JEDEC's role is to: 1. Maintain a **central registry** of outline numbers (TO-3, TO-92, etc.). 2. Publish the **mechanical drawings and dimensions** (in mm and inches) for each outline. 3. Ensure **multi-vendor interchangeability** so a transistor from manufacturer A is mechanically identical to one from manufacturer B. 4. Provide **standardized thermal test conditions** so that $\theta_{JC}$ and $\theta_{JA}$ values are measured uniformly. Without JEDEC standardization, every PCB design would require a unique footprint per vendor, making design reuse and second-sourcing impossible. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between a TO-92 and a TO-220 package in terms of construction, lead pitch, and typical power dissipation.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TO-92 | TO-220 | |:---|:---:|:---:| | **Encapsulation** | Plastic epoxy, no separate tab | Plastic epoxy with a metal tab and mounting hole | | **Number of leads** | 3 (Collector, Base, Emitter) | 3 (Drain, Gate, Source) plus the tab as the 4th thermal terminal | | **Lead pitch** | 1.27 mm (compact) | 2.54 mm (standard 0.1″) | | **Lead alignment** | In-line, flat | Staggered (middle lead offset) for high-voltage clearance | | **Typical power dissipation (free air)** | ~0.6 W | ~2 W (without heatsink) | | **Power dissipation with heatsink** | Not normally heatsinked | ~50–75 W with adequate heatsink | | **$\theta_{JA}$ (typical)** | ~83 °C/W | ~62 °C/W (without heatsink) | | **Typical applications** | Small-signal transistors (BC547), regulators (78L05) | Power MOSFETs, LM7812 regulators, motor drivers | **Key Distinction:** The TO-220's defining feature is the **integrated metal tab with a mounting hole**, which allows the case itself to be bolted to a heatsink — a feature absent in the TO-92. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations**, every Module question in Part B carries an **internal choice** — the student answers EITHER Option A OR Option B in full. Each option is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` **Option A:** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the internal construction of a TO-220 package. List any three applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A power transistor in a TO-3 package has the following specifications: - $\theta_{JC} = 1.5\,°C/W$, $\theta_{CS} = 0.5\,°C/W$ (mica insulator with grease) - $T_J(\max) = 175\,°C$, $T_A = 40\,°C$, $P_D = 25\,\text{W}$ Calculate the **required heatsink thermal resistance** $\theta_{SA}$ to keep the junction below **150 °C** (25 °C derating margin). **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 Marks] **Internal Construction of a TO-220 Package:** A TO-220 package is built in seven distinct functional layers: 1. **Silicon die** — the active transistor junctions are fabricated on a thin silicon chip (typically 1 mm × 1 mm to 4 mm × 4 mm depending on current rating). 2. **Die attach** — the die is soldered (or epoxy-bonded for cost reasons) onto a **copper heat-spreader slug** that forms the metal tab. 3. **Bond wires** — fine aluminum wires (25 µm diameter) connect the die bond pads to the **three lead frame posts** (corresponding to the three device terminals). 4. **Lead frame** — a stamped copper or Alloy 42 strip provides the external leads, designed to be inserted into PCB through-holes at 2.54 mm pitch. 5. **Metal tab** — the copper tab extends out of the plastic body, featuring a **3.5 mm diameter mounting hole** for an M3 screw to attach a heatsink. 6. **Plastic encapsulant** — black epoxy molding compound surrounds and seals the die, lead frame, and bond wires, providing electrical insulation and moisture protection. 7. **Lead plating** — external leads are plated with tin (or tin-lead) for solderability. **Three applications of TO-220 packages:** 1. **LM78xx / LM79xx linear voltage regulators** — the canonical TO-220 use case in power supplies. 2. **IRF540 / IRF3205 power MOSFETs** — motor control, SMPS switching stages. 3. **TRIAC dimmers (BT136, BTA16)** — AC phase-angle control in lamp dimmers and fan regulators. **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 above for the layered Mermaid block). --- #### Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** - $\theta_{JC} = 1.5\,°C/W$ - $\theta_{CS} = 0.5\,°C/W$ - $T_J(\max) = 175\,°C$, design target $T_J^{\text{target}} = 150\,°C$ (25 °C derating margin) - $T_A = 40\,°C$ - $P_D = 25\,\text{W}$ **Step 1 — Compute the total allowable thermal resistance from junction to ambient:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{JA}^{\max} &= \dfrac{T_J^{\text{target}} - T_A}{P_D} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{150 - 40}{25} = \dfrac{110}{25} = 4.4\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract the known internal resistances:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{SA}^{\text{required}} &= \theta_{JA}^{\max} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} \\[4pt] &= 4.4 - 1.5 - 0.5 \\[4pt] &= 2.4\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Select a standard heatsink and verify:** Select a heatsink rated at $\theta_{SA} = 2.0\,°C/W$ (slightly better than the 2.4 °C/W requirement for safety margin). **Verification of $T_J$:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}) \\[4pt] &= 40 + 25 \cdot (1.5 + 0.5 + 2.0) \\[4pt] &= 40 + 25 \cdot 4.0 \\[4pt] &= 40 + 100 = 140\,°C \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $T_J = 140\,°C < 150\,°C$ target. ✅ **Final Answer:** The required heatsink thermal resistance is $\theta_{SA} \le 2.4\,°C/W$. A commercially available 2 °C/W extruded aluminum heatsink satisfies the design with a 10 °C safety margin on the junction temperature. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Option B:** **(a)** List and briefly describe any **four common TO package outlines** used for power transistors. Compare their thermal resistance ($\theta_{JC}$) values. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A voltage regulator in a **TO-220 package** is to operate at $P_D = 7\,\text{W}$ in an environment with $T_A = 60\,°C$. The regulator's $T_J(\max) = 150\,°C$ and the package has $\theta_{JC} = 3.0\,°C/W$ with an interface resistance of $\theta_{CS} = 1.0\,°C/W$. **Without any heatsink**, will the device survive? If not, determine the **minimum heatsink $\theta_{SA}$** required to keep $T_J \le 125\,°C$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part B (a) [7 Marks] **Four Common TO Power-Transistor Outlines:** **1. TO-126 (Plastic, with center tab)** - **Construction:** Plastic body, three inline leads, small center tab with a 3.2 mm mounting hole. - **Power rating:** ~1.3 W free air, ~10 W with small heatsink. - **Typical $\theta_{JC}$:** ~10 °C/W. - **Applications:** Low-power audio amplifiers, small linear regulators (78L series in some variants). **2. TO-220 (Plastic, with large tab)** - **Construction:** Plastic body, three staggered leads, full-width copper tab with 3.5 mm hole. - **Power rating:** ~2 W free air, up to ~50–75 W with adequate heatsink. - **Typical $\theta_{JC}$:** ~3.0 °C/W. - **Applications:** LM78xx regulators, IRF series power MOSFETs, motor drivers, TRIACs. **3. TO-247 (Plastic, wide tab with two holes)** - **Construction:** Plastic body, three leads, wide copper tab with **two** 3.5 mm mounting holes for distributed clamping force. - **Power rating:** ~3 W free air, up to ~125 W with proper heatsinking. - **Typical $\theta_{JC}$:** ~1.5 °C/W. - **Applications:** High-current IGBTs in UPS systems, induction heating, three-phase inverter modules. **4. TO-3 (Metal can with flange)** - **Construction:** Solid metal can (Kovar or steel), two large pins emerging from the bottom, and the **entire metal case acts as the heatsink flange** with two 4.1 mm mounting holes. - **Power rating:** Up to ~250 W with adequate heatsink. - **Typical $\theta_{JC}$:** ~0.5–1.0 °C/W (lowest among common outlines). - **Applications:** 2N3055 power transistor, MJ15003, audio amplifier output stages, military-grade power conversion. **Thermal Resistance Comparison Summary:** | Outline | $\theta_{JC}$ (°C/W) | Indicative $P_D$ max with heatsink | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | TO-126 | ~10 | ~10 W | | TO-220 | ~3.0 | ~50 W | | TO-247 | ~1.5 | ~125 W | | TO-3 | ~0.5–1.0 | ~250 W | **Conclusion:** As the package size and tab area increase, $\theta_{JC}$ decreases, allowing greater power dissipation for the same temperature rise. --- #### Model Solution — Part B (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** - Package: TO-220, $P_D = 7\,\text{W}$ - $T_A = 60\,°C$, $T_J(\max) = 150\,°C$, design target $T_J^{\text{target}} = 125\,°C$ - $\theta_{JC} = 3.0\,°C/W$, $\theta_{CS} = 1.0\,°C/W$ - TO-220 free-air $\theta_{JA} \approx 62\,°C/W$ (datasheet value) **Step 1 — Check survival without a heatsink:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_J(\text{no heatsink}) &= T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} \\[4pt] &= 60 + 7 \cdot 62 \\[4pt] &= 60 + 434 \\[4pt] &= 494\,°C \end{aligned} $$ This is **catastrophically above** $T_J(\max) = 150\,°C$ — the device will **fail within milliseconds** if operated without a heatsink. The plastic body will melt and the die will thermally run away. **Step 2 — Compute the allowable total thermal resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{JA}^{\max} &= \dfrac{T_J^{\text{target}} - T_A}{P_D} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{125 - 60}{7} = \dfrac{65}{7} \approx 9.286\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the required heatsink resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{SA}^{\text{required}} &= \theta_{JA}^{\max} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} \\[4pt] &= 9.286 - 3.0 - 1.0 \\[4pt] &= 5.286\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Choose a standard heatsink and verify:** Select a standard TO-220 clip-on heatsink rated at $\theta_{SA} = 5.0\,°C/W$ (a widely available commercial value). **Verification of $T_J$:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_J &= T_A + P_D \cdot (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}) \\[4pt] &= 60 + 7 \cdot (3.0 + 1.0 + 5.0) \\[4pt] &= 60 + 7 \cdot 9.0 \\[4pt] &= 60 + 63 = 123\,°C \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $T_J = 123\,°C \le 125\,°C$ target. ✅ **Final Answer:** Without a heatsink, the device fails immediately ($T_J = 494\,°C$). A heatsink with $\theta_{SA} \le 5.29\,°C/W$ is mandatory. A commercially available 5 °C/W clip-on heatsink satisfies the design, yielding $T_J = 123\,°C$, leaving a 2 °C safety margin. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit Confusion in Thermal Resistance** — Students often confuse $\theta_{JA}$ and $\theta_{JC}$ or write "thermal resistance = 9" without the unit **°C/W**. Always include units. > > 2. **Forgetting the $\theta_{CS}$ Term** — Many students omit the case-to-sink interface resistance. This is a **mandatory 1-mark penalty** in KTU valuation. Always include the insulator pad and thermal grease contribution. > > 3. **Confusing Junction Maximum with Design Target** — Students compute with $T_J(\max) = 150\,°C$ instead of the derated design target of **125 °C**. Industry practice (and KTU board answers) **always** apply a 25 °C derating margin. > > 4. **Misidentifying Tab as a Pin** — In TO-220/TO-247, students often draw only 3 terminals. The metal tab is the **4th terminal** (electrically, it is usually the Drain/Collector, but thermally it is the heat-spreader). > > 5. **Mixing Pin Numbering Conventions** — The TO-220 viewed from the front (leads facing you, tab up) is **B-C-E** for BJTs, but the **datasheet** view (leads down, tab away) reverses the order to **E-C-B**. State the viewing convention explicitly. > > 6. **Wrong Derating Baseline** — Power derating starts at $T_A = 25\,°C$, not at 0 °C or 50 °C. Using the wrong baseline gives a non-conservative (unsafe) derated power. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transistor Outline (TO) Packages** > > - **TO** stands for **Transistor Outline**, registered by **JEDEC** (Joint Electron Device Engineering Council). > - TO numbers are **registration indices**, not a ranking of power or size. > - The three main structural elements are: **die + lead frame + encapsulant**. > - The **mounting tab/flange** is the **primary thermal path** in medium- and high-power outlines. > - **TO-18 / TO-39** = metal can, hermetic, low-power signal. > - **TO-92** = plastic, low-power, **1.27 mm** lead pitch (NOT 2.54 mm). > - **TO-220** = plastic with tab, medium-power, the **industry default** for power discrete devices. > - **TO-3** = metal can with flange, high-power, lowest $\theta_{JC}$. > - **TO-247 / TO-264** = plastic with wide tab, high-power, modern IGBT/MOSFET packaging. > - **Lead pitch is 2.54 mm** (0.1 inch) for most through-hole TO outlines, except TO-92 family. > - **Thermal Ohm's Law:** $P_D = \Delta T / \theta$, directly analogous to electrical $I = V/R$. > - **Total thermal resistance** is a **series sum:** $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$. > - **Derating equation:** $P_D(T_A) = P_D(\max) \times \dfrac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{T_J(\max) - 25\,°C}$. > - **Design target junction temperature** is $T_J(\max) - 25\,°C$ (industry standard derating margin). > - **Thermal compound** between case and heatsink contributes $\approx 0.5\,°C/W$ in $\theta_{CS}$. > - **SMD equivalents:** TO-252 (DPAK) ≈ TO-220, TO-263 (D²PAK) ≈ TO-220 (larger), TO-252 (DPAK) is the most common SMD power package. > - **Hermetic packages** are required for military, aerospace, and extended-temperature applications. > - **MOQ (Minimum Order Quantity) matters** — TO-92 is the cheapest outline; TO-3 is the most expensive per unit.

topics.title: Pin Grid Array (PGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pin Grid Array (PGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin Grid Array (PGA)** is a type of **surface-mount or through-hole integrated circuit (IC) packaging** in which the electrical contact pins are arranged in a regular, two-dimensional **square or rectangular matrix (grid pattern)** on the **underside (bottom face)** of the package. Each pin protrudes vertically from the package base and mates with a corresponding receptacle in a Zero Insertion Force (ZIF) or low-insertion-force socket mounted on the printed circuit board (PCB).

The PGA standard is governed by **JEDEC** (Joint Electron Device Engineering Council) specifications and is widely adopted in **microprocessors, FPGAs, ASICs, and high-pin-count VLSI devices** where mechanical robustness, thermal dissipation, and high I/O density are required.

### Key Classification of PGA Variants

| Variant | Full Form | Substrate Material | Typical Application |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **CPGA** | Ceramic Pin Grid Array | Alumina ceramic | High-reliability, military/aerospace |
| **PPGA** | Plastic Pin Grid Array | Thermoset plastic | Cost-effective consumer CPUs |
| **OPGA** | Organic Pin Grid Array | Organic laminate | High-performance desktop/server CPUs |
| **SPGA** | Staggered Pin Grid Array | Various | High-density pin configurations |
| **IPGA** | Interstitial Pin Grid Array | Various | Pin rows offset to increase density |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bristle Brush and Socket"**
>
> Imagine a **stiff hairbrush** (the PGA chip) with hundreds of fine metallic bristles (the pins) sticking straight down from its flat back. Now imagine a **foam pad with matching holes** (the PGA socket on the motherboard) where each hole lines up exactly with a bristle. You press the brush down, and every bristle slides into its corresponding hole, forming a complete electrical connection.
>
> - If the bristles were along only the edges, you'd have a **QFP (Quad Flat Pack)**.
> - If instead the bottom had tiny **solder balls**, you'd have a **BGA (Ball Grid Array)**.
> - With vertical metal **pins in a grid**, you have a **PGA**.

This makes PGA distinct: the **pins are mechanical, separable, and replaceable**, unlike soldered BGA or gull-wing QFP packages.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

- **Standard pin pitch**: **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** for classic through-hole PGA; **1.27 mm (0.05 inch)** for modern high-density variants.
- **Pin material**: Typically **Kovar** (Fe-Ni-Co alloy) or **phosphor bronze**, plated with **gold (Au)** or **tin (Sn)** for low contact resistance.
- **Operating temperature range**: **-65 °C to +150 °C** (ceramic variants); **0 °C to +85 °C** (plastic variants).
- **Pin resistance**: < **30 mΩ** per pin (typical).
- **Insertion/withdrawal cycles**: ≥ **50 cycles** for ZIF sockets (per JEDEC JESD22-B104).
- **Pin count range**: From **68 pins** (early Intel 486) to **> 2011 pins** (high-end server processors).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Keyword Cluster (Memorize These):**
> Pin Grid Array · Through-Hole / Surface Mount · ZIF Socket · Ceramic / Plastic / Organic PGA · Pin Pitch · JEDEC Standard · Pin Count Density · Thermal Performance · Motherboard Socket Compatibility
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The PGA is more than just a pin arrangement; it represents a deliberate **engineering trade-off** between four competing parameters:

1. **Pin Count (I/O Density)**
2. **Electrical Performance (Signal Integrity)**
3. **Thermal Conductivity**
4. **Mechanical Serviceability**

### 2.1.1 Structural Hierarchy of a PGA Package

- **Top Surface**: Heat slug / Integrated Heat Spreader (IHS) for thermal coupling with a heatsink.
- **Substrate Layer**: Ceramic (Al₂O₃) or organic (FR-4/BT resin) — provides mechanical rigidity and houses internal trace routing.
- **Die Cavity**: A central recess where the silicon die is mounted using **die attach epoxy** or **eutectic solder** (Au-Sn).
- **Wire Bonds / Flip-Chip Connections**: Aluminum or gold wires (or solder bumps) connecting the die pads to the substrate's internal lead frame.
- **Pin Field**: The bottom array of protruding pins, each connected through the substrate to a die I/O signal.

### 2.1.2 Why a Grid? — The "Why" Behind the Geometry

- **Perimeter-limited packages** (DIP, QFP) suffer from the **perimeter-growth problem**: to double the pin count, the package area must **quadruple** because pins only sit on the edges.
- A 2D **interior grid** allows pin count to scale with **area (N²)**, not perimeter (N), enabling hundreds of I/O pins in a compact footprint.
- This makes PGA ideal for **VLSI era microprocessors** requiring hundreds of address/data/control/ground/power pins.

### 2.1.3 Pin Orientation and Staggering

- **Standard PGA (SPGA)**: Pins form a simple square matrix, one pin per grid cell.
- **Staggered PGA**: Alternate rows/columns are offset by **half-pitch**, doubling pin density without changing pitch.
- **Interstitial PGA (IPGA)**: Pins placed in the gaps between standard positions — used in Intel Pentium Pro/II/III.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **high-yield quantitative tools** KTU examiners use in Part B numerical/analytical questions on PGA and packaging density.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **N = (L / p)²** | N = number of pins; L = package side length; p = pin pitch | Pin count for square grid |
| 2 | **L = p · √N** | L = side length; p = pitch; N = pin count | Compute package size given pin count |
| 3 | **ρ = N / A** | ρ = pin density (pins/cm²); A = package area | Compare packaging technologies |
| 4 | **A_package = (p·(√N - 1) + 2·r)²** | r = pin pad radius (PCB side) | Accurate footprint including pad geometry |
| 5 | **A_die / A_package ≤ 0.7** | Die-to-package area ratio (Keller's theorem limit) | Bond-wire length limitation |
| 6 | **R_pin = ρ_material · L_pin / A_cross** | R = pin resistance; ρ = resistivity | DC voltage drop / current carrying |
| 7 | **L_parasitic ≈ 1 nH / mm** | Inductance per pin length | Signal integrity / crosstalk estimation |
| 8 | **θ_JA = (T_J - T_A) / P** | Thermal resistance junction-to-ambient | Heat sink sizing |
| 9 | **N_staggered = (L/p)² · 2** | Doubled density for staggered pin layout | Staggered / interstitial PGA density |
| 10 | **Z_if (characteristic Z) = √(L/C)** | L = pin inductance; C = pin capacitance | Impedance matching for high-speed signals |

### 2.2.1 Boundary Conditions and Validity Ranges

- Formula 1 holds for **square matrices** only. For rectangular grids, use **N = (L₁/p) · (L₂/p)**.
- Formula 5 (Keller's theorem) is a **statistical limit** from wire-bond manufacturing — modern flip-chip PGA packages violate it deliberately.
- Formula 7 is a **rule-of-thumb**; actual parasitic inductance depends on pin geometry and is measured via TDR (Time-Domain Reflectometry).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

PGA packaging is the **backbone of upgradable computing platforms**:

- **Intel Socket 5/7/8** (Pentium I/II/III era) → used **PGA** for CPU mounting.
- **AMD Athlon XP (Socket A, 462-pin OPGA)** → organic PGA on a Socket-A motherboard.
- **Server-class CPUs** (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC older generations) → use **LGA today**, but earlier generations were **Socket F (1207-pin PGA)**.
- **FPGA prototyping boards** (Xilinx, Altera/Intel) → use PGA for high I/O reconfigurability.
- **Aerospace & military systems** → use **CPGA** for radiation tolerance and hermetic sealing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Why use PGA instead of BGA?", the answer is **serviceability** — PGA allows chip **removal and replacement** (essential for field upgrades, prototyping, and socketed motherboards), whereas BGA requires **re-flow soldering** and is essentially permanent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Pin Count from Package Dimensions (Square Matrix)

**Given:** A square PGA package with side length **L = 25.4 mm (1 inch)** and a pin pitch **p = 2.54 mm (0.1 inch)**.

**Find:** Maximum number of pins N, and the pin density ρ.

**Step 1 — Number of pin positions per row.**
The pins are placed at regular intervals, starting from the edge. The number of pitches fitting in L is:

$$n = \frac{L}{p}$$

Substituting the values:

$$n = \frac{25.4 \text{ mm}}{2.54 \text{ mm}} = 10$$

**Textual explanation:** A 1-inch edge can hold 10 pins spaced at 0.1 inch.

**Step 2 — Total pins in a square grid.**
Since the grid is square, the number of rows equals the number of columns. Total pins = rows × columns:

$$N = n \times n = n^2$$

$$N = 10^2 = 100 \text{ pins}$$

**Textual explanation:** A 10×10 grid yields 100 pin positions.

**Step 3 — Package area.**

$$A = L^2 = (25.4 \text{ mm})^2 = 645.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Pin density.**

$$\rho = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{100}{645.16} = 0.155 \text{ pins / mm}^2$$

Converting to per cm²:

$$\rho = 15.5 \text{ pins / cm}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Numerical:** This is a **classic 7-mark question pattern**. The examiner will give L and p and ask for N and ρ. Always show units and box your final answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Staggered PGA Doubling Density

**Given:** Same L = 25.4 mm, p = 2.54 mm, but with a **staggered** layout where a second pin occupies the **midpoint** of each adjacent row.

**Step 1 — Stagger offset.**
The offset is half-pitch:

$$\Delta = \frac{p}{2} = 1.27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective pitch between rows becomes:**

$$p_{\text{eff,row}} = \frac{p}{2} = 1.27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — New number of row positions.**

$$n_{\text{row}} = \frac{L}{p_{\text{eff,row}}} = \frac{25.4}{1.27} = 20$$

**Step 4 — New pins per row (columns unchanged, n = 10).**

$$N_{\text{staggered}} = n \times n_{\text{row}} = 10 \times 20 = 200 \text{ pins}$$

**Step 5 — Verify using Formula 9.**

$$N_{\text{staggered}} = \left(\frac{L}{p}\right)^2 \times 2 = (10)^2 \times 2 = 200 \text{ pins} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — New density.**

$$\rho_{\text{staggered}} = \frac{200}{645.16} = 0.31 \text{ pins / mm}^2 = 31 \text{ pins / cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU:** Staggering **doubles** the pin count for the same package area — a critical concept for explaining why modern PGA packages pack 1000+ pins.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Package Side Length for a Target Pin Count

**Given:** A modern microprocessor requires **N = 1366 pins** (e.g., Intel Itanium-class) using a standard pin pitch **p = 1.27 mm** in a square grid.

**Find:** Required side length L of the PGA package.

**Step 1 — Rearrange Formula 2:**

$$L = p \cdot \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$L = 1.27 \times \sqrt{1366}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square root:**

$$\sqrt{1366} \approx 36.96$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$L = 1.27 \times 36.96 = 46.94 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check via Formula 1:**

$$N_{\text{check}} = \left(\frac{46.94}{1.27}\right)^2 = (36.96)^2 = 1366 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to take the **square root** and instead compute L² = p²·N, giving an incorrect area answer in mm². Always remember: L is a **length** (linear dimension), not area.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Parasitic Inductance and Signal Integrity

**Given:** A PGA pin of length **ℓ = 5 mm** carries a high-speed signal at **f = 1 GHz**.

**Step 1 — Estimate inductance using Formula 7:**

$$L_{\text{parasitic}} \approx 1 \text{ nH/mm} \times 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \text{ nH}$$

**Step 2 — Compute inductive reactance at 1 GHz:**

$$X_L = 2 \pi f L = 2 \pi \times (1 \times 10^9) \times (5 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$X_L = 31.4 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
At 1 GHz, the pin's parasitic inductance presents **31.4 Ω** reactance, which can cause significant signal reflection if the trace impedance is mismatched (typical PCB trace is 50 Ω). Therefore, **shorter pins** or **matched-impedance sockets** are required for high-speed PGA applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** This is why **LGA (Land Grid Array)** has largely replaced PGA in modern high-speed CPUs — LGA uses flat contact pads (shorter electrical path, lower inductance, ~0.5 nH/pin).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a PGA Package

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Top Heat Spreader IHS] --> B1[Thermal Interface Material TIM]
    B1 --> C1[Silicon Die with Integrated Circuit]
    C1 --> D1[Die Attach Epoxy or Eutectic Solder]
    D1 --> E1[Substrate Ceramic or Organic]
    E1 --> F1[Internal Trace Routing Layer]
    F1 --> G1[Pin Field Bottom Grid of Pins]
    G1 --> H1[PGA Socket ZIF on Motherboard]
    H1 --> I1[PCB Copper Pads and Traces]

    C1 --> J1[Wire Bonds or Flip Chip Bumps]
    J1 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Follow the data flow from the silicon die (where computation occurs) through wire bonds to the substrate, then down through the pin grid into the socket, finally reaching the PCB. The heat spreader is a **separate thermal path** that does not carry electrical signals.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — PGA vs. BGA vs. LGA Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    PGA[PGA Pin Grid Array] -->|Vertical Pins| SOCK[ZIF Socket]
    SOCK -->|Replaceable| PCB1[PCB]

    BGA[BGA Ball Grid Array] -->|Solder Balls| REFLOW[Reflow Soldering]
    REFLOW -->|Permanent| PCB2[PCB]

    LGA[LGA Land Grid Array] -->|Flat Lands| SOCK2[Contact Socket]
    SOCK2 -->|Replaceable| PCB3[PCB]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Comparison Question Hook:** Use this diagram as a base to compare packaging types on **serviceability, electrical performance, cost, and thermal characteristics**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pin Grid Array Layout (Top View of Pin Field)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PG[Pin Grid Top View] --> R1[Row 1: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
    PG --> R2[Row 2: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
    PG --> R3[Row 3: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
    PG --> R4[Row 4: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
    PG --> R5[Row i: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
    PG --> Dot[...]
    PG --> R6[Row N: Pin1 Pin2 Pin3 ... PinN]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **logical representation** of the bottom view of a PGA. In a real package, you would see a **photograph or 3D CAD rendering** of the pin matrix, but the logic is identical: pins are indexed by (row, column) coordinates and each pin is assigned a unique signal name (e.g., A1, A2, B1, ..., per JEDEC nomenclature).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — PGA Manufacturing and Assembly Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Wafer Fabrication] --> S2[Wafer Dicing into Individual Dies]
    S2 --> S3[Die Attach to PGA Substrate]
    S3 --> S4[Wire Bonding or Flip Chip]
    S4 --> S5[Sealing or Encapsulation]
    S5 --> S6[Pin Attachment and Trimming]
    S6 --> S7[Electrical Test and Burn In]
    S7 --> S8[Marking and Packaging]
    S8 --> S9[Shipment to Motherboard OEM]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Define Pin Grid Array (PGA). Mention any two advantages of PGA over QFP packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pin Grid Array (PGA)** is an integrated circuit packaging technology in which the connecting pins are arranged in a **regular 2D array (grid)** on the **underside** of the package, rather than along the periphery. The pins are typically inserted into a **ZIF (Zero Insertion Force) socket** on the PCB.

**Two advantages of PGA over QFP:**

1. **Higher pin density:** A 2D interior grid allows **N² scaling** of pin count with package area, whereas QFP pins scale with perimeter. A 25 mm × 25 mm PGA can hold **~100+ pins**, while a same-size QFP holds only ~48 pins.
2. **Better mechanical robustness and replaceability:** Vertical pins seated in a ZIF socket are **mechanically strong** (resistant to bending) and allow easy **chip removal/replacement** for upgrades or servicing, unlike QFP's fragile gull-wing leads.

**Two more advantages (for extra credit):**
3. Shorter lead length → **lower parasitic inductance** → better high-frequency performance.
4. Superior **thermal conduction** through the ceramic substrate to the bottom pins (acting as heat conduits).

*(Word count ~110. 3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for advantages with brief justification.)*

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **Differentiate between Ceramic PGA (CPGA) and Plastic PGA (PPGA).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **CPGA (Ceramic PGA)** | **PPGA (Plastic PGA)** |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Substrate material** | Alumina (Al₂O₃) ceramic | Thermoset plastic (e.g., phenolic) |
| **Cost** | High (3–5× more expensive) | Low (mass-production friendly) |
| **Thermal conductivity** | Excellent (~25 W/m·K) | Moderate (~0.2 W/m·K) |
| **Hermeticity** | Hermetic seal (moisture-proof) | Non-hermetic, absorbs moisture |
| **Operating temperature** | -65 °C to +150 °C | 0 °C to +85 °C |
| **Reliability / Use case** | Military, aerospace, server | Consumer PCs, embedded systems |
| **Dimensional stability** | Excellent (low CTE mismatch) | Moderate |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for any valid distinction + 2 marks for elaborating two or more key differences.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEEVT612 Module 1]**
> *(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **constructional features of a Pin Grid Array (PGA) package**. Clearly label the die, substrate, wire bonds, and pin field. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* A square PGA package has a side length of **40 mm** and a pin pitch of **1.27 mm**. Calculate:
>   (i) Total number of pins.
>   (ii) Pin density per cm².
>   (iii) The new pin count and density if the pins are arranged in a **staggered** layout. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features of PGA (7 marks):**

A PGA package consists of the following stacked layers (from top to bottom):

1. **Heat Spreader / Integrated Heat Spreader (IHS):** A metallic cap (copper or aluminum) bonded to the top of the die to spread heat and provide a flat interface for an external heatsink.
2. **Silicon Die:** The actual integrated circuit containing the transistors and interconnects. It is mounted in a central cavity.
3. **Die Attach Material:** Either a conductive **epoxy** or **eutectic solder (Au-Sn)** that bonds the die to the substrate and provides thermal conduction.
4. **Substrate (Ceramic / Organic / Plastic):** A multi-layer board that provides:
   - Mechanical rigidity for the package.
   - Internal trace routing from the die pads to the pin field.
   - Power and ground plane distribution.
5. **Interconnections:** **Wire bonds** (aluminum or gold) or **flip-chip solder bumps** that connect the die's bond pads to the substrate's internal lead traces.
6. **Pin Field:** A regular 2D array of **vertical metallic pins** (Kovar or phosphor bronze, gold-plated) protruding from the underside. Each pin makes contact with the corresponding hole in the ZIF socket.
7. **Sealing (in CPGA):** A metal or ceramic lid hermetically seals the die cavity.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for layer-by-layer description with materials.)*

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above).**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** L = 40 mm, p = 1.27 mm

**(i) Total pins in a standard square grid (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{L}{p} = \frac{40}{1.27} = 31.5$$

Since pins must be whole numbers, we take **n = 31** (round down):

$$N = n^2 = 31^2 = 961 \text{ pins}$$

*(If students round to 32, N = 1024. KTU accepts either with a clear note about the rounding convention; examiners usually award full marks for 31 × 31 = 961 or 32 × 32 = 1024.)*

**(ii) Pin density per cm² (2 marks):**

Convert area to cm²:

$$A = (40 \text{ mm})^2 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2 = 16 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$\rho = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{961}{16} \approx 60.06 \text{ pins/cm}^2$$

(If using N = 1024: ρ = 64 pins/cm².)

**(iii) Staggered layout (3 marks):**

For staggered PGA, density doubles:

$$N_{\text{staggered}} = 2 \cdot N = 2 \times 961 = 1922 \text{ pins}$$

$$\rho_{\text{staggered}} = 2 \times 60.06 = 120.1 \text{ pins/cm}^2$$

**Conclusion:** Staggered PGA **doubles** the pin count for the same package footprint, demonstrating the power of the staggered layout for high-density microprocessor packages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample, OEEVT612 Module 1]**
> *(a)* Compare **PGA, BGA, and LGA** packaging technologies on the basis of **mounting technique, replaceability, electrical performance, and pin density**. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* Explain the significance of **JEDEC standardization** in PGA package design. Discuss the role of **pin pitch** in determining both **electrical performance** and **manufacturing yield**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | **PGA** | **BGA** | **LGA** |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Contact type** | Vertical pins | Solder balls | Flat contact lands |
| **Mounting technique** | Through-hole / socket | Surface-mount (reflow) | Surface-mount (socket) |
| **Replaceability** | Easy (ZIF socket) | Difficult (re-soldering) | Easy (socket) |
| **Pin density** | Medium (~60 pins/cm²) | High (~200+ pins/cm²) | High (~150+ pins/cm²) |
| **Parasitic inductance** | Moderate (~5 nH) | Low (~1 nH) | Very low (~0.5 nH) |
| **Signal integrity @ 1 GHz** | Acceptable | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Thermal performance** | Good (CPGA: excellent) | Excellent (ball = thermal path) | Good |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low (mass produced) | High |
| **Typical use** | Older CPUs, FPGAs | Modern SoCs, GPUs | Modern server CPUs |

**Key Takeaway:** BGA offers highest density but is non-replaceable. LGA offers best balance for modern server CPUs. PGA is favored when **serviceability and prototyping** matter most.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for each well-validated row, plus 1 mark for the concluding statement.)*

---

**(b) JEDEC Standardization and Pin Pitch Analysis (7 marks):**

**JEDEC Standardization (3 marks):**
JEDEC (Joint Electron Device Engineering Council) sets the **pin count, pin pitch, package outline, and thermal specifications** for PGA packages. Standardization ensures:

- **Interoperability** between CPUs from different vendors and motherboards from different OEMs.
- **Predictable manufacturing** — automated pick-and-place machines can handle standardized footprints.
- **Quality assurance** — defined test methods (e.g., JESD22-B104 for mechanical shock, JESD22-A104 for thermal cycling).
- **Cost reduction** — common tooling across the industry.

**Pin Pitch Analysis (4 marks):**

The pin pitch **p** is the **center-to-center distance** between adjacent pins. It has **inverse effects** on density and yield:

$$\text{Pin density } \rho = \frac{1}{p^2}$$

For example:
- p = 2.54 mm → ρ ≈ 15.5 pins/cm²
- p = 1.27 mm → ρ ≈ 62 pins/cm²
- p = 1.00 mm → ρ ≈ 100 pins/cm²

**Effects of finer pitch:**

1. **Higher density** (good).
2. **Shorter pins possible** → lower parasitic inductance → better high-speed signaling (good).
3. **Smaller PCB pad and via sizes** → more difficult soldering, higher probability of **bridging shorts** and **opens** (bad for yield).
4. **Tighter alignment tolerances** between package and socket → higher manufacturing cost and lower assembly yield.
5. **Reduced socket contact area** → potentially higher contact resistance and lower reliability.

**Conclusion:** A **p = 1.27 mm pitch** is the **industry sweet spot** for high-density PGA — fine enough for high pin counts, yet coarse enough for reliable manufacturing and ZIF-socket assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-Ups:** Students often compute density in pins/mm² when the question asks for pins/cm². Always convert: 1 cm² = 100 mm², so multiply by 100.
> 2. **Forgetting the Square Root:** In the formula **L = p·√N**, students write **L = p·N** and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Rounding Errors:** When L/p is not a whole number (e.g., 31.5), students must **explicitly state the rounding convention** and recompute.
> 4. **Confusing PGA with LGA:** PGA has **pins**; LGA has **lands (flat pads)**. This is the single most common confusion in KTU answers.
> 5. **Missing the Staggered-Doubling Insight:** Many students miss that staggered/interstitial PGA **doubles** pin density — this is a favorite 2-mark follow-up.
> 6. **No Diagram = -2 Marks:** For any 7-mark constructional question, a labeled diagram is **mandatory**. KTU 2024 scheme strictly enforces this.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **PGA = Pins on the bottom in a 2D grid**, inserted into a ZIF socket on the motherboard.
- [ ] **Three main variants:** CPGA (ceramic, military/hi-rel), PPGA (plastic, consumer), OPGA (organic, performance desktop).
- [ ] **Key formula:** `N = (L/p)²` for square grids; `N = (L/p)·(W/p)` for rectangular.
- [ ] **Staggered PGA doubles density** by offsetting alternate rows by `p/2`.
- [ ] **Pin pitch trade-off:** finer pitch → higher density, lower inductance, **but** lower manufacturing yield.
- [ ] **Standard pitches:** 2.54 mm (legacy), 1.27 mm (modern high-density).
- [ ] **Standard pin count range:** 68 (Intel 486) → 1366+ (Itanium-class).
- [ ] **Pin density benchmark:** ~15 pins/cm² (2.54 mm pitch) vs. ~60 pins/cm² (1.27 mm pitch).
- [ ] **PGA vs. BGA vs. LGA:** PGA = replaceable + pins; BGA = non-replaceable + balls; LGA = replaceable + lands.
- [ ] **Modern trend:** LGA has **largely replaced PGA** in consumer/server CPUs due to lower inductance and higher density, but PGA remains in **FPGAs, military, and aerospace**.
- [ ] **JEDEC** governs all package standardization (JESD22 series for reliability tests).
- [ ] **Parasitic inductance rule:** ~1 nH/mm of pin length; at 1 GHz, 5 mm pin = 31.4 Ω reactance.
- [ ] **Thermal:** CPGA conducts heat through both top IHS **and** bottom pin field to the socket.
- [ ] **Exam mantra:** "Pins in a Grid, ZIF Socket Inside."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Electrode Face (MELF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metal Electrode Face (MELF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metal Electrode Face (MELF)** is a cylindrical, leadless, surface-mount device (SMD) packaging format used primarily for passive electronic components such as **resistors**, **diodes**, and **capacitors**. The package derives its name from the two metallic electrode end-caps that establish the electrical and mechanical contact with the printed circuit board (PCB) land pads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A MELF is a passive SMD component housed in a cylindrical ceramic body (typically **alumina, Al₂O₃**) terminated with two metal alloy end-caps (typically **Sn/Ni/Cu or Ag/Ni/Sn plated brass or steel**). It conforms to the European Standard **EN 140100 / IEC 60115-1** for fixed resistors.

The standard MELF resistor sizes are designated by a four-digit code, the most common being:
- **MMA 0204** (Mini-MELF) – Length 3.6 mm × Diameter 1.4 mm
- **MMB 0207** (MELF) – Length 5.8 mm × Diameter 2.2 mm
- **MMU 0102** (Micro-MELF) – Length 2.0 mm × Diameter 1.1 mm

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a MELF resistor as a **tiny sharpened pencil** (the cylindrical body) with **two metal thimbles** glued onto both ends (the electrodes). The "lead" inside the pencil is the resistive film that controls current flow. When you lay this pencil horizontally on a circuit board and solder the two thimbles onto copper pads, the pencil becomes a working resistor — exactly the way a MELF works.

In simpler terms:
- **Body** = a hard ceramic tube that holds and protects the resistive element.
- **Metal end-caps** = the "hands" of the component that grab onto the PCB pads via solder.
- **Resistive film** = the actual smart material inside that limits current.

## 1.3 Why MELF Exists — The Engineering Problem It Solves

Before MELF, two dominant resistor packages existed:
