# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Define the term **Quad Flat Pack (QFP)**. Mention any two advantages it offers over the older DIP (Dual In-line Package) for modern electronic assemblies.

**Model Answer:**

The **Quad Flat Pack (QFP)** is a surface-mount integrated circuit package in which the leads (gull-wing-shaped) extend from all **four sides** of a square or rectangular flat plastic/ceramic body. It conforms to JEDEC standards and is widely used for microcontrollers, ASICs, and DSPs.

**Two advantages over DIP:**

1. **Higher pin density** — QFP supports pin counts from 32 to over 300 in a compact footprint, whereas DIP is practically limited to about 64 pins.
2. **Lower parasitic inductance and capacitance** — The short, flat leads minimize signal degradation at high frequencies, making QFP suitable for high-speed digital circuits.
3. **Compatibility with automated SMT assembly** — Reduces manufacturing cost and improves reliability compared to through-hole DIP.

*(Any two valid points — 3 marks = 1 definition + 2 advantages.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between **QFP** and **TQFP** in terms of body thickness, typical applications, and pin count range.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | QFP | TQFP |
|---|---|---|
| Body Thickness | 1.6 mm to 3.1 mm | **1.0 mm** (reduced height) |
| Typical Pin Count | 32 to 304 | 32 to 176 |
| Applications | Industrial controllers, ASICs, microcontrollers, prototypes | Slim handheld devices, mobile phones, card PCs, low-profile consumer electronics |

Both share the **gull-wing lead** configuration on all four sides and are JEDEC-standard surface-mount packages. The TQFP is essentially a **height-optimized variant** of the QFP, intended for space-constrained designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the constructional features of a **Quad Flat Pack (QFP)**. Explain the function of the **gull-wing lead** and the role of **coplanarity** in reliable soldering. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A QFP-80 IC is to be mounted on a PCB. The lead pitch is **0.65 mm** and the lead finger width at the package body is **1.2 mm**. Determine:
> - (i) The number of leads per side.
> - (ii) The minimum package body side length.
> - (iii) The package footprint area, assuming a square body.
> - (iv) The pin density in pins/mm². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **Quad Flat Pack** consists of the following constructional elements:

1. **Silicon Die** — The active IC chip, typically 2–10 mm on a side.
2. **Die Attach Pad (DAP)** — A central metal platform (often copper) that supports the die and provides a thermal path.
3. **Bond Wires** — Fine gold (Au) or aluminum (Al) wires of ~25 µm diameter connect die pads to the leadframe fingers.
4. **Leadframe** — A copper-alloy sheet stamped or etched into a tree pattern; the inner fingers connect to bond wires, while the outer portions become the gull-wing leads.
5. **Encapsulant** — Black epoxy molding compound (EMC) molded around the die and inner leadframe.
6. **External Gull-Wing Leads** — Trimmed, formed leads exiting all four sides.

**Function of the gull-wing lead:**
The gull-wing profile (resembling a seagull's wing in cross-section) provides mechanical compliance (absorbs CTE mismatch stress between the package and PCB) and exposes a flat **foot** at the bottom for solder fillet formation. The foot length and coplanarity directly determine solder joint quality.

**Role of coplanarity:**
**Coplanarity** refers to the degree to which all lead feet lie in a single plane. JEDEC specifies coplanarity within **0.10 mm**. If violated, one or more leads will not touch the PCB pad during reflow soldering, causing **open joints**. Hence, coplanarity is critical for reliable solder joint formation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Number of leads per side:**

Since the QFP has leads on all four sides, the number of leads per side is the total pin count divided by the number of sides.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{per side}} &= \frac{N}{4} \\
n_{\text{per side}} &= \frac{80}{4} \\
n_{\text{per side}} &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum package body side length:**

The minimum side length is calculated from the number of gaps between leads (which is one less than the number of leads per side) multiplied by the pitch, plus the lead finger width at the body edge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\min} &= (n_{\text{per side}} - 1) \times p + W_{\text{pad}} \\
L_{\min} &= (20 - 1) \times 0.65\,\text{mm} + 1.2\,\text{mm} \\
L_{\min} &= 19 \times 0.65 + 1.2 \\
L_{\min} &= 12.35 + 1.2 \\
L_{\min} &= 13.55\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Package footprint area (square body):**

Since the package body is assumed to be square, the footprint area is the side length squared.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{body}} &= L_{\min}^2 \\
A_{\text{body}} &= (13.55)^2 \\
A_{\text{body}} &= 183.6025\,\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Pin density:**

Pin density is the total pin count divided by the package footprint area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{pin}} &= \frac{N}{A_{\text{body}}} \\
\rho_{\text{pin}} &= \frac{80}{183.6025} \\
\rho_{\text{pin}} &\approx 0.4357 \,\text{pins/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **20 leads per side**
- (ii) **13.55 mm**
- (iii) **183.60 mm²**
- (iv) **0.436 pins/mm²**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**

> **(a)** Explain the salient features of a **Thin Quad Flat Pack (TQFP)**. Why is it preferred over standard QFP in portable electronic devices? List any four typical applications of TQFP. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A microcontroller in a **TQFP-64** package has the following specifications:
> - Maximum junction temperature $T_{J,\max} = 150\,°\text{C}$
> - Ambient temperature $T_A = 60\,°\text{C}$
> - Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance $\theta_{JA} = 45\,°\text{C/W}$
>
> Determine:
> - (i) The maximum allowable power dissipation of the IC.
> - (ii) If the IC actually dissipates **1.5 W**, find the steady-state junction temperature.
> - (iii) Comment on whether the device is operating within safe limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Salient features of TQFP:**

1. **Reduced body thickness** of **1.0 mm** (vs. 1.6–3.1 mm for standard QFP).
2. **Gull-wing leads** on all four sides, identical to QFP.
3. Lead pitch typically **0.4 mm to 0.8 mm**.
4. Lead count range **32 to 176** pins.
5. Conforms to **JEDEC MS-026** standards.
6. Available in both **plastic** (most common) and **ceramic** variants.
7. Compatible with **reflow soldering** and standard SMT processes.

**Why preferred for portable devices:**
Portable electronics (smartphones, digital cameras, tablets, wearables) demand **minimal vertical height** to fit slim enclosures. The 1.0 mm TQFP body allows engineers to achieve compact stack-ups, often combined with **chip-scale packages** and **PoP (Package-on-Package)** configurations on the same board.

**Four typical applications:**

1. **Mobile phone baseband and application processors.**
2. **Digital camera image signal processors.**
3. **Portable media player controllers.**
4. **Wireless sensor nodes and IoT edge devices.**

*(Any four relevant examples — accept Bluetooth SoCs, GPS modules, smart-watch MCUs, etc.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Maximum allowable power dissipation:**

The maximum allowable power dissipation is the temperature margin between the maximum junction temperature and ambient, divided by the thermal resistance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D,\max} &= \frac{T_{J,\max} - T_A}{\theta_{JA}} \\
P_{D,\max} &= \frac{150 - 60}{45} \\
P_{D,\max} &= \frac{90}{45} \\
P_{D,\max} &= 2.0 \,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state junction temperature at 1.5 W dissipation:**

The steady-state junction temperature is the ambient temperature plus the temperature rise caused by the power dissipation across the thermal resistance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_D \times \theta_{JA} \\
T_J &= 60\,°\text{C} + 1.5\,\text{W} \times 45\,°\text{C/W} \\
T_J &= 60 + 67.5 \\
T_J &= 127.5\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Comment on safe operation:**

The calculated junction temperature of **127.5 °C** is **less than** the maximum rated junction temperature of **150 °C**. Furthermore, the actual power dissipation of **1.5 W** is **less than** the maximum allowable dissipation of **2.0 W**.

**Conclusion:** The device is operating **within safe thermal limits**, with a margin of **22.5 °C** before reaching the maximum junction temperature. However, for long-term reliability, designers typically derate to operate at junction temperatures below **125 °C** to extend the operational life of the IC (following the **Arrhenius equation** for semiconductor aging).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in QFP/TQFP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Writing "$L_{\min} = 13.55$" without "mm" loses 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong formula application** — Confusing $L_{\min} = n_{\text{per side}} \times p$ (which INCORRECTLY adds an extra pitch). The correct formula uses $(n - 1) \times p + W_{\text{pad}}$.
> 3. **Ignoring pin-1 marker** — In sketches, examiners expect indication of the **Pin-1 dot/indent** on QFP packages. Omitting it costs 0.5 mark in descriptive questions.
> 4. **Saying "TQFP is smaller in all dimensions"** — Wrong. TQFP differs **mainly in thickness (height)**, not in length/width footprint area.
> 5. **Mixing up QFP and QFN** — QFN (Quad Flat No-lead) is a *different* package; leads are flush pads under the body, not gull-wings. Examiners WILL deduct marks if confused.
> 6. **Not mentioning IPC-7351 / JEDEC standards** — Always cite the governing standard for PCB footprint design.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **QFP (Quad Flat Pack)** = surface-mount IC package with **gull-wing leads on all four sides**.
- **TQFP (Thin Quad Flat Pack)** = QFP variant with **body thickness reduced to 1.0 mm**.
- **Pitch (p)** is the **center-to-center distance** between adjacent leads; common values: **0.4, 0.5, 0.65, 0.8, 1.0 mm**.
- **Gull-wing lead** = downward + outward bend; provides solder fillet and stress relief.
- **Coplanarity** of all leads must be **≤ 0.10 mm** (JEDEC) for reliable reflow soldering.
- **Total pin count formula**: $N = 4 \times n_{\text{per side}}$.
- **Minimum body side**: $L_{\min} = (n_{\text{per side}} - 1) \times p + W_{\text{pad}}$.
- **Maximum power dissipation**: $P_{D,\max} = (T_{J,\max} - T_A) / \theta_{JA}$.
- **Junction temperature check**: $T_J = T_A + P_D \times \theta_{JA}$; must remain **≤ 150 °C** (typically derated to **≤ 125 °C**).
- **Standards to cite**: JEDEC MS-026 (QFP), JEDEC MS-022 (QFP), IPC-7351 (PCB land patterns).
- **TQFP preferred for**: portable, handheld, slim-profile consumer electronics.
- **Pin-1 indicator**: dot, indent, or chamfered corner on package.
- **Comparison note**: QFP is **hand-solderable**; BGA (Ball Grid Array) is **not** — a major reason QFP/TQFP remain popular in prototyping and low-volume production.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball Grid Array (BGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ball Grid Array (BGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Ball Grid Array (BGA)** is a high-density surface-mount integrated circuit (IC) packaging technology in which the entire bottom surface of the package is populated with an array of small, spherically-shaped solder balls (typically made of lead-free alloys like **SAC305** — Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5) that serve as the input/output (I/O) interconnection terminals between the semiconductor die and the printed circuit board (PCB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *BGA is a surface-mount packaging technology that uses an array of solder balls on the underside of the package to provide high I/O count, superior electrical performance, and excellent thermal dissipation characteristics compared to peripheral-lead packages like QFPs.*

**Key Physical Metrics (KTU Standard Values):**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ball Pitch | 1.27, 1.0, 0.8, 0.65, 0.5, 0.4 | mm |
| Ball Diameter | 0.6 – 0.75 (typical) | mm |
| I/O Count Range | 4 – 2500+ | contacts |
| Operating Frequency | Up to 10+ GHz | – |
| Thermal Resistance ($\theta_{JA}$) | 10 – 40 | °C/W |
| Body Sizes | 5×5 to 55×55 | mm |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Upside-Down Pincushion" Model:**
Imagine a **pin-cushion** turned upside-down on a wooden board. Instead of pins sticking up, picture small metallic balls (BB-gun pellets) sitting in a perfect grid on the bottom face of a small flat chip. When you heat the chip in a reflow oven, these balls melt slightly and "stick" the chip onto a matching grid of copper pads on a circuit board.

**The "Parking Lot" Analogy:**
Think of a large shopping mall parking lot from an aerial view:
- The **chip** = the mall building.
- The **solder balls** = the parking spots arranged in neat rows and columns.
- The **PCB pads** = the driveways connecting each parking spot to the highway (circuit).

The more parking spots (balls), the more cars (signals) can enter and exit the building (chip) simultaneously — this is why BGAs handle **hundreds to thousands of I/O connections** in a tiny footprint.

### 1.3 Why BGA Exists — The Limitation of Perimeter Packages

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitch Limit Law:** For Quad Flat Packages (QFP), leads are placed on all four edges. As pitch drops below **0.5 mm**, lead coplanarity, lead skew, and soldering defects explode — BGA eliminates this problem by placing contacts across the entire **bottom face (area array)** rather than only the perimeter.

**Key Advantages Driving BGA Adoption:**
- **Higher I/O density** per unit area (area-array vs. perimeter).
- **Lower lead inductance** (~1–5 nH vs. QFP's 5–15 nH) → better high-frequency performance.
- **Superior thermal path** via center balls and thermal vias.
- **Self-alignment** during reflow (surface tension of molten solder pulls the package into precise alignment, providing up to **±0.3 mm** placement tolerance).
- **Robust mechanical joint** — shorter, fatter solder columns withstand thermal stress better.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a BGA Package

A standard BGA package is a multi-layered sandwich. The hierarchy from die to PCB is:

1. **Semiconductor Die** — The silicon chip (size: 3×3 mm to 20×20 mm typical).
2. **Die Attach / Adhesive Layer** — Conductive epoxy or solder (electrical + thermal bonding).
3. **Interposer / Substrate** — A 2–6 layer organic (BT resin) or ceramic laminate carrying the **re-distribution layer (RDL)** that fans-out the die's peripheral bond pads into the full ball-grid pattern.
4. **Solder Mask / Underfill Reservoir** — Defines ball pad openings.
5. **Solder Balls** — Spheres (typically diameter = **0.6 mm**) of eutectic or lead-free solder alloy.
6. **PCB Pad Land Pattern** — Solder-mask-defined (SMD) or non-solder-mask-defined (NSMD) copper pads on the motherboard.
7. **Underfill (Optional but standard)** — Capillary-flow epoxy that fills the gap between package and PCB, dramatically improving thermomechanical fatigue life.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**How a BGA Connects to the World (Signal Path):**
- **Step 1:** Die bond pads (e.g., 200 pads along the die edges, 100 µm pitch) are routed via the **RDL** to wider traces.
- **Step 2:** RDL traces terminate at **ball pads** on the substrate's bottom face (typically 0.4–0.8 mm pitch).
- **Step 3:** Solder balls are attached to these pads via flux + reflow.
- **Step 4:** When the BGA is placed on the PCB, surface tension during reflow causes the molten solder to wet both the package pad and the PCB pad, forming a mechanical + electrical + thermal bridge.
- **Step 5:** **Capillary underfill** is dispensed along one or two package edges; it flows beneath the package and cures, locking the assembly against thermal cycling fatigue.

### 2.3 Taxonomy of BGA Variants

| BGA Type | Substrate | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PBGA** (Plastic BGA) | BT-resin organic laminate | Consumer, mobile, ASICs |
| **CBGA** (Ceramic BGA) | Alumina/AlN ceramic | Aerospace, military, high-temp |
| **TBGA** (Tape BGA) | Polyimide tape | High-frequency RF |
| **CCGA** (Ceramic Column Grid Array) | Ceramic with solder **columns** | Large ceramic packages (column = better CTE match) |
| **μBGA / CSP** | Substrate = die itself or thin film | Memory chips, smartphones |
| **FC-BGA** (Flip-Chip BGA) | Built-up organic with flip-chip die | CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs |
| **PoP** (Package-on-Package) | Stacked BGA | Mobile SoC + memory |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference — All symbols are standard SI.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A_{\text{die}} = N_{\text{balls}} \cdot d_{\text{pad}}^{2}$ | Approximate footprint area for ball grid | mm² |
| 2 | $N_{\text{rows}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{L_{\text{pkg}}}{P_{\text{ball}}} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Number of ball rows in a BGA package | – |
| 3 | $N_{\text{balls}} = N_{\text{rows}}^{2} - N_{\text{depop}}$ | Total balls after depopulation | – |
| 4 | $Z_{\text{ball}} = R_{\text{ball}} + j\omega L_{\text{ball}}$ | Per-ball RF impedance | Ω |
| 5 | $L_{\text{ball}} \approx \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{h}{0.5} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2h}{r}\right)\right] \cdot 10^{-3}$ | Self-inductance of a short solder column (h ≈ 0.5 mm, r ≈ 0.3 mm) → ~1 nH | nH |
| 6 | $\theta_{JA} = \dfrac{T_J - T_A}{P_{\text{diss}}}$ | Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 7 | $\theta_{JA} \vert_{\text{total}} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$ | Total thermal resistance (junction → case → sink → ambient) | °C/W |
| 8 | $I_{\text{max}} = k \cdot d_{\text{ball}}^{1.5}$ | Current carrying capacity of a solder ball (k ≈ 30 for SAC305) | A |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermally-induced shear stress (E = modulus, α = CTE mismatch, ΔT = temp cycle) | Pa |
| 10 | $\text{CTE}_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\sum V_i \, CTE_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Rule of mixtures for underfill/substrate effective CTE | ppm/°C |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

**Where BGAs Are Indispensable in Modern Engineering:**

- **Microprocessors & GPUs:** Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, NVIDIA GPUs — all use **FC-BGA** because thousands of power/ground balls are needed for current delivery plus signal balls for high-speed SerDes.
- **FPGAs:** Xilinx Virtex, Intel Stratix series use large BGAs (>1500 balls) for high-speed parallel I/O.
- **Mobile SoCs:** Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series in **PoP** stacks combining logic + memory.
- **Networking ASICs:** 100G/400G/800G switch chips use **2.5D / 3D BGA** with silicon interposers.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** **CBGA** for rad-hard and high-temperature (up to 200 °C) operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Self-Inductance of a Cylindrical Solder Ball

We model the solder ball as a short cylindrical conductor of height $h$ and radius $r$, carrying uniform current. The total inductance is the sum of **internal** and **external** contributions.

**Starting Point — Inductance of a Straight Wire Segment:**

$$L_{\text{wire}} = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \left[ h \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{h + \sqrt{h^2 + r^2}}{r}\right) - \sqrt{h^2 + r^2} + \frac{h}{4} + r \right]$$

**Step 1: Apply the "short conductor" limit.** Since $h \gg r$ is **not** true here ($h \approx 0.5$ mm, $r \approx 0.3$ mm), we instead use the **partial-inductance approximation** for a via-like structure.

**Step 2: External (magnetic field outside the conductor) contribution:**

$$L_{\text{ext}} = \frac{\mu_0 \, h}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{2h}{r}\right)$$

- Here, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- Substituting $h = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $r = 0.3 \times 10^{-3}$ m:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{2h}{r}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(3.333) = 1.204$$

$$L_{\text{ext}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(0.5 \times 10^{-3})}{2\pi} \cdot 1.204 = 1.2 \times 10^{-10} \text{ H} = 0.12 \text{ nH}$$

**Step 3: Internal (magnetic flux inside the conductor) contribution:**
For uniform current in a cylinder:

$$L_{\text{int}} = \frac{\mu_0 \, h}{8\pi}$$

$$L_{\text{int}} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(0.5 \times 10^{-3})}{8\pi} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ H} = 0.025 \text{ nH}$$

**Step 4: Total ball inductance:**

$$L_{\text{ball}} = L_{\text{ext}} + L_{\text{int}} = 0.12 + 0.025 = 0.145 \text{ nH}$$

**Step 5: Compare to a QFP lead (typical L ≈ 5–15 nH).** The BGA ball has roughly **30× lower inductance**, justifying its dominance at GHz frequencies.

### 3.2 Derivation: I/O Density Ratio — BGA vs. QFP

For a square package of side $L$ with perimeter-only QFP leads at pitch $P$, and a BGA at pitch $P$:

**QFP (perimeter only, 4 sides):**

$$N_{\text{QFP}} = \frac{4L}{P}$$

**BGA (full area array):**

$$N_{\text{BGA}} = \left(\frac{L}{P}\right)^2$$

**Density ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{\text{BGA}}}{N_{\text{QFP}}} = \frac{(L/P)^2}{4(L/P)} = \frac{L}{4P}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $L = 20$ mm, $P = 1.0$ mm:

$$\eta = \frac{20}{4 \times 1.0} = 5$$

A 20 mm BGA at 1 mm pitch has **5× more I/Os** than a QFP of the same size and pitch.

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Resistance of a BGA via Array

A BGA uses thermal balls (and underlying PCB thermal vias) to conduct heat from the die to inner copper planes.

**Steady-state 1-D heat conduction through a cylindrical via of radius $r$, height $h$, thermal conductivity $k$:**

$$R_{\text{th,via}} = \frac{h}{k \cdot \pi r^2}$$

For copper ($k = 385$ W/m·K), $h = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m (typical PCB thickness), $r = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m:

$$R_{\text{th,via}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{385 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{385 \cdot \pi \cdot 4 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{4.84 \times 10^{-5}} = 33.06 \text{ °C/W (per via)}$$

**Total thermal resistance with N parallel thermal vias (heat spreaders assumed):**

$$R_{\text{th,total}} = \frac{R_{\text{th,via}}}{N} + R_{\text{th,spreading}} + R_{\text{th,PCB}}$$

For $N = 25$ thermal vias, $R_{\text{th,spreading}} \approx 2$ °C/W, $R_{\text{th,PCB}} \approx 1$ °C/W:

$$R_{\text{th,total}} = \frac{33.06}{25} + 2 + 1 = 1.32 + 3 = 4.32 \text{ °C/W}$$

This is far lower than peripheral-leaded packages, enabling high-power dissipation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes and Mitigation

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Solder joint fatigue** | CTE mismatch (Si ≈ 3 ppm/°C vs. PCB ≈ 17 ppm/°C) | Underfill + compliant ball (SAC405) |
| **Head-in-pillow (HiP)** | Warped BGA + insufficient reflow soak | Controlled soak profile 60–90 s at 150 °C |
| **Bridging** | Excess solder or insufficient paste | Stencil aperture design (50–75% of pad area) |
| **Voiding** | Entrapped flux gas | Vacuum reflow or low-outgassing flux |
| **Pad cratering** | PCB pad lifted by shear stress | NSMD pads + high-Tg PCB |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — BGA Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["SILICON DIE"] --> nodeB["DIE ATTACH EPOXY"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BT RESIN SUBSTRATE 4 LAYERS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["RE DISTRIBUTION LAYER RDL"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SOLDER BALL PAD"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["SAC305 SOLDER BALL 0.6MM"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["PCB COPPER PAD SMD"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["PCB THERMAL VIAS ARRAY"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["INTERNAL COPPER GROUND PLANE"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["TOP HEAT SPREADER OPTIONAL"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["THERMAL INTERFACE MATERIAL"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["EXTERNAL HEATSINK"]
    nodeA -.signal.-> nodeD
    nodeD -.ground.-> nodeF
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — BGA vs. QFP Functional Comparison

```mermaid

    subgraph BGA ["BGA PACKAGE ARCHITECTURE"]
        bgaA["BOTTOM FACE FULL BALL GRID"] --> bgaB["DIE RDL ROUTED"]

    end

    end

    bgaC --> comp["SUPERIOR ELECTRICAL THERMAL AND DENSITY PERFORMANCE"]
    qfpC --> comp
    comp --> app["USED IN CPUS GPUS FPGAS AND ASIC PACKAGING"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — BGA Mounting Process

```mermaid

    stepA["STEP 1 STENCIL PRINT SOLDER PASTE ON PCB PADS"]
    stepB["STEP 2 SOLDER BALL ATTACHMENT TO PACKAGE"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Direct definition or descriptive answers, typically 80–120 words. Marks are awarded for keyword density (pitch, solder ball, area array, CTE, etc.).

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define Ball Grid Array (BGA) package. List any four advantages of BGA over Quad Flat Package (QFP).**

**Model Answer:**
A Ball Grid Array (BGA) is a surface-mount IC package in which **solder balls** arranged in a grid on the package's underside serve as I/O contacts. Advantages over QFP:
1. **Higher I/O density** due to area-array layout.
2. **Lower lead inductance** (~1 nH vs. 5–15 nH) → better high-frequency performance.
3. **Superior thermal dissipation** via center thermal balls and PCB vias.
4. **Self-alignment** during reflow due to solder surface tension, reducing placement defects.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**What is the function of underfill in a BGA assembly? Why is it critical for reliability?**

**Model Answer:**
Underfill is a **capillary-flow epoxy resin** dispensed along one or two edges of a BGA after reflow. It flows into the gap beneath the package by capillary action and cures to form a rigid bond between the BGA and PCB. It is critical because the **CTE mismatch** between the silicon die (~3 ppm/°C) and the PCB (~17 ppm/°C) creates cyclic shear stress on solder balls during thermal excursions. Underfill redistributes this stress to the entire package area, improving thermal cycling fatigue life by **10×–100×**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both sub-parts together = 14 marks. Internal choice: students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A — Full 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a Plastic Ball Grid Array (PBGA) package with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the redistribution layer (RDL). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A square PBGA package has body size $27 \text{ mm} \times 27 \text{ mm}$ and ball pitch $P = 1.0 \text{ mm}$. The package has a peripheral depopulation of one outer row on all four sides (i.e., the outermost ring is removed for routing escape). Calculate:
- (i) The total number of solder balls in the package.
- (ii) The I/O density in balls per mm².
- (iii) Compare this with an equivalent QFP of pitch 0.5 mm. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Constructional Features of PBGA (Block Diagram — refer Section 4.1):**

A PBGA consists of the following layers from top to bottom:

1. **Silicon Die:** The active semiconductor chip (e.g., ASIC, microprocessor) with bond pads along its periphery. Typical die size is 30–60% of the package area.
2. **Die Attach Material:** A conductive or non-conductive epoxy that mechanically bonds the die to the substrate. Also provides a thermal path.
3. **Substrate (Interposer):** A **2–4 layer BT-resin (bismaleimide triazine)** organic laminate with copper traces. It performs the **redistribution function**.
4. **Redistribution Layer (RDL):** Traces that fan-out the fine-pitch die bond pads (e.g., 80 µm) to the coarser ball pad pitch (e.g., 0.5–1.0 mm).
5. **Solder Mask:** A polymer layer (typically green/black) with openings exposing the ball pads for solder attachment.
6. **Solder Balls:** SAC305 or Sn63/Pb37 spheres attached via flux and reflow.

**Role of the RDL:**
The RDL performs three critical functions:
- **Pitch translation:** Converts the dense peripheral die pad pattern into an area-array ball pattern.
- **Signal integrity routing:** Provides matched-length traces for high-speed signals.
- **Power distribution:** Wide power/ground traces fan out from die pads to multiple power/ground balls.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**(i) Total number of solder balls:**

For a square 27 mm package at 1 mm pitch, balls are placed at:

$$N_{\text{rows, full}} = \frac{27}{1.0} + 1 = 28 \text{ rows per side}$$

A full area array without depopulation would have:

$$N_{\text{full}} = 28^2 = 784 \text{ balls}$$

Now, removing the outermost ring of balls (the perimeter ring) eliminates the boundary row. The number of balls in one outer ring (perimeter ring of an $n \times n$ array):

$$N_{\text{outer ring}} = 4(n - 1) = 4(28 - 1) = 4 \times 27 = 108$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\text{BGA}} = 784 - 108 = \boxed{676 \text{ balls}}$$

**(ii) I/O density:**

$$\rho_{\text{I/O}} = \frac{676}{27 \times 27} = \frac{676}{729} = \boxed{0.927 \text{ balls/mm}^2}$$

**(iii) Comparison with QFP at 0.5 mm pitch:**

For the same 27 mm square package, a QFP has leads on all four sides at 0.5 mm pitch:

$$N_{\text{QFP}} = \frac{4 \times 27}{0.5} = \frac{108}{0.5} = \boxed{216 \text{ leads}}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{N_{\text{BGA}}}{N_{\text{QFP}}} = \frac{676}{216} = \boxed{3.13 \times \text{ higher I/O count}}$$

The PBGA delivers over 3× more interconnects than the same-size QFP — demonstrating the area-array advantage.

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice, Full 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** Explain the various failure modes encountered in BGA solder joints during field operation. Discuss the phenomenon of **head-in-pillow (HiP)** defect in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A flip-chip BGA has 400 thermal vias of radius 0.15 mm through a 1.6 mm thick PCB with copper thermal conductivity $k = 385 \text{ W/m·K}$. The spreading resistance under the die is 2 °C/W and the PCB-to-ambient resistance is 1 °C/W. Calculate:
- (i) Thermal resistance of a single via.
- (ii) Equivalent thermal resistance of 400 parallel vias.
- (iii) Total junction-to-ambient thermal resistance. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Major BGA Failure Modes:**

1. **Solder Joint Fatigue Cracking** — Caused by CTE mismatch. Cyclic thermal loading creates shear strain that nucleates cracks, typically at the package-side intermetallic compound (IMC) layer.
2. **Head-in-Pillow (HiP)** — See below.
3. **Black Pad / Embrittlement** — Poor ENIG (Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold) plating causes nickel-phosphorus hyper-corrosion, weakening the joint.
4. **Kirkendall Voids** — Vacancies form at the Cu₃Sn/Cu interface during aging, eventually splitting the joint.
5. **Pad Cratering** — PCB laminate tears beneath the pad under high shear stress.
6. **Electrochemical Migration** — Dendritic growth under humidity + bias, leading to shorts.

**Head-in-Pillow (HiP) Defect — Detailed Explanation:**

The HiP defect appears on X-ray as a solder ball "head" resting on a "pillow" of reflowed solder paste that never merged with the ball.

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**
- **Stage 1:** Solder paste is printed on PCB pads; BGA balls are placed with their own solidified solder coating.
- **Stage 2:** During the first reflow pass, BGA balls melt but warpage of either the package or PCB (especially large BGAs >25 mm) causes some balls to **lift off** the molten paste.
- **Stage 3:** When the board returns to flatness during cooldown, the ball re-contacts the paste — but the paste has **oxidized** and is no longer fully molten.
- **Stage 4:** The result: a grainy oxide boundary between ball and paste, with a tiny gap that may pass initial electrical test but fails mechanically under thermal cycling or vibration.

**Detection:** 2D / 3D X-ray inspection, cross-sectioning, or SAM (Scanning Acoustic Microscopy).

**Prevention:**
- Use a **longer soak zone** (90–120 s at 150–180 °C) to equalize temperatures.
- Reduce peak temperature gradient.
- Use **N₂ atmosphere** to suppress oxidation.
- Vacuum reflow systems eliminate voids and HiP simultaneously.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**(i) Thermal resistance of a single via:**

$$R_{\text{th,via}} = \frac{h}{k \cdot \pi r^2}$$

$$= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{385 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.15 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{385 \cdot \pi \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2.72 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= \boxed{58.8 \text{ °C/W per via}}$$

**(ii) Equivalent resistance of 400 parallel vias:**

$$\frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{400} \frac{1}{R_i} = \frac{400}{58.8}$$

$$R_{\text{eq}} = \frac{58.8}{400} = \boxed{0.147 \text{ °C/W}}$$

**(iii) Total junction-to-ambient thermal resistance:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{eq}} + R_{\text{spreading}} + R_{\text{PCB-to-amb}}$$

$$= 0.147 + 2 + 1 = \boxed{3.147 \text{ °C/W}}$$

This low thermal resistance allows the BGA to dissipate:

$$P_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_J_{\text{max}} - T_A}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{85 - 45}{3.147} \approx 12.7 \text{ W}$$

— sufficient for typical high-performance ASICs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in BGA Questions (KTU Examiner Notes):**
>
> 1. **Confusing BGA pitch with ball diameter** — Pitch is center-to-center spacing (0.5/0.8/1.0 mm). Diameter is the ball itself (~0.6 mm for 1.0 mm pitch). Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting depopulation** — When asked to count balls, students often compute a full $N \times N$ grid without subtracting the missing outer ring. Always check the problem statement.
> 3. **Wrong thermal formula** — Mixing parallel and series combinations. Vias are **parallel** (heat divides); spreading/PCB layers are **series** (heat passes through).
> 4. **Ignoring units** — Convert mm to m before plugging into thermal equations.
> 5. **Omitting self-alignment advantage** — When asked "advantages," students forget to mention the **self-aligning property** during reflow — a favorite KTU marking point.
> 6. **Mis-naming package variants** — CBGA ≠ PBGA ≠ TBGA. Examiners want specific substrate material recognition.
> 7. **Skipping the RDL explanation** — For PBGA construction questions, the role of the redistribution layer is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** BGA = surface-mount package with **array of solder balls on the bottom face** for I/O.
- **Pitch Standards:** 1.27 → 1.0 → 0.8 → 0.65 → 0.5 → 0.4 mm (industry trend is finer).
- **Major Variants:** PBGA, CBGA, TBGA, FC-BGA, μBGA/CSP, CCGA, PoP.
- **Key Advantages:** High I/O density, low inductance (~1 nH), low thermal resistance, self-alignment, robust joint.
- **Key Disadvantages:** Difficult visual inspection (needs X-ray), requires underfill, non-compliant to socket mounting, expensive rework.
- **Critical Materials:** SAC305 lead-free solder; BT-resin substrate; conductive die-attach epoxy; capillary underfill epoxy.
- **Thermal Rule:** $\theta_{JA} = (T_J - T_A) / P_{\text{diss}}$; vias in **parallel**, layers in **series**.
- **Failure Modes to Memorize:** Solder fatigue, head-in-pillow (HiP), pad cratering, Kirkendall voids, black pad.
- **Inspection Methods:** AOI (limited — only outer rows), **2D/3D X-ray** (mandatory), SAM, cross-section.
- **Self-Alignment:** Surface tension of molten solder corrects up to **±50%** of pitch offset during reflow.
- **Underfill Function:** Redistributes CTE-mismatch shear stress, increasing fatigue life by **10×–100×**.
- **Why BGA dominates GHz design:** Lower loop inductance + shorter electrical path = superior signal integrity at multi-GHz SerDes speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra:**
> *"BGA trades visual inspectability for electrical performance — and wins everywhere that GHz speed and high power matter."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC)** is a square, surface-mount integrated circuit (IC) package constructed from a molded plastic (typically epoxy-novolac) body, characterized by **J-shaped metallic leads** that curl underneath the package body on all four sides. The PLCC belongs to the family of **Quad Flat Packages (QFP)** with compliant lead geometry, standardized under **JEDEC MS-018** with a standard lead pitch of **1.27 mm (50 mils)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PLCC is classified as a **Surface Mount Technology (SMT)** component with **peripheral lead termination**, capable of being both **PCB-soldered** and **socket-mounted** — a unique dual-mode mounting feature.

### Key Geometric & Electrical Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value | Tolerance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lead Pitch (e)** | **1.27 mm** | ± 0.05 mm |
| **Lead Width (b)** | 0.40 mm – 0.50 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
| **Package Thickness (A)** | 3.5 mm – 4.5 mm | ± 0.20 mm |
| **J-Lead Foot Length (L)** | 0.80 mm – 1.27 mm | ± 0.10 mm |
| **Operating Temperature** | **-40 °C to +85 °C** (commercial) | Industrial: -40 to +105 °C |
| **Lead Material** | Copper Alloy (C19400) with Sn/Pb or Sn finish | — |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **little square "armored box"** (the plastic body) that hugs a silicon microchip. On each of its four sides, **curly metal "fingers"** (the J-leads) tuck inward like the legs of a tiny square crab. These fingers do two clever jobs:

1. They **grip the solder pads** firmly when melted onto a PCB.
2. They **flex slightly** to absorb thermal stress — like springs — preventing the brittle silicon die inside from cracking when the PCB heats up or cools down.

The "J" in J-lead refers to the cross-sectional shape of each lead — it looks exactly like the **English alphabet letter "J"**, curling under the package. This makes PLCC much more resistant to lead bending during handling compared to flat gull-wing leads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** PLCC = Plastic square box + J-curled metal legs on 4 sides + 1.27 mm spacing. It is the **chip carrier equivalent of a "Swiss Army Knife"** — usable on either PCBs directly or in removable sockets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Package Architecture Breakdown

The PLCC architecture comprises five distinct functional layers:

- **Layer 1 — Lead Frame (C194 Copper Alloy):** Provides the electrical pathway from the die pad to the outside world. The lead frame is **stamped or etched** into a precise pattern.
- **Layer 2 — Die Attach Pad (DAP):** A central exposed metal island (typically 5 mm × 5 mm to 12 mm × 12 mm depending on pin count) onto which the silicon die is bonded using **silver-filled epoxy** or **eutectic Au-Si solder**.
- **Layer 3 — Wire Bonds:** **25 µm gold (Au) or aluminum (Al) wires** ultrasonically wedge-bonded from the die bond pads to the inner ends of the lead frame fingers.
- **Layer 4 — Plastic Encapsulant (Molding Compound):** A **thermosetting epoxy-novolac resin** with silica filler (~85% by weight) molded under pressure (~1000 psi) and heat (~175 °C).
- **Layer 5 — J-Lead Outer Termination:** The leads exit the package body, curl downward in a "J" shape, and end with a **solderable foot** of length 0.80–1.27 mm.

### 2.2 Operational "Why & How" Logic

| Engineering Requirement | Design Mechanism in PLCC | Underlying Physics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High pin density | Peripheral 4-sided lead arrangement | Spatial efficiency: $4 \times N/4$ pins per side |
| Mechanical robustness during handling | J-lead curl geometry (compliant spring) | Elastic deformation absorbs shear stress |
| Socketing capability | Lead geometry matches PLCC socket contacts | Standardized JEDEC socket footprint |
| Thermal dissipation | Copper lead-frame DAP + mold compound with $\kappa \approx 0.7 \text{ W/m·K}$ | Heat conduction through leads to PCB |
| Low lead inductance | Short, wide leads with minimal loop area | $L \propto \ln(l/w)$ — short leads reduce $L$ |
| Solder joint reliability | Lead foot length matches PCB pad geometry | Forms optimal fillet during reflow |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Equations for PLCC Analysis** — Memorize these for KTU ESE 2024.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(1) Package Footprint Area: } & A_{pkg} &= E \times D \quad [\text{mm}^2] \\
&\text{(2) Lead Inductance (per lead): } & L_{lead} &\approx 2 \times 10^{-4} \, l \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2l}{w+t}\right) + 0.5\right] \quad [\text{nH}] \\
&\text{(3) Lead Pitch & Pin Count Relation: } & N &= 4n \quad \text{where } n = \text{pins per side} \\
&\text{(4) Lead Spacing (center-to-center): } & e &= \frac{D - W_{b}}{n - 1} \quad [\text{mm}] \\
&\text{(5) Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Ambient): } & \theta_{JA} &= \frac{T_J - T_A}{P_{diss}} \quad [{}^\circ\text{C/W}] \\
&\text{(6) Junction Temperature: } & T_J &= T_A + P_{diss} \cdot \theta_{JA} \quad [{}^\circ\text{C}] \\
&\text{(7) Lead Compliance Stress: } & \sigma &= \frac{F \cdot L_{cantilever}}{Z} \quad [\text{MPa}] \\
&\text{(8) Solder Fillet Wetting Force: } & F_w &= \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \cdot P_{perimeter} \quad [\text{N/m}] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E, D$ | Package body length & width | mm |
| $l$ | Lead length (from package to foot) | mm |
| $w, t$ | Lead width and thickness | mm |
| $W_b$ | Lead shoulder width (per side) | mm |
| $\theta$ | Solder contact angle (wetting angle) | degrees |
| $\gamma_{LV}$ | Liquid-vapor surface tension of solder | N/m |
| $P_{diss}$ | Power dissipated by the IC die | W |
| $\kappa$ | Thermal conductivity of mold compound | W/m·K |
| $Z$ | Section modulus of the J-lead | mm³ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLCC packages were the **industry workhorse from 1985 to 2005** for:

1. **Microcontroller ICs** — e.g., **Intel 8051 family** (e.g., 87C51 in PLCC-44), **Microchip PIC** mid-range controllers.
2. **EPROM and Flash memory** — boot ROMs and BIOS chips on legacy motherboards.
3. **Programmable Logic Devices (PLDs)** — e.g., Altera MAX series, Xilinx XC9500.
4. **ASICs and Gate Arrays** — high-pin-count logic consolidation.
5. **Communication ICs** — UARTs, Ethernet controllers, glue logic.
6. **Retrofit & Repair** — PLCC sockets allowed easy field replacement of failed chips.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Context:** In modern hardware design courses, PLCC is studied primarily for **legacy design analysis, socketed prototyping, and understanding the transition from through-hole (DIP) to surface-mount technology (SMT)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lead Pitch and Footprint Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A KTU design problem requires you to determine the package dimensions and pin-per-side count for a PLCC-68.

**Given:**
- Total pin count $N = 68$
- Lead pitch $e = 1.27 \text{ mm}$ (JEDEC standard)
- Standard lead shoulder width $W_b = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ on each side
- Package square ($D = E$)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine pins per side ($n$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{N}{4} = \frac{68}{4} = 17 \text{ pins per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the lead-spacing formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{D - W_b}{n - 1} \\
1.27 &= \frac{D - 1.0}{17 - 1} \\
1.27 &= \frac{D - 1.0}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D - 1.0 &= 1.27 \times 16 \\
D - 1.0 &= 20.32 \\
D &= 20.32 + 1.0 \\
D &= 21.32 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with JEDEC standard table:** PLCC-68 has standard $D = E = 24.13 \text{ mm}$ (accounting for 1.78 mm shoulder). Our simplified calculation gives the **minimum** envelope; the actual JEDEC value includes additional corner-relief and lead standoff allowances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $(n - 1)$ rather than $n$ is used because $n$ pins occupy $(n-1)$ **intervals**, just as 5 fence posts create only 4 gaps between them.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lead Inductance Per Pin

**Given:**
- Lead length $l = 2.5 \text{ mm}$
- Lead width $w = 0.45 \text{ mm}$
- Lead thickness $t = 0.20 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Identify the inductance formula for a flat, rectangular conductor over ground plane (modified for PLCC's curved J-lead):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lead} \approx 2 \times 10^{-4} \, l \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2l}{w+t}\right) + 0.5\right] \quad [\text{nH}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lead} &= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.5 \times \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2 \times 2.5}{0.45 + 0.20}\right) + 0.5\right] \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{5.0}{0.65}\right) + 0.5\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{5.0}{0.65} &= 7.692 \\
\ln(7.692) &= 2.040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Complete the calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lead} &= 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \left[2.040 + 0.5\right] \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.540 \\
&= 1.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is dimensionally inconsistent. **Correct the unit analysis:** The $2 \times 10^{-4}$ factor already includes length normalization; for $l$ in mm and dimensions in mm, the formula gives inductance in **nH only when the dimensionless logarithm is multiplied by a constant $K$**. The corrected practical form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lead} &\approx K \cdot l \left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2l}{w+t}\right) + 0.5\right] \quad [\text{nH}]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K \approx 0.002$ for thin rectangular leads above ground at typical heights. Recomputing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{lead} &= 0.002 \times 2.5 \times [2.040 + 0.5] \\
&= 0.005 \times 2.540 \\
&= 0.0127 \text{ nH per mm} \times 2.5 = \approx 2.5 \text{ nH per lead}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway for ESE:** Each PLCC lead contributes approximately **2–4 nH of parasitic inductance**, which is critical for high-speed (>50 MHz) digital signals and must be considered in PCB layout. This is **one of the key reasons PLCC is now limited to moderate-speed applications.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Junction Temperature Estimation

**Problem Setup:** A PLCC-44 microcontroller dissipates $P_{diss} = 1.2 \text{ W}$ in still air at $T_A = 50 {}^\circ\text{C}$. Standard PLCC-44 has $\theta_{JA} \approx 45 {}^\circ\text{C/W}$ without heatsink.

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_{diss} \cdot \theta_{JA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= 50 + 1.2 \times 45 \\
&= 50 + 54 \\
&= 104 {}^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with absolute maximum $T_J(\max) = 150 {}^\circ\text{C}$ for commercial-grade ICs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= 150 - 104 = 46 {}^\circ\text{C} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Safe operation confirmed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine maximum allowable power at $T_A = 70 {}^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \frac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{\theta_{JA}} = \frac{150 - 70}{45} = 1.78 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** If the required $P_{diss}$ exceeds 1.78 W, the designer must either (a) reduce ambient temperature via forced-air cooling, (b) attach a heatsink to the PLCC DAP exposed on top, or (c) migrate to a smaller-thermal-resistance package such as QFN.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | PLCC | SOIC (Small Outline IC) | QFP (Quad Flat Pack) | DIP (Dual In-line Package) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lead Type | **J-Lead** | Gull-Wing | Gull-Wing | Through-Hole |
| Lead Sides | 4 (peripheral) | 2 (opposing) | 4 (peripheral) | 2 (opposing) |
| Pin Count Range | 20 – 84 | 8 – 28 | 44 – 304 | 8 – 64 |
| Lead Pitch | **1.27 mm** | 1.27 mm | 0.4 – 0.8 mm | 2.54 mm |
| Socketable | **Yes** | No | No (mostly) | Yes |
| Body Material | Plastic (Epoxy) | Plastic | Plastic / Ceramic | Plastic / Ceramic |
| Typical $\theta_{JA}$ | 40 – 50 °C/W | 70 – 100 °C/W | 30 – 70 °C/W | 50 – 90 °C/W |
| Frequency Limit | ~75 MHz | ~100 MHz | >200 MHz | ~50 MHz |
| Board Density | **High** | Medium | Very High | Low |
| Mounting Process | SMT (reflow) | SMT (reflow) | SMT (reflow) | THT (wave) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PLCC Package Physical Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ext[EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT]
        pcb[PCB Solder Pad]
        sock[PLCC Socket Contact]
        air[Ambient Air T_A]
    end

    subgraph pkg[PLCC PACKAGE BODY]
        direction TB
        jlead["J LEAD TERMINATION Cu Alloy C19400"]
        body["MOLD COMPOUND Epoxy Novolac with 85 percent Silica Filler"]
        frame["LEAD FRAME Stamped or Etched Pattern"]
        wb["WIRE BONDS 25 micron Gold or Aluminum"]
        die["SILICON DIE Active Circuit"]
        dap["DIE ATTACH PAD Copper Center Island"]
    end

    subgraph func[FUNCTIONAL PATHWAYS]
        elec["ELECTRICAL PATHWAY Signal and Power"]
        therm["THERMAL PATHWAY Heat Dissipation"]
        mech["MECHANICAL PATHWAY Stress Absorption via J Lead Curl"]
    end

    pcb -->|solder fillet| jlead
    sock -->|normal contact force 50 to 100 grams| jlead
    air -->|convection| body
    air -->|convection| jlead

    jlead -->|current flow| elec
    elec --> frame
    frame --> wb
    wb --> die
    die --> dap

    die -->|heat q dot| therm
    therm --> dap
    therm --> wb
    therm --> frame
    therm --> jlead
    therm --> body

    jlead -->|spring deflection| mech
    body -->|encapsulation protection| die
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Lead Geometry (J-Lead Shape)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph jprofile[J LEAD CROSS SECTION PROFILE]
        direction TB
        A["1 PACKAGE BODY TOP"] --> B["2 LEAD SHOULDER EXIT"]
        B --> C["3 VERTICAL DROP"]
        C --> D["4 J CURL FOOTPRINT"]
        D --> E["5 SOLDERABLE FOOT 0.8 to 1.27 mm"]
    end

    A -.->|heat transfer| F["PCB COPPER PAD"]
    E -->|solder fillet forms here| F
```

### 4.3 Pin Numbering Convention (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph topview[PLCC 44 TOP VIEW]
        p1["PIN 1 MARKER DOT"] --> p2["LEFT SIDE PINS 1 to 11"]
        p2 --> p3["BOTTOM SIDE PINS 12 to 22"]
        p3 --> p4["RIGHT SIDE PINS 23 to 33"]
        p4 --> p5["TOP SIDE PINS 34 to 44"]
        p5 --> p1
    end
    topview --> desc["NUMBERING COUNTERCLOCKWISE FROM PIN 1 INDICATOR"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Note:** Always draw PLCC from the **TOP VIEW** (component side) when numbering pins. The **counterclockwise** convention is mandated by JEDEC, distinguishing PLCC from many QFPs which may use clockwise numbering.

### 4.4 PLCC Socket Mounting Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PCB["PCB BOARD"] --> SOLDER["SOLDER PASTE REFLOWED"]
    SOLDER --> SOCKET["PLCC SOCKET THROUGH HOLE OR SMT MOUNTED"]
    SOCKET --> CONTACTS["BEAM CONTACTS 4 SIDED"]
    CONTACTS --> JLEADS["J LEADS OF PLCC CHIP"]
    JLEADS --> CHIP["PLCC PACKAGED IC"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Socket?** Sockets allow **field-replaceable units (FRUs)** — if the IC fails, you can pull it out with a PLCC extractor tool and insert a new one without re-soldering. This is critical for **EPROM programming, BIOS upgrade, and prototyping.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
**Define Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC). List any two salient features.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC)** is a square, surface-mount integrated circuit package made of plastic (epoxy molding compound), featuring **J-shaped metal leads** on all four sides with a standardized lead pitch of **1.27 mm**.

**Salient Features:**
1. **Socket-mountable** — can be plugged into a PLCC socket for field replacement.
2. **J-lead geometry** — provides mechanical compliance to absorb thermal stress on PCB.
3. Four-sided peripheral lead arrangement allows higher pin density than DIP packages.

*(Any two features — 1 mark each, 1 mark for definition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]** 
**Compare PLCC and DIP packages based on (a) mounting technique, (b) lead pitch, and (c) typical pin count.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PLCC | DIP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mounting Technique | **Surface Mount (SMT)** — reflow soldering | **Through-Hole (THT)** — wave soldering |
| Lead Pitch | **1.27 mm** | 2.54 mm |
| Typical Pin Count | 20 – 84 | 8 – 64 |

*(1 mark each for any two correct comparisons, 1 mark for the third.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the construction of a Plastic Leaded Chip Carrier (PLCC) package with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of the die attach pad and wire bonds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLCC-68 IC has 17 leads per side with a standard shoulder width of 1.0 mm and lead pitch of 1.27 mm. Calculate the minimum package body dimension. If each lead has an inductance of 2.5 nH and the IC operates at 50 MHz, comment on signal integrity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction of PLCC (7 marks):**

The PLCC package is built layer-by-layer as follows:

1. **Lead Frame Fabrication (2 marks):** A flat copper-alloy (C19400) strip is either **stamped** or **chemically etched** to produce the precise pattern of leads radiating from a central die-attach paddle (DAP). The lead frame extends outward on all four sides and pre-forms the J-lead curl geometry.

2. **Die Attach (1 mark):** The silicon die (5 mm × 5 mm to 12 mm × 12 mm) is bonded onto the DAP using **silver-filled epoxy** (conductive for ground) or **non-conductive epoxy**, depending on electrical requirements.

3. **Wire Bonding (2 marks):** Fine gold (Au) or aluminum (Al) wires of 25 µm diameter are **ultrasonically wedge-bonded** between the die bond pads (typically 80 µm × 80 µm) and the inner ends of the lead-frame fingers. This creates the electrical pathway from the silicon to the outside world.

4. **Encapsulation (1 mark):** The assembly is placed in a transfer-mold press, where heated (175 °C) epoxy-novolac molding compound with 85% silica filler is injected at ~1000 psi, encapsulating the die and wires, leaving only the outer J-leads exposed.

5. **Lead Forming and Plating (1 mark):** The outer leads are formed into the J-shape (down then under) and plated with **tin-lead (SnPb)** or **matte tin (Sn)** for solderability.

**Role of Die Attach Pad:** Provides **mechanical support**, **thermal conduction path** from the die to the package, and often serves as the **electrical ground reference**.

**Role of Wire Bonds:** Provide the **electrical interconnection** between the IC die pads and the package leads, carrying both signals and power.

*(Diagram: students should sketch the top view showing square body, leads on 4 sides, and indicate the die, DAP, and wire bonds.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 68$, $n = 17$ pins/side, $e = 1.27 \text{ mm}$, $W_b = 1.0 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute minimum body dimension:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= W_b + e \times (n - 1) \\
&= 1.0 + 1.27 \times 16 \\
&= 1.0 + 20.32 \\
&= 21.32 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reactance of lead inductance at 50 MHz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_L &= 2 \pi f L = 2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 7.854 \times 10^{-1} \\
&\approx 0.785 \text{ ohm per lead}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop on a signal carrying 10 mA:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I \times X_L = 0.01 \times 0.785 = 7.85 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

This drop is small relative to a 3.3 V logic swing (~0.24%), so **PLCC is acceptable for 50 MHz operation** but marginal for >100 MHz applications. Above 100 MHz, parasitic effects cause impedance mismatches and signal reflections.

**Conclusion:** PLCC-68 with 21.32 mm minimum body dimension is **acceptable for moderate-speed digital circuits (≤75 MHz)** but is **not recommended for high-speed (>100 MHz)** designs. QFP or BGA packages with shorter leads should be preferred for GHz-range signals.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the J-lead geometry of PLCC. Why is the J-lead preferred over the gull-wing lead for certain applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microcontroller in a PLCC-44 package dissipates 1.5 W at an ambient temperature of 55 °C. The package thermal resistance is $\theta_{JA} = 45 {}^\circ\text{C/W}$. Calculate: (i) Junction temperature, (ii) Maximum allowable power dissipation if $T_J(\max) = 150 {}^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_A = 70 {}^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) J-Lead Geometry (7 marks):**

The **J-lead** in a PLCC package curls **downward and then inward beneath the package body**, ending in a solderable foot. The lead exits horizontally from the package shoulder, bends down 90°, curls under, and terminates with the foot.

*(Diagram: Draw side cross-section showing package body, vertical lead drop, J-curl, and foot resting on PCB pad.)*

**Advantages of J-Lead over Gull-Wing:**

1. **Mechanical Robustness (2 marks):** The J-curl acts as a **spring** that absorbs mechanical stress from PCB bending, vibration, and thermal expansion mismatch (CTE between PCB and IC). Gull-wing leads are flat and prone to bending or fatigue fracture.

2. **Socket Compatibility (2 marks):** The curved J-lead naturally mates with **spring-loaded socket contacts** in PLCC sockets. Gull-wing leads are too flat to be reliably socketed.

3. **Reduced Lead Damage During Handling (1 mark):** The tucked-under curl protects the lead from accidental bending during transport and placement.

4. **Inspection Limitation (1 mark):** J-leads are **harder to visually inspect** for solder defects than gull-wing leads — this is the main disadvantage.

5. **Co-Planarity Issues (1 mark):** Strict co-planarity is needed for all four sides; PLCC's standardized molding ensures this.

---

**(b) Thermal Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_{diss} = 1.5 \text{ W}$, $T_A = 55 {}^\circ\text{C}$, $\theta_{JA} = 45 {}^\circ\text{C/W}$.

**Part (i) — Junction Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_J &= T_A + P_{diss} \times \theta_{JA} \\
&= 55 + 1.5 \times 45 \\
&= 55 + 67.5 \\
&= 122.5 {}^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with $T_J(\max) = 150 {}^\circ\text{C}$:** Margin = 27.5 °C → **Operation is safe.**

**Part (ii) — Maximum Power at $T_A = 70 {}^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \frac{T_J(\max) - T_A}{\theta_{JA}} \\
&= \frac{150 - 70}{45} \\
&= \frac{80}{45} \\
&= 1.78 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** At $T_A = 70 {}^\circ\text{C}$, the PLCC-44 can safely dissipate up to **1.78 W**. If the design requires more, forced-air cooling or heatsinking is mandatory.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing J-lead with Gull-wing:** Students often interchange these in diagrams. **J-lead curls UNDER; Gull-wing extends OUTWARD like bird wings.**
> 
> 2. **Wrong Pin Numbering Direction:** PLCC pins are numbered **counterclockwise from Pin 1**. Drawing them clockwise costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the (n–1) factor:** In pitch calculations, students use $n$ instead of $n-1$, which is the most common arithmetic error — examiners specifically look for this.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Units in Thermal Equations:** Mixing °C and K, or forgetting that $\theta_{JA}$ is in °C/W (not K/W) when temperature differences are in °C, both work numerically but notation matters.
> 
> 5. **Confusing PLCC with PQFP:** PQFP (Plastic Quad Flat Pack) uses gull-wing leads, NOT J-leads. Many students write "PLCC is also called PQFP" — **wrong**. PQFP has much finer pitch (0.4–0.8 mm).
> 
> 6. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark question without a diagram typically loses 2 marks even if the description is correct.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PLCC = Plastic + J-leads + 4 sides + 1.27 mm pitch.**
- Standard pin counts: **20, 28, 32, 44, 52, 68, 84** (always multiples of 4).
- J-leads curl **downward and inward** under the package body.
- **Socketable** — unique among surface-mount packages.
- Pin numbering: **counterclockwise from Pin 1 indicator (dot or beveled corner).**
- Standard body sizes follow JEDEC MS-018; common $D = E$ values: 9.27 mm (20-pin), 16.66 mm (44-pin), 24.13 mm (68-pin).
- **Lead pitch formula:** $e = (D - W_b) / (n - 1)$.
- **Thermal model:** $T_J = T_A + P \cdot \theta_{JA}$; typical $\theta_{JA} = 40$ – $50 {}^\circ\text{C/W}$.
- **Lead inductance:** ~2–4 nH/lead → suitable up to **~75 MHz**.
- **Comparisons to remember:**
  - PLCC vs DIP → PLCC is SMT, DIP is THT.
  - PLCC vs QFP → PLCC has J-leads, QFP has gull-wing.
  - PLCC vs SOIC → PLCC is 4-sided, SOIC is 2-sided.
- **Typical applications:** microcontrollers (8051), EPROMs, PLDs, glue logic, BIOS chips.
- **Tools required:** PLCC **extractor tool** to safely remove chip from socket.
- **Molding compound:** Epoxy-novolac with **85% silica filler** by weight.
- **Wire bonds:** **25 µm gold or aluminum**, ultrasonically wedge-bonded.
- **Sockets:** Plastic body with **beryllium-copper spring contacts**.
- **Industry history:** Dominant 1985–2005; replaced by QFP/QFN/BGA in modern designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schematic Entry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Schematic Entry # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Schematic Entry** is the foundational stage of the **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** flow in which an electronic circuit is graphically captured as a logical interconnection of components using standardized symbols, wires (nets), buses, and power ports within an EDA tool such as **KiCad Eeschema, OrCAD Capture, Altium Designer Schematic Editor, or Cadence Virtuoso Schematic Editor**. The output of this process is a human-readable circuit diagram and a machine-readable **Netlist**, which is the formal language understood by downstream PCB layout (place-and-route), simulation, and verification engines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Schematic Entry is the mandatory *first step* before any Printed Circuit Board (PCB) fabrication or SPICE simulation. The KTU examiner expects students to know the *flow*, *libraries*, *annotations*, and *netlist formats* (e.g., **SPICE, EDIF, ODB++, Tango, PADS-ASCII**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are an **architect designing a multi-storey building**: - The **Schematic** is the *floor plan* drawn on paper — it shows where every room, door, and electrical socket is *logically*. - The **Symbol** (e.g., resistor zig-zag, op-amp triangle) is the *icon* representing a physical component. - The **Wire (Net)** is the *electrical wiring* connecting rooms. - The **Netlist** is the *bill of materials + wiring schedule* handed to the contractor (PCB designer). - **ERC (Electrical Rules Check)** is the *building inspector* who checks if you've accidentally connected a water pipe to an electrical line. Just as no construction begins without a floor plan, **no PCB layout or fabrication can begin without a completed and verified schematic**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Schematic** | A logical circuit diagram using standardized graphical symbols. | | **Symbol** | A graphical icon (e.g., zig-zag for resistor) representing a real component. | | **Footprint / Land Pattern** | The physical pad geometry on the PCB for soldering the component. | | **Net** | A uniquely named electrical conductor connecting two or more pins. | | **Netlist** | A text file enumerating every component and the nets its pins belong to. | | **Refdes** | Reference Designator — the unique alpha-numeric ID (R1, U2, C5). | | **Bus** | A graphical grouping of multiple nets (used for data/address buses). | | **Hierarchy** | A multi-sheet design technique using sub-circuits and sheet symbols. | > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Standard:** In industry, the schematic is a **legal design document**. It is reviewed, signed off, and version-controlled before any layout work begins. Under **KTU 2024 OBE**, schematic capture directly maps to **CO2: Apply EDA tools for electronic circuit design**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Schematic Entry Workflow (Step-by-Step) A KTU-validated schematic entry process follows this rigorous sequence: ### Step 1 — Project Initialization - Launch the EDA tool (e.g., KiCad 8.x Project Manager). - Create a new project workspace. A *project* in KiCad is a directory containing the schematic file (`.kicad_sch`), the PCB file (`.kicad_pcb`), and supporting cache files. - Set global preferences: **sheet size (A4/A3/A2), grid resolution (typically 50 mil or 1.27 mm), and title block metadata**. ### Step 2 — Library Setup - **Symbol Libraries** must be configured. Common formats are `.lib` (OrCAD), `.OLB` (legacy), or `.kicad_sym` (KiCad 8+). - A *symbol library* contains graphical symbols and their pin-to-pad mapping. The pin names (e.g., `D`, `~Q`, `VCC`) become the link to the footprint pad numbers. ### Step 3 — Component Placement - Place symbols onto the schematic sheet using *Place Component* commands. - Each placed symbol carries three mandatory properties: - **Reference Designator (Refdes):** e.g., `R1`, `U3`, `C12` - **Value:** e.g., `10k`, `0.1uF`, `LM358` - **Footprint:** e.g., `0805`, `DIP-8`, `SOIC-8` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of KTU:** *A symbol without a footprint is incomplete.* Without the footprint assignment, the netlist cannot drive a PCB layout. This is a frequent cause of part marks in KTU exams. ### Step 4 — Wiring (Nets) and Power Ports - Use the **Wire** tool to draw logical connections between component pins. - Use **Net Labels** to name nets (e.g., `CLK`, `DATA`, `VCC_5V`). - Use **Power Ports / Power Symbols** (VCC, GND, +12V) to tie nets to global power rails. - **Buses** are used when more than 4–5 parallel signals (e.g., an 8-bit data bus `D[0..7]`) must be grouped visually. ### Step 5 — Annotation - The EDA tool auto-assigns unique reference designators (e.g., `R1, R2, R3...`) using the *Annotate Schematic* command. - This step is mandatory before netlist generation; duplicate refdeses cause **ERC errors**. ### Step 6 — Electrical Rules Check (ERC) - ERC validates logical integrity: - Unconnected pins (floating inputs) - Power-pin mismatches (e.g., VCC connected to GND) - Output-to-output shorts - Undriven inputs - Warnings/errors must be resolved or explicitly justified. ### Step 7 — Netlist Generation - The **Netlist** is the bridge to PCB layout and SPICE simulation. It is a structured ASCII file listing every component and the nets connected to its pins. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Syntax Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Although schematic entry is graphical, the underlying **netlist format** is textual. KTU examiners love asking students to **write/read a netlist snippet**. ### Generic Netlist Line Structure A SPICE-style netlist line follows the rigid format: $$\text{Netlist Line} = \{\,\text{NetName}_1,\ \text{NetName}_2,\ \dots,\ \text{NetName}_N,\ \text{Refdes},\ \text{Value}\,\}$$ ### Common Netlist File Extensions | Tool | Netlist Extension | Format | |---|---|---| | KiCad | `.net` | KiCad Legacy / SPICE | | OrCAD Capture | `.net` | PADS-ASCII / EDIF | | Altium Designer | `.NET` / `.NETXML` | Protel / Tango | | LTspice / PSpice | `.cir` / `.net` | SPICE 2G6 / 3F5 | | Cadence | `.net` / `.si` | Spectre / CDL | | Mentor Graphics | `.net` | DxDesigner | ### ERC Severity Levels | Severity | Description | Action | |---|---|---| | **Error** | Definite fault (e.g., power short) | Must be fixed | | **Warning** | Suspicious condition (e.g., floating input) | Should be justified | | **Info / Note** | Stylistic remark | Optional fix | ### Standard Power Rail Symbol Names $$\text{VCC},\ \text{VDD},\ \text{V+},\ \text{+5V},\ \text{+3V3},\ \text{GND},\ \text{VSS},\ \text{V-},\ \text{VEE}$$ > [!NOTE] > Power ports with the *same symbol name* are **globally connected** across the entire schematic (and across all hierarchical sub-sheets). This is why you never wire a global ground pin with a wire — you simply place the GND power symbol. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mapping a Schematic to a SPICE Netlist **Problem:** Given the following simple RC low-pass filter schematic, write its SPICE-compatible netlist manually. **Schematic Description:** - Resistor `R1` (Value = 1k) connected between node `IN` and node `OUT`. - Capacitor `C1` (Value = 100n) connected between node `OUT` and node `GND`. - Input source `V1` (Value = 5V AC sine) connected between `IN` and `GND`. - Output node `OUT` is probed. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Identify all unique nets (nodes).** By inspection of the schematic, the nodes are: - `IN` - `OUT` - `GND` (reference node, conventionally numbered `0` in SPICE) **Step 2 — Identify all components and their terminal connections.** | Component | Terminal 1 | Terminal 2 | |---|---|---| | V1 (Source) | IN | GND | | R1 (Resistor) | IN | OUT | | C1 (Capacitor) | OUT | GND | **Step 3 — Translate each component row into a SPICE card.** A SPICE card for a resistor uses the format: $$\text{R\textlangle refdes\textrangle}\quad \text{Node+}\quad \text{Node-}\quad \text{Value}$$ For a capacitor: $$\text{C\textlangle refdes\textrangle}\quad \text{Node+}\quad \text{Node-}\quad \text{Value}$$ For an independent DC/AC voltage source: $$\text{V\textlangle refdes\textrangle}\quad \text{Node+}\quad \text{Node-}\quad \text{Type}\quad \text{Value(s)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the values.** For `R1` (between `IN` and `OUT`, 1kΩ): $$\text{R1}\quad \text{IN}\quad \text{OUT}\quad \text{1k}$$ For `C1` (between `OUT` and `0`, 100nF): $$\text{C1}\quad \text{OUT}\quad 0\quad \text{100n}$$ For `V1` (SIN source between `IN` and `0`, amplitude 5): $$\text{V1}\quad \text{IN}\quad 0\quad \text{SIN}(0\ \ 5\ \ 1k\ \ 0\ \ 0\ \ 0)$$ **Step 5 — Assemble the complete netlist file.** The assembled netlist becomes: ``` * RC Low-Pass Filter Netlist * Generated manually from schematic .INCLUDE library_file.lib V1 IN 0 SIN(0 5 1k) R1 IN OUT 1k C1 OUT 0 100n .TRAN 1u 5m .PROBE .END ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This derivation illustrates why the **schematic must be error-free**. Every net label and every power symbol becomes either a node name or a `0` (ground) node in the netlist. A single typo — e.g., labelling a net `CLK` on one sheet and `CKL` on another — will cause the simulator/layout tool to treat them as **two isolated nets**, leading to silent functional failure. ## 3.2 Derivation: Hierarchical Sheet Pin Resolution In multi-sheet (hierarchical) designs, a **Sheet Symbol** on the parent sheet contains **Sheet Pins** that map one-to-one with **Hierarchical Pins (or Power Ports)** on the child sheet. ### Resolution Rule: $$\text{Net}_{parent\_pin} \equiv \text{Net}_{child\_pin} \quad \text{if and only if Pin Names Match Exactly}$$ **Example Hierarchy:** - **Parent Sheet:** Top-level block diagram with sheet symbol `U_MCU` having pins `VCC`, `GND`, `CLK_IN`. - **Child Sheet:** `U_MCU.sch` containing the MCU sub-circuit with hierarchical pins `VCC`, `GND`, `CLK_IN`. All three named pins are electrically continuous. If the parent sheet connects `VCC` to a `+3V3` power rail, then any net named `VCC` inside `U_MCU.sch` is also at **+3.3 V**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Hierarchical Designs):** Students often confuse *global power symbols* (VCC, GND) with *hierarchical pins*. Power symbols are project-global; hierarchical pins are *local* to the sub-sheet. Misnaming a hierarchical pin breaks the connection silently. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Entry Position in the EDA Flow The schematic entry step sits at the very beginning of the design chain. The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete flow: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Design Specification"] --> B["Schematic Entry
(Schematic Capture Tool)"] B --> C{"ERC Check
Electrical Rules Check"} C -->|"Errors Found"| B C -->|"Pass / Justified Warnings"| D["Netlist Generation
SPICE / EDIF / PADS-ASCII"] D --> E["SPICE Simulation
Analog or Mixed-Signal"] E --> F{"Simulation
Meets Spec?"} F -->|"No"| B F -->|"Yes"| G["PCB Footprint Mapping
Assign Land Patterns"] G --> H["PCB Layout
Place and Route"] H --> I["DRC Design Rules Check"] I --> J["Gerber / ODB++ Output"] J --> K["Fabrication and Assembly"] K --> L["Board Bring-Up and Test"] ``` ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Schematic Capture Tool The following diagram shows the modular architecture of a typical EDA schematic editor (KiCad Eeschema / OrCAD Capture): ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USER["User Interface Layer"] UI1["Symbol Picker"] UI2["Wire / Bus Tool"] UI3["Property Editor"] UI4["Annotation Tool"] end subgraph CORE["Core Engine"] ENG1["Schematic Database
Objects, Nets, Pins"] ENG2["Library Manager
Symbol and Footprint Libs"] ENG3["ERC Engine
Electrical Rules"] ENG4["Netlister
Format Converters"] end subgraph OUT["Output Stage"] OUT1["SPICE Netlist
for Simulation"] OUT2["PCB Netlist
for Layout"] OUT3["BOM Bill of Materials"] OUT4["PDF / SVG Print"] end UI1 --> ENG2 UI2 --> ENG1 UI3 --> ENG1 UI4 --> ENG1 ENG1 --> ENG3 ENG3 --> ENG4 ENG4 --> OUT1 ENG4 --> OUT2 ENG1 --> OUT3 ENG1 --> OUT4 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Design Structure For complex designs (e.g., a microcontroller-based data acquisition system), the schematic is split into hierarchical sub-sheets. The following Mermaid block demonstrates the mapping: ```mermaid graph TD TOP["Parent Sheet
Top Block Diagram"] TOP --> S1["Sheet Symbol U1
Power Supply Sub-circuit"] TOP --> S2["Sheet Symbol U2
MCU and Clock Sub-circuit"] TOP --> S3["Sheet Symbol U3
Sensor Front-End Sub-circuit"] TOP --> S4["Sheet Symbol U4
Communication Interface Sub-circuit"] S1 -.->|VCC, GND, +5V, +3V3| C1["Child Sheet U1.sch
Linear Regulator, Decoupling"] S2 -.->|VCC, GND, CLK, RESET| C2["Child Sheet U2.sch
MCU + Crystal + Reset"] S3 -.->|VCC, GND, SENSE, REF| C3["Child Sheet U3.sch
Op-amp + Filter"] S4 -.->|VCC, GND, TX, RX| C4["Child Sheet U4.sch
UART or SPI"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Best Practice:** A KTU-grade hierarchical schematic should never exceed **5–7 sub-sheets** without a clearly labelled *top-level functional diagram*. Industrial practice (per IPC-2611) recommends one functional block per sheet. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define Schematic Entry. List any four EDA tools used for schematic capture.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Schematic Entry is the process of graphically capturing an electronic circuit design using standardized symbols and wires within an EDA tool, producing a logical circuit diagram and a netlist for downstream PCB layout and simulation.* Four widely used EDA tools for schematic capture are: 1. **KiCad Eeschema** (open-source, industry-standard for students) 2. **OrCAD Capture** (Cadence) 3. **Altium Designer Schematic Editor** 4. **Cadence Virtuoso Schematic Editor** *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing tools with brief relevance = 3 marks)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **What is a Netlist? Mention any two netlist file formats.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *A Netlist is a structured, machine-readable text file generated at the end of the schematic capture process. It enumerates every component (with reference designator and value) and lists the electrical nets to which each of its pins is connected. It serves as the bridge between schematic capture and PCB layout or SPICE simulation.* Two netlist file formats: 1. **SPICE format** (`.cir` or `.net`) — used for analog/mixed-signal simulation. 2. **PADS-ASCII format** (`.asc` / `.net`) — used for PCB place-and-route tools. *(1.5 marks definition + 1.5 marks formats = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the complete step-by-step procedure for **Schematic Entry** using any standard EDA tool. Clearly state the role of each step and the output produced. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is **ERC (Electrical Rules Check)**? Explain at least **four common ERC violation types** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Step-by-step Schematic Entry Procedure (7 Marks):** 1. **Project Initialization** — Create a new project workspace, set sheet size (typically A4 or A3) and grid pitch (50 mil or 1.27 mm). (1 mark) 2. **Library Setup** — Load or configure symbol libraries containing standard primitives (resistors, capacitors) and ICs (e.g., LM358, 8051). (1 mark) 3. **Component Placement** — Place symbols on the canvas; each component carries a *Refdes*, a *Value*, and a *Footprint* property. (1 mark) 4. **Wiring (Net Routing)** — Use the wire tool to electrically connect pins; use **Net Labels** to assign names to nets (e.g., `CLK`, `D[0]`). Use **Power Ports** (VCC, GND) for global rails. (1 mark) 5. **Bus Routing** *(if applicable)* — Group multi-bit buses like `D[0..7]` or `A[0..15]` using the bus tool, with bus entries labelling individual bits. (0.5 mark) 6. **Annotation** — Run *Annotate Schematic* to auto-assign unique reference designators (R1, R2...). (0.5 mark) 7. **ERC and Netlist Generation** — Run ERC to validate, then export the netlist (`.net`) for PCB layout and SPICE simulation. (1 mark) 8. **Output** — A schematic file (`.kicad_sch`), a netlist, a BOM, and PDF/SVG printout.
