(1 mark) **(b) ERC and its Violation Types (7 Marks):** *Electrical Rules Check (ERC) is a built-in static verification engine in EDA tools that flags logical inconsistencies in the schematic without performing a full simulation.* Four common ERC violation types: 1. **Unconnected (floating) input pins** — A logic gate input that is not tied to any driver or pull-up/down resistor. *(Example: a NAND gate input left dangling.)* (1.75 marks) 2. **Power-pin mismatch** — A component's VCC pin connected to a net labelled `+5V` while the GND pin is left floating, or VCC shorted to GND. *(Example: Op-amp V+ pin wired to GND.)* (1.75 marks) 3. **Output-to-output conflict** — Two output pins from different gates driving the same net (bus contention). *(Example: two tri-state buffers both enabled simultaneously.)* (1.75 marks) 4. **Undeclared power on a power pin** — A pin labelled `VCC` on a symbol is connected to a net not named `VCC` or any recognized power symbol. The ERC warns of a *power pin not driven by a power port*. (1.75 marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B:** > 1. **Do not confuse ERC with DRC.** ERC runs on the *schematic* (logical); DRC runs on the *PCB layout* (physical). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Never skip the ERC step in your answer.** KTU examiners give 1–2 marks specifically for stating that ERC must be run *after* annotation and *before* netlist generation. > 3. **A floating input is a *Warning*, not always an Error.** Mentioning this nuance earns a bonus mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Differentiate between a **Schematic Symbol** and a **Footprint**. Explain the process of **Footprint Assignment** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **Hierarchical Schematic Design**. Draw a labelled block diagram showing the parent sheet, sheet symbols, sheet pins, and child sheets, and discuss two advantages of hierarchical design over flat (single-sheet) design. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Symbol vs Footprint and Footprint Assignment (7 Marks):** | Aspect | Schematic Symbol | PCB Footprint | |---|---|---| | **Domain** | Logical / Schematic | Physical / PCB | | **Contains** | Pin names, Refdes, Value | Copper pads, silkscreen, drill holes | | **File location** | Symbol library (`.kicad_sym`) | Footprint library (`.pretty/`) | | **Visibility** | Shown in Eeschema / OrCAD | Shown in PCBNew / Allegro | *Symbol* represents the **logical function** of a component (e.g., a resistor zig-zag with two pins named `1` and `2`). *Footprint* represents the **physical land pattern** on the PCB (e.g., two SMD pads spaced 2.54 mm apart for an axial resistor). **Footprint Assignment Process:** During schematic entry, every placed symbol's *Footprint property field* is populated with the exact library name of its corresponding land pattern. Example: For an 0805 resistor `R1` of value `10k`, the Footprint property is set to **`Resistor_SMD:R_0805_2012Metric`**. (3.5 marks for comparison table + 3.5 marks for assignment process with example.) **(b) Hierarchical Schematic Design (7 Marks):** *Hierarchical design* is a multi-sheet schematic organization technique in which a complex circuit is partitioned into functional blocks. The **parent sheet** (also called the *root sheet*) contains **Sheet Symbols** — labelled rectangles that visually represent each sub-circuit. Each sheet symbol exposes **Sheet Pins** that correspond to **Hierarchical Pins** on the **Child Sheet**. **Labelled Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Parent Sheet Root
Top Level Block Diagram"] SS1["Sheet Symbol: Power
Pins: VCC IN, +5V OUT, GND"] SS2["Sheet Symbol: MCU
Pins: VCC, GND, CLK, RESET, TX, RX"] SS3["Sheet Symbol: Sensor
Pins: VCC, GND, SENSE_OUT"] CS1["Child Sheet Power.sch
Linear Regulator + Caps"] CS2["Child Sheet MCU.sch
Microcontroller + Crystal"] CS3["Child Sheet Sensor.sch
Op-amp + Filter"] P --> SS1 P --> SS2 P --> SS3 SS1 -.->|Pins Match| CS1 SS2 -.->|Pins Match| CS2 SS3 -.->|Pins Match| CS3 ``` **Two Advantages of Hierarchical Design:** 1. **Readability and Maintainability:** Each functional block is on its own sheet, making the design easier to review, debug, and reuse. (1.5 marks) 2. **Reusability:** A child sheet (e.g., a UART sub-circuit) can be instantiated multiple times in the same project or copied into a new project without redrawing. (1.5 marks) *(2 marks for the labelled diagram and remaining marks for accurate explanation.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** > 1. **Do not confuse Footprint with Symbol.** Saying "footprint is the symbol drawn on the PCB" will cost full marks. Footprint is the *land pattern with pads*, not a schematic symbol. > 2. **For hierarchical diagrams,** always label both the *sheet pins on the parent* and the *hierarchical pins on the child* — KTU examiners allocate marks specifically for the *matching pin names*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Schematic Entry is the first step** in the EDA flow — schematic → netlist → PCB layout → fabrication. - A complete schematic entry requires **Refdes, Value, and Footprint** on every component. - **Power Symbols (VCC, GND)** are project-global; **Hierarchical Pins** are local to a sub-sheet. - **ERC** is run *after annotation* and *before netlist generation* — it catches logical, not physical, errors. - **Netlist formats** to remember: SPICE, PADS-ASCII, EDIF, Tango, ODB++. - **Hierarchy** uses sheet symbols + matching pin names; mismatch causes silent disconnection. - **Buses** group related nets visually but require *bus entries* to label individual bits. - **Annotation** must be re-run after any addition/deletion to avoid duplicate RefDes errors. - **Never fabricate references** — only describe tools that are documented (KiCad, OrCAD, Altium, Cadence, LTspice). - For KTU exams, **always draw the EDA flow diagram** whenever asked about schematic entry — it is a guaranteed 2–3 marks. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — Schematic Entry** > **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OBE-Compliant | CO2 Mapped**

topics.title: Net Listing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Net Listing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition In the domain of **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)**, a **Net** is formally defined as an electrically continuous conductive path connecting two or more component pins, terminals, or junctions within an electronic circuit. **Net Listing** (also called **Netlist Generation** or **Connectivity Extraction**) is the systematic, automated process of identifying, naming, cataloguing, and outputting every distinct electrical net in a schematic design into a structured, machine-readable textual or data file. The resulting output — the **Netlist** — is the canonical, technology-independent intermediate representation of the circuit. It acts as the **critical interface** between the *Schematic Capture* (front-end) and downstream tools such as *Simulation*, *PCB Layout*, *Design Rule Checking (DRC)*, and *Manufacturing Output Generation*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A netlist is *not* a schematic. It contains no graphical or spatial information. It is purely a **list of electrical connections** and component instances — the abstract "wiring diagram" of the circuit in pure text form. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are the **architect of a large office building** and you have a beautiful, detailed *blueprint* showing where every room, door, pipe, and wire is located spatially. The blueprint tells you **"where things are"** — that is your **schematic**. Now, before the electricians start pulling actual cables through the walls, you hand them a separate, boring-looking **spreadsheet** that says: - "Wire #A14 → Connects Switch #3 to Light #7 to Light #8 to Fuse #2" - "Wire #B07 → Connects Outlet #5 to Outlet #6 to Circuit Breaker #12" - "Component #R1 (1 kΩ) → Pin 1 on Wire #A14, Pin 2 on Wire #GND" That spreadsheet — the boring but absolutely essential wiring list — is the **Netlist**. The electricians don't care about the pretty floor plan; they just need to know **"what connects to what."** The netlist tells the EDA tools (and ultimately the PCB fabrication machines) exactly which component pins must be physically joined by copper traces. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Schematic = *Visual/Graphical* description (spatial). **Netlist = *Connectivity* description (topological/relational)**. The schematic is for *humans to read and design*; the netlist is for *machines to manufacture and verify*. ### Standard Metrics & Terminology - **Node:** A single point of electrical connection; in a netlist, a node usually corresponds to one net. - **Pin / Terminal:** A physical connection point on a component (e.g., pin 1 of an IC). - **Instance:** A placed copy of a component in the design (e.g., `R1`, `U2`, `C5`). - **Power Nets:** Special nets like **VCC (+5V)**, **VDD (+3.3V)**, **GND (0V)**, **VEE (-5V)** that are usually implicitly handled. - **Floating Net:** A net connected to only one pin (a serious design error called *dangling net*). - **Short Circuit (in netlist):** Two distinct nets that the netlist tool believes are connected but should not be — a *critical* fault. - Standard industry EDA tool file extensions for netlists: **`.net`**, **`.edf`**, **`.spc`**, **`.ckt`**, **`.txt`**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Netlist — Structural Elements Every netlist, regardless of its specific format dialect, contains three mandatory structural sections: 1. **Header / Comment Block** — Metadata: design name, date, author, tool version, units. 2. **Component Instance List** — Declares every part placed in the design. 3. **Net Connectivity List** — Declares every unique net and lists all pins joined by that net. > [!NOTE] > Optional sections include: **Parameter values** (resistance, capacitance), **Hierarchical path descriptors** (for sub-circuits), and **Simulation directives** (only in SPICE dialects). ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic of Netlist Generation The EDA tool performs the following deterministic operations in sequence: **Step 1 — Schematic Parsing** The tool ingests the schematic database. Every placed symbol (resistor, IC, connector) is recognized as an *instance* and assigned a unique **Reference Designator** (e.g., `R1`, `U3`, `J2`). **Step 2 — Wire & Junction Traversal (Graph Traversal)** The schematic's connectivity graph is traversed. Wires, bus entries, and explicit pin-to-pin connections form **edges**; pins and junctions form **nodes**. The tool performs a **graph connectivity analysis** (typically using Union-Find or BFS algorithms internally) to find all pins reachable from one another through unbroken conductive paths. **Step 3 — Net Identification** All pins connected together electrically are grouped into a single **Equivalence Class** (a *net*). The tool assigns each group a name. Naming priority is: 1. **User-assigned net label** (e.g., `CLK_50MHZ`, `DATA_BUS[3]`) — *highest priority*. 2. **Power symbol name** (e.g., `VCC`, `GND`). 3. **Auto-generated name** (e.g., `N0001`, `N$142`) — *lowest priority*. **Step 4 — Property Harvesting** The tool extracts parametric values from each component: `R1 = 1k`, `C5 = 100nF`, `U2 = LM358`. These are attached to the instance declarations. **Step 5 — Output Formatting** The collected graph is serialized into the chosen **Netlist Format** (SPICE, EDIF, PADS, etc.) and written to disk. ### 2.3 Hierarchical vs. Flat Netlist | Feature | Flat Netlist | Hierarchical Netlist | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | Single, monolithic list of all nets in the entire design | Preserves sub-circuit / module boundaries | | **Readability** | Poor for large designs | Excellent — matches design intent | | **Reuse** | Difficult to re-use sub-blocks | Sub-blocks can be instantiated multiple times | | **Tool Support** | Universally supported | Requires EDA tools with hierarchy awareness | | **Use Case** | Final PCB fabrication, simple simulation | Large ASIC / FPGA / SoC designs, team-based projects | ### 2.4 Common Netlist Formats (Industry-Standard Dialects) | Format | Extension | Primary Use | Key Characteristics | |---|---|---|---| | **SPICE** | `.cir`, `.sp`, `.net` | Circuit simulation (analog/RF) | Human-readable, line-based, includes values and simulation commands | | **EDIF** | `.edf`, `.edn` | Inter-tool data exchange (generic) | LISP-like syntax, fully machine-targeted, very verbose | | **Protel/Altium** | `.net` | Altium Designer PCB flow | Compact, tagged format | | **Cadence / OrCAD** | `.net` | Cadence Allegro / OrCAD Capture | Hierarchical, supports properties | | **Mentor PADS** | `.asc`, `.txt` | PADS Layout / DX Designer | Sectional format with `*PART*`, `*NET*`, `*END*` tags | | **VHDL/Verilog** | `.vhd`, `.v` | Digital synthesis & simulation | Structural netlist generated post-synthesis (gate-level) | | **IBIS** | `.ibs` | Signal integrity simulation | Behavioral I/O buffer models, not a true netlist | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept / Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Convention | |---|---|---| | $G = (V, E)$ | Graph representation of a netlist | $V$ = nodes (pins), $E$ = edges (wires) | | $N$ | Total number of unique nets in a design | $N = \vert \text{Equivalence Classes of Pins} \vert$ | | $P$ | Total pin count | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i$ where $p_i$ = pins on component $i$ | | $C$ | Total component instances | — | | Floating Net Count | $\text{Floating} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\text{pins}(n) = 1)$ | Must equal **0** in a clean design | | Short Net Count | $\text{Shorts} = \text{Number of merged distinct net labels against design intent}$ | Must equal **0** in a clean design | | **Default GND net name** | `0`, `GND`, `VSS`, `GND_ANA`, `GND_DIG` | Convention varies; modern designs use `GND` | | **Default Power net names** | `VCC`, `VDD`, `VEE`, `VPP`, `VBAT` | Convention varies by logic family | | File extension norms | `.net`, `.edf`, `.cir` | Tool-dependent | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every single PCB ever manufactured — from a ₹50 toy to a ₹50,000,000 satellite — has had a netlist as the bridge between the engineer's design intent and the physical copper board. Without a correct netlist, **no automated PCB fabrication is possible**. The netlist is the *single source of truth* for connectivity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Counting Nets from a Schematic **Problem Statement:** Given a simple schematic with $C$ components and a total of $P$ pins, where the tool has identified the connectivity equivalence classes, derive the formula relating the total number of nets $N$ to the design parameters. **Step 1 — Define the connectivity model.** Every pin belongs to exactly **one** net (by definition of the equivalence relation). If we let $n_k$ denote the number of pins connected to net $k$, then: $$ \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k = P $$ *Explanation:* Every pin in the entire design is a member of exactly one net, and we are simply partitioning the set of $P$ pins into $N$ disjoint groups (nets). Summing the sizes of all groups yields the total pin count. **Step 2 — Account for the implicit ground/power rails.** In EDA convention, the symbol `0` or `GND` is treated as the universal reference. Let $N_p$ be the number of explicit power nets. Then: $$ N = N_{\text{signal}} + N_p $$ *Explanation:* Signal nets carry data, clock, or control; power nets distribute supply. They are counted separately for filtering and clearance-rule purposes in PCB layout. **Step 3 — Detect floating nets using an indicator function.** Define $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ as the indicator function, which equals **1** if its argument is true, else **0**. A floating net is one with exactly one pin: $$ N_{\text{float}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(n_k = 1) $$ *Explanation:* This formula is the diagnostic used by the EDA tool's **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)** module to flag dangling pins. A clean design requires $N_{\text{float}} = 0$. **Step 4 — Detect shorts (merged distinct labels).** If two user-assigned labels `CLK_50` and `CLK_100` are found in the same equivalence class, this is a **short**: $$ N_{\text{short}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \max\left(0,\; L_k - 1\right) $$ where $L_k$ is the number of *user-assigned, distinct* labels on net $k$. *Explanation:* A correctly designed net has at most one user label. If two different labels share the same electrical class, it is a connection error. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Netlist Extraction for an RC Low-Pass Filter **Given Circuit:** An RC low-pass filter with a 1 kΩ resistor and a 1 µF capacitor driven by a 5 V DC source. **Schematic (logical description):** - `V1`: Positive terminal (pin 1) connects to net `IN`. Negative terminal (pin 2) connects to `GND`. - `R1`: Pin 1 on `IN`. Pin 2 on node `N1`. - `C1`: Pin 1 on `N1`. Pin 2 on `GND`. - Output node `OUT` is also tapped from `N1`. **Step 1 — Identify all nets:** | Net Name | Pins Connected | |---|---| | `IN` | V1.pin1, R1.pin1 | | `N1` (= `OUT`) | R1.pin2, C1.pin1, (output probe) | | `GND` | V1.pin2, C1.pin2 | **Step 2 — Verify using the sum equation:** $$ P = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ pins total} $$ $$ \sum_{k=1}^{3} n_k = n_{\text{IN}} + n_{\text{N1}} + n_{\text{GND}} = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \;\checkmark $$ *Explanation:* Three nets (IN, N1, GND) collectively account for all 6 pins. The math balances, confirming the netlist extraction is consistent. **Step 3 — Floating-net audit:** No net has exactly one pin (the smallest is `GND` with 2). Thus $N_{\text{float}} = 0$ — design is clean. **Step 4 — Generate the SPICE Netlist Output:** ```spice * RC Low-Pass Filter — Netlist * Generated: KTU Exam Example V1 IN GND DC 5V R1 IN N1 1k C1 N1 GND 1uF .END ``` **Step 5 — Line-by-line decoding (model answer style for KTU):** | Line | Element | Node A | Node B | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `V1 IN GND DC 5V` | Independent Voltage Source | `IN` | `GND` | 5 V DC | Source between IN and ground | | `R1 IN N1 1k` | Resistor instance | `IN` | `N1` | 1 kΩ | Series resistor | | `C1 N1 GND 1uF` | Capacitor instance | `N1` | `GND` | 1 µF | Shunt capacitor to ground | | `.END` | SPICE directive | — | — | — | Marks end of netlist | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** In a SPICE netlist, the **first node listed** for a two-terminal element is conventionally the **positive (or top) terminal**. The first letter of the element name (`R`, `C`, `L`, `V`, `I`, `D`, `Q`) **determines the type**. Knowing these conventions is a guaranteed mark-scoring point. ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Netlist Formats at a Glance | Parameter | SPICE Netlist | EDIF Netlist | Protel/Altium Netlist | |---|---|---|---| | **Syntax** | Free-form, line-based | LISP-style S-expressions | Tagged bracket format | | **Human Readable** | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Very poor | ⚠️ Moderate | | **Includes Values** | ✅ Yes (e.g., `1k`, `1uF`) | ❌ No — references external library | ⚠️ Optional | | **Includes Simulation Commands** | ✅ Yes (`.TRAN`, `.DC`) | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Hierarchy Support** | ⚠️ Via `.SUBCKT` | ✅ Native, robust | ✅ Native | | **Industry Primary Use** | Analog/RF simulation | Tool-agnostic data exchange | Altium PCB flow | | **KTU Exam Likelihood** | **Highest** | Medium | Low | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Netlist Generation Flow in the EDA Pipeline This diagram maps the **position of netlist generation** in the overall electronic hardware design flow — from concept to manufactured board. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Design Concept
and Specifications] --> B[Schematic Capture
Place symbols and wires] B --> C{Netlist Generation
Connectivity Extraction} C --> D[Simulation
SPICE Engine] C --> E[PCB Layout
Component Placement and Routing] C --> F[ERC and DRC
Electrical and Design Rule Check] D --> G[Simulation Results
Waveforms] E --> H[Gerber Files
Manufacturing Output] F --> I[Verified Design] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Fabrication and Assembly] ``` *Reading the diagram:* The **schematic** is the single human-authored input. The **netlist** is the central hub — it branches out to three independent consumers: the simulator, the PCB layout tool, and the rule-checker. All downstream artifacts eventually converge into a verified, fabrication-ready design. ### 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Netlist Extraction Engine This block diagram shows the *internal modules* the EDA tool activates during netlist generation. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input S1[Schematic Database
.DSN or .SCH] L1[Component Library
.OLB or .LIA] end subgraph Engine [Netlist Extraction Engine] P1[Parser Module] --> G1[Connectivity Graph Builder] L1 --> G1 G1 --> EC1[Equivalence Class Finder
Union-Find Algorithm] EC1 --> NM1[Net Naming Module
User Labels then Auto IDs] NM1 --> PV1[Parameter Value Harvester] PV1 --> FR1[Format Serializer
SPICE or EDIF etc.] end subgraph Output N1[Netlist File
.NET or .CIR or .EDF] end S1 --> P1 FR1 --> N1 ``` *Reading the diagram:* The schematic and the library enter the engine. Five internal processing blocks transform connectivity information into a published netlist file. The **Equivalence Class Finder** is the mathematical core — it implements the graph theory that groups pins into nets. ### 4.3 Hierarchical Design and Netlist Structure This diagram illustrates how a hierarchical schematic maps to a hierarchical netlist. ```mermaid flowchart TD Top[Top Level Schematic
ROOT_NETLIST] --> SubA[Sub-circuit A
Power Supply] Top --> SubB[Sub-circuit B
Analog Frontend] Top --> SubC[Sub-circuit C
Microcontroller] SubA --> Net1[Net VCC_5V] SubA --> Net2[Net GND] SubB --> Net3[Net SENSOR_IN] SubB --> Net4[Net AMP_OUT] SubC --> Net5[Net CLK_8MHZ] SubC --> Net6[Net UART_TX] Net1 -.Shared.-> Top Net2 -.Shared.-> Top Net3 -.Shared.-> Top ``` *Reading the diagram:* The top-level netlist references each sub-circuit as a hierarchical block. Nets that cross sub-circuit boundaries (e.g., `VCC_5V`, `GND`, `SENSOR_IN`) are promoted to the top-level scope. This matches exactly what the textual hierarchical netlist will show. ### 4.4 Sample Netlist Visualization (Connectivity Graph Form) This Mermaid graph renders the *logical connectivity* of a small circuit — directly equivalent to what the netlist encodes textually. ```mermaid graph LR V1P[V1 Pin 1] --- N_IN[Net IN] V1N[V1 Pin 2] --- N_GND[Net GND] R1A[R1 Pin 1] --- N_IN R1B[R1 Pin 2] --- N_N1[Net N1] C1A[C1 Pin 1] --- N_N1 C1B[C1 Pin 2] --- N_GND OUT[Output Probe] --- N_N1 ``` *Reading the diagram:* Every horizontal line is a wire (edge). Every labelled box is a pin or a net (node). Pins grouped under the same net label (e.g., `V1 Pin 1`, `R1 Pin 1` both connect to `Net IN`) are **electrically identical** — that is the *net* in its purest form. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *Define a 'net' in the context of Electronic Design Automation. Why is a netlist considered a technology-independent representation of a circuit?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **net** is an electrically continuous conductive path that connects two or more component pins in a circuit, forming a single node of electrical equipotentiality. A **netlist** is called technology-independent because it does **not** contain any information about: - Physical component packages (e.g., SOIC, QFP, BGA) - PCB layer stack-up - Trace widths, copper pours, or physical routing geometry - Specific manufacturer part numbers beyond functional identity It contains only the **abstract connectivity and parametric values**. The same netlist can be used to fabricate a board with 0805 resistors, 0603 resistors, or a completely different technology — proving its technology-independence. *(3 marks — full credit for: definition 1m, reason 1m, justification with examples 1m.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *List any three standard netlist formats used in EDA tools and state one distinguishing feature of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Format | Distinguishing Feature | |---|---|---| | 1 | **SPICE** (`.cir`) | Human-readable, includes component *values* and *simulation directives* like `.TRAN` | | 2 | **EDIF** (`.edf`) | Tool-agnostic interchange format using LISP-style S-expressions; machine-targeted, not human-friendly | | 3 | **Protel/Altium** (`.net`) | Uses tagged bracket syntax; native to Altium Designer PCB flow; supports hierarchical blocks | *(1 mark per correctly stated format with valid feature. 3 marks for three rows.)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Note:** A 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either sub-part may be split into further sub-clauses at the examiner's discretion. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **process of netlist generation** from a schematic in an EDA tool. List the **three mandatory sections** of any netlist file. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Differentiate between a **flat netlist** and a **hierarchical netlist**. Explain with a small example how a hierarchical schematic containing two sub-circuits and a shared power rail is represented in each. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Process of Netlist Generation (7 marks):** The EDA tool executes the following deterministic pipeline: 1. **Schematic Parsing (1 mark):** The schematic database file (e.g., `.DSN` in OrCAD, `.SchDoc` in Altium) is loaded. Every placed symbol is recognized as an *instance* and assigned a unique **Reference Designator** (`R1`, `U3`, etc.). Component values are extracted from properties. 2. **Connectivity Graph Construction (2 marks):** The tool builds a graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ are pins/junctions and edges $E$ are wires. **Graph traversal algorithms** (typically Breadth-First Search or Union-Find) are applied to find all pins reachable from each starting pin. 3. **Equivalence Class Formation (1 mark):** All pins that are mutually reachable form a single **equivalence class** — this is a *net*. Each class is assigned a name: user-defined labels win, then power symbols (`VCC`, `GND`), then auto-generated IDs (`N0001`). 4. **Property Harvesting & Serialization (1 mark):** Component values and net properties are attached. The final structure is written to a file in the chosen **format** (SPICE, EDIF, etc.). **Three Mandatory Sections of any Netlist File (2 marks):** - **Header / Comment Block** — design metadata - **Component Instance List** — declarations of all parts - **Net Connectivity List** — list of all nets and the pins they join **(b) Flat vs. Hierarchical Netlist (7 marks):** | Parameter | Flat Netlist | Hierarchical Netlist | |---|---|---| | Structure | Single global list | Sub-circuits preserved as blocks | | Readability | Poor for large designs | Excellent, matches design intent | | Reusability | None — monolithic | Sub-circuits can be instanced repeatedly | | File size | Smaller for tiny designs | Can be larger due to hierarchy overhead | **Example — A design with a `+5V Power Supply` sub-circuit and a `Microcontroller` sub-circuit sharing `VCC_5V` and `GND`:** - In a **flat netlist**, both `VCC_5V` and `GND` are listed once at the top, with all pins from *both* sub-circuits listed together (e.g., `R1.2, U1.5, U2.10` all on the `VCC_5V` line). - In a **hierarchical netlist**, there is a top-level block that *references* sub-circuit `A` and sub-circuit `B`. The shared nets (`VCC_5V`, `GND`) are declared in the top-level scope and *imported* into the sub-circuits via **port declarations**. Each sub-circuit has its own internal nets that are invisible to the other. *(4 marks for differentiation table + 3 marks for the worked example = 7 marks.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **SPICE netlist format** in detail. Write the SPICE netlist for a circuit consisting of a **5 V DC source** connected through a **1 kΩ resistor** to a node, where the node also connects to a **1 µF capacitor** to ground and a **10 kΩ load resistor** to ground. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What is the role of the netlist in the **PCB design flow**? Explain what is meant by **forward annotation** and **back annotation** in this context. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) SPICE Netlist Format and Generation (7 marks):** **Format Conventions (3 marks):** - One element per line. - First character determines element type: `R` = Resistor, `C` = Capacitor, `L` = Inductor, `V` = Voltage Source, `I` = Current Source, `D` = Diode, `Q` = BJT, `M` = MOSFET. - Syntax: ` ` for two-terminal elements. - Comments begin with `*`; simulation directives begin with `.` (e.g., `.TRAN`, `.DC`, `.AC`, `.END`). - Ground is conventionally node `0`. **Circuit Description (1 mark):** - `V1` — 5 V DC source between `IN` and `GND` - `R1` — 1 kΩ from `IN` to `N1` - `C1` — 1 µF from `N1` to `GND` - `R2` — 10 kΩ from `N1` to `GND` (load) **SPICE Netlist (3 marks):** ```spice * KTU Example - RC Network with Load V1 IN GND DC 5V R1 IN N1 1k C1 N1 GND 1uF R2 N1 GND 10k .END ``` **Verification (model answer bonus):** Net `N1` has 3 pins attached (R1.pin2, C1.pin1, R2.pin1). Net `GND` has 4 pins (V1.pin2, C1.pin2, R2.pin2, plus implicit ground reference). Net `IN` has 2 pins (V1.pin1, R1.pin1). Sum = 3 + 4 + 2 = 9 = total pins of 4 two-terminal components. ✓ --- **(b) Role of Netlist in PCB Design Flow (7 marks):** **Role of the Netlist (3 marks):** The netlist is the **connectivity contract** between schematic capture and PCB layout. The PCB layout tool reads the netlist to: 1. Identify which components to place and in what quantity. 2. Know which pins must be joined by copper traces. 3. Allow the layout engineer to view the **rat's nest** (a visualization of all unrouted connections as straight lines). **Forward Annotation (2 marks):** The process of pushing design data **from schematic to PCB layout** (e.g., schematic edits like adding a new resistor flow into the PCB tool so a new footprint appears and unrouted connections are added). It ensures the PCB reflects the current schematic. **Back Annotation (2 marks):** The reverse process — pushing design data **from PCB layout back to schematic** (e.g., when components are swapped to equivalents from a different library, or when reference designators are reassigned to optimize placement/routing, the schematic is updated to match). It keeps both views synchronized. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing netlist with schematic:** Many students describe the schematic's graphical appearance instead of the netlist's *textual connectivity structure*. The netlist has **no** coordinates, **no** symbols, **no** wires drawn — it is a *list*. > 2. **Wrong SPICE syntax order:** A common mistake is writing `R1 1k IN N1` (value before nodes). The correct order is **` `**. One mark deduction per error. > 3. **Forgetting the ground node convention:** SPICE requires a node `0` (or named `GND` with `0` aliased) for the simulator to compute voltages. Students who leave the source floating lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Mixing up forward and back annotation:** Forward = schematic → PCB; Back = PCB → schematic. Reversing these loses full marks on the sub-part. > 5. **Not labelling nets:** In a SPICE netlist, you can use any node name, but the *names must be consistent across all elements that share the net*. Misspelling a node name (`GND` vs `Gnd` vs `gnd`) creates an unintended separate net — a **classic short-circuit simulation error**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Net** = electrically continuous path joining 2+ pins; identified by **graph connectivity analysis** in the EDA tool. - **Netlist** = textual, **technology-independent**, connectivity-only representation of a circuit — *not* a graphical drawing. - Netlists are the **mandatory bridge** between schematic capture and PCB layout, simulation, and DRC. - The three **mandatory sections** of any netlist: *Header*, *Component Instance List*, *Net Connectivity List*. - **Net naming priority:** user label → power symbol → auto-generated ID. - **Floating net** (one pin only) and **short net** (multiple user labels in one class) are the two most common ERC errors — both must equal **0** in a clean design. - **SPICE netlist syntax:** ` `; element type is determined by the *first letter* of the name (`R`, `C`, `L`, `V`, `I`, `D`, `Q`, `M`). - **SPICE simulation directives** begin with `.` — e.g., `.DC`, `.TRAN`, `.AC`, `.END`. - Ground is conventionally node **`0`**; multi-character names like `GND` are aliases of `0`. - **Flat netlist** = single global list; **Hierarchical netlist** = preserves sub-circuit blocks with port declarations. - **Forward annotation** = schematic → PCB; **Back annotation** = PCB → schematic. - **EDIF** is the universal, tool-agnostic interchange format; SPICE is the simulation standard; Protel/Altium format is PCB-flow specific. - For any 14-mark KTU question on netlist, expect a sub-part asking you to **write a SPICE netlist from a given schematic** — practice this conversion until it is automatic. - Always **verify** your generated netlist by checking $\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k = P$ (sum of pin-counts-per-net equals total pin count). - Power nets (`VCC`, `GND`) are typically handled via **special global symbols** and are automatically recognized by the ERC. - A netlist with **no** floating nets, **no** shorts, and **consistent** power-rail naming is considered *connectivity-clean* and ready for downstream PCB layout.

topics.title: PCB Layout Designing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PCB Layout Designing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PCB Layout Designing** is the systematic process of translating an electronic schematic (logical netlist) into a physical, manufacturable geometric representation of conductor paths, component footprints, vias, and copper planes on a printed circuit board substrate, while strictly adhering to electrical, mechanical, thermal, electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), and Design for Manufacturing (DFM) constraints as specified by industry standards such as **IPC-2221**, **IPC-2222**, and **IPC-2152**.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, PCB layout is not merely a drafting exercise; it is a **multiphysics engineering discipline** that simultaneously optimizes:
- **Signal Integrity (SI)** – maintaining waveform fidelity
- **Power Integrity (PI)** – stable voltage distribution
- **Thermal Management** – heat dissipation
- **Manufacturability (DFM)** – yield and cost efficiency
- **Testability (DFT)** – in-circuit test access

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** PCB layout is the bridge between *electronic design* (logical intent) and *electronic manufacturing* (physical reality). A perfect schematic can produce a non-functional board if layout rules are violated.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: PCB Layout = City Road Network Planning**

Imagine designing a city:
- The **schematic** is the list of buildings and who needs to be connected to whom (logic).
- The **PCB layout** is the actual placement of buildings (components) and the roads (traces) that connect them.

| City Planning | PCB Layout Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Highways (wide roads) | Power traces (high current paths) |
| Narrow lanes | Signal traces (low current) |
| Roundabouts | Vias (layer transitions) |
| Underground sewage | Ground planes (return paths) |
| Zoning laws | Design Rule Check (DRC) constraints |
| Traffic signals | Termination resistors (impedance matching) |

A city planner must ensure no two roads cross unnecessarily, roads are wide enough for the traffic, and emergency vehicles can reach everywhere. Similarly, a PCB layout engineer must ensure **no short circuits, traces are wide enough for current, and high-speed signals have clean return paths**.

## 1.3 PCB Layer Stackup – The Vertical Cityscape

A modern PCB is a **multi-storey building** of dielectric and copper layers:

1. **Top Layer (Signal/Component)** – Surface-mount components, fine signal traces
2. **Inner Layer 1 (Ground Plane)** – Continuous copper for return current
3. **Inner Layer 2 (Power Plane)** – Power distribution
4. **Inner Layer 3 (Signal)** – Routing dense signals
5. **Bottom Layer (Signal/Component)** – Bottom-side components, signal routing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 4-Layer Stackup (1 oz copper, FR-4, ε_r ≈ 4.3):**
> - L1: Signal — 0.2 mm prepreg — L2: GND — 1.2 mm core — L3: PWR — 0.2 mm prepreg — L4: Signal
> - This is the **industry default** for digital designs with frequencies up to ~100 MHz.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value / Constant | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Copper resistivity (ρ) | **1.724 × 10⁻⁸** | Ω·m |
| Copper conductivity (σ) | **5.8 × 10⁷** | S/m |
| FR-4 dielectric constant (ε_r) | **4.2 – 4.5** (typical 4.3) | dimensionless |
| Standard copper weight | **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm** thickness | µm |
| IPC-2152 internal conductor k | **0.024** (for ΔT = 10°C) | A·mm⁻¹·°C⁻¹ |
| Skin depth (δ) at 100 MHz in Cu | **~6.6 µm** | µm |
| Speed of signal in FR-4 | **~1.5 × 10⁸** m/s | m/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PCB Design Flow (Industry Standard Sequence)

The complete KTU/industry design flow follows a strict linear sequence:

1. **Schematic Capture** → Logical netlist generation
2. **Component Selection & Footprint Creation** → Mechanical land patterns per IPC-7351
3. **Component Placement (Floorplanning)** → Logical grouping, decoupling placement
4. **Routing (Manual + Auto-router with constraints)** → Trace, via, plane definition
5. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** → Verification against manufacturing rules
6. **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** → Net connectivity verification
7. **Signal Integrity / Power Integrity Simulation** → Pre-fabrication validation
8. **Gerber File / ODB++ / IPC-D-356 Generation** → Manufacturing output
9. **DFM Check** → Fabrication-friendly verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule of PCB Layout:** ~80% of layout time is spent on **placement and stackup decisions**, while only ~20% goes to actual routing. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptual understanding.

## 2.2 Trace Width Calculation (IPC-2152 Modified)

The fundamental equation governing how wide a copper trace must be to safely carry a given current without exceeding a temperature rise limit:

$$I = k \cdot (\Delta T)^{b} \cdot A^{c}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Current carrying capacity (Amperes)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise above ambient (°C)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of trace (mils² or mm²)
- $k, b, c$ = Empirical constants from IPC-2152

**Standard IPC-2152 Constants:**

| Conductor Type | k | b | c |
|---|---|---|---|
| External (outer layer) | 0.0480 | 0.44 | 0.725 |
| Internal (inner layer) | 0.0240 | 0.44 | 0.725 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Internal traces carry **~half the current** of external traces of the same cross-section because internal layers are thermally insulated by surrounding dielectric, preventing efficient heat dissipation.

## 2.3 Trace Resistance (DC)

For a rectangular copper trace of length $L$, width $W$, and thickness $T$:

$$R_{DC} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{W \cdot T}$$

For a 1 oz copper ($T = 35\ \mu m$), the **sheet resistance** is:

$$R_{sheet} = \frac{\rho}{T} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8}}{35 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.00049\ \Omega/\text{sq} \approx 0.5\ \text{m}\Omega/\text{sq}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sheet Resistance Rule:** A trace of width $W$ and length $L$ has resistance $R = R_{sheet} \times (L/W)$, independent of thickness (as long as thickness is constant). This is a KTU favorite shortcut.

## 2.4 AC Behavior — Skin Effect

At high frequencies, current crowds toward the conductor surface. The skin depth $\delta$ is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

For copper ($\mu_r = 1$):

$$\delta = \frac{66.1\ \text{mm}}{\sqrt{f\ (\text{Hz})}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **At 100 MHz, δ ≈ 6.6 µm.** When $T < 2\delta$, current uses the full cross-section. For 1 oz copper (35 µm) above ~200 MHz, skin effect becomes significant, and effective cross-section is reduced to perimeter × δ.

## 2.5 Microstrip Impedance (Controlled Impedance Routing)

For a surface microstrip on FR-4 with trace width $W$, thickness $T$, and dielectric height $H$ above ground plane (for $0.1 < W/H < 3.0, 1 < \varepsilon_r < 15$):

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right) \quad \Omega$$

Effective dielectric constant:

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 H/W}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** USB, HDMI, DDR, and Ethernet all require **50 Ω single-ended** or **90/100 Ω differential** controlled impedance. Trace geometry must be calculated to match these impedances to prevent reflections.

## 2.6 Via Current Capacity

For a plated through-hole via of finished hole diameter $d$ (mils) and plating thickness $t$ (mils), the approximate current capacity is:

$$I_{via} = k_{via} \cdot d^{0.65} \cdot \Delta T^{0.35}$$

A common engineering heuristic: **1 A per 10 mils of via diameter** for 10°C rise (e.g., a 20-mil via → ~2 A capacity).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| IPC-2152 Current | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | Trace width design |
| DC Trace Resistance | $R = \rho L / (WT)$ | Voltage drop, power loss |
| Sheet Resistance | $R_\square = \rho / T$ | Quick trace R estimation |
| Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/(\pi f \mu_0)}$ | HF current distribution |
| Microstrip Z₀ | $Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \ln\left(\frac{5.98H}{0.8W+T}\right)$ | Controlled impedance |
| Via Current (approx) | $I = 0.1 \cdot d$ (d in mils) | Power via sizing |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{pd} = 3.33 \cdot \sqrt{0.475 \varepsilon_r + 0.67}$ | ns/ft (FR-4 ≈ 1.5 ns/ft) |
| Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{trace}$ | Power integrity |
| Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{JA} = 1 / (h \cdot A)$ | Heat dissipation |
| Return Path Inductance | $L_{loop} \approx 10\ \text{nH/inch}$ | EMI/EMC critical |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** Students often write the IPC-2152 formula using wrong exponents. The exponent of $A$ is **0.725**, NOT 0.75. Examiners specifically check this.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCB layout directly impacts:
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone SoCs use 10+ layer HDI boards with laser-drilled micro-vias
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Engine ECUs must survive 150°C ambient — heavy copper (2-4 oz) needed
- **Aerospace:** Flight control PCBs use **flex-rigid** designs with 6-8 mil trace/space
- **Medical Implants:** Hermetic PCBs with biocompatible substrates
- **5G mmWave:** Substrate Integrated Waveguide (SIW) structures on PCB

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Trace Width for Power Rail (14-mark standard)

**Question:** A 5V power rail must deliver 3A to a load. The trace is on the **outer layer** of a 1 oz copper PCB (35 µm thick), FR-4 substrate. Maximum allowable temperature rise is 20°C above ambient. Calculate the required minimum trace width.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify IPC-2152 constants for **external conductor**:
- $k = 0.0480$
- $b = 0.44$
- $c = 0.725$

**Step 2:** Express cross-sectional area $A$ in terms of width $W$ (in mils):
$$A = W \times T_{mil}$$
where $T_{mil} = 35\ \mu m = 1.378\ \text{mils}$ (since 1 mil = 25.4 µm)

**Step 3:** Substitute into IPC-2152 equation:
$$I = 0.0480 \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in known values: $I = 3$ A, $\Delta T = 20$°C:
$$3 = 0.0480 \cdot (20)^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Delta T^{0.44}$:
$$(20)^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln(20)} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.996} = e^{1.318} \approx 3.736$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:
$$3 = 0.0480 \cdot 3.736 \cdot A^{0.725}$$
$$3 = 0.1793 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 7:** Isolate $A^{0.725}$:
$$A^{0.725} = \frac{3}{0.1793} \approx 16.73\ \text{mil}^2$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $A$:
$$A = (16.73)^{1/0.725} = (16.73)^{1.379}$$

Computing the natural log:
$$\ln(16.73) = 2.817$$
$$1.379 \times 2.817 = 3.884$$
$$A = e^{3.884} \approx 48.5\ \text{mil}^2$$

**Step 9:** Solve for width $W$:
$$W = \frac{A}{T_{mil}} = \frac{48.5}{1.378} \approx 35.2\ \text{mils}$$

**Step 10:** Add manufacturing safety margin (typical +25%):
$$W_{design} = 35.2 \times 1.25 \approx 44\ \text{mils} \approx 1.12\ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Check:** 1 mil = 0.0254 mm, so 44 mils × 0.0254 = 1.1176 mm ≈ 1.12 mm ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Minimum trace width = **~45 mils (1.15 mm)** for 3A on external 1 oz copper with 20°C rise.

## 3.2 Problem 2: Internal vs External Trace Comparison

**Question:** Repeat the calculation for an **internal** layer with same current and temperature rise. Why is the result different?

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Use internal layer constant $k = 0.0240$ (half of external).

**Step 2:** Same equation structure:
$$3 = 0.0240 \cdot (20)^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$
$$3 = 0.0240 \cdot 3.736 \cdot A^{0.725}$$
$$3 = 0.0897 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 3:** Solve:
$$A^{0.725} = \frac{3}{0.0897} \approx 33.45\ \text{mil}^2$$
$$A = (33.45)^{1.379} = e^{1.379 \times 3.510} = e^{4.840} \approx 126.5\ \text{mil}^2$$
$$W_{internal} = \frac{126.5}{1.378} \approx 91.8\ \text{mils} \approx 2.33\ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Internal trace must be **~2.6× wider** than external for same current capacity. This is why **power planes** (entire layer) are preferred for high-current rails on internal layers.

## 3.3 Problem 3: Microstrip Impedance Calculation

**Question:** A 4-layer FR-4 PCB ($\varepsilon_r = 4.3$) has a microstrip on the top layer with $H = 8\ \text{mil}$ (dielectric to ground plane), $W = 12\ \text{mil}$, $T = 1.4\ \text{mil}$. Calculate the characteristic impedance.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate effective dielectric constant:
$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.3 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.3 - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 \cdot 8/12}}$$
$$= 2.65 + 1.65 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 8}}$$
$$= 2.65 + 1.65 \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$
$$= 2.65 + 0.55 = 3.20$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $Z_0$:
$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{4.3 + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98 \cdot 8}{0.8 \cdot 12 + 1.4}\right)$$
$$= \frac{87}{\sqrt{5.71}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{47.84}{9.6 + 1.4}\right)$$
$$= \frac{87}{2.39} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{47.84}{11.0}\right)$$
$$= 36.4 \cdot \ln(4.349)$$
$$= 36.4 \cdot 1.470$$
$$= 53.5\ \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is close to the **50 Ω** standard required for USB, RF signals, and most high-speed digital interfaces. KTU board exams often test this calculation.

## 3.4 Problem 4: Voltage Drop on Long Trace

**Question:** A 50 mm long, 0.5 mm wide, 1 oz copper trace carries 1A. Calculate the voltage drop.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert dimensions:
- $L = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$
- $W = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $T = 35\ \mu m = 35 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$

**Step 2:** Calculate cross-section:
$$A = W \times T = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 35 \times 10^{-6} = 17.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply $R = \rho L / A$:
$$R = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05}{17.5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{8.62 \times 10^{-10}}{17.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0493\ \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Voltage drop:
$$V_{drop} = I \times R = 1 \times 0.0493 = 49.3\ \text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a 3.3V rail, 49.3 mV drop is **1.5%** — generally acceptable. For 1.2V core voltage, this same drop is **4.1%** — borderline, requiring wider trace or copper pour.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Effect on Cost | Effect on SI | Effect on PI | Effect on Thermal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase trace width | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Better | ↑ Better (lower R) | ↑ Better (heat spread) |
| More layers (4→6) | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑↑ Better routing | ↑ Better planes | ↑ Better heat spread |
| Thicker copper (1→2 oz) | ↑ Moderately | Neutral | ↑↑ Better | ↑↑ Better |
| Smaller via diameter | Neutral | ↑ Better for SI | Neutral | ↓ Worse |
| Buried/blind vias | ↑↑ Significantly | ↑↑ Better | ↑ Better | Neutral |
| Use ground plane | Neutral | ↑↑ Critical | ↑ Critical | ↑ Better |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete PCB Design Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Schematic Capture] --> B[Netlist Generation]
    B --> C[Component Library Selection]
    C --> D[Footprint Mapping IPC-7351]
    D --> E[Board Outline Definition]
    E --> F[Layer Stackup Planning]
    F --> G[Component Placement Floorplanning]
    G --> H[Power Plane Pouring]
    H --> I[Decoupling Capacitor Placement]
    I --> J[Signal Routing]
    J --> K[Via Optimization]
    K --> L[DRC Design Rule Check]
    L --> M{Pass?}
    M -- No --> G
    M -- Yes --> N[ERC Electrical Rule Check]
    N --> O[Signal Integrity Simulation]
    O --> P{Pass?}
    P -- No --> J
    P -- Yes --> Q[Gerber and Drill File Generation]
    Q --> R[DFM Check by Fabricator]
    R --> S[Manufacturing Output]
```

## 4.2 PCB Layer Stackup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[L1 - Top Signal Layer]
        L1a[Components and High Speed Traces]
    end
    subgraph Prepreg1[Prepreg - 0.2 mm - ER 4.3]
        D1[Dielectric Spacer]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[L2 - Ground Plane]
        L2a[Continuous GND Pour with Stitching Vias]
    end
    subgraph Core1[Core - 1.2 mm - FR4]
        C1[Mechanical Substrate]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[L3 - Power Plane]
        L3a[PWR Rails as Copper Islands]
    end
    subgraph Prepreg2[Prepreg - 0.2 mm]
        D2[Dielectric Spacer]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[L4 - Bottom Signal Layer]
        L4a[Bottom Components and Slow Signals]
    end
    Layer1 --> Prepreg1
    Prepreg1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Core1
    Core1 --> Layer3
    Layer3 --> Prepreg2
    Prepreg2 --> Layer4
```

## 4.3 Component Placement Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Placement] --> Q1{High Frequency > 50 MHz?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Place near connector, short traces, isolated GND]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Standard placement]
    A2 --> Q2{Analog and Digital Mixed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A3[Separate AGND and DGND, single-point connect]
    Q2 -- No --> A4[Unified GND]
    A3 --> Q3[Place Decoupling Caps]
    A4 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4{Crystals or Oscillators?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A5[Place close to IC, guard ring with GND]
    Q4 -- No --> A6[Continue routing]
    A1 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> End[Proceed to Routing]
```

## 4.4 Trace Routing Strategies (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Strategy1[Strategy 1 - 45 Degree Routing]
        S1A[Trace goes horizontal]
        S1B[Then 45 degree turn]
        S1C[Then vertical]
    end
    subgraph Strategy2[Strategy 2 - Curved Routing]
        S2A[Used for high speed differential pairs]
        S2B[Maintains constant impedance]
    end
    subgraph Strategy3[Strategy 3 - Differential Pair]
        S3A[Two traces parallel]
        S3B[Constant spacing W]
        S3C[Matched length plus minus 5 mil]
    end
    subgraph Strategy4[Strategy 4 - Serpentine]
        S4A[Used for length matching]
        S4B[Maintains timing skew under 50 ps]
    end
    Strategy1 --> Compare{Application}
    Strategy2 --> Compare
    Strategy3 --> Compare
    Strategy4 --> Compare
    Compare --> Output[Choose based on SI requirements]
```

## 4.5 Via Types — Visual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Via Types] --> B[Through Hole Via]
    A --> C[Buried Via]
    A --> D[Blind Via]
    A --> E[Micro Via]
    B --> B1[All Layers Drilled - Lowest Cost]
    C --> C1[Internal Only - Higher Cost]
    D --> D1[Outer to Inner Only]
    E --> E1[Laser Drilled - Smallest - HDI]
    E1 --> E2[Typical Diameter 4 to 6 mil]
```

```mermaid

    GND[GND Strategy Selection] --> A{Single or Mixed Signal?}
    A -- Single --> B[Single Unified Ground Plane]

    B --> D[Best SI - Preferred]
    C --> E[AGND]
    C --> F[DGND]
    C --> G[PGND]

    G --> H
    H --> I[Join at PCB Entry Point]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the term "Design Rule Check (DRC)" in PCB layout. List any four critical DRC parameters checked during PCB design.**

**Model Answer:**

Design Rule Check (DRC) is an automated verification process in PCB design software (e.g., KiCad, Altium, OrCAD) that checks the physical layout against a set of manufacturing and electrical constraints to ensure the design is producible and functional.

**Four critical DRC parameters:**

1. **Trace clearance** — Minimum spacing between adjacent copper features (e.g., 6 mils for standard fab, 4 mils for advanced).
2. **Trace width** — Minimum width of signal/power traces (must not fall below fab limit, typically 5-6 mils).
3. **Drill-to-copper spacing** — Minimum distance from via pad edge to nearest trace.
4. **Annular ring** — Minimum copper ring around drilled via (typically ≥ 4 mils).
5. **Edge clearance** — Distance from any copper feature to board outline (typically ≥ 0.2 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks distribution:** Definition (1M) + 4 parameters (0.5M each = 2M).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is a "via" in PCB design? Differentiate between through-hole via and blind via.**

**Model Answer:**

A **via** is a plated through-hole that electrically connects two or more copper layers of a PCB, allowing signals and power to transition between layers.

| Feature | Through-Hole Via | Blind Via |
|---|---|---|
| Layers connected | All layers (top to bottom) | Outer layer to one or more inner layers only |
| Drilling | Mechanical drill, after lamination | Controlled-depth drill or laser |
| Cost | **Lowest** | **Higher** (requires sequential lamination) |
| Typical use | General signal/power | High-density interconnect (HDI) escape routing |
| Stub effect | Yes (creates reflection at high freq) | Minimal (controlled depth) |

**Marks distribution:** Definition (1M) + Comparison table (2M).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the IPC-2152 standard for current carrying capacity of PCB traces. Derive the required trace width for a power rail with the following specifications: **Current = 2.5 A, External trace, 1 oz copper (35 µm thick), Maximum temperature rise ΔT = 15°C, Manufacturing safety margin = 25%.** (7 Marks)

**(b)** Define **characteristic impedance of a microstrip line**. A 4-layer FR-4 PCB (ε_r = 4.4) has a microstrip on the top layer with dielectric height H = 10 mil, trace width W = 14 mil, and copper thickness T = 1.4 mil. Calculate the characteristic impedance. Why is controlled impedance critical for high-speed digital signals? (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (a):

**Step 1: IPC-2152 Standard Explanation**
The IPC-2152 (formerly IPC-D-275) is the industry standard that provides empirical formulas and charts for determining the current carrying capacity of PCB traces based on cross-sectional area, temperature rise, and conductor location (internal vs external).

The governing equation is:
$$I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

For **external** conductors: $k = 0.0480$
For **internal** conductors: $k = 0.0240$

**Step 2: Convert thickness to mils**
$$T = 35\ \mu m \div 25.4\ \mu m/\text{mil} = 1.378\ \text{mils}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**
$$2.5 = 0.0480 \cdot (15)^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 4: Compute $15^{0.44}$**
$$15^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln 15} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.708} = e^{1.192} = 3.293$$

**Step 5: Substitute**
$$2.5 = 0.0480 \cdot 3.293 \cdot A^{0.725} = 0.1581 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 6: Isolate A**
$$A^{0.725} = \frac{2.5}{0.1581} = 15.81\ \text{mil}^2$$
$$A = 15.81^{1/0.725} = 15.81^{1.379}$$

$$\ln(15.81) = 2.760$$
$$1.379 \times 2.760 = 3.806$$
$$A = e^{3.806} = 44.96\ \text{mil}^2$$

**Step 7: Compute W**
$$W = \frac{A}{T} = \frac{44.96}{1.378} = 32.6\ \text{mils}$$

**Step 8: Add 25% safety margin**
$$W_{design} = 32.6 \times 1.25 = 40.8\ \text{mils} \approx 41\ \text{mils}$$

$$\boxed{W_{design} \approx 41\ \text{mils} \approx 1.04\ \text{mm}}$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (b):

**Definition:** Characteristic impedance ($Z_0$) of a microstrip line is the ratio of voltage to current of a propagating electromagnetic wave along the transmission line, determined by the trace geometry and dielectric properties. For a surface microstrip on FR-4:

$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98 H}{0.8 W + T}\right) \quad \Omega$$

**Step 1: Calculate effective dielectric constant**
$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.4 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.4 - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12 \cdot 10/14}}$$
$$= 2.70 + 1.70 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 8.571}}$$
$$= 2.70 + 1.70 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{9.571}}$$
$$= 2.70 + 1.70 \cdot \frac{1}{3.094}$$
$$= 2.70 + 0.549 = 3.249$$

**Step 2: Apply $Z_0$ formula**
$$Z_0 = \frac{87}{\sqrt{4.4 + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{5.98 \times 10}{0.8 \times 14 + 1.4}\right)$$
$$= \frac{87}{\sqrt{5.81}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{59.8}{12.6}\right)$$
$$= \frac{87}{2.410} \cdot \ln(4.746)$$
$$= 36.10 \cdot 1.557$$
$$= 56.2\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{Z_0 \approx 56.2\ \Omega \approx 56\ \Omega}$$

**Why controlled impedance is critical for high-speed digital signals:**

1. **Reflection prevention:** When signal rise time is shorter than propagation delay across trace, the trace behaves as a transmission line. Mismatched impedance causes reflections (echoes) that corrupt signal integrity.

2. **Timing margins:** Reflections cause overshoot, undershoot, ringing, and jitter — reducing timing margins in synchronous digital systems (DDR, PCIe, etc.).

3. **EMI reduction:** Impedance discontinuities radiate electromagnetic energy, causing EMI failures.

4. **Eye diagram closure:** In high-speed serial links, reflections close the data eye, increasing bit error rate (BER).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **complete PCB design flow** from schematic capture to Gerber generation. List the design outputs and verify the role of DFM (Design for Manufacturing) at each stage. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Define **thermal management in PCB layout**. A SOT-23 MOSFET dissipates 1.2 W on a 1 oz copper PCB. The component thermal pad is 2 mm × 2 mm connected to a copper pour of 10 mm × 10 mm. Calculate the approximate junction-to-ambient thermal resistance improvement by enlarging the pour to 20 mm × 20 mm. State two layout techniques to improve PCB heat dissipation. (7 Marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (a):

The PCB design flow consists of **nine sequential stages**, each with specific inputs and outputs. The block diagram in **Section 4.1** (above) illustrates the complete flow.

**Stage 1: Schematic Capture**
- Input: Design requirements, datasheets
- Output: Schematic diagram, logical netlist
- Tools: KiCad Eeschema, Altium Schematic, OrCAD Capture

**Stage 2: Component Library Selection**
- Input: Datasheet mechanical drawings
- Output: Footprint files following **IPC-7351** standard
- Verification: Land pattern vs. IPC-7351 nominal, least, most tolerances

**Stage 3: Board Outline Definition**
- Input: Mechanical constraints (enclosure dimensions)
- Output: PCB edge profile, mounting holes, fiducials

**Stage 4: Layer Stackup Planning**
- Input: SI/PI/Thermal requirements, fab house capabilities
- Output: Stackup table (layers, dielectric thickness, copper weight)

**Stage 5: Component Placement (Floorplanning)**
- Input: Netlist, schematic functional blocks
- Output: XY coordinates of each component
- Rules: Decoupling caps within 100 mils of IC power pins; crystals close to ICs; no analog-digital crossover.

**Stage 6: Power Plane Pouring**
- Input: Power rail netlist
- Output: Copper pours for VCC, GND with proper isolation
- DFM check: ≥ 0.2 mm clearance from board edge; adequate via stitching (one via per λ/20 of highest frequency).

**Stage 7: Signal Routing**
- Input: Netlist, SI constraints
- Output: Routed traces with length-matching, impedance control
- DFM check: No acute angles (< 90°); 45° bends preferred; width matching per current.

**Stage 8: Design Rule Check (DRC) and Electrical Rule Check (ERC)**
- Automated verification
- Outputs: DRC report, error markers

**Stage 9: Gerber / ODB++ / IPC-D-356 Output**
- Standardized manufacturing files
- RS-274X Gerber, Excellon drill, BOM, pick-and-place

**Role of DFM:**
DFM must be applied at **every stage**, not just the end. Post-layout DFM is too late — many issues (incompatible stackup, unplated slots, insufficient clearances) cannot be fixed without re-spin.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR (b):

**Definition:** Thermal management in PCB layout is the practice of designing copper geometry, vias, and component placement to conduct heat from active devices to ambient (or to a heatsink), keeping junction temperatures within safe operating limits (typically $T_j < 125°C$ for silicon).

**Thermal Resistance Approximation:**

For a copper pour acting as a heatsink, the thermal resistance is approximated as:
$$\theta_{JA} \approx \frac{1}{h \cdot A_{eff}}$$

Where $h$ is the combined convection + radiation coefficient (~10 W/m²·K for natural convection) and $A_{eff}$ is the effective copper area.

**For 10 mm × 10 mm pour (100 mm² = 10⁻⁴ m²):**
$$\theta_{10} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-4}} = 1000\ \text{K/W}$$

**For 20 mm × 20 mm pour (400 mm² = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m²):**
$$\theta_{20} = \frac{1}{10 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}} = 250\ \text{K/W}$$

**Improvement:**
$$\Delta\theta = 1000 - 250 = 750\ \text{K/W}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Thermal resistance reduced by } 75\%}$$

**Two layout techniques to improve PCB heat dissipation:**

1. **Thermal vias (via stitching):** Place an array of small vias (0.3 mm drill, 0.6 mm pad, 1 mm pitch) directly under the component thermal pad. These vias conduct heat from the top copper to internal copper planes (or bottom layer), effectively multiplying the heat-spreading area. Typical improvement: 30-50% reduction in $\theta_{JA}$.

2. **Copper pouring on both sides:** Connect the top thermal pad to a large bottom copper pour using thermal vias, creating a 3D heat spreader. Additional internal power planes further improve heat distribution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - DO NOT confuse **thermal vias** (small, for heat conduction) with **stitching vias** (small, for GND return path). Both are 0.3 mm, but their purpose is different.
> - DO NOT use **thermal relief spokes** on power pads connected to thermal vias — they block heat flow. Use **direct connection** for thermal pads.
> - In your answers, **always state both** the equation AND the substituted numerical values. KTU examiners deduct marks for "phantom numbers."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **PCB Design Flow:** Schematic → Footprint → Placement → Routing → DRC → Gerber (9 stages)
- [ ] **IPC-2152 Formula:** $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ with $k = 0.048$ (external) and $k = 0.024$ (internal)
- [ ] **Internal vs External:** Internal traces need **~2× the width** of external for the same current
- [ ] **Copper weight conversion:** 1 oz/ft² = **35 µm** = 1.378 mils
- [ ] **Sheet resistance:** 1 oz Cu = **~0.5 mΩ/sq**
- [ ] **Standard stackup:** 4-layer with SIG-GND-PWR-SIG and 1.2 mm core
- [ ] **Microstrip Z₀ formula** with $\varepsilon_r$, $H$, $W$, $T$ — must be memorized
- [ ] **Standard impedances:** 50 Ω single-ended, 90/100 Ω differential
- [ ] **Skin depth at 100 MHz in Cu ≈ 6.6 µm**
- [ ] **Via rule of thumb:** 1 A per 10 mils of via diameter
- [ ] **Decoupling cap placement:** Within 100 mils (2.54 mm) of IC power pin
- [ ] **Return path:** High-speed signals need a **continuous reference plane** below
- [ ] **Thermal vias:** 0.3 mm drill, 1 mm pitch, direct connection (not thermal relief)
- [ ] **Bend rules:** Use 45° bends, never 90°; 3× trace width for curve radius
- [ ] **DRC clearance minimums:** 6 mil trace/space standard, 4 mil advanced
- [ ] **Annular ring minimum:** 4 mils
- [ ] **Edge clearance:** ≥ 0.2 mm from board edge to copper
- [ ] **DFT pads:** Test points on every net, ≥ 1 mm pad, on one side if possible
- [ ] **Standards:** IPC-2221 (generic), IPC-2222 (rigid), IPC-7351 (footprints), IPC-2152 (current)
- [ ] **Units used in exam:** Always carry units throughout derivation; show conversions explicitly
- [ ] **Safety margin:** Add 20-25% to calculated trace width for manufacturing tolerance
- [ ] **For high-speed:** Use ground plane as return path; avoid splits in plane under high-speed traces
- [ ] **Crosstalk:** Maintain 3W rule (trace spacing ≥ 3× trace width) to minimize coupling
- [ ] **Length matching:** For parallel buses (DDR), match within ± 25 mils (or per spec)

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Marks Lost in KTU Exams:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert copper thickness to **mils** (1.378 mils for 1 oz)
> 2. Using wrong $k$ constant (0.024 vs 0.048) for internal vs external
> 3. Writing IPC formula as $\Delta T^{0.4}$ instead of $\Delta T^{0.44}$ (exponent matters!)
> 4. Not adding manufacturing safety margin to final width
> 5. Confusing characteristic impedance with resistance (impedance is frequency-dependent)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
