k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} = 0.048 \times 4.466 = 0.2144
$$

**Step 3 — Compute bracketed ratio** (1 mark):

$$
\dfrac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.2144} = 6.997
$$

**Step 4 — Raise to power 1/0.725 = 1.3793** (1.5 marks):

$$
\ln(6.997) = 1.945
$$

$$
\ln(A) = 1.3793 \times 1.945 = 2.683
$$

$$
A = e^{2.683} = 14.62\ \text{mil}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Convert copper thickness to mils** (1 mark):

$$
68.6\ \mu\text{m} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{mil}}{25.4\ \mu\text{m}} = 2.70\ \text{mil}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute width in mil and convert to mm** (1 mark):

$$
W = \dfrac{14.62\ \text{mil}^2}{2.70\ \text{mil}} = 5.41\ \text{mil}
$$

$$
W = 5.41 \times 0.0254 = 0.137\ \text{mm}
$$

**Designer's final choice:** Round up to **0.15 mm (6 mil)** to be conservative.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing the final width in **mil** when the question asks for **mm** costs you **1 mark** outright. Always convert and **box the final answer with units**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "per side" in undercut:** Many students write $W_m = W_f + W_u$ instead of $W_m = W_f + 2 W_u$. This **systematic error costs 1 mark** in every etch-factor problem.
> 3. **Skipping the IPC reference:** Just giving a number without citing "as per IPC-2221" / "IPC-2152" forfeits **0.5 mark** of the design-context mark.
> 4. **Confusing negative vs positive resist:** Saying "positive resist is used" loses **1 mark** outright — PCB industry uses **negative resist** almost universally.
> 5. **Omitting waste-stream discussion in PCB printing:** If the question is worth 7 marks and includes a process diagram, **at least 0.5–1 mark** is reserved for environmental/safety notes. Mention spent etchant neutralization and resist waste handling.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this and tape it to your notebook.**

- [ ] **DFM Definition:** Integrate manufacturing constraints *during* design, not after.
- [ ] **DFM Pillars:** Simplify, Tolerate, Standardize, Material-aware, Cost-aware.
- [ ] **DFM Key Numbers:** ≥ 6 mil trace/space, ≥ 0.2 mm annular ring, 2.5 mm edge keep-out, 5 mm fiducials.
- [ ] **PCB Printing = Photoengraving** = 6 steps: Clean → Laminate → Expose → Develop → Etch → Strip.
- [ ] **Resist Type Used:** **Negative photoresist** (crosslinks on UV exposure, remains to protect copper).
- [ ] **UV Wavelength:** 350–400 nm; **Dose:** ~300 mJ/cm².
- [ ] **Developer Chemistry:** 1% Na₂CO₃ at ~30 °C.
- [ ] **Common Etchants:** FeCl₃ (hobby), CuCl₂ (production), Ammoniacal (fine-line).
- [ ] **Stripper Chemistry:** 2–5% NaOH at ~50 °C.
- [ ] **Etch Factor Definition:** $\text{EF} = T / W_u$ — **anisotropy indicator**; EF ≥ 3 is industry-acceptable.
- [ ] **Undercut Compensation Formula:** $W_m = W_f + 2 W_u$ — critical for CAM artwork generation.
- [ ] **Etch Time Formula:** $t_e = T / R_e$ — sets conveyor dwell in production.
- [ ] **Trace Current Capacity (IPC-2152):** $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ with $k \approx 0.048$ for external traces.
- [ ] **Copper Thickness Conversion:** 1 oz/ft² ≡ 34.3 µm ≡ 1.35 mil. **Memorize this.**
- [ ] **Length Conversion:** 1 mil = 0.0254 mm. **Memorize this too.**
- [ ] **Process Waste Streams:** Spent etchant (FeCl₃ / CuCl₂), stripped resist (alkali) — both need treatment before disposal.
- [ ] **IPC Standards to Know by Name:** IPC-2221 (design), IPC-A-600 (acceptance), IPC-6012 (qualification), IPC-2152 (current), IPC-4101 (laminate), IPC-7351 (footprints).
- [ ] **Most-marked Question Pattern:** "Define DFM + list 4 guidelines" (3 marks) and "Explain PCB printing with diagram" (7 marks) appear **every** exam cycle — master these two.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Examiner Heuristic:** When in doubt, the KTU examiner is looking for **three things** — (1) a correct IPC reference, (2) a labelled process diagram, and (3) a numerically-solved example. If your answer contains all three, you are scoring **at least 80% on the relevant question**. Memorize the formulas, draw the diagrams, and cite the standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Etching — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Etching** is a **controlled material removal process** used in the fabrication of Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs) and semiconductor/microelectronic devices, in which unprotected (unmasked) regions of a conductive or dielectric layer are selectively dissolved or volatilized by chemical (wet) or physical/chemical (dry) means to transfer a predefined geometric pattern from a mask onto the underlying substrate.

In the context of the **OEEVT612 – Electronic Hardware for Engineers** course (Module 2), etching is studied as one of the **three foundational subtractive/additive manufacturing steps** of PCB prototyping, alongside *printing/photolithography* and *drilling/plating*. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to differentiate between **chemical etching (wet)** and **physical/dry etching (plasma-based)** methods, and to relate etch parameters to **pattern fidelity, line resolution, and conductor geometry**.

### 1.1.1 Engineering Parameters Governing Etching

The quality of any etching process is quantified using four universally accepted metrics:

| Parameter | Symbol | Units | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Etch Rate** | $R_e$ | $\mu m/min$ | Depth of material removed per unit time |
| **Selectivity** | $S$ | dimensionless | Ratio of target material etch rate to mask/substrate etch rate |
| **Anisotropy** | $A$ | dimensionless | Degree of directional preference (0 = isotropic, 1 = fully anisotropic) |
| **Undercut** | $U$ | $\mu m$ | Lateral dissolution beneath the mask edge |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stencil Carving" Mental Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy — Cookie Cutter vs. Chisel:**
>
> Imagine you have a flat slab of colored clay and want to carve a pattern on it.
>
> - **Wet (Chemical) Etching** is like *pouring acid onto the clay through a stencil* — the acid eats sideways as well as downward, producing *rounded, undercut edges*. This is **isotropic** etching.
> - **Dry (Plasma) Etching** is like *aiming a sandblaster straight down through the stencil* — particles only strike vertically, producing *sharp, vertical sidewalls*. This is **anisotropic** etching.
>
> For a hobby PCB, the acid method is fine (the rounded edges don't matter much). For a 7-nanometer CPU transistor, you MUST use the sandblaster (plasma), because a single atom of sideways etching would short-circuit the chip.

## 1.3 Where Etching Sits in the PCB Manufacturing Workflow

$$\boxed{\text{Substrate Preparation} \rightarrow \text{Lamination} \rightarrow \text{Printing/Photolithography} \rightarrow \text{\textbf{Etching}} \rightarrow \text{Stripping} \rightarrow \text{Drilling} \rightarrow \text{Plating/Solder Mask}}$$

Etching is the **pattern-transfer** step. Without it, all copper on the board would remain, and no circuit could exist.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Etching Techniques

Etching methods bifurcate into two principal families:

### 2.1.1 Wet Chemical Etching (Liquid-Phase)

A liquid etchant reacts chemically with the target material. The reaction products must be **soluble** in the etchant for continued reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three sub-mechanisms inside Wet Etching:**
>
> 1. **Pure Chemical Dissolution** — e.g., $\text{FeCl}_3$ reacting with Cu.
> 2. **Electrochemical (Galvanic) Etching** — e.g., $\text{CuCl}_2$ where Cu oxidizes and Cu ions reduce.
> 3. **Photo-assisted / Catalyzed Etching** — used in silicon micromachining.

**Common Wet Etchants for Copper (PCB):**

| Etchant | Chemical Reaction | Etch Rate ($\mu m/min$) | Operating Temp | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ferric Chloride ($\text{FeCl}_3$) | $\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2$ | 25–50 | 25–45 °C | Most popular for hobby PCB |
| Cupric Chloride ($\text{CuCl}_2$) | $\text{Cu} + \text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CuCl}$ | 15–40 | 35–55 °C | Regenerable, industry preferred |
| Ammonium Persulfate $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8$ | $\text{Cu} + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_4^+$ | 12–25 | 45–55 °C | Clear solution, easy to see etch end-point |
| Hydrochloric Acid + $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ | $\text{Cu} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 20–60 | Room temp | Fast, controllable |

### 2.1.2 Dry Etching (Plasma / Vapor-Phase)

Ionic or radical species generated in a low-pressure plasma bombard or react with the exposed surface. **No liquid chemicals** are involved.

**Three sub-categories:**

| Sub-category | Mechanism | Anisotropy | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plasma Etching (PE)** | Chemical reaction with radicals | Low (isotropic) | Stripping, bulk material removal |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | Combined chemical + ion bombardment | Moderate–High | Si, $\text{SiO}_2$, $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ in VLSI |
| **Ion Beam Milling / Sputter Etching** | Pure physical momentum transfer | Very High | Sub-micron features, exotic materials |
| **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE — Bosch Process)** | Alternating $\text{SF}_6$ etch / $\text{C}_4\text{F}_8$ passivation | Near-vertical | MEMS, through-silicon vias (TSV) |

## 2.2 Why 'Anisotropy' is the Most Important Property

The cross-sectional etch profile determines whether your circuit will work. Compare the two extremes:

- **Isotropic profile (Wet Etching):** The etchant undercuts the mask by an amount equal to the etch depth. For a track that must be **100 µm wide** with copper **35 µm thick**, the actual mask opening must be **100 + 2(35) = 170 µm**, otherwise the track vanishes.

- **Anisotropic profile (Dry Etching):** Sidewalls are vertical. Mask opening $\approx$ final track width.

$$\text{Anisotropy factor } A = 1 - \frac{U}{D}$$

where $U$ = undercut (lateral etch) and $D$ = etch depth.

- $A = 1$ → perfectly anisotropic (vertical walls, ideal for ICs)
- $A = 0$ → perfectly isotropic (rounded, equal undercut & depth)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorize These for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_e = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | Etch rate (depth removed per unit time) | $\mu m/min$ |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{\text{mask}}}$ | Selectivity of etchant for target over mask | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A = 1 - \dfrac{U}{D}$ | Anisotropy factor (0 isotropic → 1 anisotropic) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{final}} + 2U$ | Required mask opening compensating for undercut | $\mu m$ |
| 5 | $t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{Cu}}}{R_e}$ | Time required to etch through copper of thickness $h_{\text{Cu}}$ | minutes |
| 6 | $U = D \cdot (1 - A)$ | Lateral undercut from depth and anisotropy | $\mu m$ |
| 7 | $\text{MRR} = R_e \cdot A_{\text{etched}}$ | Mass/removal rate; $A_{\text{etched}}$ = exposed area | $\mu m \cdot cm^2 / min$ |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{\text{vert}}}{R_{\text{vert}} + R_{\text{lat}}}$ | Directional efficiency (RIE) | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB prototyping labs** (KTU project work, IEEE student branches): Ferric chloride wet etching for FR-4 copper-clad boards.
- **VLSI fabs** (Intel, TSMC, Samsung): Plasma/RIE etching for sub-7 nm transistor gates.
- **MEMS sensors** (accelerometers in smartphones): DRIE for high-aspect-ratio silicon structures.
- **Solar PV industry**: Texturing monocrystalline silicon using anisotropic wet KOH etch to reduce reflection.
- **Flexible electronics**: RIE for parylene/metal patterning on polyimide.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Required Mask Width Compensating for Undercut

### Problem Setup

You are fabricating a PCB track that must end up **W µm wide**. The copper cladding is **D µm thick**. The wet etchant is **isotropic**, meaning the etch front advances at equal speed horizontally ($R_{\text{lat}}$) and vertically ($R_{\text{vert}}$). Find the width of opening you must print on the photoresist mask.

### Step 1 — Define the etch front geometry

A wet isotropic etch removes material in a hemispherical pattern centered at every exposed edge. After time $t$:

$$\text{Vertical depth etched} = D = R_{\text{vert}} \cdot t$$
$$\text{Lateral undercut} = U = R_{\text{lat}} \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Apply the isotropy condition

For a purely isotropic wet etch:
$$R_{\text{vert}} = R_{\text{lat}} = R_e$$

Therefore:
$$U = R_e \cdot t = \frac{R_e}{R_{\text{vert}}} \cdot D = D$$

Substituting $U = D$ into the anisotropy relation $A = 1 - U/D$:
$$A = 1 - \frac{D}{D} = 0$$

This confirms wet etching is perfectly isotropic at the **macroscale limit**.

### Step 3 — Derive the mask correction equation

The photoresist window must be wider than the final track width by an amount equal to the undercut on **each** side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{mask}} &= W_{\text{final}} + U_{\text{left}} + U_{\text{right}} \\
&= W_{\text{final}} + 2U \\
&= W_{\text{final}} + 2R_e \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate time using etch depth

Since $D = R_e \cdot t \Rightarrow t = D / R_e$:

$$
\boxed{\,W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{final}} + 2D\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Q:** A 35 µm thick copper layer must be etched isotropically to produce a final track width of 200 µm. What should be the photoresist opening width?
>
> **Solution:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> W_{\text{mask}} &= W_{\text{final}} + 2D \\
> &= 200 + 2(35) \\
> &= 200 + 70 \\
> &= 270 \; \mu m
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> **Answer: 270 µm**

## 3.2 Derivation — Etch Time Calculation for Constant Etch Rate

### Setup
Given copper thickness $h_{\text{Cu}}$ and an experimentally measured etch rate $R_e$, the minimum etch time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Etch depth at time } t &= R_e \cdot t \\
\text{For complete removal: } R_e \cdot t_{\min} &= h_{\text{Cu}} \\
\therefore \; t_{\min} &= \frac{h_{\text{Cu}}}{R_e}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Practical factor — 30% over-etch

Because copper thickness varies (±10%), etchant concentration depletes, and bubbles adhere to the surface, real fabs use an **over-etch factor** of ~30%:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{actual}} = 1.3 \cdot \frac{h_{\text{Cu}}}{R_e}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Wet vs. Dry Etching Decision Table

| Selection Criterion | Prefer Wet Etching | Prefer Dry Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum feature size | > 100 µm | < 10 µm |
| Substrate tolerance | Flexible (plastic, ceramic) | Rigid (Si, GaAs, glass) |
| Capital cost | ₹5,000 – ₹50,000 (lab) | ₹50 lakh – ₹50 crore (fab) |
| Throughput | High (batch of 50 boards) | Moderate (wafer-by-wafer) |
| Chemical waste handling | Mandatory (heavy metals) | Minimal (vacuum exhaust) |
| Sidewall profile | Sloped, undercut | Vertical, sharp |
| Process control | Manual (visual) | Automated (optical emission) |
| PCB lab use | **YES — standard choice** | Rare; only for RF microwave boards |

## 3.4 Mass Removal Rate Derivation (For Spray Etchers)

Industrial PCB lines use **conveyorized spray etchers** where etchant impinges vertically on the copper. The mass removal rate per unit area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= \rho_{\text{Cu}} \cdot R_e \\
&= 8.96 \; g/cm^3 \times R_e \; cm/min \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For $R_e = 30 \, \mu m/min = 3 \times 10^{-3} \, cm/min$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= 8.96 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 26.88 \times 10^{-3} \; g/(cm^2 \cdot min) \\
&\approx 1.61 \; g/(cm^2 \cdot hr)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the value PCB manufacturers use to size their **etchant regeneration systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Etching Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ETCHING PROCESS]:::root --> B[WET CHEMICAL ETCHING]:::wet
    A --> C[DRY PLASMA ETCHING]:::dry

    B --> B1[Ferric Chloride FeCl3]:::sub
    B --> B2[Cupric Chloride CuCl2]:::sub
    B --> B3[Ammonium Persulfate APS]:::sub
    B --> B4[Alkaline Ammonia Etchant]:::sub

    C --> C1[Plasma Etching PE]:::sub
    C --> C2[Reactive Ion Etching RIE]:::sub
    C --> C3[Sputter Ion Beam Milling]:::sub
    C --> DRIE[Deep RIE Bosch Process]:::sub

    B1 --> B1a[Isotropic, PCB Hobby]:::note
    B2 --> B2a[Regenerable, Industrial PCB]:::note
    C2 --> C2a[Anisotropic, VLSI Gates]:::note
    DRIE --> D3a[Vertical, MEMS TSV]:::note

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef wet fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#075985,color:#ffffff
    classDef dry fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef note fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f,font-style:italic
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Etching Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRE[PRE-ETCH STAGE]
        P1[Copper Clad Laminate] --> P2[Surface Cleaning & De-smearing]
        P2 --> P3[Photoresist Lamination]
        P3 --> P4[UV Exposure via Photo-tool]
        P4 --> P5[Photoresist Development]
    end

    subgraph ETCH[ETCHING STAGE]
        E1[Masked Board Loaded] --> E2[Etchant Spray or Dip]
        E2 --> E3{Etch End-Point Detection}
        E3 -->|Not Clear| E2
        E3 -->|All Cu Removed| E4[Etchant Drain & Rinse]
    end

    subgraph POST[POST-ETCH STAGE]
        Q1[Photoresist Stripping] --> Q2[Optical Inspection AOI]
        Q2 --> Q3[Tin-Lead or ENIG Plating]
        Q3 --> Q4[Final PCB with Patterned Tracks]
    end

    P5 --> E1
    E4 --> Q1

    classDef pre fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    classDef etch fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    classDef post fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
    class PRE pre
    class ETCH etch
    class POST post
```

## 4.3 Etch Profile Comparison (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ISO[ISOTROPIC WET ETCH PROFILE]
        I1[Photoresist Mask] --> I2[Sloped Sidewall 45 deg]
        I2 --> I3[Undercut U = D]
    end

    subgraph ANISO[ANISOTROPIC DRY ETCH PROFILE]
        D1[Hard Mask SiO2 or Si3N4] --> D2[Vertical Sidewall 89-90 deg]
        D2 --> D3[Negligible Undercut U approx 0]
    end

    classDef iso fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    classDef ani fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    class ISO iso
    class ANISO ani
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Differentiate between wet etching and dry etching. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wet Etching | Dry Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Liquid chemical etchant | Plasma / vapor phase |
| Mechanism | Pure chemical reaction | Chemical + physical ion bombardment |
| Anisotropy | Isotropic (undercut present) | Anisotropic (vertical walls) |
| Equipment cost | Low (tank + heater) | High (vacuum chamber + RF source) |
| Minimum feature | > 50 µm | < 1 µm achievable |

**Wet advantages:** (i) Low cost, suitable for PCB prototyping labs; (ii) High throughput, batch processing of many boards.

**Dry advantages:** (i) Excellent anisotropy → critical dimension control for ICs; (ii) No liquid chemical waste disposal issues.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Define etch rate and selectivity. State the formula for selectivity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Etch rate ($R_e$):** The thickness of target material removed per unit time during the etching process, typically expressed in µm/min or nm/s.
> $$R_e = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$$
>
> **Selectivity ($S$):** The ratio of the etch rate of the target material to the etch rate of the mask or underlying layer — a measure of how "selectively" the etchant attacks only the desired material.
> $$S = \frac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{\text{mask}}}$$

A high selectivity (S >> 1) means the mask will survive long enough for complete removal of the target film.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Wet Etching Focus [14 Marks]

> **(a)** Describe the ferric chloride ($\text{FeCl}_3$) wet etching process for PCB fabrication. Explain the chemistry, procedure, and safety precautions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A PCB manufacturer needs to produce 150 µm wide copper tracks on a 35 µm thick copper-clad FR-4 board using an isotropic ferric chloride etchant. Calculate: (i) the etch rate if complete removal takes 4 minutes; (ii) the required photoresist opening width; (iii) the actual etch time using a 30% over-etch factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Ferric Chloride Wet Etching — Full Description (7 marks)**

**Chemistry:**
$$\text{Cu (solid)} + 2\,\text{FeCl}_3 \text{ (aq)} \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 \text{ (aq)} + 2\,\text{FeCl}_2 \text{ (aq)}$$

Copper is oxidized from Cu⁰ to Cu²⁺, while Fe³⁺ is reduced to Fe²⁺. The resulting copper chloride dissolves in the aqueous solution, exposing fresh copper beneath.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the copper-clad board with isopropyl alcohol and abrasive scrub to remove oxide and grease.
2. Laminate photoresist film onto the board at ~110 °C.
3. Expose through a UV photo-tool artwork, then develop in sodium carbonate solution.
4. Hard-bake the developed resist at 120 °C for 15 minutes to improve chemical resistance.
5. Immerse board in $\text{FeCl}_3$ solution at 40 °C, agitate gently (bubbler or rocking).
6. Monitor etch end-point — board turns from copper-orange to pale laminate yellow.
7. Rinse thoroughly in de-ionized water.
8. Strip photoresist using NaOH or acetone.
9. Dry and visually inspect.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Wear **chemical-resistant gloves, apron, and safety goggles** — $\text{FeCl}_3$ is corrosive and stains skin permanently brown.
- Work in a **fume hood** — evolved chlorine fumes are toxic.
- **Never pour $\text{FeCl}_3$ down the drain** — heavy-metal wastewater must be neutralized with NaOH to precipitate iron hydroxides, then filtered and disposed via authorized chemical waste handler.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Etch rate calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_e &= \frac{\text{Copper thickness}}{\text{Etch time}} \\
&= \frac{35 \, \mu m}{4 \, \text{min}} \\
&= 8.75 \, \mu m / min
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mask opening width:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{mask}} &= W_{\text{final}} + 2D \\
&= 150 + 2(35) \\
&= 150 + 70 \\
&= 220 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Actual etch time with 30% over-etch:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{min}} &= \frac{h_{\text{Cu}}}{R_e} = \frac{35}{8.75} = 4.0 \; \text{min} \\
t_{\text{actual}} &= 1.3 \times 4.0 = 5.2 \; \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **(2 marks)** Students forget the **factor of 2** in $W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{final}} + 2D$, using only $W_{\text{final}} + D$. There are **TWO undercut edges** (left + right) — this is the #1 mistake.
> - **(1 mark)** Forgetting unit conversion: copper thickness must be in **same units** as etch rate. Mixing µm and mm costs marks.
> - **(1 mark)** Omitting the over-etch safety factor. KTU expects students to mention the **practical ~30% over-etch** in industrial scenarios.
> - **(1 mark)** Not stating the assumption of isotropy ($U = D$). If you assume partial anisotropy ($A = 0.7$ for example), the answer changes — always declare your assumption.

---

### Question B — Dry Etching Focus [14 Marks]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** process. Discuss the role of RF power, chamber pressure, and gas chemistry. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare RIE and Deep RIE (Bosch process). For a silicon wafer etched to 100 µm depth, calculate the lateral undercut if the measured anisotropy factor $A = 0.85$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Reactive Ion Etching — Explanation (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (described):**
```
   RF Electrode (top, grounded) -----
                                   |
   ⟵ Gas Inlet (SF₆, CHF₃, O₂)    |  ← Plasma glow region
   ⟵                          ⚡ RF Plasma
   ⟵                              |
   ⟵ Wafer on Cathode (-)       |  ← DC bias builds up
   ⟵                              |
   RF Electrode (bottom, powered) ---
   ⟵ Vacuum Pump
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The chamber is evacuated to $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ Torr.
2. Process gas (e.g., $\text{SF}_6$ for Si) is introduced.
3. RF power (13.56 MHz) applied to the lower electrode ionizes the gas into a plasma of electrons, ions, and reactive radicals.
4. A self-biased DC potential (~ -200 to -800 V) develops on the cathode (wafer holder), accelerating positive ions **vertically downward** onto the wafer.
5. Ion bombardment + chemical reaction of F· radicals simultaneously etch the exposed Si:
$$\text{Si (solid)} + 4\,\text{F· radicals} \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4 \text{ (volatile gas)} \uparrow$$

**Role of Key Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Etch |
|---|---|
| **RF Power ↑** | Higher ion energy → more physical sputtering → better anisotropy but lower selectivity and possible radiation damage |
| **Pressure ↑** | More gas collisions → less directional ions → reduced anisotropy, more chemical (isotropic) etching |
| **Gas chemistry** | Determines selectivity — e.g., $\text{CHF}_3$ + O₂ for $\text{SiO}_2$; $\text{SF}_6$ for Si; $\text{Cl}_2$ / BCl₃ for Al; $\text{O}_2$ for polymers |

**(b) Comparison + Numerical (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | RIE | Deep RIE (Bosch) |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum depth | < 10 µm typical | Up to 600 µm+ |
| Aspect ratio | 5:1 typical | 50:1 achievable |
| Mechanism | Continuous etch | Alternating etch + passivation cycles |
| Sidewall | ~ 80–88° | ~ 89–90° (near vertical) |
| Scalloping | None | Visible "scallops" of ~50–200 nm |

**Bosch Process Cycle (key insight):**
- Step 1 (Etch): $\text{SF}_6$ plasma → isotropic Si etch (~5 s)
- Step 2 (Passivation): $\text{C}_4\text{F}_8$ plasma → fluorocarbon polymer coats all surfaces (~3 s)
- Cycle repeats → anisotropic vertical walls with characteristic scalloping.

**Numerical — Lateral Undercut:**

Given $D = 100 \, \mu m$ and $A = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 - \frac{U}{D} \\
\frac{U}{D} &= 1 - A = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \\
U &= 0.15 \times 100 \\
U &= 15 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the etched trench will undercut the mask by **15 µm** on each side.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Dry Etching Questions:**
> - **(2 marks)** Students confuse **Plasma Etching** (PE, isotropic) with **Reactive Ion Etching** (RIE, anisotropic). PE has the wafer on the grounded electrode; RIE has the wafer on the **powered** electrode. This distinction is the most-asked KTU question on this topic.
> - **(1 mark)** Failing to mention the **self-bias DC voltage** mechanism that makes RIE anisotropic. Without it, your answer is incomplete.
> - **(1 mark)** Writing $U = D \times A$ instead of $U = D \times (1 - A)$. The anisotropy factor subtracts from unity, not multiplies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Etching**

- ⚡ **Etching** = selective material removal via wet (chemical) or dry (plasma) means to transfer a mask pattern.
- ⚡ **Wet etchant for Cu on PCB:** Ferric Chloride ($\text{FeCl}_3$) — most common in academic labs.
- ⚡ **Etch rate $R_e = \Delta d / \Delta t$** — measured in µm/min.
- ⚡ **Selectivity $S = R_{\text{target}} / R_{\text{mask}}$** — higher is better.
- ⚡ **Anisotropy $A = 1 - U/D$** — 1 means perfectly vertical walls (RIE, DRIE), 0 means fully isotropic (wet etch).
- ⚡ **Mask width correction: $W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{final}} + 2U$** — never forget the factor of 2.
- ⚡ **Over-etch factor 1.3×** in industrial wet etching ensures complete Cu clearance.
- ⚡ **RIE chamber:** wafer sits on **powered** cathode to build up negative DC self-bias.
- ⚡ **Bosch process** = alternating $\text{SF}_6$ etch + $\text{C}_4\text{F}_8$ passivation → vertical DRIE for MEMS / TSV.
- ⚡ **Safety:** $\text{FeCl}_3$ stains skin; never pour heavy-metal etchant into drain — neutralize with NaOH first.
- ⚡ **Industrial PCB:** Cupric chloride is preferred over $\text{FeCl}_3$ because it is **regenerable** with $\text{Cl}_2$ gas or $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$.
- ⚡ **KTU favourite question pair:** (i) wet vs dry etching comparison table; (ii) calculate mask opening for given track width and Cu thickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) fabrication**, **Drilling** is the precision subtractive manufacturing process of creating cylindrical holes (plated or non-plated) through a laminated copper-clad substrate using a numerically controlled (CNC) drilling machine equipped with rotating micro-drill bits, in order to provide pathways for **through-hole component leads**, **inter-layer electrical connections (vias)**, and **mechanical mounting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference (Industry Standard):**
> Drilling is a mechanical material removal operation where a rotating multi-toothed cutter (drill bit) is fed into a stationary workpiece (PCB panel) along its axis to produce a hole of specified diameter and positional accuracy, governed by the **spindle speed (N)**, **feed rate (F)**, and **chip load (f)** parameters.

The drilled holes subsequently undergo **electroless copper deposition (de-smear + PTH – Plated Through-Hole)** to become electrically conductive barrels connecting different layers of the multilayer PCB.

## 1.2 Key Terminology & Classification

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PTH (Plated Through Hole)** | Hole drilled through entire board, walls plated with copper |
| **NPTH (Non-Plated Through Hole)** | Mechanical hole, no copper plating (e.g., mounting holes) |
| **Via** | Small conductive hole connecting two or more layers |
| **Blind Via** | Connects outer layer to inner layer only |
| **Buried Via** | Connects inner layers, invisible from outside |
| **Microvia** | Diameter $\le 150\ \mu m$, typically laser-drilled |
| **Hit Count** | Total number of holes to be drilled in a panel |
| **Stack Height** | Number of PCB panels stacked and drilled simultaneously |
| **Drill Bit** | Solid tungsten-carbide tool used to cut the hole |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wall Hanging Story" 🪛

Imagine you want to hang a photo frame on a brick wall:
- You press the tip of a rotating drill bit at a marked point.
- The bit spins thousands of times per minute (spinning motion = **spindle speed**).
- You slowly push the bit forward into the wall (pushing motion = **feed rate**).
- Small chips of brick dust come out (these are the **swarf/chips**).
- Once through, you retract, leaving a clean cylindrical hole.

In a **PCB**, the brick wall = the **copper-clad laminate**, and we drill millions of such tiny holes at exact coordinates fed by a **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing) file**. Just as a carpenter worries about the drill bit heating up or breaking inside the wall, a PCB manufacturer carefully controls **cutting speed**, **coolant/entry material**, and **chip evacuation** to avoid damaging expensive drill bits (which cost ₹200 to ₹5000 each!).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard PCB substrate thickness:** **0.2 mm to 3.2 mm**
- **Standard drill bit diameter range:** **0.15 mm to 6.5 mm**
- **Spindle speed range (modern CNC):** **15,000 RPM to 150,000 RPM**
- **Maximum hit count for a micro-drill (0.3 mm):** ~**1000–1500 hits**
- **Entry material (aluminum/phenolic):** 0.10–0.15 mm thick
- **Back-up material:** 1.5–2.5 mm phenolic or composite board

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> The topic "Drilling" bridges **Module 1 (PCB Design & Fabrication Overview)** and **Module 2 (PCB Manufacturing Processes)**. The student is expected to understand *why* drilling parameters matter for **signal integrity** (controlled impedance) and *how* drilled hole quality affects downstream **PTH plating yield**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CNC Drilling Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The PCB drilling operation is a **closed-loop, servo-controlled mechanical process** with the following sequential logic:

1. **CAM File Interpretation** — The fabricator receives a **Gerber / Excellon drill file** containing *(X, Y)* coordinates, hole diameter, and tool number.
2. **Panel Loading & Stack Assembly** — Multiple bare PCBs are stacked (typically 1–3 panels) on top of an **entry material** (aluminum foil 0.10–0.15 mm) and backed by a **back-up board** (phenolic / composite).
3. **Tool Selection** — A **robotic tool changer** loads the appropriate drill bit from a rack of up to 250 tools.
4. **Spindle Acceleration** — The spindle ramps up to programmed RPM (typically 30,000–80,000 RPM for microvia work).
5. **Approach & Plunge** — The Z-axis drives the rotating bit down at the programmed **infeed rate** until it pierces the stack.
6. **Retraction & Chip Evacuation** — The bit retracts rapidly; coolant air blasts chips upward (vacuum exhausts them).
7. **Index to Next Hole** — The X-Y table moves to the next coordinate at speeds up to **50 m/min**.
8. **Loop Completion** — Steps 4–7 repeat for the **entire hit count** (often 5,000–50,000 holes per panel).

## 2.2 The Three Critical Drilling Parameters — The "Why & How"

| Parameter | Why It Matters | How It's Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Spindle Speed (N)** in RPM | Determines **cutting velocity**. Too low → rubbing, heat, bit wear. Too high → vibration, breakage. | Set in CNC program. Inverse of drill diameter. |
| **Feed Rate (F)** in mm/min | Determines **chip thickness**. Too low → rubbing, work-hardening. Too high → chip crowding, breakage. | Set per drill diameter. Lower for small bits. |
| **Chip Load (f)** in mm/rev | The actual thickness of material removed per revolution. The **key performance metric**. | $f = F / N$. Must stay in safe range for bit diameter. |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb Used in Industry:**
> For **tungsten carbide micro-drills** in FR-4 laminate, the safe **chip load** is:
> $f \approx 0.025 \text{ to } 0.075\ \text{mm/rev}$ for $D = 0.3 \text{ to } 1.0\ \text{mm}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $N$ = Spindle speed, $V_c$ = Cutting velocity, $D$ = Drill diameter | $N$ in RPM, $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm | Calculate spindle speed for given drill size |
| 2 | $F = f \cdot N$ | $F$ = Feed rate, $f$ = Feed per rev, $N$ = Spindle speed | $F$ in mm/min, $f$ in mm/rev | Compute feed rate |
| 3 | $f_{in} = \dfrac{F}{n_{sp}}$ | $f_{in}$ = Infeed per spindle, $n_{sp}$ = Number of drills on spindle | mm/min | Multi-spindle machines |
| 4 | $T_{drill} = \dfrac{H \cdot d}{F}$ | $T_{drill}$ = Drilling time, $H$ = Hit count, $d$ = Hole depth | minutes | Estimate machine cycle time |
| 5 | $R_a = \dfrac{\text{chip thickness}}{\text{cutting edge count}}$ | $R_a$ = Surface roughness metric | $\mu m$ | Hole wall quality |
| 6 | $AR = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | $AR$ = Aspect ratio, $d$ = Board thickness, $D$ = Hole diameter | dimensionless | Plating feasibility check |
| 7 | $V_{chip} = f \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $V_{chip}$ = Material removal rate | mm³/min | Productivity metric |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table above, the division lines use **fraction display**, not the markdown pipe character. Always write $\dfrac{a}{b}$ for inline clarity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

Drilling is the **single most expensive step in PCB fabrication**, accounting for **30–40% of total fabrication cost**. The drilled hole quality directly determines:
- **PTH plating yield** — rough walls cause plating voids.
- **Signal integrity** — poor hole position causes stub resonance at high frequencies (>5 GHz).
- **Via-in-pad reliability** — drill wander breaks BGA pads.
- **Component lead insertion** — oversized holes cause solder joint fatigue.

In the **automotive, aerospace, and 5G telecom** industries, drilling precision is now a **yield-limiting factor** in HDI (High Density Interconnect) boards with microvias <100 $\mu m$ diameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Velocity

The **cutting velocity** $V_c$ is the linear speed at which the outer diameter of the drill bit contacts the hole wall. For one full revolution, the bit travels a circular path of circumference $\pi \cdot D$.

**Step 1:** Distance covered by outer edge in 1 revolution:
$$l_{rev} = \pi \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the drill diameter (in mm).

**Step 2:** For $N$ revolutions per minute, the total linear distance per minute is:
$$l_{min} = N \cdot \pi \cdot D$$

This is the cutting velocity in mm/min. Converting to m/min by dividing by 1000:
$$V_c = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi \cdot D}{1000}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $N$:
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

> **Final derived equation (used in industry and KTU problems):**
> $$\boxed{N\ (\text{RPM}) = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c\ (\text{m/min})}{\pi \cdot D\ (\text{mm})}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A PCB fabricator uses a **0.5 mm diameter** drill on FR-4 laminate. The recommended cutting velocity is **80 m/min**. Find the spindle speed.

**Given:** $V_c = 80$ m/min, $D = 0.5$ mm.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the derived equation:
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 80}{\pi \cdot 0.5}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator:
$$\pi \cdot 0.5 = 1.5708$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator and divide:
$$N = \dfrac{80000}{1.5708} \approx 50929\ \text{RPM}$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 50{,}929$ RPM ≈ **51 kRPM**, which falls comfortably within the standard 15k–80k RPM CNC range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate (F) from Chip Load

The **chip load** $f$ is defined as the linear advance of the drill per revolution. For $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear advance per minute is:
$$F = f \cdot N$$

This is the **infeed rate** of the Z-axis. It must be chosen such that:
- Chips do not clog the flute (too high).
- The bit does not rub and overheat (too low).

> **Final derived equation:**
> $$\boxed{F\ (\text{mm/min}) = f\ (\text{mm/rev}) \times N\ (\text{RPM})}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Continuing the previous example with $N = 50{,}929$ RPM and a recommended chip load of $f = 0.04$ mm/rev for a 0.5 mm drill:

**Step 1:** Apply the equation:
$$F = 0.04 \times 50929$$

**Step 2:** Compute:
$$F \approx 2037.16\ \text{mm/min}$$

**Answer:** $F \approx 2.04$ m/min — a typical feed rate for small diameter drilling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Drilling Time Estimation

For a board with $H$ total holes, each of depth $d$ (board thickness), drilled at feed rate $F$:

**Step 1:** Time per hole (neglecting tool change and air-cut time):
$$t_{hole} = \dfrac{d}{F}$$

**Step 2:** For $H$ holes:
$$T_{drill} = H \cdot t_{hole} = \dfrac{H \cdot d}{F}$$

> **Final derived equation:**
> $$\boxed{T_{drill}\ (\text{min}) = \dfrac{H \times d\ (\text{mm})}{F\ (\text{mm/min})}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A 4-layer PCB has 8000 holes ($H = 8000$), board thickness $d = 1.6$ mm, feed rate $F = 2000$ mm/min.

**Step 1:** Apply the equation:
$$T_{drill} = \dfrac{8000 \cdot 1.6}{2000}$$

**Step 2:** Compute:
$$T_{drill} = \dfrac{12800}{2000} = 6.4\ \text{min}$$

> **Note:** This is the *pure drilling* time. Real cycle time also includes tool changes (~3 sec each), air moves between holes, and panel loading — typically multiplying actual time by **1.5× to 2×**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aspect Ratio (AR) Constraint

The **aspect ratio** is the ratio of board thickness to drill diameter:
$$AR = \dfrac{d_{board}}{D_{hole}}$$

**Why it matters:** Plating solution cannot reach the bottom of very deep, narrow holes. The industry rule is:
$$AR \le 10:1$$
for reliable electroless copper plating.

> **Final equation:**
> $$\boxed{AR = \dfrac{d_{board}}{D_{hole}} \le 10}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Board thickness = 1.6 mm, smallest hole = 0.3 mm.
$$AR = \dfrac{1.6}{0.3} \approx 5.33$$
**Feasible** for standard PTH plating. ✅

If the board is **3.2 mm thick** with the same 0.3 mm hole:
$$AR = \dfrac{3.2}{0.3} \approx 10.67$$
**Marginal** — staggered via or laser drilling recommended. ⚠️

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Drilling Method Selection

| Board Type | Min Hole Dia | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single/Double-sided | $\ge 0.5$ mm | Mechanical (CNC) | Cost effective, robust |
| Multilayer (4–8 layers) | 0.3–0.5 mm | Mechanical (CNC, HSS bits) | Standard process |
| HDI with microvias | 0.10–0.15 mm | **Laser drilling (CO₂ / UV)** | Mechanical bits too fragile |
| Flex PCB | 0.2–0.5 mm | Mechanical or UV laser | Heat-sensitive material |
| Substrate-like-PCB (SLP) | 0.075–0.10 mm | UV laser (LDI) | Extreme precision needed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC PCB Drilling Machine — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gerber and Excellon Drill File Input] --> B[CAM Software Verification]
    B --> C[Panel Stack Assembly on Vacuum Table]
    C --> D[Tool Rack with 250 plus Drill Bits]
    D --> E[Robotic Tool Changer]
    E --> F[High Speed Spindle 15k to 80k RPM]
    F --> G[Z Axis Infeed with Servo Motor]
    G --> H[XY Gantry Table for Positioning]
    H --> I[Drill Bit Contact with Entry Foil]
    I --> J[Material Removal in PCB Stack]
    J --> K[Vacuum Chip Extraction]
    K --> L[Position Verification via CCD Camera]
    L --> M{All Holes Drilled Question}
    M -->|No| H
    M -->|Yes| N[Panel Unloading and Inspection]
    N --> O[Output to De smear and PTH Process]
    P[Coolant Air Nozzle] --> F
    Q[Back Up Phenolic Board] --> J
    R[Entry Aluminum Foil 0.10 mm] --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **drill file** is the *digital blueprint* (analogy: Google Maps for the drill bit).
- The **CAM verification** catches errors before expensive tools break.
- The **spindle + Z-axis + XY table** is the *mechanical triangle* of precision.
- The **vacuum extraction** removes chips — without it, chips re-enter the hole and cause *epoxy smear*.
- The **CCD camera** verifies positional accuracy of every hole (sub-25 $\mu m$ tolerance).

## 4.2 Drilling Process — Cause-Effect Quality Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drilling Parameters] --> B[Hole Quality Outcome]
    A1[Spindle Speed N] --> A
    A2[Feed Rate F] --> A
    A3[Chip Load f] --> A
    A4[Entry and Back Up Material] --> A
    B --> B1[Smooth Hole Wall]
    B --> B2[Accurate Position]
    B --> B3[No Epoxy Smear]
    B --> B4[No Burrs]
    B --> B5[Good PTH Plating Yield]
    B1 --> C[Reliable PCB]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    D[Wrong Parameters] --> E[Defects]
    D1[Too High F] --> D
    D2[Too Low N] --> D
    D3[Dull Drill Bit] --> D
    D4[No Back Up] --> D
    E --> E1[Nail Heading]
    E --> E2[Drill Wander]
    E --> E3[Epoxy Smear]
    E --> E4[Burrs and Rough Walls]
    E --> E5[Drill Bit Breakage]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Downstream Process Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Drilling] --> B[Step 2 De smear with Potassium Permanganate]
    B --> C[Step 3 Electroless Copper Plating]
    C --> D[Step 4 Electrolytic Copper Plating]
    D --> E[Step 5 Outer Layer Imaging]
    E --> F[Step 6 Etching and Solder Mask]
    F --> G[Step 7 Surface Finish ENIG or HASL]
    G --> H[Step 8 Electrical Test and V Cut]
    H --> I[Finished PCB Panel]
```

> **KTU Insight:** Drilling is the **upstream bottleneck**. If holes are not correctly drilled, *every subsequent chemical and plating step* will fail. This is why drilling carries a **yield-cost multiplier of ~5×** — one drilled defect causes 5+ downstream losses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define drilling in PCB fabrication. List any four drilling parameters that affect hole quality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Drilling in PCB fabrication is the precision mechanical process of creating cylindrical holes through the copper-clad laminate using a rotating tungsten-carbide drill bit driven by a CNC-controlled high-speed spindle, to accommodate component leads, vias, and mounting hardware (1 mark).

**Four key parameters:**
1. **Spindle speed (N)** — affects cutting temperature and bit life (0.5 mark).
2. **Feed rate (F)** — determines chip thickness and hole wall roughness (0.5 mark).
3. **Chip load (f)** — defines safe material removal per revolution (0.5 mark).
4. **Entry/back-up material** — prevents burrs and exit damage (0.5 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between mechanical drilling and laser drilling in PCB manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Drilling | Laser Drilling |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool** | Rotating tungsten-carbide drill | CO₂ or UV laser beam |
| **Min diameter** | 0.15 mm | 0.025 mm (microvia) |
| **Aspect ratio** | Up to 10:1 | Up to 1:1 (blind vias) |
| **Best for** | Through-holes, PTH | Microvias, HDI boards |
| **Cost per hole** | Low | High |
| **Heat affected zone** | Low (coolant air) | High (must manage thermal damage) |

(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for the comparison table.)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Module 2 carries internal choice. Below are **TWO fully-worked alternatives** — answer **ONE** in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a CNC PCB drilling machine. Discuss the role of entry material and back-up board. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PCB fabricator needs to drill 0.4 mm diameter holes through a 1.6 mm thick FR-4 board. The recommended cutting velocity is 90 m/min and the recommended chip load is 0.035 mm/rev. Calculate the **(i)** Spindle Speed, **(ii)** Feed Rate, and **(iii)** Total drilling time for 6000 holes. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A CNC PCB drilling machine consists of the following subsystems (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **CNC Controller** (1 mark) — Interprets the **Excellon drill file** and orchestrates motion of the X-Y table and Z-axis spindle with sub-25 $\mu m$ positioning accuracy.
2. **High-Speed Spindle** (1 mark) — Houses the drill bit; rotates at programmed RPM (15,000–150,000). Direct-drive or air-bearing types are used for vibration-free operation.
3. **X-Y Gantry Table** (1 mark) — Moves the panel under the stationary spindle at speeds up to 50 m/min; uses linear encoders for closed-loop feedback.
4. **Tool Changer & Tool Rack** (0.5 mark) — Holds up to 250+ drill bits; auto-changes in <3 seconds.
5. **Vacuum Chuck & Stack Assembly** (0.5 mark) — Holds the entry-foil + PCB stack + back-up board under vacuum.

**Role of Entry Material (Aluminum foil 0.10–0.15 mm):** (1.5 marks)
- Prevents **drill bit wandering** at the moment of entry on copper.
- Provides a clean, hard surface to start the cut.
- Reduces **burr formation** on the top copper layer.
- Prevents **top-layer chipping** of the copper foil.

**Role of Back-up Board (Phenolic 1.5–2.5 mm):** (1.5 marks)
- Prevents **burr formation on the bottom copper layer** during drill exit.
- Supports the workpiece to avoid **delamination**.
- Absorbs the **axial thrust** of the drill bit at breakthrough.
- Catches chips and prevents them from flying out.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $D = 0.4$ mm, $d = 1.6$ mm, $V_c = 90$ m/min, $f = 0.035$ mm/rev, $H = 6000$ holes.

**(i) Spindle Speed (2 marks):**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 90}{\pi \cdot 0.4}$$
$$N = \dfrac{90000}{1.2566} \approx 71{,}619\ \text{RPM}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate (2 marks):**
$$F = f \cdot N = 0.035 \times 71619 \approx 2506.7\ \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Total Drilling Time (3 marks):**
$$T_{drill} = \dfrac{H \cdot d}{F} = \dfrac{6000 \times 1.6}{2506.7}$$
$$T_{drill} = \dfrac{9600}{2506.7} \approx 3.83\ \text{minutes}$$

> **Realistic cycle time** (including tool changes and air moves) ≈ $3.83 \times 1.8 \approx 6.9$ minutes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** List and briefly explain **six common drilling defects** encountered in PCB fabrication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Aspect Ratio**. A 6-layer PCB of thickness 2.0 mm is to be drilled with a 0.3 mm drill bit. Calculate the aspect ratio and comment on its plating feasibility. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| # | Defect | Cause | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drill Wander** (1.5 marks) | Off-center entry on copper | Misaligned holes, broken vias |
| 2 | **Nail Heading** (1 mark) | Drill tip flattens due to improper feed | Oversized holes, rough walls |
| 3 | **Epoxy Smear** (1.5 marks) | Heat melts resin, smears on hole wall | Breaks PTH electrical connection |
| 4 | **Burr Formation** (1 mark) | No back-up board or dull bit | Solder bridging, plating defects |
| 5 | **Drill Breakage** (1 mark) | Excessive feed rate or chip clog | Machine downtime, scrap panel |
| 6 | **Hole Wall Roughness** (1 mark) | Wrong chip load or worn bit | Poor plating adhesion, signal loss |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Aspect Ratio (AR) is the ratio of board thickness to the diameter of the drilled hole:
$$AR = \dfrac{d_{board}}{D_{hole}}$$

It is a critical parameter because **electroless copper plating solution** must reach the bottom of the hole uniformly; high AR values make this difficult.

**Calculation (3 marks):**
$$AR = \dfrac{2.0\ \text{mm}}{0.3\ \text{mm}} \approx 6.67$$

**Comment (2 marks):** The calculated AR of 6.67 is **less than the industry limit of 10:1**, so it is **feasible for standard PTH plating** using conventional horizontal conveyorized plating lines. However, the fabricator should:
- Use a **plasma or permanganate de-smear** process for the 6-layer board.
- Maintain **panel-to-solution flow** agitation for uniform copper deposition.
- Consider **staggered via** design if signal integrity at 5G frequencies is required.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in spindle speed formula** — Using $V_c$ in m/s instead of m/min gives 60× wrong answer. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the cutting velocity equation (factor of 1000 in numerator).
> 3. **Confusing chip load and feed rate** — Chip load is *per revolution*; feed rate is *per minute*.
> 4. **Ignoring the back-up board** in block diagrams — KTU examiners *specifically* check for this.
> 5. **Using the wrong constant** $\pi = 3.14$ vs $\pi = 3.14159$ — for $N$ problems, $3.14$ is acceptable but for very small drill bits, the error compounds.
> 6. **Drawing single-sided diagrams** — Always show a cross-section of the stack: *Entry foil | PCB layers | Back-up board*.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Drilling** is the CNC subtractive process of creating precise cylindrical holes in PCBs for PTH, NPTH, and vias.
- The **three primary parameters** are Spindle Speed $N$, Feed Rate $F$, and Chip Load $f$.
- **Master Formula #1:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$
- **Master Formula #2:** $F = f \cdot N$
- **Master Formula #3:** $T_{drill} = \dfrac{H \cdot d}{F}$
- **Master Formula #4:** $AR = \dfrac{d_{board}}{D_{hole}} \le 10$
- **Entry material** (aluminum 0.10 mm) prevents drill wander and top burrs.
- **Back-up board** (phenolic) prevents bottom burrs and supports breakthrough.
- **Common defects:** drill wander, nail heading, epoxy smear, burrs, drill breakage, hole wall roughness.
- **Laser drilling** is preferred for **microvias (<0.15 mm)** in HDI boards.
- **Mechanical drilling** remains the cost-effective workhorse for **standard PTH holes (0.3–6.5 mm)**.
- **Aspect ratio > 10:1** requires staggered vias, laser drilling, or sequential lamination.
- **Drilling is the costliest step** in PCB fabrication — ~30–40% of total cost.
- **Stack height** typically 1–3 panels, balancing productivity vs. hole quality.
- **Hit count** of 1000–1500 is the practical limit for 0.3 mm drill bit life.
- After drilling, the **de-smear** step (KMnO₄ or plasma) is mandatory to remove epoxy smear before PTH plating.

> **One-Line Memory Trick:** *“**S**peed **F**eeds **C**hip = **N** × **f** = **F**.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly of Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly of Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembly of Components** in the context of electronic hardware engineering refers to the systematic, sequential process of mechanically mounting and electrically interconnecting discrete electronic components (passive, active, electromechanical) onto a Printed Circuit Board (PCB) substrate using standardized soldering, fastening, or conductive adhesive technologies, conforming to **IPC-A-610** acceptability standards and ESD-safe handling protocols (ANSI/ESD S20.20).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for OEEVT612, *Assembly of Components* encompasses the full manufacturing chain from bare PCB reception to a fully populated, tested, and conformal-coated electronic module. The discipline is broadly divided into two dominant paradigms: **Through-Hole Technology (THT)** and **Surface Mount Technology (SMT)**, with hybrid assemblies being common in industrial practice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Furniture Assembly Workshop"**
> Imagine you are building an IKEA wardrobe. Some parts are screwed in from the back (you must drill holes all the way through — this is **THT**). Other parts are tiny pegs that just stick onto a flat board using a dab of glue (this is **SMT**). The glue must be melted (cured) at exactly the right temperature for exactly the right time — too hot and the wood burns, too cold and the joint is weak. Soldering works exactly the same way: it is the "glue" that becomes a permanent electrical and mechanical bond when heated to its melting point (typically **183 °C** for lead-free SAC305 or **183 °C – 227 °C** eutectic range).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Lead-free solder melting point (SAC305) | **217 °C – 221 °C** |
| Sn-Pb eutectic melting point | **183 °C** |
| Reflow peak temperature (lead-free) | **235 °C – 245 °C** |
| Wave solder pot temperature | **245 °C – 260 °C** |
| Acceptable humidity level for MSDs | **< 5 %** (per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033) |
| ESD-safe workstation wrist-strap resistance | **1 MΩ** (typical) |
| Stencil aperture wall polish | **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** |
| Pick-and-place placement accuracy | **± 0.05 mm** (modern SMD lines) |
| AOI minimum defect detection size | **0.05 mm²** pad coverage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEEVT612 Module 2):** The student must be able to differentiate THT, SMT, mixed-technology assembly, identify defects (bridging, tombstoning, voids, cold joints), and describe the complete assembly line flow from stencil printing to AOI and rework.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Dominant Assembly Paradigms

#### 2.1.1 Through-Hole Technology (THT)
- Component leads are inserted through **plated through-holes (PTHs)** drilled in the PCB.
- The lead protrudes on the solder side and is joined by **wave soldering** or **hand soldering**.
- **Mechanical strength** is superior — used for connectors, transformers, large capacitors subjected to vibration.
- Drill hole diameter is typically **0.2 mm – 0.5 mm** larger than the lead diameter to allow solder wicking (capillary action).
- Hole aspect ratio (hole depth / drill diameter) is generally kept **≤ 10:1** for reliable barrel fill.

#### 2.1.2 Surface Mount Technology (SMT)
- Components (SMDs — Surface Mount Devices) are placed directly onto **pads** on the PCB surface.
- Solder paste (a mixture of tin-alloy spheres, flux, and viscosity modifiers) is screen-printed or jet-printed, then reflow-soldered.
- Enables **miniaturization**, higher component density, better high-frequency performance (shorter lead lengths → less parasitic inductance).
- Component size codes follow **Imperial** (0402, 0603, 0805, 1206) or **Metric** (1005, 1608, 2012, 3216) conventions.

### 2.2 The SMT Assembly Line (Sequential Logic)

1. **Solder Paste Printing** → Stencil (laser-cut, electropolished stainless steel) deposits paste via squeegee at 45° angle, pressure 1–10 kg/cm².
2. **SPI (Solder Paste Inspection)** → 3-D optical scan checks volume, height, area, registration.
3. **Component Placement** → Pick-and-place machine (e.g., Siemens Siplace, Yamaha YSM) using vision system, vacuum nozzles.
4. **Pre-Reflow AOI (optional)** → Detect missing/misaligned parts.
5. **Reflow Soldering** → Convection, vapor phase, IR, or laser reflow oven with controlled thermal profile.
6. **Post-Reflow AOI** → Detect tombstoning, bridging, insufficient solder, solder balls.
7. **X-Ray Inspection (for BGA/QFN)** → Detect voids, head-in-pillow defects.
8. **ICT / Flying Probe / FCT** → Electrical test of nets, components, functionality.
9. **Rework Station** → For defective units (hot air, hot plate, micro-soldering iron).
10. **Conformal Coating / Potting** → For harsh-environment protection (humidity, salt fog).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Why" matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is the reflow profile ramp rate kept between 1–3 °C/s?"* — The answer: a too-fast ramp causes **solder splatter** (flux volatilization explosive), while a too-slow ramp causes **excessive intermetallic growth** (brittle Cu₆Sn₅ / Cu₃Sn layers) and **solder beading**.

### 2.3 The Reflow Soldering Thermal Profile (Four Zones)

| Zone | Function | Typical Temperature | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-heat (Zone 1)** | Activates flux, drives off volatiles | 150 °C – 180 °C | 60 s – 120 s |
| **Soak / Thermal Equalization (Zone 2)** | Equalizes ΔT across board, removes solvents | 180 °C – 200 °C | 60 s – 120 s |
| **Reflow (Zone 3)** | Peak temperature, solder melts and wets pads/leads | 235 °C – 245 °C | 45 s – 90 s above liquidus |
| **Cooling (Zone 4)** | Solidifies joints, controls intermetallic formation | -3 °C/s to -6 °C/s | until 100 °C |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stencil Aperture Area | $A = L \cdot W$ (rectangular) or $A = \pi r^2$ (circular) | mm² | Solder paste volume calc |
| Paste Volume (theoretical) | $V = A \cdot t$ where $t$ = stencil thickness | mm³ | Print process control |
| Aspect Ratio (stencil) | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{W}{t}$ (must be $\geq 1.5$) | dimensionless | Stencil design rule |
| Area Ratio (stencil) | $\text{AR}_{area} = \dfrac{A_{aperture}}{A_{wall}}$ (must be $\geq 0.66$) | dimensionless | Paste release quality |
| Cooling rate limit | $C_{rate} \leq 6 \, \degree C/s$ | °C/s | Avoid thermal shock |
| Time above liquidus (TAL) | $\text{TAL} = t_{peak} - t_{liquidus}$ | s | Typically 45 s – 90 s |
| CTE mismatch stress | $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | N/m² | Reliability of large BGA |
| Thermal fatigue (Coffin-Manson) | $N_f = A \cdot (\Delta T)^{-n}$ | cycles | Solder joint life prediction |
| Pad-to-lead clearance (SMT) | $L_{pad} = L_{lead} + 0.5\,\text{mm}$ (typical) | mm | Land pattern design |
| Pick-and-place throughput | $\text{CPH} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | components/hour | Line balancing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The **Coffin-Manson relation** is used by reliability engineers (especially in automotive and aerospace electronics — e.g., Bosch, Honeywell) to predict how many thermal cycles a solder joint can survive between -40 °C and +125 °C before fatigue cracking. The exponent $n$ typically falls in the range **1.9 – 2.7** for SAC solders.

### 2.5 THT Wave Soldering Process

The PCB (pre-fluxed, pre-heated) passes over a standing wave of molten solder (~250 °C). Two waves are used:
- **First wave (chip wave / turbulent wave):** Aggressive flow to ensure hole fill.
- **Second wave (lambda wave / smooth laminar):** Removes bridging, gives clean finish.

**Critical parameters:** Pre-heat temp (100 °C – 130 °C), conveyor angle (4° – 7°), contact time with wave (2 s – 4 s), solder pot dross skimming.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Stencil Aperture Area Ratio (the Industry Rule for Reliable Paste Release)

**Why this derivation matters:** During squeegee printing, the solder paste must cleanly *release* from the stencil aperture onto the PCB pad. If the aperture is too small relative to the wall thickness, the paste sticks to the aperture walls (poor release → insufficient solder → open joints). The **Area Ratio** quantifies this.

Let $W$ be the aperture width, $L$ the length, and $t$ the stencil thickness. The aperture bottom area (paste release surface) is:

$$A_{aperture} = W \cdot L$$

The four aperture walls (paste adhesion surface) have total area:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \cdot t \cdot (W + L)$$

The Area Ratio is defined as the ratio of release surface to adhesion surface:

$$\text{AR}_{area} = \dfrac{A_{aperture}}{A_{wall}} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 t (W + L)}$$

**Conversion logic:** For release to be reliable, the bottom area must exceed the wall area by a factor of at least 0.66 (this is the empirical industry threshold, established by solder-paste manufacturers like Indium and Heraeus):

$$\text{AR}_{area} \geq 0.66$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 t (W + L)} \geq 0.66$$

Solving for the minimum stencil thickness given an aperture dimension:

$$t \leq \dfrac{W \cdot L}{1.32 \cdot (W + L)}$$

**Example:** For a 0402 chip resistor pad aperture of $W = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ and $L = 0.6\,\text{mm}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t &\leq \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 0.6}{1.32 \cdot (0.5 + 0.6)} \\
t &\leq \dfrac{0.30}{1.32 \cdot 1.10} \\
t &\leq \dfrac{0.30}{1.452} \\
t &\leq 0.2066\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, for a 0402 pad, a stencil thickness of **0.10 mm – 0.15 mm** is typical in industry (a step-down stencil with reduced thickness over fine-pitch areas is often used).

### 3.2 Derivation — Solder Joint Thermal Fatigue Life (Coffin-Manson)

**Why this matters:** Solder joints in automotive under-the-hood electronics experience daily thermal cycling. The number of cycles to failure follows:

$$N_f = A \cdot (\Delta T)^{-n}$$

where $\Delta T$ is the cyclic temperature swing and $A$, $n$ are material-dependent constants.

For SAC305 (Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5) lead-free solder, the typical Norris-Landzberg modified form is used:

$$N_f = C \cdot f^{-m} \cdot (\Delta T)^{-n} \cdot e^{\dfrac{Q}{k T_{max}}}$$

where $f$ is cycle frequency, $Q$ is activation energy (~0.8 eV), and $T_{max}$ is peak temperature in Kelvin.

**Step-by-step calculation** (simplified form) for a BGA experiencing $\Delta T = 100\,\text{K}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_f &= A \cdot (100)^{-n}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking typical values for SAC305: $A = 6000$, $n = 2.0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_f &= 6000 \cdot (100)^{-2.0} \\
N_f &= 6000 \cdot 0.0001 \\
N_f &= 0.6 \text{ cycles} \quad \text{(unrealistic for raw form)}
\end{aligned}$$

This indicates the simple Coffin-Manson form is for comparative studies only; the Norris-Landzberg modification must be used. The key teaching point: **higher $\Delta T$ dramatically reduces joint life** — this is why derating (lowering operational $\Delta T$) is a design rule for high-reliability boards.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common SMT Defects and Root Causes

| Defect | Visual Signature | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tombstoning** | One end of chip lifted | Uneven solder paste, pad asymmetry, warp | Use balanced land patterns, reduce ramp rate |
| **Solder Bridging** | Two adjacent pads shorted | Excessive paste, misaligned stencil, poor gasketing | Clean stencil, adjust squeegee pressure |
| **Solder Balling** | Stray solder spheres near joints | Solder splatter, moisture in paste | Bake paste, control pre-heat ramp |
| **Voids (BGA)** | Air pockets inside BGA balls | Outgassing of flux, contaminated pad | Optimize reflow profile, pad cleanliness |
| **Head-in-Pillow (HIP)** | BGA ball contacts paste but doesn't coalesce | Warpage, oxidation, insufficient TAL | Increase TAL, use nitrogen atmosphere |
| **Cold Joint** | Dull, grainy, non-convex fillet | Insufficient heat, contaminated surface | Raise tip temp, clean pads, use fresh flux |
| **Insufficient Solder** | Concave fillet, exposed pad | Stencil clog, low squeegee pressure | Clean stencil, increase pressure |
| **Lifted Pad** | Copper pad detached from PCB | Excessive rework heat, mechanical force | Use proper rework profile, pre-heat board |
| **Billboarding** | Component stands vertical on edge | Placement force, vibration, pad geometry | Reduce placement acceleration |

### 3.4 Derivation — Pre-heat Time Required for Flux Activation

**Why this matters:** Flux must reach its activation temperature (~150 °C for rosin-based) *uniformly across the PCB* before reaching the reflow zone, otherwise the volatiles flash violently in the reflow zone, causing spatter.

For a PCB of mass $m$ and specific heat capacity $c_p$, with convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$, surface area $A_{s}$, entering temperature $T_i$ and target temperature $T_t$, the time to reach $T_t$:

$$T(t) = T_{\infty} - (T_{\infty} - T_i) \cdot e^{-\frac{h A_s}{m c_p} t}$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t = -\dfrac{m c_p}{h A_s} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{T_{\infty} - T_t}{T_{\infty} - T_i} \right)$$

**Example:** Let $m = 0.2\,\text{kg}$, $c_p = 1200\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, $h = 25\,\text{W/(m²·K)}$, $A_s = 0.04\,\text{m}^2$, $T_{\infty} = 180\,°C$, $T_i = 25\,°C$, $T_t = 150\,°C$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= -\dfrac{0.2 \cdot 1200}{25 \cdot 0.04} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{180 - 150}{180 - 25} \right) \\
t &= -\dfrac{240}{1.0} \cdot \ln\left( \dfrac{30}{155} \right) \\
t &= -240 \cdot \ln(0.1935) \\
t &= -240 \cdot (-1.642) \\
t &= 394\,\text{s} \approx 6.5\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the *minimum* pre-heat time; in practice, line speed is tuned to give 60 s – 120 s exposure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level SMT Assembly Line Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bare PCB Incoming] --> B[Stencil Printing Station]
    B --> C[Solder Paste Inspection SPI]
    C --> D[Pick and Place Machine]
    D --> E[Pre Reflow AOI]
    E --> F[Reflow Oven 4 Zones]
    F --> G[Post Reflow AOI]
    G --> H[X Ray Inspection BGA QFN]
    H --> I[In Circuit Test ICT]
    I --> J{Defect Detected}
    J -->|Yes| K[Rework Station]
    J -->|No| L[Conformal Coating]
    K --> I
    L --> M[Final Functional Test]
    M --> N[Packaging and Shipping]
```

### 4.2 Reflow Profile Thermal Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z1[Zone 1 Preheat 150 to 180 C]
        direction LR
        Z1A[PCB Entry 25 C] --> Z1B[Flux Activation 150 C]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Zone 2 Soak 180 to 200 C]
        direction LR
        Z2A[Thermal Equalization] --> Z2B[Volatile Removal]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Zone 3 Reflow 235 to 245 C]
        direction LR
        Z3A[TAL 45 to 90 s] --> Z3B[Solder Wetting]
    end
    subgraph Z4[Zone 4 Cooling 6 C per s]
        direction LR
        Z4A[Controlled Solidify] --> Z4B[Intermetallic Formation]
    end
    Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 --> Z4
```

### 4.3 THT versus SMT Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Assembly Decision Node] --> Q1{Component Type}
    Q1 -->|Through Hole| THT[THT Path: Insert Leads into PTH]
    THT --> T1[Wave Solder or Hand Solder]
    T1 --> T2[Stronger Joint High Power]
    Q1 -->|Surface Mount| SMT[SMT Path: Place on Pads]
    SMT --> S1[Reflow Solder Paste]
    S1 --> S2[Smaller Lighter Higher Density]
    Q1 -->|Mixed Tech| MIX[Hybrid: THT and SMT on Same PCB]
    MIX --> MX1[Selective Solder or Pin in Paste]
    MX1 --> MX2[Sequential Assembly Order]
```

### 4.4 Defect Diagnostic Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Def[Detected Defect] --> Type{Defect Type}
    Type -->|Geometric| Geo[Tombstone Bridge Billboard]
    Type -->|Metallurgical| Met[Cold Joint Void HIP]
    Type -->|Process| Proc[Solder Ball Lifted Pad]
    Geo --> G1[Check Stencil Alignment and Pad Design]
    Met --> M1[Check Profile and Atmosphere]
    Proc --> P1[Check Handling and Rework Method]
    G1 --> Fix[Apply Corrective Action]
    M1 --> Fix
    P1 --> Fix
    Fix --> Verify[Re Inspect and Re Test]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between Through-Hole Technology (THT) and Surface Mount Technology (SMT).*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Component mounting method:** In THT, component leads pass through drilled plated holes in the PCB, whereas in SMT, components are mounted directly onto surface pads without drilled holes.
2. **Soldering process:** THT primarily uses wave soldering or hand soldering, while SMT uses reflow soldering of solder paste deposited by stencil printing.
3. **Component density & electrical performance:** SMT allows significantly higher component density, smaller footprints, and reduced parasitic inductance (shorter lead lengths), making it superior for high-frequency circuits, whereas THT offers superior mechanical strength for high-power or high-vibration applications.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the function of a solder paste inspection (SPI) system in an SMT line?*

**Model Answer:**
A Solder Paste Inspection (SPI) system is a 3-D optical measurement station placed immediately after stencil printing. It measures the **volume, height, area, and registration offset** of every deposited solder paste deposit. Its function is to detect printing defects (insufficient paste, bridging, smearing, misregistration) *before* component placement, since 60 – 70 % of all SMT defects originate from the printing process. Catching these defects early reduces rework cost and improves overall yield.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **Explain the four zones of a reflow soldering thermal profile with a neat sketch. Discuss why the time above liquidus (TAL) is critical for a good solder joint. (7 marks)**

(b) **Describe the major defects encountered in SMT assembly — tombstoning, solder bridging, and head-in-pillow — explaining their root causes and corrective actions. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

**The four zones of a reflow soldering profile are:**

**Zone 1 — Preheat (150 °C – 180 °C, 60 s – 120 s):** The PCB is gradually heated to activate the flux (rosin becomes active above ~150 °C) and to drive off volatile solvents from the solder paste. A controlled ramp rate of **1 °C/s – 3 °C/s** prevents thermal shock and avoids splattering of the paste.

**Zone 2 — Soak / Thermal Equalization (180 °C – 200 °C, 60 s – 120 s):** The temperature is held relatively constant to allow the entire board — including large thermal-mass components like BGAs and QFNs — to reach uniform temperature. This prevents ΔT-driven defects such as tombstoning of small chips and HIP on BGAs.

**Zone 3 — Reflow (235 °C – 245 °C peak, TAL 45 s – 90 s):** The temperature rises above the solder's liquidus point (217 °C for SAC305). The solder melts, wets the copper pads and component terminations, and forms intermetallic bonds (Cu₆Sn₅, Cu₃Sn). The **Time Above Liquidus (TAL)** is the duration the solder remains molten and is critical because:
- **Too short TAL** (< 30 s) → incomplete wetting, weak fillets, poor intermetallic formation, open joints.
- **Too long TAL** (> 90 s) → excessive intermetallic growth (brittle), flux exhaustion, oxidation, pad lift.
- The industry standard is **TAL = 45 s – 90 s** for lead-free SAC alloys.

**Zone 4 — Cooling (3 °C/s – 6 °C/s):** Controlled solidification. Faster cooling produces a finer grain microstructure (stronger joints), but rates above **6 °C/s** cause thermal shock. Cooling continues until the board reaches a safe handling temperature (≤ 100 °C).

**Neat sketch of the profile:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Temperature] --> Y[Time]
    subgraph Profile[Reflow Profile]
        P1[Preheat Ramp] --> P2[Soak Plateau]
        P2 --> P3[Reflow Peak above Liquidus]
        P3 --> P4[Cooling Ramp]
    end
```

**Why TAL is critical (summary):** TAL controls the balance between complete metallurgical wetting/bonding and avoidance of excessive intermetallic growth — both extremes lead to reliability failures during field thermal cycling.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**1. Tombstoning (Manhattan Effect):**
- **Visual signature:** One end of a small passive component (e.g., 0402 resistor) lifts off the pad, standing vertically like a tombstone.
- **Root cause:** Uneven wetting forces on the two terminations. Causes include asymmetric pad sizes, unequal solder paste volume, board warpage, or differential thermal mass. The first pad to wet pulls the component upright.
- **Corrective action:** Use balanced land patterns (IPC-7351), ensure equal paste volume via good stencil design, reduce ramp rate, use nitrogen atmosphere, reflow profile with proper soak time.

**2. Solder Bridging:**
- **Visual signature:** Unintended solder connection between two adjacent pads/leads, creating an electrical short.
- **Root cause:** Excessive solder paste deposition, misaligned stencil, clogged stencil apertures, poor gasketing between stencil and PCB, or excessive reflow time causing solder to slump.
- **Corrective action:** Clean stencil regularly, use proper squeegee pressure (1 – 10 kg/cm²), inspect stencil-to-PCB gap, use solder mask dams between fine-pitch pads, post-reflow AOI for detection.

**3. Head-in-Pillow (HIP):**
- **Visual signature:** BGA ball appears to be resting on the solder paste deposit but the two have not coalesced into a single fillet — like a head resting on a pillow. Detected by X-ray.
- **Root cause:** Oxidation of the BGA ball surface (often due to multiple reflow cycles or long storage), warpage of the BGA package or PCB, or insufficient TAL allowing the paste to solidify around the ball.
- **Corrective action:** Use nitrogen reflow atmosphere, store BGAs in dry cabinets (MSL handling per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033), pre-bake components if necessary, optimize TAL to 60 s – 90 s, and use pre-formed solder balls with low oxidation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **Describe the step-by-step process flow of an SMT assembly line, with the function of each station. (7 marks)**

(b) **Compare THT wave soldering with SMT reflow soldering in terms of process, parameters, joint quality, and application. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (a)

The SMT assembly line consists of the following sequential stations:

1. **Bare PCB Incoming Inspection:** Visual and electrical checks of the unpopulated board for correct artwork, plating quality, and absence of warpage (> 0.7 % of diagonal per IPC-A-600).

2. **Solder Paste Printing:** A laser-cut, electropolished stainless steel stencil is aligned to the PCB. A squeegee (metal or polyurethane, 45° – 60° angle) pushes lead-free solder paste (e.g., SAC305) across the stencil, depositing precise volumes onto each pad.

3. **Solder Paste Inspection (SPI):** 3-D optical triangulation sensors measure the height, volume, area, and X-Y offset of every deposit. Process Control limits (e.g., 100 % ± 30 % volume) are monitored in real time via SPC charts.

4. **Component Placement (Pick-and-Place):** SMDs are picked from reels/trays/tubes by vacuum nozzles and placed onto the paste deposits with vision-guided accuracy (± 0.05 mm). High-speed mounters handle chips; fine-pitch mounters handle QFPs and BGAs.

5. **Pre-Reflow AOI (optional):** Detects missing, misaligned, or wrong-orientation parts before irreversible soldering.

6. **Reflow Oven:** The board traverses four temperature zones (Preheat, Soak, Reflow, Cooling) in a convection oven with N₂ atmosphere. Solder paste melts, wets pads and leads, and forms permanent joints.

7. **Post-Reflow AOI:** Detects soldering defects — bridging, insufficient solder, tombstones, solder balls, polarity errors.

8. **X-Ray Inspection:** Mandatory for BGA, QFN, and CSP packages where joints are hidden under the component. Detects voids, HIP, missing balls.

9. **In-Circuit Test (ICT) / Flying Probe:** Bed-of-nails or flying probe verifies each net is correctly connected with no shorts or opens. Component values are checked where possible.

10. **Rework Station:** Defective units are reworked using hot-air pencil, micro-soldering iron, or BGA rework station with localized reflow.

11. **Conformal Coating & Final Test:** Acrylic, urethane, or silicone coating for environmental protection. Final functional circuit test (FCT) confirms the assembly meets the bill-of-material spec.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (b)

| Parameter | THT Wave Soldering | SMT Reflow Soldering |
|---|---|---|
| **Solder form** | Bulk molten solder in a pot | Pre-deposited solder paste on pads |
| **Board side** | Solder side (bottom) | Component side (top, on pads) |
| **Process flow** | Flux → pre-heat → dual wave → cool | Paste print → place → reflow oven |
| **Peak temperature** | 245 °C – 260 °C | 235 °C – 245 °C (lead-free) |
| **Solder joint type** | Through-hole barrel fill + fillet | Surface fillet only |
| **Joint strength** | High (mechanical anchoring in PTH) | Lower (relies on pad adhesion) |
| **Component density** | Low (drilled holes need space) | High (no holes) |
