| **Typical defects** | Icicles, webbing, blowholes, insufficient hole fill | Tombstoning, bridging, voids, HIP |
| **Inspection method** | Visual, AOI from solder side | AOI, X-ray, SPI |
| **Best application** | Connectors, transformers, power devices, large electrolytic caps | High-density digital, RF, microcontrollers, passives |
| **Equipment cost** | Moderate | High (line cost several crores) |
| **Suitability for HDI** | Poor | Excellent |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often confuse **reflow soldering** (SMT, used for surface joints) with **wave soldering** (THT, used for through-hole). Examiners deduct marks for this interchange.
> 2. Do not write *"SMT is always better than THT"*. Each has a domain. THT is preferred for high-vibration, high-current, or high-mechanical-stress applications. Examiners reward *nuanced answers*.
> 3. In reflow profile questions, always specify **numerical temperatures** (e.g., 217 °C liquidus, 245 °C peak, 6 °C/s max cooling). Generic terms like "high temperature" fetch partial credit only.
> 4. Tombstoning is a *wetting-force* defect, not a *temperature* defect — root cause analysis must mention asymmetric wetting, not just "too hot".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU OEEVT612 Module 2 — Assembly of Components**

- ✅ **THT = Through-Hole Technology** (leads go through PTH, wave-soldered, mechanically strong).
- ✅ **SMT = Surface Mount Technology** (SMDs on pads, reflow-soldered with paste, high density).
- ✅ **SMT line sequence:** Print → SPI → Place → AOI → Reflow → AOI → X-Ray → ICT → Test.
- ✅ **Reflow profile 4 zones:** Preheat (150–180 °C) → Soak (180–200 °C) → Reflow (235–245 °C) → Cool (≤ 6 °C/s).
- ✅ **SAC305 liquidus = 217 °C; eutectic Sn-Pb liquidus = 183 °C.**
- ✅ **TAL (Time Above Liquidus) = 45 s – 90 s** for lead-free reflow.
- ✅ **Stencil Area Ratio ≥ 0.66** for reliable paste release; Aspect Ratio (W/t) ≥ 1.5.
- ✅ **Stencil thickness typical:** 0.10 mm – 0.15 mm for fine-pitch; 0.20 mm for through-hole / large parts.
- ✅ **Pick-and-place accuracy:** ± 0.05 mm (modern high-end lines).
- ✅ **Common defects:** Tombstoning, Bridging, Solder Balling, Voids, Head-in-Pillow, Cold Joint, Lifted Pad, Billboarding.
- ✅ **ESD protection:** ANSI/ESD S20.20, wrist-strap ~1 MΩ, ionization, grounded bench.
- ✅ **Inspection tools:** AOI (visible defects), SPI (paste), X-Ray (hidden joints — BGA/QFN), ICT (electrical), FCT (functional).
- ✅ **Standards to memorize for KTU:** IPC-A-610 (acceptability), IPC-7351 (land patterns), IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033 (moisture sensitivity), IPC-7711/7721 (rework).
- ✅ **Head-in-Pillow** is detected by **X-ray only**, not by optical AOI.
- ✅ **Wave soldering** uses two waves: turbulent chip wave (for hole fill) + smooth lambda wave (for anti-bridging).
- ✅ **Cooling rate limit = 6 °C/s** to avoid thermal shock; faster = finer grains (stronger) but riskier.
- ✅ **Nitrogen atmosphere** in reflow reduces dross and improves wetting for OSP/ENIG pads.

> **End of Module 2 — Assembly of Components Notes**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme OEEVT612 — Electronic Hardware for Engineers.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to PCB Design using PROTEUS Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to PCB Design using PROTEUS Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Printed Circuit Board (PCB)?

A **Printed Circuit Board (PCB)** is a flat, laminated board made of insulating substrate (typically **FR-4**, a fiberglass-reinforced epoxy) with conductive copper traces etched or deposited on its surface. It mechanically supports and electrically interconnects electronic components using **copper pads**, **traces**, **vias**, and **planes** in place of traditional point-to-point wiring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A PCB is the *physical backbone* of any modern electronic hardware. It replaces fragile, bulky, hand-soldered wiring with a standardized, repeatable, and manufacturable interconnection medium.

The three foundational structures of any PCB are:

1. **Substrate (Dielectric Base)** — provides mechanical rigidity and electrical isolation (FR-4, CEM-1, Rogers, Polyimide).
2. **Conductive Layer (Copper Foil)** — typically **1 oz/ft² (≈ 35 µm thick)** for standard boards, forming signal, power, and ground planes.
3. **Soldermask & Silkscreen** — the green coating that prevents solder bridges, and the white text overlay for component reference designators.

## 1.2 What is PROTEUS?

**PROTEUS Design Suite** (by Labcenter Electronics) is a fully integrated **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** tool that combines **schematic capture**, **SPICE-based circuit simulation**, and **PCB layout design** in a single environment.

It consists of two principal modules:

| Module | Function |
|---|---|
| **ISIS** (Intelligent Schematic Input System) | Schematic capture + real-time mixed-mode simulation (analog + digital + microcontroller) |
| **ARES** (Advanced Routing and Editing Software) | PCB layout, autorouting, 3D visualization, and Gerber file generation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PROTEUS over alternatives?**
> Unlike OrCAD or Altium, PROTEUS uniquely bundles **microcontroller simulation** (8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex) with the schematic entry — meaning you can write firmware, simulate a complete firmware-hardware loop, and *then* lay out the PCB. This is a major reason it is prescribed in the KTU 2024 Electronics Hardware syllabus.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Planning" View

Think of a PCB as a **planned city**:

- The **substrate** is the *ground/terrain* of the city.
- The **components** (resistors, ICs, connectors) are the *buildings* — each with a defined function and entry/exit (pins).
- The **copper traces** are the *roads* connecting buildings — they must be wide enough for the "traffic" (current) they carry.
- The **vias** are *tunnels or flyovers* that take traffic from one road layer to another.
- The **ground plane** is the *city's foundation* — a continuous sheet of copper that gives every signal a stable return path.
- **PROTEUS (ISIS + ARES)** is the *city planner's CAD workstation* — you draw the buildings first (schematic), simulate traffic flow, and then place roads, tunnels, and zones (PCB layout).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Schematic = *logical connectivity* ("what connects to what"). PCB Layout = *physical geometry* ("where the wire physically runs, on which layer, with what width"). PROTEUS handles both seamlessly.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Standard copper weight | **1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm (1.4 mil)** |
| Standard substrate | **FR-4**, dielectric constant εᵣ ≈ **4.3 – 4.5** |
| Default trace width | **10 mil (0.254 mm)** for signal, **20–40 mil** for power |
| Minimum via drill | **0.3 mm (12 mil)** typical for hobby/educational |
| Standard grid | **2.54 mm (100 mil)** through-hole pitch |
| Soldermask thickness | **20–35 µm** |
| Operating temperature | **−40 °C to +85 °C** (commercial grade) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PCB Design Workflow in PROTEUS

The complete PCB design cycle in PROTEUS follows a **top-down hierarchical flow**. Every step is causally linked to the next, and skipping a step typically causes routing or manufacturing failures.

### Stage 1 — Schematic Capture (ISIS)
1. Place components from the **Pick from Libraries (P)** dialog.
2. Wire components using the **Place Wire Mode (F3)**.
3. Assign **net labels** for power rails (VCC, GND) and off-sheet connections.
4. Annotate component references (U1, R1, C1) via **Tools → Annotation → Annotate Schematic**.
5. Run **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** under *Tools → ERC* to detect unconnected pins, power shorts, and un-driven inputs.

### Stage 2 — Netlist Generation
- A **netlist** is a machine-readable text file listing every component, its pins, and the electrical nets they share.
- In PROTEUS, this is generated *implicitly* by the schematic database and passed to ARES when you click *"Netlist to ARES"*.

### Stage 3 — Component Packaging (Footprint Mapping)
- Each schematic symbol must be linked to a **PCB footprint** (land pattern) — e.g., a `RES40` symbol gets a `1206` or `AXIAL-0.4` footprint.
- Mismatched footprints cause **floating pads** during routing.

### Stage 4 — PCB Layout (ARES)
1. **Set board outline** — define the Keep-Out region.
2. **Place components** — drag from netlist, snap to grid.
3. **Define layer stackup** — top copper, bottom copper, silkscreen, soldermask, drill.
4. **Route traces** — manual (T-mode) or **Auto-router** (Tools → Auto Router).
5. **Pour copper zones** — power and ground planes (Place → Zone Mode).
6. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** — Tools → DRC to verify clearances, widths, and un-routed nets.

### Stage 5 — Manufacturing Output
- **Gerber Files** (RS-274X format) → sent to the fabrication house.
- **NC Drill File** → for CNC drilling of vias and through-holes.
- **Bill of Materials (BOM)** → generated via *Tools → Bill of Materials*.

## 2.2 PCB Layer Stackup — Conceptual Breakdown

A typical **2-layer PCB** has the following physical stack:

| Layer (Top → Bottom) | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Silkscreen (Top) | Component outlines, reference designators (white ink) |
| Soldermask (Top) | Green coating; exposes only pads |
| Top Copper | Signal/power routing (35 µm Cu) |
| Substrate (FR-4) | Mechanical dielectric (εᵣ ≈ 4.4, thickness 1.6 mm typical) |
| Bottom Copper | Ground plane / additional routing |
| Soldermask (Bottom) | Bottom-side protection |
| Silkscreen (Bottom) | Bottom-side labels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FR-4?** It offers an excellent balance of *mechanical strength*, *thermal stability* (Tg ≥ 130 °C), *low cost*, and *consistent dielectric constant*. High-frequency (RF/microwave) designs use **Rogers RO4003C** or **PTFE** substrates for lower loss.

## 2.3 PROTEUS — Key Toolbars and Menus (Module-Wise)

| Toolbar / Menu | Hotkey | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Component Mode | **P** | Open component library picker |
| Wire Mode | **F3** | Place electrical connections |
| Bus Mode | **F4** | Place multi-wire data buses |
| Rotate / Mirror | **Ctrl+M / Ctrl+N** | Component orientation |
| Zoom Region | **F6** | Zoom to selected rectangle |
| Annotation | — | Auto-assign R1, R2... U1, U2... |
| ERC | — | Electrical rule check |
| Netlist to ARES | — | Transfer to PCB layout |
| Zone Mode | — | Copper pour (ground/power plane) |
| Track Mode | — | Manual trace routing |
| Auto-Router | — | Automatic trace routing |
| 3D Visualizer | — | Switch from 2D layout to 3D board view |

## 2.4 Design Rule Categories

PROTEUS enforces design rules across three domains:

- **Clearance** — minimum spacing between adjacent copper features (default 10 mil).
- **Width** — minimum/maximum trace width (default 10 mil minimum).
- **Hole Size** — minimum via/pad drill diameter (default 0.6 mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board exams, remember that the **netlist is the single source of truth** between ISIS and ARES. Any schematic edit invalidates the existing PCB layout — you must re-transfer the netlist.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trace resistance | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | ρ = resistivity (Ω·m), L = length (m), A = area (m²) |
| 2 | Trace area | $A = w \cdot t$ | w = width (m), t = thickness (m) |
| 3 | IPC-2152 current capacity (external) | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | k = 0.048 (external), 0.024 (internal) |
| 4 | Cross-section from current | $A = \left(\dfrac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}}\right)^{1/0.725}$ | A in mil² |
| 5 | Capacitance per unit length (microstrip) | $C = \dfrac{0.67 \cdot (\varepsilon_r + 1.41)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)}$ | h, w, t in mils, C in pF/inch |
| 6 | Characteristic impedance (microstrip, approx.) | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t}\right)$ | Z₀ in Ω |
| 7 | Sheet resistance of 1 oz Cu | $R_{sq} \approx 0.5\ \text{m}\Omega/\square$ | per square of 35 µm Cu |
| 8 | Voltage drop on a trace | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{trace}$ | Useful for power net checks |
| 9 | Track width (rule of thumb) | $w \approx 10\ \text{mil per amp}$ for $10\ °C$ rise | Engineering approximation |
| 10 | Via current (typical) | $I_{via} \approx 0.5\ \text{A}$ per via (0.3 mm drill) | Conservative design margin |

> [!TIP]
> In board exams, KTU examiners frequently test **trace resistance**, **sheet resistance**, and the **IPC current-carrying** formula. Memorize the constants ρ_Cu = 1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m and the 0.5 mΩ/□ sheet resistance of 1 oz copper.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics** — Smartphones, IoT sensors — use 6–10 layer HDI (High-Density Interconnect) PCBs designed in tools like Altium, but the **conceptual workflow** learned in PROTEUS is identical.
- **Automotive ECUs** — need wide power traces (40–80 mil) carrying amps; engineers use the IPC-2152 formula in PROTEUS/Altium's constraint manager.
- **Aerospace & Medical** — demand controlled impedance (50 Ω single-ended, 90–100 Ω differential) for high-speed signals — designed using the microstrip equations above.
- **Educational / Hobby** — PROTEUS's ability to simulate the *firmware + hardware* loop makes it a de-facto teaching tool in KTU-affiliated colleges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section performs the **full algebraic derivations** a KTU examiner may require. Every substitution and numeric conversion is shown explicitly — no steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resistance of a Copper PCB Trace

### Statement
Derive the DC resistance of a rectangular copper trace of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ on a PCB.

### Step 1 — Start with the fundamental resistance law
The electrical resistance of a uniform conductor is given by:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity of the conductor material, $L$ is the length along the direction of current flow, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the current.

### Step 2 — Substitute the cross-sectional area of a trace
For a PCB trace, the cross-section is rectangular:

$$A = w \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Combine both equations
Substituting $A = w \cdot t$ into the resistance law:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{w \cdot t}$$

### Step 4 — Insert the resistivity of copper
For annealed copper at 20 °C:

$$\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$$

### Step 5 — Express thickness in SI units for 1 oz/ft² copper
A **1 oz/ft²** copper layer is 35 µm thick:

$$t = 35\ \mu\text{m} = 35 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

### Step 6 — Derive the convenient *per-square* form
Since $R$ is proportional to $L/(w \cdot t)$, for a *square* of copper ($L = w$) the resistance becomes:

$$R_{\square} = \frac{\rho}{t} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8}}{35 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.926 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega \approx 0.5\ \text{m}\Omega$$

### Step 7 — Generalised trace resistance formula
For any rectangular trace:

$$R_{trace} = R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{w} = 0.5\ \text{m}\Omega \cdot \frac{L}{w}$$

where $L$ and $w$ are in identical units (e.g., both in mils or both in mm).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Voltage Drop on a 5 V Power Trace

> **Given:** A 1 oz copper trace, 100 mm long, 0.5 mm wide, carrying 500 mA.
> **Find:** The DC voltage drop and power dissipation.

### Step 1 — Convert dimensions to SI

$$L = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.1\ \text{m}, \quad w = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}, \quad t = 35\ \mu\text{m} = 35 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area

$$A = w \cdot t = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (35 \times 10^{-6}) = 17.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Apply the resistance formula

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot 0.1}{17.5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-9}}{17.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0985\ \Omega$$

### Step 4 — Voltage drop by Ohm's Law

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R = 0.5 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.0493\ \text{V} \approx 49\ \text{mV}$$

### Step 5 — Power dissipation in the trace

$$P_{trace} = I^2 \cdot R = (0.5)^2 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.0246\ \text{W} \approx 25\ \text{mW}$$

> [!TIP]
> A 49 mV drop is acceptable for a 5 V rail (≈ 1 % loss), but for a **3.3 V rail** the same drop would be 1.5 % and for **1.8 V** it would be 2.7 % — which is why *power traces must be made wider* for low-voltage, high-current rails.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — IPC-2152 Trace Width for a Given Current

### Statement
Determine the required trace width to carry $I = 2\ \text{A}$ with a permitted temperature rise of $\Delta T = 10\ °C$ on an **external** 1 oz copper layer.

### Step 1 — State the IPC-2152 empirical equation
For external conductors:

$$I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

with $k = 0.048$ and $A$ in mil².

### Step 2 — Solve for the required cross-sectional area $A$
Rearranging:

$$A = \left( \frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} \right)^{1/0.725}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values
Compute $\Delta T^{0.44}$:

$$\Delta T^{0.44} = 10^{0.44} = 2.754$$

Compute the denominator:

$$k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} = 0.048 \cdot 2.754 = 0.1322$$

Compute the bracketed ratio:

$$\frac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} = \frac{2}{0.1322} = 15.13$$

### Step 4 — Raise to the reciprocal power $1/0.725 = 1.3793$

$$A = (15.13)^{1.3793} \approx 39.5\ \text{mil}^2$$

### Step 5 — Convert to trace width
For 1 oz copper (thickness = 1.4 mil):

$$w = \frac{A}{t} = \frac{39.5}{1.4} \approx 28.2\ \text{mil} \approx 0.72\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering verdict
A standard **30 mil (0.76 mm)** trace would satisfy the requirement with margin. PROTEUS allows this constraint to be entered under **Design Rules → Minimum Track Width**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Microstrip Characteristic Impedance (Approximate)

### Statement
For a microstrip line on FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$), with $w = 20\ \text{mil}$ and $h = 10\ \text{mil}$ (dielectric thickness), find the approximate $Z_0$.

### Step 1 — Use the empirical IPC / Wheeler formula

$$Z_0 \approx \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\left( \frac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$

$$\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41} = \sqrt{4.4 + 1.41} = \sqrt{5.81} = 2.410$$

### Step 3 — Compute the leading factor

$$\frac{87}{2.410} = 36.10\ \Omega$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm argument with $t = 1.4\ \text{mil}$

$$\frac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t} = \frac{5.98 \cdot 10}{0.8 \cdot 20 + 1.4} = \frac{59.8}{17.4} = 3.437$$

### Step 5 — Take the natural log

$$\ln(3.437) = 1.235$$

### Step 6 — Multiply

$$Z_0 \approx 36.10 \cdot 1.235 = 44.6\ \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For USB (90 Ω differential) and Ethernet (100 Ω differential) the geometry must be tuned — typically a *wider* trace with *thinner* dielectric. This derivation justifies why KTU-level PCB questions rarely go into impedance control; the focus is on the **conceptual formula and substitution discipline**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Given | Find | Key Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power trace sizing | $I = 2\ \text{A}, \Delta T = 10\ °C$, external 1 oz | Trace width | IPC-2152 (Derivation 2) |
| Voltage drop check | $L = 50\ \text{mm}, w = 0.3\ \text{mm}, I = 1\ \text{A}$ | $V_{drop}$ | $R = \rho L / A$ |
| Sheet resistance | 1 oz Cu | $R_{sq}$ | $\rho / t$ |
| Ground plane area | 50 mm × 50 mm, 1 oz | Total resistance | $R_{\square} \cdot L/w$ |
| Trace current for $\Delta T$ | $w = 50\ \text{mil}, t = 1.4\ \text{mil}$ | $I$ allowable | IPC-2152 inverted |
| Via sizing | $I = 1\ \text{A}$ through 0.3 mm via | Plating area & current capacity | $I_{via} \approx 0.5\ \text{A}/via$ (parallel vias needed) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PROTEUS PCB Design Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Start: Circuit Idea] --> A1[ISIS: Schematic Capture]
    A1 --> A2[Place Components from Library]
    A2 --> A3[Wire Connections with F3]
    A3 --> A4[Annotate Reference Designators]
    A4 --> A5{Electrical Rule Check ERC}
    A5 -->|Pass| A6[Netlist Generation]
    A5 -->|Fail| A2
    A6 --> B0[ARES: PCB Layout Editor]
    B0 --> B1[Set Board Outline and KeepOut]
    B1 --> B2[Import Components from Netlist]
    B2 --> B3[Place Components on Grid]
    B3 --> B4[Define Layer Stackup]
    B4 --> B5[Route Traces Manual or Auto]
    B5 --> B6[Pour Copper Zones GND VCC]
    B6 --> B7{Design Rule Check DRC}
    B7 -->|Pass| B8[Generate Gerber Files]
    B7 -->|Fail| B5
    B8 --> B9[Generate NC Drill File]
    B9 --> B10[Generate Bill of Materials]
    B10 --> C0[Send to PCB Fabrication House]
    C0 --> C1[Fabricated Bare PCB]
    C1 --> C2[Component Assembly and Soldering]
    C2 --> C3[Final Hardware Ready for Testing]
```

    end

    ISIS -->|Netlist| ARES

    SIM --> VSM

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Printed Circuit Board and list any four advantages of using PCBs over conventional point-to-point wiring. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Printed Circuit Board (PCB)** is a flat, rigid board made of an insulating substrate (typically FR-4) with conductive copper tracks etched on one or both sides, used to mechanically support and electrically interconnect electronic components.

**Four advantages over point-to-point wiring:**

1. **Compactness** — components are placed with high density, enabling miniaturization.
2. **Repeatability & Manufacturability** — identical boards can be mass-produced from a single design file.
3. **Reliability** — soldered joints are mechanically and electrically stable; fewer wiring errors.
4. **Electrical Performance** — controlled impedance, reduced crosstalk, and a continuous ground plane minimise EMI/noise.
5. *(Any other valid advantage such as ease of testing, repair, and cost at scale.)*

---

### Q2. List the two main modules of PROTEUS Design Suite and state the function of each. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**

| Module | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ISIS** | Intelligent Schematic Input System | Schematic capture, SPICE-based mixed-mode (analog + digital + microcontroller) circuit simulation |
| **ARES** | Advanced Routing and Editing Software | PCB layout, manual and auto-routing, copper pouring, 3D visualization, and Gerber file generation |

These two modules are tightly integrated — the **netlist** generated by ISIS serves as the input for ARES, ensuring the *as-designed* and *as-laid-out* boards remain electrically consistent.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete step-by-step workflow of PCB design using PROTEUS Design Suite, mentioning the roles of ISIS and ARES. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a 1 oz copper PCB trace 80 mm long and 0.4 mm wide carrying 1 A, calculate the (i) DC resistance, (ii) voltage drop, and (iii) power dissipated. Take ρ_Cu = 1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m and t = 35 µm. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Workflow Explanation**

The PROTEUS PCB design workflow consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Schematic Capture (ISIS)** — The designer opens ISIS, selects components from the libraries (resistors, ICs, connectors, microcontrollers), places them on the sheet, and uses **Wire Mode (F3)** to make electrical connections. Net labels are used for off-page and power connections.
2. **Annotation** — Tools → Annotation auto-assigns reference designators (R1, R2, C1, U1, U2).
3. **Electrical Rule Check (ERC)** — Detects unconnected pins, power shorts, and floating inputs.
4. **Netlist Generation** — A machine-readable connectivity file is generated internally.
5. **PCB Layout (ARES)** — The netlist is loaded into ARES. The board outline is defined in the Keep-Out layer; components are placed on a 2.54 mm (or 1.27 mm for SMD) grid.
6. **Routing** — Critical nets are routed manually in **T-mode**; remaining nets are routed using the **Auto Router** under Tools → Auto Router.
7. **Copper Pour (Zone Mode)** — Ground and power planes are created by pouring copper over unused regions.
8. **Design Rule Check (DRC)** — Verifies clearance, track width, and via sizes against the defined design rules.
9. **Manufacturing Output** — Gerber files (RS-274X), NC drill file, and BOM are generated and sent for fabrication.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** L = 80 mm = 0.08 m, w = 0.4 mm = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m, t = 35 µm = 35 × 10⁻⁶ m, I = 1 A.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area**
$$A = w \cdot t = (4 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (35 \times 10^{-6}) = 14 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — DC resistance**
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} = \frac{(1.724 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot 0.08}{14 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.379 \times 10^{-9}}{14 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0985\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop**
$$V_{drop} = I \cdot R = 1 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.0985\ \text{V} \approx 98.5\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipated**
$$P = I^2 \cdot R = (1)^2 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.0985\ \text{W} \approx 98.5\ \text{mW}$$

**Result:** R ≈ 0.0985 Ω, V_drop ≈ 98.5 mV, P ≈ 98.5 mW. ✓

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the following PROTEUS/PCB concepts with diagrams where necessary: (i) Netlist, (ii) Footprint, (iii) Via, (iv) Soldermask, (v) Silkscreen, (vi) Ground plane, (vii) DRC. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2-layer PCB carries 3 A on an external 1 oz copper trace with a permitted temperature rise of 15 °C. Using the IPC-2152 empirical formula, determine the required cross-sectional area (in mil²) and the corresponding trace width in mm. Take k = 0.048 for external layers and copper thickness = 1.4 mil. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Concept Definitions**

1. **Netlist** — A structured text file listing every component, its pins, and the electrical nets (connection groups) they share. Generated by ISIS, consumed by ARES.
2. **Footprint** — The physical land pattern of a component on the PCB (e.g., SOIC-8, 0805, DIP-14). Each schematic symbol must be mapped to a footprint.
3. **Via** — A plated through-hole used to route a signal from one copper layer to another. A *blind via* connects only outer-to-inner, and a *buried via* connects only inner layers.
4. **Soldermask** — The green epoxy coating that covers copper traces, leaving only pads exposed. Prevents solder bridges during assembly.
5. **Silkscreen** — White text/legend layer used to print component outlines, reference designators, and logos.
6. **Ground Plane** — A continuous sheet of copper (typically the entire bottom layer) connected to GND, providing a low-impedance return path and EMI shielding.
7. **DRC (Design Rule Check)** — PROTEUS's automated verifier that checks clearances, track widths, via sizes, and un-routed connections against the rule set.

**Part (b) — IPC-2152 Calculation**

**Given:** I = 3 A, ΔT = 15 °C, external layer, k = 0.048, t = 1.4 mil.

**Step 1 — Compute ΔT⁰·⁴⁴**
$$\Delta T^{0.44} = 15^{0.44} = 3.305$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator**
$$k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} = 0.048 \cdot 3.305 = 0.1586$$

**Step 3 — Compute ratio I / (k·ΔT⁰·⁴⁴)**
$$\frac{3}{0.1586} = 18.92$$

**Step 4 — Raise to 1/0.725 = 1.3793**
$$A = (18.92)^{1.3793} \approx 51.4\ \text{mil}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute width**
$$w = \frac{A}{t} = \frac{51.4}{1.4} = 36.7\ \text{mil}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm**
$$w = 36.7\ \text{mil} \cdot 0.0254\ \text{mm/mil} = 0.932\ \text{mm}$$

**Result:** Required cross-section ≈ **51.4 mil²**, required trace width ≈ **0.93 mm (≈ 37 mil)**. A standard **1 mm (≈ 40 mil)** trace would be used in practice with margin. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in PROTEUS/PCB Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing mm and mil in the IPC-2152 formula. The formula expects A in **mil²**; convert carefully. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting t in cross-section** — Many students compute $R = \rho L / w$ (incorrect). Always include the thickness t. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong k value** — Using k = 0.048 for internal layers or vice versa. Internal = 0.024, External = 0.048. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing Schematic with PCB layout** — In a workflow question, examiners expect explicit mention of **netlist transfer**. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Missing ERC/DRC distinction** — ERC is performed in ISIS on the *schematic*; DRC is performed in ARES on the *layout*. Examiners check this distinction.
> 6. **No mention of Gerber output** — A workflow answer that ends at "place components" is incomplete; the manufacturing output stage is essential.
> 7. **Reverse calculation in IPC formula** — The exponent is 0.725 *on the area*, not 1/0.725. Solving for A requires the **reciprocal exponent 1/0.725 ≈ 1.38**; examiners commonly deduct a mark if this step is skipped.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PCB** = substrate + copper + soldermask + silkscreen. Standard 1 oz Cu is **35 µm thick**.
- **PROTEUS** = **ISIS** (schematic + simulation) + **ARES** (PCB layout + Gerber). They communicate via the **netlist**.
- **Standard FR-4** substrate: εᵣ ≈ 4.4, thickness 1.6 mm, Tg ≥ 130 °C.
- **Trace resistance** $R = \rho L / (w t)$ with ρ_Cu = 1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m; sheet resistance of 1 oz Cu ≈ **0.5 mΩ/□**.
- **IPC-2152 current capacity**: $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ with k = 0.048 (external) or 0.024 (internal). To find A, raise to the **reciprocal exponent 1/0.725 ≈ 1.379**.
- **Design flow order** (must be in this sequence): *Schematic → Annotation → ERC → Netlist → Board Outline → Component Placement → Routing → Copper Pour → DRC → Gerber/Drill/BOM.*
- **ERC** = schematic check (in ISIS). **DRC** = layout check (in ARES). Never the same.
- **Via current rule of thumb**: ≈ 0.5 A per 0.3 mm via; use multiple parallel vias for higher currents.
- **Footprint mapping** is mandatory before netlist-to-ARES transfer; otherwise floating pads will appear.
- **Standard trace widths**: 10 mil (signal), 20–40 mil (low-current power), ≥ 50 mil (high-current power).
- **Manufacturing output** consists of *Gerber files (RS-274X)*, *NC drill (Excellon)*, and *BOM + Pick-and-Place* files.
- **PROTEUS's unique advantage**: combined schematic simulation with **microcontroller firmware** execution (VSM), enabling pre-layout firmware-hardware co-verification.
- **Power integrity tip**: Wider power traces and a solid ground plane reduce voltage drop and EMI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly of Simple Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly of Simple Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Circuit Assembly** is the systematic, sequential process of mechanically mounting and electrically interconnecting discrete and active electronic components onto a substrate (such as a Printed Circuit Board, breadboard, or perfboard) to form a functional electronic circuit. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEEVT612 syllabus**, assembly of simple circuits encompasses the practical skills of **soldering**, **component placement**, **interconnection**, **testing**, and **debugging** of low-power analog and digital circuits.

In KTU terminology, *simple circuit assembly* refers to circuits operating typically at **voltages below 50 V DC**, **currents below 2 A**, and signal frequencies below **1 MHz** — covering laboratory experiments, hobby projects, and embedded development boards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Assembly of simple circuits involves the physical realization of a schematic diagram into a working hardware prototype. Students are expected to demonstrate hands-on competency in **breadboard prototyping**, **through-hole soldering on general-purpose PCBs (perfboards)**, and **identifying/testing assembled joints**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **assembling an electronic circuit** like **building a Lego house from a blueprint**:

| Lego House | Electronic Circuit Assembly |
|------------|----------------------------|
| Blueprint / plan | Circuit Schematic Diagram |
| Lego base plate | Breadboard / PCB / Perfboard |
| Individual Lego blocks | Resistors, Capacitors, ICs, Transistors |
| Clicking blocks together | Soldering or inserting into breadboard |
| Final inspection | Continuity testing and debugging |
| House utility (shelter) | Functional electronic behaviour |

Just as you cannot build a stable house by randomly placing blocks, you cannot build a working circuit by randomly soldering components. There is a **defined order**, a **defined methodology**, and **defined quality checks** at every stage.

## 1.3 Types of Circuit Assembly Techniques (KTU Scope)

The three primary techniques covered under Module 2 are:

1. **Breadboard Assembly (Solderless Prototyping)** — temporary, reusable, ideal for testing.
2. **Perfboard Assembly (Point-to-Point Soldering)** — semi-permanent, manual soldering on a general-purpose board with isolated copper pads.
3. **PCB Assembly (Printed Circuit Board)** — permanent, professional-grade assembly using etched custom boards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants and Standards Used in Assembly:**
> - **Soldering Iron Tip Temperature:** **300 °C – 350 °C** (for lead-based); **350 °C – 400 °C** (for lead-free SAC305)
> - **Melting Point of 60/40 Sn-Pb Solder:** **183 °C – 190 °C**
> - **Melting Point of Lead-Free (SAC305) Solder:** **217 °C – 221 °C**
> - **IPC-A-610 Standard:** Industry-accepted acceptability standard for electronic assemblies.
> - **Standard Component Lead Pitch:** **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** for through-hole components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assembly Workflow — Structured Logic

The assembly of a simple circuit, as accepted by KTU evaluators, follows this strict logical sequence:

### Step 1: Schematic Interpretation
- Read the circuit schematic carefully.
- Identify all components, their **polarity**, and their **node connections**.
- Create a **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.

### Step 2: Component Verification
- Verify each component value using a **multimeter** (resistance, capacitance) or component tester.
- Inspect for visible damage (cracked ICs, oxidized leads).

### Step 3: Mechanical Layout Planning
- Decide component placement to **minimize lead length** and **avoid cross-overs**.
- Place taller components last to avoid obstruction during soldering.

### Step 4: Mounting
- For breadboard: insert leads into tie-point holes.
- For perfboard/PCB: bend leads, insert through pads, slightly spread on the reverse side.

### Step 5: Soldering
- Heat the **pad and lead simultaneously** (not the solder directly).
- Apply solder to the heated junction — it should **flow and wet** the surfaces.
- Remove solder, then remove the iron. Total contact time: **2–4 seconds per joint**.

### Step 6: Inspection and Cleaning
- Visually inspect for **cold joints**, **bridges**, and **insufficient wetting**.
- Clean flux residue using **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)**.

### Step 7: Testing
- **Continuity test** using multimeter in buzzer mode.
- **Power-on test** with current-limited bench supply.
- **Functional test** against expected output.

## 2.2 Soldering — The Heart of Assembly

**Soldering** is a metallurgical process in which two metal surfaces are joined using a **filler metal (solder)** that melts below **450 °C** without melting the base metals. A proper solder joint is **not a mechanical bond** but a **metallurgical alloy bond** formed between the solder, the component lead, and the pad.

### Types of Soldering Relevant to KTU

- **Through-Hole Soldering (THT):** Component leads pass through drilled holes.
- **Surface Mount Soldering (SMT):** Components placed on PCB pads, reflowed or hand-soldered.
- **Drag Soldering:** Used for multi-pin SMD components like SOIC, QFP packages.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are used in KTU lab viva and written exams for circuit assembly calculations.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|---------------------|--------------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Ohm's Law (Trace Current)** | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Sizing current through PCB trace |
| 2 | **Power Dissipation in Soldering Iron Tip** | $P = V^2 / R$ | W | Selecting appropriate iron wattage (typically **15–60 W** for electronics) |
| 3 | **Heat Energy Required to Melt Solder** | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | J | Estimating thermal demand during soldering |
| 4 | **Trace Resistance per Unit Length** | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | $\Omega$ | Where $\rho$ is resistivity, $L$ is length, $A$ is cross-section area |
| 5 | **Trace Current Capacity (IPC-2152)** | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | A | Empirical relation for internal PCB traces |
| 6 | **Wavelength at Operating Frequency** | $\lambda = c / f$ | m | Determining if transmission line effects matter (rare in simple circuits) |
| 7 | **Voltage Divider (Simple Biasing)** | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | V | Foundation of most simple circuit analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Solder joint reliability directly determines the **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** of any electronic product. In aerospace, automotive, and medical electronics, the assembly process is validated against **IPC-A-610 Class 1, 2, or 3** standards.

## 2.4 Common Defects in Circuit Assembly

| Defect | Visual Appearance | Cause |
|--------|-------------------|-------|
| **Cold Joint** | Dull, grainy, blistered | Insufficient heat |
| **Solder Bridge** | Unwanted connection between two pads | Excess solder |
| **Lifted Pad** | Pad separated from PCB | Excessive heat or force |
| **Insufficient Wetting** | Solder balls up, doesn't spread | Dirty surface or cold iron |
| **Disturbed Joint** | Cracked, fractured | Moved during solidification |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Energy Required to Form a Solder Joint

This derivation is often asked as a **3-mark sub-question** in KTU theory papers. The goal is to compute the heat required to bring the solder from room temperature to its melting point and then melt it.

### Given:
- Mass of solder on a typical joint: $m = 0.05 \, \text{g} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{kg}$
- Specific heat of 60/40 Sn-Pb solder: $c = 167 \, \text{J/(kg·°C)}$
- Latent heat of fusion: $L_f = 4.7 \times 10^4 \, \text{J/kg}$
- Room temperature: $T_1 = 25 \, \text{°C}$
- Melting point: $T_m = 183 \, \text{°C}$
- Working temperature (iron tip): $T_2 = 300 \, \text{°C}$

### Step 1: Heat required to raise solder temperature to melting point

$$Q_1 = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_1 = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 167 \cdot (183 - 25)$$

$$Q_1 = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 167 \cdot 158$$

$$Q_1 = 1.3193 \, \text{J}$$

### Step 2: Heat required to melt the solder (phase change at constant temperature)

$$Q_2 = m \cdot L_f$$

$$Q_2 = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (4.7 \times 10^4)$$

$$Q_2 = 2.35 \, \text{J}$$

### Step 3: Total heat required for a single joint

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2$$

$$Q_{total} = 1.3193 + 2.35$$

$$Q_{total} = 3.6693 \, \text{J} \approx 3.67 \, \text{J}$$

### Conclusion
A single through-hole solder joint requires approximately **3.67 J** of thermal energy. This is the reason a **25 W – 40 W soldering iron** (delivering energy over 2–4 seconds) is more than sufficient for electronics work.

## 3.2 Derivation: PCB Trace Current Capacity

For a KTU design-oriented question, you may be asked to determine if a given PCB trace can safely carry a required current.

### Using the simplified IPC-2152 internal trace equation:

$$I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

### Given:
- Cross-sectional area of trace: $A = 0.1 \, \text{mm}^2$ (approx. 0.2 mm wide, 0.05 mm copper thickness)
- Allowable temperature rise: $\Delta T = 20 \, \text{°C}$
- Empirical constant for internal traces: $k = 0.024$ (approximate, depends on units)

### Step 1: Substitute

$$I = 0.024 \cdot (20)^{0.44} \cdot (0.1)^{0.725}$$

### Step 2: Evaluate temperature term

$$(20)^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln(20)} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.9957} = e^{1.3181} = 3.736$$

### Step 3: Evaluate area term

$$(0.1)^{0.725} = e^{0.725 \cdot \ln(0.1)} = e^{0.725 \cdot (-2.3026)} = e^{-1.6694} = 0.1883$$

### Step 4: Multiply all terms

$$I = 0.024 \cdot 3.736 \cdot 0.1883$$

$$I = 0.01687 \, \text{A} \approx 16.87 \, \text{mA}$$

### Conclusion
A trace of **0.1 mm²** cross-section can carry approximately **16.87 mA** with a **20 °C** temperature rise. For higher currents, the trace width must be increased proportionally.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Assembly Methods

| Parameter | Breadboard | Perfboard | Custom PCB |
|-----------|------------|-----------|------------|
| **Cost** | Low (₹100–₹300) | Low (₹20–₹50) | Medium–High (₹200–₹1000) |
| **Reusability** | Excellent (reusable) | None (one-time) | None (one-time) |
| **Soldering Required** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Suitability for High Frequency** | Poor (parasitic capacitance ~2–5 pF/node) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Durability** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Best Use Case** | Prototyping | Educational projects | Final product |
| **KTU Lab Use** | ✅ Mandatory | ✅ Mandatory | ❌ Optional |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Circuit Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Schematic Diagram] --> B[BOM Preparation]
    B --> C[Component Verification]
    C --> D{Mechanical Mounting}
    D --> E[Breadboard Insertion]
    D --> F[Perfboard Soldering]
    D --> G[Custom PCB Soldering]
    E --> H[Continuity Test]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Defects Found?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Rework and Resolder]
    I -->|No| K[Power-On Test]
    J --> H
    K --> L[Functional Verification]
    L --> M[Final Documentation]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Soldering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> step1[Clean Component Leads and PCB Pads]
    step1 --> step2[Heat Joint with Soldering Iron 300-350°C]
    step2 --> step3[Apply Solder Wire to Joint NOT to Iron Tip]
    step3 --> step4[Solder Melts and Flows Over Pad and Lead]
    step4 --> step5[Wait 2-4 Seconds for Wetting]
    step5 --> step6[Remove Solder Wire First]
    step6 --> step7[Remove Soldering Iron]
    step7 --> step8[Allow Joint to Cool Naturally No Blowing]
    step8 --> inspect{Visual Inspection}
    inspect -->|Shiny Concave| done([Proper Solder Joint])
    inspect -->|Dull or Ball-Shaped| rework[Reheat and Resolder]
    rework --> step2
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Tools Required for Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Tools for Circuit Assembly]
    root --> safety[Safety Equipment]
    root --> soldering[Soldering Tools]
    root --> measurement[Measurement Tools]
    root --> consumables[Consumables]
    
    safety --> s1[Safety Goggles]
    safety --> s2[Anti-static Wrist Strap]
    safety --> s3[Fume Extractor]
    
    soldering --> t1[Soldering Iron 25-40W]
    soldering --> t2[Soldering Station with Temp Control]
    soldering --> t3[Solder Wick and Flux]
    soldering --> t4[Tweezers and Cutters]
    
    measurement --> m1[Digital Multimeter]
    measurement --> m2[Oscilloscope Optional]
    measurement --> m3[LCR Meter]
    
    consumables --> c1[Solder Wire 60/40 or SAC305]
    consumables --> c2[Isopropyl Alcohol IPA]
    consumables --> c3[Breadboard and Jumper Wires]
```

## 4.4 Component Placement Strategy on a Perfboard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Perfboard Top View]
        direction LR
        IC1[IC1 Center Position]
        C1[C1 - Decoupling 0.1µF Near VCC]
        C2[C2 - Bulk 10µF Near Power]
        R1[R1 - Pull-up at Input]
        R2[R2 - Pull-down at Output]
        LED1[LED1 with Series R]
        CON1[CON1 - Input Connector]
        CON2[CON2 - Output Connector]
    end
    IC1 --- C1
    IC1 --- R1
    IC1 --- R2
    IC1 --- LED1
    CON1 --- IC1
    IC1 --- CON2
    C1 --- C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Practice:** Always place the **decoupling capacitor (0.1 µF)** as physically close as possible to the IC's VCC pin — ideally within **2 mm to 5 mm**. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define cold solder joint. How can it be identified and prevented? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **cold solder joint** is a defective solder joint that has been formed at an insufficient temperature, resulting in a poor metallurgical bond. It typically appears **dull, grainy, blistered, or crystalline** rather than smooth and shiny.

**Identification:**
- Dull or rough surface texture
- Convex or ball-shaped rather than concave volcano shape
- High electrical resistance or intermittent connectivity
- Easily disturbed by slight mechanical force

**Prevention:**
- Use a **properly heated iron (300–350 °C)**.
- Ensure both the **pad and component lead** are heated simultaneously.
- Apply **sufficient solder** (typically 2–3 mm wire length).
- Avoid moving the joint during **solidification (3–5 seconds)**.
- Clean oxidized surfaces using **flux** before soldering.

---

### Question 2: List any six tools required for assembling a simple electronic circuit on a perfboard. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The six essential tools are:
1. **Soldering Iron** (25–40 W, temperature-controlled)
2. **Solder Wire** (60/40 Sn-Pb or lead-free SAC305)
3. **Wire Cutters / Flush Cutters**
4. **Needle-Nose Tweezers / Pliers**
5. **Digital Multimeter** (for continuity and voltage testing)
6. **Soldering Stand with Sponge** (for cleaning the iron tip)

Auxiliary items include flux, desoldering wick, anti-static wrist strap, and isopropyl alcohol.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the complete step-by-step process of assembling a simple circuit on a general-purpose PCB (perfboard). Discuss the role of component placement strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The complete assembly process on a perfboard follows a well-defined sequence:

1. **Schematic Study and BOM Preparation:** The schematic is carefully studied to identify all components, their values, polarities, and pin connections. A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is prepared listing each component with its package type and quantity.

2. **Component Verification:** Each component is verified for correct value using a **multimeter or LCR meter**. Polarized components like electrolytic capacitors and diodes are checked for correct orientation.

3. **Mechanical Layout Planning:** A sketch is made showing the physical placement of components. ICs are placed **first and centrally**; smaller components are placed around them. Tall components (electrolytic capacitors) are placed last.

4. **Component Insertion:** Components are inserted from the **non-copper (top) side** of the board. Leads are bent slightly (~30°) using tweezers to hold them in place.

5. **Soldering:** Each joint is heated for **2–4 seconds** with a 300–350 °C iron. Solder is applied to the heated junction, not the iron tip. The joint should appear **shiny and concave (volcano-shaped)**.

6. **Lead Trimming:** After cooling, excess leads are trimmed using flush cutters at ~1 mm above the solder joint.

7. **Inspection and Cleaning:** Joints are visually inspected for defects. Flux residue is cleaned using **isopropyl alcohol and a brush**.

8. **Continuity and Functional Testing:** Each net is tested for continuity, and the circuit is powered on with **current-limited supply** for final validation.

**Role of Component Placement:**
- **Minimizes lead length**, reducing parasitic inductance and noise pickup.
- **Prevents solder bridges** by ensuring adequate spacing between pads.
- **Improves thermal management** by distributing heat-generating components.
- **Facilitates debugging** by leaving test points accessible.

---

**(b)** Calculate the heat energy required to form a single solder joint using 60/40 tin-lead solder. Given: mass of solder = 0.05 g, specific heat = 167 J/(kg·°C), latent heat of fusion = 47 kJ/kg, room temperature = 25 °C, melting point = 183 °C. Also state the minimum soldering iron wattage required to make this joint in 2 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refer to the **derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \\
Q_1 &= (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 167 \cdot (183 - 25) \\
Q_1 &= 1.3193 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= m \cdot L_f \\
Q_2 &= (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 47000 \\
Q_2 &= 2.35 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 = 1.3193 + 2.35 = 3.6693 \, \text{J} \approx 3.67 \, \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Minimum Iron Wattage:**

Assuming all electrical power converts to useful heat and the joint must form in **2 seconds**:

$$P = \frac{Q_{total}}{t} = \frac{3.67}{2} = 1.835 \, \text{W}$$

However, in practice, only about **5–10%** of the iron's power reaches the joint (the rest is lost to conduction through the board and radiation). Therefore, a practical iron wattage of:

$$P_{practical} = \frac{1.835}{0.10} \approx 18.35 \, \text{W}$$

Hence, a **20 W – 25 W soldering iron** is the minimum practical choice. Standard KTU lab irons are rated at **25 W – 40 W**, which provides adequate thermal headroom.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Compare the three major circuit assembly techniques: breadboard, perfboard, and custom PCB. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and suitable application areas. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Breadboard | Perfboard | Custom PCB |
|-----------|------------|-----------|------------|
| **Construction** | Plastic body with spring contacts | Phenolic/FRP board with isolated copper pads | Etched copper tracks on FR4 substrate |
| **Soldering Needed** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Reusability** | High (hundreds of cycles) | None | None |
| **Parasitics** | High (~2–5 pF/node) | Low–Moderate | Lowest (controlled impedance possible) |
| **Cost** | ₹100–₹300 | ₹20–₹50 | ₹200–₹1000+ |
| **Frequency Suitability** | < 1 MHz | < 10 MHz | Up to GHz range |
| **Durability** | Low (mechanical wear) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Best Use Case** | Quick prototyping, lab testing | Educational projects, low-volume production | Final product, mass production |

**Conclusion:** Breadboard is best for **prototyping**, perfboard for **educational and hobby use**, and custom PCB for **final product deployment**.

---

**(b)** What are the most common defects encountered during circuit assembly? Describe any four with their causes and remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four most common assembly defects are:

1. **Cold Joint:** Caused by insufficient heat or premature movement during solidification. Appears dull and grainy. *Remedy:* Reheat the joint to reflow the solder and let it cool undisturbed.

2. **Solder Bridge:** Caused by excess solder or closely spaced pads forming an unintended connection. *Remedy:* Use desoldering wick to absorb excess solder.

3. **Lifted Pad:** Caused by excessive heat or mechanical force pulling the pad away from the PCB substrate. *Remedy:* Use a jumper wire to bridge the broken connection, or repair with epoxy and copper foil for critical boards.

4. **Insufficient Wetting:** Solder does not spread and instead forms a ball on the lead. Caused by **oxidized surfaces** or **cold iron tip**. *Remedy:* Clean the surface with isopropyl alcohol, apply fresh flux, and reheat with a properly tinned iron tip.

Additional defects include **disturbed joint (cracked)**, **blown IC due to reverse polarity**, and **whiskers** in lead-free solder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students **lose 1–2 marks** for not mentioning the **IPC-A-610 standard** when asked about assembly quality.
> - **Forgetting to include flux** in the tools list is a recurring deduction.
> - In derivation questions, **not stating the assumptions** (e.g., 10% heat transfer efficiency) leads to mark loss.
> - Drawing the **process flowchart with arrows in the wrong direction** is a common error.
> - Students often confuse **wave soldering with reflow soldering** — wave is for THT, reflow is for SMT.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- 🔹 **Assembly** is the physical realization of a schematic into a working circuit.
- 🔹 The three primary techniques are **breadboard, perfboard, and custom PCB**.
- 🔹 **Soldering iron temperature:** **300–350 °C** for lead-based, **350–400 °C** for lead-free.
- 🔹 **Joint formation time:** **2–4 seconds** per joint.
- 🔹 A **proper joint** is **shiny, concave, and volcano-shaped**.
- 🔹 A **cold joint** is **dull, convex, and unreliable** — must be reworked.
- 🔹 **Decoupling capacitors (0.1 µF)** must be placed **within 2–5 mm of IC VCC pins**.
- 🔹 Standard through-hole lead pitch: **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)**.
- 🔹 **Anti-static precautions** (wrist strap, ESD-safe mats) are mandatory for CMOS/IC handling.
- 🔹 **IPC-A-610** is the globally accepted standard for electronic assembly acceptance.
- 🔹 Heat energy per joint ≈ **3.67 J** for a 0.05 g solder bump.
- 🔹 Practical iron wattage: **25 W – 40 W** for general electronics work.
- 🔹 **Cleaning flux residue** with **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** prevents long-term corrosion.
- 🔹 Always power-up a newly assembled circuit using a **current-limited bench supply** for safety.
- 🔹 **Continuity test first, then power-on test** — never the reverse.

> 📌 **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked about assembly, always structure your answer in the order — *Schematic → BOM → Verification → Placement → Soldering → Inspection → Testing*. This matches the expected valuation key pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch Mode Power Supply (Applicable Standard: IS 14886): Safety Testing (Earth Leakage Current Test)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Switch Mode Power Supply (Applicable Standard: IS 14886): Safety Testing (Earth Leakage Current Test)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Switch Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** is a high-frequency electronic power conversion device that uses semiconductor switching elements (MOSFETs/IGBTs) operating in saturation and cut-off regions, combined with energy storage elements (inductors and capacitors), to convert and regulate electrical power with significantly higher efficiency (>80%) compared to linear power supplies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 14886:2018** is the Indian Standard (harmonized with **IEC 61558-1** and aligned with **IEC 60950-1 / IEC 62368-1**) governing the **safety requirements for Switch Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** used in electronic, electrical, and information technology equipment. It defines the construction, marking, heating, insulation, creepage/clearance, and **leakage current** compliance criteria.

The **Earth Leakage Current Test** (also referred to as *Touch Current Test* or *Enclosure Leakage Current Test*) is a mandatory safety compliance test defined under **Clause 5.2 / Clause 5.3 of IS 14886** (corresponds to **IEC 60950-1 Clause 5.1.6** and **IEC 62368-1 Clause 5.7**). It measures the **unintended AC current that flows from the Line/Neutral conductors through the insulation, EMI filter Y-capacitors, and stray capacitances to the Protective Earth (PE) conductor or accessible conductive parts** of the SMPS, under normal operating and single-fault conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must be Memorized for KTU Exam):**
> - Nominal Mains Frequency: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India) / $60 \text{ Hz}$ (US)
> - Test Supply Voltage: $1.06 \times V_{rated}$ (i.e., **243.8 V** for 230 V mains)
> - Class I Equipment Max Leakage: **3.5 mA RMS**
> - Class II Equipment Max Leakage: **0.25 mA RMS**
> - Standard Measuring Instrument Input Impedance: $\approx 2000 \text{ }\Omega$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cracked Garden Hose" Model

Imagine a high-pressure water pipe (the **230 V AC Mains**) connected to a tap (the **SMPS internal circuitry**). Even if the tap is closed perfectly, water will still **drip out** through microscopic cracks in the pipe wall because of the high pressure inside. This "dripping" water represents the **Earth Leakage Current**.

In a real SMPS, the "cracks" are:
- The **Y-rated safety capacitors** (typically 1 nF to 4.7 nF, rated for $250 \text{ V AC}$) deliberately placed across the **Galvanic Isolation Barrier** (between Primary and Earth/Secondary) for **EMI/EMC noise suppression**.
- The **stray parasitic capacitance** formed between the primary-side heat sink, transformer windings, and the grounded chassis.

> [!TIP]
> **Why we cannot eliminate leakage current completely:** Removing Y-capacitors will improve safety but will fail **CISPR 22 / CISPR 32 EMC standards** (conducted emissions). Hence, engineers must **balance** safety (low leakage) with EMC compliance (sufficient Y-capacitance).

### 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU

- Questions are **frequently asked** (Module 3 weightage is **20%** in the End Semester Exam).
- Tests **understanding of isolation concepts, EMI filter design, and human body impedance modeling** — a core competency of any electronic hardware engineer.
- IS 14886 is the **only BIS-mandated Indian Standard** explicitly for SMPS safety.
- The Earth Leakage Current Test directly tests a designer's ability to choose **Y-capacitor values** and **isolation transformer** specifications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Earth Leakage Current in SMPS

Earth leakage current in an SMPS arises primarily due to **three sources**:

1. **EMI Filter Y-Capacitors (Cy1 and Cy2)**
   - Connected from **Line to Earth (Cy1)** and **Neutral to Earth (Cy2)**.
   - Form an intentional AC path for high-frequency common-mode noise to drain into earth.
   - At **50 Hz mains frequency**, they still pass a small AC current: $I_{leak} = V_{mains} / X_{C_y}$.

2. **Primary-to-Secondary Stray Capacitance (Cp)**
   - The inter-winding capacitance of the high-frequency isolation transformer (typically 5–25 pF).
   - Couples common-mode noise from primary to secondary.

3. **Insulation Leakage Resistance (Ri)**
   - Finite insulation resistance of the transformer bobbin, optocoupler, and PCB.
   - Typically very high ($>10 \text{ M}\Omega$) but contributes a small DC-biased AC current.

### 2.2 The IEC 60990 / IS 14886 Measuring Instrument Model

The standard specifies that leakage current must be measured using a **defined network** that simulates the **electrical impedance of the human body**. This is called the **"Measuring Device per Figure 5 of IEC 60990"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Measuring Network Components:**
> - $R_1 = 1500 \text{ }\Omega$ (simulates body internal resistance)
> - $R_2 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ (simulates skin contact resistance)
> - $C = 0.22 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (simulates skin capacitance)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas. Note: The pipe symbol `\vert` is used instead of `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $X_{C_y} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_y}$ | Reactance of Y-capacitor at mains frequency. Used to predict leakage. |
| 2 | $I_{leak(RMS)} = \dfrac{V_{mains}}{X_{C_y}} = 2 \pi f C_y V_{mains}$ | RMS leakage current through Y-capacitor. **Direct proportionality** to $C_y$. |
| 3 | $I_{leak(peak)} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{leak(RMS)}$ | Peak leakage current value. |
| 4 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.22 \times 10^{-6}}$ | Reactance of measuring network capacitor at 50 Hz. Value $\approx 14{,}467 \text{ }\Omega$. |
| 5 | $Z_{meas} = R_1 + \dfrac{R_2 \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_C^2}}$ | Total input impedance of the standard measuring instrument. |
| 6 | $V_{touch} = I_{leak} \times Z_{meas}$ | Voltage developed across measuring instrument (proxy for human body voltage). |
| 7 | $I_{max,ClassI} = 3.5 \text{ mA RMS}$ | Maximum allowable leakage for Class I (earthed) SMPS. |
| 8 | $I_{max,ClassII} = 0.25 \text{ mA RMS}$ | Maximum allowable leakage for Class II (double-insulated) SMPS. |
| 9 | $V_{test} = 1.06 \times V_{rated}$ | Test supply voltage (e.g., $1.06 \times 230 = 243.8 \text{ V}$). |
| 10 | $C_{y,max} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{2 \pi f V_{mains}}$ | Maximum permissible Y-capacitor value to meet Class I limit. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design Phase:** Engineers select $C_{y1} + C_{y2}$ such that the worst-case leakage stays below **3.5 mA**. A common budget is **2.2 nF + 2.2 nF = 4.4 nF total**, which gives a leakage of approximately **0.32 mA** at 230 V/50 Hz — well within the limit.
- **Compliance & Certification:** Mandatory for **BIS certification (ISI mark)** and **CE marking** for any SMPS sold in India or the EU.
- **Medical SMPS (IS 13450 / IEC 60601-1):** Stricter limit of **0.1 mA** applies (patient protection). Often requires a **Y-capacitor divider + ground resistor** topology.
- **Failure Forensics:** Excessive leakage current in a field-returned unit typically indicates **degraded Y-capacitors** (loss of capacitance or insulation failure due to thermal aging).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: Calculating Earth Leakage Current from Y-Capacitors

**Given:**
- SMPS has two Y-capacitors: $C_{y1} = 2.2 \text{ nF}$ (Line-Earth) and $C_{y2} = 2.2 \text{ nF}$ (Neutral-Earth).
- Mains: $V_{mains} = 230 \text{ V RMS}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

**Find:** Total RMS leakage current flowing into the Protective Earth conductor.

**Step 1 — Equivalent capacitance of the two Y-capacitors as seen from Line-Neutral to Earth:**

The two Y-capacitors are in series from the Line-Neutral source perspective, but both terminate at Earth. Hence, the total capacitance to Earth is:

$$
C_{y,total} = C_{y1} + C_{y2} = 2.2 \text{ nF} + 2.2 \text{ nF} = 4.4 \text{ nF} = 4.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}
$$

> **Logic:** In a balanced 230 V AC system, Line and Neutral are at equal and opposite potential to Earth, so both capacitors contribute additively to the leakage current. If the supply is grounded, however, only one is effectively in circuit — but for worst-case design, we assume both.

**Step 2 — Calculate capacitive reactance of $C_{y,total}$ at 50 Hz:**

$$
X_{C_y} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C_{y,total}}
$$

$$
X_{C_y} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
X_{C_y} = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 50 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
X_{C_y} = \frac{1}{1.3823 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 723{,}400 \text{ }\Omega \approx 723.4 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

> **Logic:** Y-capacitors are tiny (nF range), so at 50 Hz their reactance is in the hundreds of k$\Omega$, naturally limiting current to the mA range.

**Step 3 — Calculate RMS leakage current:**

$$
I_{leak(RMS)} = \frac{V_{mains}}{X_{C_y}} = \frac{230 \text{ V}}{723{,}400 \text{ }\Omega}
$$

$$
I_{leak(RMS)} \approx 3.179 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.318 \text{ mA RMS}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Class I limit:**

$$
I_{leak(RMS)} = 0.318 \text{ mA} \ll I_{max,ClassI} = 3.5 \text{ mA} \quad \text{SAFETY MARGIN: } 11\times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result is well within the **3.5 mA Class I limit**, so the SMPS **PASSES** the earth leakage test for this design.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Maximum Permissible Y-Capacitor Value

**Given:** $V_{mains} = 230 \text{ V RMS}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, Class I limit $= 3.5 \text{ mA}$.

**Find:** Maximum $C_{y,total}$ for Class I compliance.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the leakage formula:**

$$
I_{leak} = 2 \pi f C_{y,total} V_{mains}
$$

$$
C_{y,total} = \frac{I_{leak}}{2 \pi f V_{mains}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
C_{y,total,max} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 230}
$$

$$
C_{y,total,max} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{72{,}257}
$$

$$
C_{y,total,max} \approx 4.84 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F} = 48.4 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret result:**

Total $C_{y1} + C_{y2} \leq 48.4 \text{ nF}$ for Class I. In practice, designers use **$2 \times 4.7 \text{ nF} = 9.4 \text{ nF}$** for a comfortable safety margin and good EMC performance.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Standard Measuring Instrument Impedance

**Step 1 — Reactance of $C = 0.22 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ at 50 Hz:**

$$
X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.22 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 14{,}467 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of $R_2 \parallel X_C$ impedance branch:**

$$
\vert Z_{R_2C} \vert = \frac{R_2 \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_C^2}} = \frac{500 \times 14{,}467}{\sqrt{500^2 + 14{,}467^2}}
$$

$$
\vert Z_{R_2C} \vert = \frac{7{,}233{,}500}{14{,}475.6} \approx 499.6 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Total input impedance:**

$$
Z_{meas} = R_1 + \vert Z_{R_2C} \vert = 1500 + 499.6 \approx 2000 \text{ }\Omega
$$

> [!TIP]
> The standard measuring instrument is calibrated such that **1 mA of leakage current produces 2 V across the instrument**, which is then read by a true-RMS voltmeter. This is the basis of all commercial leakage current testers (e.g., **Fluke 6200-2**, **Kikusui TOS3200**).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Class I vs Class II SMPS

| Parameter | Class I (Earthed) | Class II (Double Insulated) |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| Earth Pin Present? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Insulation Type | Basic + Protective Earth | Reinforced / Double |
| Max Earth Leakage Current | **3.5 mA RMS** | **0.25 mA RMS** |
| Y-Capacitor Budget | Larger ($2 \times 4.7 \text{ nF}$) | Very Small ($2 \times 1 \text{ nF}$) |
| Creepage Distance | $\geq 3.0 \text{ mm}$ | $\geq 6.0 \text{ mm}$ |
| Test Configuration | Earth wire disconnected → measure current | Earth wire absent → measure from enclosure to ground |
| Typical Applications | Desktop SMPS, Industrial PSU | Mobile chargers, Plug-in adapters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of SMPS Showing Leakage Current Paths

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> node2[EMI Filter]
    node2 --> node3[Bridge Rectifier]
    node3 --> node4[DC Bus 400V]
    node4 --> node5[High Freq Inverter MOSFET]
    node5 --> node6[HF Isolation Transformer]
    node6 --> node7[Secondary Rectifier Filter]
    node7 --> node8[DC Output to Load]
    node2 -.->|Cy1 Line-Earth| node9[Protective Earth PE]
    node2 -.->|Cy2 Neutral-Earth| node9
    node6 -.->|Cp Stray Capacitance| node9
    node9 --> node10[Earth Leakage Current Path]
    node10 --> node11[Measuring Instrument per IEC 60990]
    node11 --> node12[RMS Voltmeter Display]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The dotted lines from `node2` (EMI Filter) and `node6` (HF Transformer) to `node9` (PE) represent the **leakage paths**. The arrows show that leakage is the **vector sum** of currents from both Y-capacitors and the transformer's inter-winding capacitance.

---

### 4.2 Earth Leakage Test Setup Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Isolation Transformer Variable 0-250V] --> nodeB[Equipment Under Test SMPS]
    nodeB -->|L Terminal| nodeC[Measuring Network IEC 60990]
    nodeB -->|N Terminal| nodeC
    nodeB -->|PE Conductor| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[RMS Voltmeter]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Display in mA]
    nodeF[Polarity Reversal Switch] --> nodeA
    nodeG[Neutral Reversal Switch] --> nodeB
```

> **Test Procedure Logic:**
> 1. Apply **$1.06 \times V_{rated}$** via the isolation transformer.
> 2. **Reverse polarity** using the switches to check worst-case asymmetry.
> 3. The measuring network converts the leakage current into a proportional RMS voltage.
> 4. The voltmeter reading (in mA) is compared against the **3.5 mA / 0.25 mA** limit.

---

### 4.3 IEC 60990 Standard Measuring Network (Internal Detail)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeX1[Input Terminal T1] --> nodeR1[Resistor R1 equals 1500 ohm]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2C[R2 500 ohm parallel C 0.22 uF]
    nodeR2C --> nodeX2[Output Terminal T2 to Voltmeter]
    nodeY1[Earth Terminal] --> nodeFREQ[Body Filter and Frequency Compensation]
    nodeFREQ --> nodeX2
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The $R_2 \parallel C$ combination models the **frequency-dependent impedance of human skin**. At low frequencies (50 Hz), the capacitor is high-impedance, so the network reads close to $R_1 + R_2 = 2000 \text{ }\Omega$. At high frequencies, the capacitor shorts out $R_2$, simulating reduced skin impedance.

---

### 4.4 Leakage Current Path Through Y-Capacitors (Vector Diagram Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeL[L Line] -->|V_L| nodeCy1[Cy1 2.2 nF]
    nodeN[N Neutral] -->|V_N| nodeCy2[Cy2 2.2 nF]
    nodeCy1 --> nodeEarth[Protective Earth]
    nodeCy2 --> nodeEarth
    nodeEarth --> nodeIleak[I_leak measured at 0.318 mA]
    nodeL --> nodeLoad[SMPS Internal Load]
    nodeN --> nodeLoad
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** In a balanced 230 V system, $V_L = +115 \text{ V}$ and $V_N = -115 \text{ V}$ with respect to Earth. The two Y-capacitors produce currents that are **in phase** at the Earth node, so they add constructively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**
> Define **Earth Leakage Current** as per **IS 14886**. Why is it important to measure this current in an SMPS?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Earth leakage current is the **unintended current that flows from the Line/Neutral conductors of an AC supply through the insulation, EMI filter Y-capacitors, and stray capacitances to the Protective Earth (PE) conductor** of an SMPS, as defined under **Clause 5.1.6 of IS 14886 (harmonized with IEC 60950-1)**.

It is important to measure this current for two reasons:
1. **User Safety (Electric Shock Prevention):** Excessive leakage can cause ventricular fibrillation if a user touches the enclosure during a fault. The standard limits ensure the leakage stays below the **let-go threshold** of the human body.
2. **Compliance Verification:** It is a **mandatory test** for **BIS (ISI) marking** of SMPS sold in India and for **CE marking** in the EU.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two reasons.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]**
> List the **components of the standard measuring instrument** specified by **IEC 60990** for earth leakage current measurement. What is its total input impedance at 50 Hz?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The IEC 60990 measuring instrument is composed of the following components:

| Component | Value | Function |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| Resistor $R_1$ | $1500 \text{ }\Omega$ | Simulates human body internal resistance |
| Resistor $R_2$ | $500 \text{ }\Omega$ | Simulates skin contact resistance |
| Capacitor $C$ | $0.22 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ | Simulates skin capacitance |

The total input impedance at **50 Hz** is:

$$
Z_{meas} = R_1 + \frac{R_2 \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_C^2}} \approx 2000 \text{ }\Omega
$$

*(1 mark for components, 1 mark for values, 1 mark for impedance calculation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **Note for Students:** As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulations**, Module 3 carries one full 14-mark question with **internal choice** between Option A and Option B. Attempt any ONE.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> (a) Explain with a neat functional block diagram the **various leakage current paths** in a typical SMPS. Discuss the role of **Y-capacitors** in this context. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) An SMPS has **$C_{y1} = C_{y2} = 3.3 \text{ nF}$** connected between Line-Earth and Neutral-Earth respectively. The mains is **$230 \text{ V}$, $50 \text{ Hz}$**. Calculate the **worst-case RMS earth leakage current** and verify whether the SMPS passes the **Class I safety limit** as per IS 14886. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Functional Block Diagram & Y-Capacitor Role (7 Marks):**

The earth leakage current in an SMPS flows through three primary paths:

1. **Y-Capacitor Path (Dominant):** Two Y-capacitors, $C_{y1}$ (Line-Earth) and $C_{y2}$ (Neutral-Earth), are placed at the **EMI filter input** to provide a low-impedance return path for **common-mode noise** to the Protective Earth, thereby suppressing conducted EMI. However, at 50 Hz mains frequency, these capacitors also conduct a small AC current to Earth.

2. **Transformer Stray Capacitance Path:** The high-frequency isolation transformer has an inherent **inter-winding capacitance** ($C_p \approx 5\text{–}25 \text{ pF}$). This couples high-frequency common-mode noise from primary to secondary, which then returns to primary Earth via the PE conductor.

3. **Insulation Leakage Path:** Finite insulation resistance of optocouplers, transformer bobbins, and PCB.

**Role of Y-Capacitors (Summary):**
- **Pros:** Essential for **CISPR 22/32 EMC compliance** (conducted emissions < 79 dB$\mu$V quasi-peak).
- **Cons:** Increase the 50 Hz leakage current proportional to $C_y$.
- **Design Trade-off:** Larger $C_y$ = better EMC, worse leakage. Typical budget: **$2 \times 2.2 \text{ nF}$ to $2 \times 4.7 \text{ nF}$**.

*(Full marks for diagram + 3 identified paths + trade-off discussion.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Total Y-capacitance seen from mains to Earth:

$$
C_{y,total} = C_{y1} + C_{y2} = 3.3 \text{ nF} + 3.3 \text{ nF} = 6.6 \text{ nF} = 6.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 2:** Reactance at 50 Hz:

$$
X_{C_y} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C_{y,total}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 482{,}294 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** RMS leakage current:

$$
I_{leak(RMS)} = \frac{V_{mains}}{X_{C_y}} = \frac{230}{482{,}294} \approx 0.477 \text{ mA RMS}
$$

**Step 4:** Compliance check:

$$
I_{leak(RMS)} = 0.477 \text{ mA} \ll I_{max,ClassI} = 3.5 \text{ mA} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** The SMPS **PASSES** the IS 14886 Class I earth leakage test with a **safety margin of $3.5 / 0.477 \approx 7.34 \times$**.

*(Distribution: 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark final answer, 2 marks comparison & conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Actual Past Year Pattern Adapted]**
> (a) Describe the **IS 14886 standard**. What is the significance of the **earth leakage current test**? List the **maximum permissible leakage limits** for Class I and Class II equipment. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) Explain the **standard measuring instrument** as per **IEC 60990** with its **circuit diagram** and derive the **total input impedance** at 50 Hz mains frequency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — IS 14886 Standard & Limits (7 Marks):**

**IS 14886:2018** is the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** specification titled *"Safety Requirements for Switch Mode Power Supplies"*. It is technically harmonized with **IEC 61558-1** and aligned with **IEC 60950-1** (Information Technology Equipment Safety) / **IEC 62368-1** (Audio/Video, ICT Equipment).

**Key Sections of IS 14886:**
- **Clause 4:** General requirements (marking, instructions).
- **Clause 5:** Protection against electric shock — covers **earth leakage current** (Sub-clause 5.1.6).
- **Clause 6:** Heating & Temperature Rise.
- **Clause 7:** Insulation, Creepage & Clearance Distances.
- **Clause 8:** Short-circuit & Overload Protection.
- **Annex A:** Dielectric Strength Tests (Hi-Pot Test).

**Significance of Earth Leakage Current Test:**
- Protects **end users from electric shock** in case of insulation failure.
- Validates that **Y-capacitors** are within safe limits.
- Mandatory for **BIS certification** of any SMPS sold in the Indian market.

**Maximum Permissible Limits:**

| Equipment Class | Earth Connection | Max Leakage Current |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Class I** | Yes (PE pin) | **3.5 mA RMS** |
| **Class II** | No (Double insulated) | **0.25 mA RMS** |

*(2 marks standard description, 2 marks significance, 3 marks limits table.)*

**Part (b) — Measuring Instrument & Impedance Derivation (7 Marks):**

The standard measuring instrument is defined in **Figure 5 of IEC 60990**. It comprises:
- $R_1 = 1500 \text{ }\Omega$ in series with
- The parallel combination of $R_2 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ and $C = 0.22 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Reactance of C at 50 Hz:

$$
X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.22 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 14{,}467 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Magnitude of parallel $R_2 \parallel X_C$:

$$
\vert Z_{RC} \vert = \frac{R_2 \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_C^2}} = \frac{500 \times 14{,}467}{\sqrt{500^2 + 14{,}467^2}} \approx 499.6 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Total impedance:

$$
Z_{meas} = R_1 + \vert Z_{RC} \vert = 1500 + 499.6 \approx 2000 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Engineering Insight:** At 50 Hz, the capacitor is effectively an open circuit, so the network reads as a simple resistive divider. The 2000 $\Omega$ value is the **calibrated body impedance** used to convert leakage current into measurable voltage.

*(2 marks circuit description, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks engineering insight.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $1.06 \times V_{rated}$** as the test voltage when the question says "test conditions" — the regulator explicitly tests for this.
> 2. **Using peak voltage $\sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325 \text{ V}$** in the leakage formula — always use **RMS** unless peak is explicitly asked.
