Calculate: (i) the line regulation in mV/V, (ii) the line regulation in %/V, (iii) the equivalent open-loop DC gain of the error amplifier (assuming $$\alpha = 1,\;\beta = 1$$). *(7 marks)* **Model Solution to (a):** Start with the closed-loop output equation (derived in Section 3.1): $$ V_{out} = \frac{A_{OL}\,\beta}{1 + A_{OL}\,\beta} \cdot V_{REF} + \frac{\alpha}{1 + A_{OL}\,\beta} \cdot V_{in} $$ Differentiate w.r.t. $$V_{in}$$: $$ \frac{\partial V_{out}}{\partial V_{in}} = \frac{\alpha}{1 + A_{OL}\,\beta} $$ Take the magnitude: $$ \boxed{LR = \left\vert \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \right\vert = \frac{\alpha}{1 + A_{OL}\,\beta}} $$ **Term meanings (1.5 marks each):** - $$A_{OL}$$ — Open-loop DC gain of the internal error amplifier. - $$\beta$$ — Feedback factor of the resistor divider that samples $$V_{out}$$ (for fixed regulators, $$\beta = 1$$). - $$\alpha$$ — Input-to-output feedthrough coefficient (ideally 0, in practice a small fraction due to pass-element leakage). **Physical interpretation (0.5 mark):** Line regulation improves (decreases) as $$A_{OL}$$ increases — the main reason why LDO designers use high-gain error amplifiers. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Compute $$\Delta V_{in}$$ and $$\Delta V_{out}$$:** $$ \Delta V_{in} = 25 - 15 = 10\,\text{V} $$ $$ \Delta V_{out} = 30\,\text{mV} = 0.030\,\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — (i) mV/V form:** $$ LR_{mV/V} = \frac{30\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{V}} $$ $$ \boxed{LR_{mV/V} = 3\,\text{mV/V}} $$ **Step 3 — (ii) % per V form:** $$ \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out,nom}} \times 100 = \frac{0.030}{12} \times 100 = 0.25\,\% $$ $$ \boxed{LR_{\%/\text{V}} = \frac{0.25\,\%}{10\,\text{V}} = 0.025\,\%/\text{V}} $$ **Step 4 — (iii) Open-loop DC gain:** $$ LR = \frac{1}{1 + A_{OL}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 + A_{OL} = \frac{1}{LR} = \frac{10}{0.030} \approx 333.3 $$ $$ \boxed{A_{OL} \approx 332.3 \;\text{(or about } 50.4\,\text{dB)}} $$ *(2 marks for mV/V, 2 marks for %/V, 3 marks for the gain derivation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Unit confusion** — Writing "$$0.4\,\%$$" without specifying whether it is per-volt, total, or referring to $$\Delta V_{out}$$ alone. Always qualify the unit. > 2. **Forgetting to normalise** by $$V_{out,nom}$$ when the question asks for % form. For a 12 V regulator and a 5 V regulator with the same $$\Delta V_{out}$$, the %/V answers will differ by a factor of 2.4. > 3. **Mixing up line regulation with load regulation** — Line regulation varies $$V_{in}$$ (load fixed), load regulation varies $$I_L$$ (input fixed). Examiners frequently set trick questions that swap these. > 4. **Sign error** — Line regulation is always quoted as a **positive magnitude**. A regulator that *decreases* $$V_{out}$$ when $$V_{in}$$ increases is still described with a positive LR. > 5. **Skipping the test-setup diagram** — For 7-mark sub-parts on measurement, a labelled block diagram is mandatory for full marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Line regulation** = variation of $$V_{out}$$ with $$V_{in}$$ at constant load and temperature. - Formulae to remember: $$LR = \Delta V_{out}/\Delta V_{in}$$ (ratio), $$LR_{\%/\text{V}} = (\Delta V_{out}/V_{out,nom})\cdot 100 / \Delta V_{in}$$ (%/V), $$LR_{tot} = (\Delta V_{out}/V_{out,nom}) \cdot 100$$ (total %). - **Test setup essentials**: variable DC source, fixed $$R_L$$, two DMMs, constant temperature. - **Typical values**: 78xx series $$\rightarrow$$ ~1–3 mV/V; LM317 $$\rightarrow$$ ~1–3 mV/V; LDOs (LM1117) $$\rightarrow$$ ~0.5–2 mV/V. - **Relationship**: $$PSRR_{DC}\,[\text{dB}] = 20\log_{10}(1/LR)$$. - **Design lever**: Increase the open-loop DC gain $$A_{OL}$$ of the internal error amplifier to reduce line regulation. - **Common pitfalls**: unit confusion, sign convention, swapping line vs load regulation, skipping the test diagram. - **Don't forget the bypass capacitors**: $$C_{in} \approx 0.33\,\mu\text{F}$$ and $$C_{out} \approx 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$$ are required for stable regulator operation — they also improve the AC PSRR, which is the natural extension of the DC line regulation measurement. ---

topics.title: Load Regulation for a Variation of Load Min to Max Load and Vice Versa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Load Regulation for a Variation of Load Min to Max Load and Vice Versa # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Load Regulation (LR)** is a fundamental steady-state performance metric of any DC voltage regulator (linear or switching) that quantifies the ability of the regulator to *maintain a constant output voltage* when the load current drawn from it is varied across its operational range. Mathematically, for a regulator operating between a **Minimum Load (Min Load / No-Load)** and a **Maximum Load (Max Load / Full-Load)**: $$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $V_{NL}$ = Output voltage at **minimum** (or no) load current - $V_{FL}$ = Output voltage at **maximum** (full) load current It can also be expressed in absolute terms (mV) or as a percentage per ampere (%/A) depending on the application context. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Load Regulation is classified under the *Performance Specification Parameters* of voltage regulators. It is intrinsically tied to the **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)** of the regulator and forms a critical design trade-off with **Line Regulation** and **Efficiency**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Tap" Model Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a municipal water tank through a long, narrow pipe: | Analogy Element | Electronic Equivalent | |---|---| | Municipal water tank | Input / Unregulated DC source | | Long narrow pipe | Internal resistance ($R_s$) of the regulator | | Tap handle (opening/closing) | Load current demand ($I_L$) | | Water pressure at tap outlet | Output voltage ($V_{out}$) | **Scenario 1 — Tap opened from trickle to full blast (Min → Max Load):** Water pressure at the tap **drops** because more flow creates greater frictional loss in the narrow pipe. Similarly, in a regulator, increasing $I_L$ causes a larger internal voltage drop ($I_L \times R_s$), so $V_{out}$ **sags**. **Scenario 2 — Tap closed from full blast back to trickle (Max → Min Load):** Water pressure at the tap **rises** (overshoot, then settles) because friction losses vanish. Similarly, $V_{out}$ in the regulator **rises** and then stabilizes through feedback action. The *better* the regulator (smaller $R_s$ / tighter feedback loop), the closer the tap pressure stays to the ideal value — this is precisely what **good load regulation** achieves. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Industry Benchmarks > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard Acceptable Values for Load Regulation:** > - **Precision regulators (e.g., LM723, LT3080):** $\leq \mathbf{0.01\%}$ to $\mathbf{0.05\%}$ > - **General purpose 3-terminal regulators (e.g., 7805, LM317):** $\mathbf{0.1\%}$ to $\mathbf{1\%}$ > - **Unregulated DC supplies:** $\mathbf{5\%}$ to $\mathbf{20\%}$ (unacceptable for modern electronics) > - **Typical reference voltage** for comparison: $\mathbf{1.25\text{ V}}$ (LM317 internal bandgap) The units of absolute load regulation are **mV** (or $\mu$V for high-precision), while percentage values are dimension-less ratios normalized to the nominal output. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Origin of Load Regulation Error Every practical regulator (linear or switching) has **non-zero output impedance**. When load current varies, the voltage divider formed between this internal impedance and the external load causes the output to shift. ### Step-by-Step Logic Flow: 1. **Source Stage:** The unregulated input $V_{in}$ feeds an active pass element (BJT/MOSFET in linear; switch in SMPS). 2. **Reference Stage:** An internal bandgap reference ($V_{REF}$) generates a temperature-stable comparison voltage. 3. **Error Amplifier:** Compares a sampled fraction of $V_{out}$ (via feedback divider $R_1, R_2$) with $V_{REF}$. 4. **Pass Element Control:** The amplified error signal drives the pass element to *correct* any deviation. 5. **Residual Imperfection:** Due to finite open-loop gain ($A_{OL}$) and non-zero $R_{DS(on)}$ / $r_{CE}$, a *small residual error* remains — this is exactly what Load Regulation measures. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Formulas — Memorize for ESE & Module Tests** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $LR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$ | Percentage Load Regulation | % | | 2 | $LR(\text{abs}) = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_{L}} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL} - I_{NL}}$ | Absolute Load Regulation (Source Resistance) | $\text{V/A} = \Omega$ | | 3 | $Z_{out} \approx \dfrac{r_{CE}}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta}$ | Closed-Loop Output Impedance of Regulator | $\Omega$ | | 4 | $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$ | LM317 Output Voltage Equation | V | | 5 | $\Delta V_{out} = I_{L} \cdot Z_{out}$ | Output Voltage Deviation vs. Load Current | V | | 6 | $V_{out,ideal} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | Buck Converter Ideal Output (switching reg.) | V | | 7 | $PSR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}/\Delta V_{in}}{V_{out}}$ | Power Supply Rejection Ratio (related metric) | dB | | 8 | $\eta_{linear} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Linear Regulator Efficiency | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipe Symbol Warning:** In KTU exam scripts, never use `|` for absolute value — always write `\vert` or `\mid`. Markdown tables above use `\vert` for the same reason. --- ## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Design Load regulation directly impacts the following engineering domains: - **Embedded Systems:** Microcontroller $V_{DD}$ stability under varying sleep/active current transients. - **RF and Analog Signal Chains:** ADC reference integrity, VCO phase noise coupling via supply. - **Battery-Powered Devices:** Dropout behavior at end-of-life (EOL) battery voltage under load. - **Automotive ECUs:** Cranking pulse + alternator load-dump transients require < 50 mV deviation. - **Industrial PLCs:** Sensor excitation rails must remain stable across multiplexed loads. A poorly regulated supply causes **logic glitches, ADC errors, oscillator drift, and Latch-Up** in CMOS ICs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Load Regulation from Output Impedance ### Starting Point — Generic Regulator Model Consider a regulator modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_{ideal}$ in series with an output impedance $Z_{out}$. When a load $R_L$ draws current $I_L$: $$V_{out} = V_{ideal} - I_L \cdot Z_{out}$$ ### Step 1 — No-Load (Min Load) Condition At minimum load, $I_L \approx I_{NL}$ (often negligible, e.g., quiescent current only). Therefore: $$V_{NL} \approx V_{ideal} - I_{NL} \cdot Z_{out}$$ ### Step 2 — Full-Load (Max Load) Condition At maximum load, $I_L = I_{FL}$, giving: $$V_{FL} = V_{ideal} - I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Voltage Difference $$\begin{aligned} V_{NL} - V_{FL} &= (V_{ideal} - I_{NL} \cdot Z_{out}) - (V_{ideal} - I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out}) \\ &= -I_{NL} \cdot Z_{out} + I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out} \\ &= (I_{FL} - I_{NL}) \cdot Z_{out} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute into the Definition $$\begin{aligned} LR(\%) &= \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{(I_{FL} - I_{NL}) \cdot Z_{out}}{V_{NL}} \times 100 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Compact Form (Crucial for KTU Numerical Problems) $$\boxed{LR(\%) = \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out}}{V_{NL}} \times 100\%}$$ This proves that **load regulation is directly proportional to output impedance**. Reducing $Z_{out}$ by increasing loop gain $A_{OL}$ is the *primary design technique*. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Output Impedance for a Series Pass Regulator ### Block Diagram Gain Analysis Let the regulator have: - Open-loop gain of error amplifier + pass transistor: $A_{OL}$ - Feedback fraction: $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ - Pass transistor small-signal output resistance: $r_{CE}$ ### Closed-Loop Output Impedance (Blackman's Theorem) $$Z_{out,closed} = \frac{r_{CE}}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta}$$ Substituting into the LR formula: $$\begin{aligned} LR(\%) &= \frac{\Delta I_L}{V_{NL}} \cdot \frac{r_{CE}}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta} \times 100 \\ LR(\%) &\approx \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot r_{CE}}{V_{NL} \cdot A_{OL} \cdot \beta} \times 100 \quad (\text{for } A_{OL}\beta \gg 1) \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Doubling the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ *halves* the load regulation error. This is why precision regulators use multi-stage error amplifiers with $A_{OL} > 80\text{ dB}$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem (Model):** A linear regulator has $V_{NL} = 12.06\text{ V}$ at $I_L = 10\text{ mA}$ and $V_{FL} = 11.94\text{ V}$ at $I_L = 1\text{ A}$. Compute: (a) Load Regulation in %, (b) Absolute Load Regulation in mV/A, (c) Approximate output impedance. ### Solution: **(a) Percentage Load Regulation:** $$LR(\%) = \frac{12.06 - 11.94}{12.06} \times 100 = \frac{0.12}{12.06} \times 100 = 0.995\%$$ **(b) Absolute Load Regulation (source resistance):** $$LR(\text{abs}) = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL} - I_{NL}} = \frac{0.12\text{ V}}{(1.000 - 0.010)\text{ A}} = \frac{0.12}{0.99} \approx 121.2\text{ mV/A}$$ **(c) Output Impedance:** $$Z_{out} \approx \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL} - I_{NL}} = 0.1212\ \Omega$$ --- ## 3.4 Comparative Matrix: Min→Max vs Max→Min Load Variation | Parameter | Min → Max Load Direction | Max → Min Load Direction | |---|---|---| | **Load Current** | Increases ($\uparrow$) | Decreases ($\downarrow$) | | **Output Voltage** | Sags / droops ($\downarrow$) | Rises / overshoots ($\uparrow$) | | **Dominant Mechanism** | Static $I \cdot R$ drop | Static recovery + transient settling | | **Transient Behavior** | Small-signal response | Possible ringing due to $L \cdot C_{out}$ resonance | | **Time Domain** | Dominated by feedback loop bandwidth | Dominated by output capacitor ESR/ESL | | **Critical Spec** | DC Load Regulation | Transient Recovery Time / Settling Time | | **Worst-Case** | Full step from $I_{NL}$ to $I_{FL}$ | Full step from $I_{FL}$ to $I_{NL}$ with inductive kick | > [!NOTE] > **In a Buck (SMPS) Regulator**, the feedback loop adjusts the duty cycle $D$ to compensate for load steps. The load regulation metric for SMPS is typically better than linear regulators (often $\leq 0.1\%$), but transient response is governed by the loop crossover frequency $f_c$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Linear Regulator with Load Step ```mermaid flowchart LR A["DC Input Source
Vin Unregulated"] --> B["Pass Element
Power BJT or MOSFET"] B --> C["Output Node
Vout"] C --> D["Feedback Divider
R1 and R2"] D --> E["Error Amplifier
High AOL Comparator"] F["Bandgap Reference
VREF 1.25 V"] --> E E --> G["Drive Signal"] G --> B C --> H["Load Resistor RL
Variable Min to Max"] I["Zout Internal
Source Impedance"] -.-> C ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The **blue dashed arrow** indicates the parasitic output impedance $Z_{out}$ that the regulator cannot fully eliminate. - The feedback loop (Error Amp + Reference + Divider) actively drives the Pass Element to *minimize* the effect of this $Z_{out}$. - The **Load $R_L$** is shown as variable — physically, it is realized by switching in/out parallel resistor networks or by an electronically programmable load (e.g., N-MOSFET in linear region acting as a current sink). --- ## 4.2 Load Step Response — Time-Domain Waveform Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T1["Region 1 Steady State No Load"] S1["Vout equals VNL flat"] end subgraph T2["Region 2 Step Transition Min to Max Load"] S2["Vout sags by delta V
Exponentially recovers to VFL"] end subgraph T3["Region 3 Steady State Full Load"] S3["Vout equals VFL flat slightly below VNL"] end subgraph T4["Region 4 Step Transition Max to Min Load"] S4["Vout overshoots by delta V overshoot
Rings then settles back to VNL"] end T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 T3 --> T4 T4 --> T1 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Region 2** shows the classic droop-and-recovery when load is suddenly increased — this is where load regulation is *measured in DC steady-state*. - **Region 4** illustrates the *reverse* transition: an overshoot spike (potentially damaging to CMOS logic if $> V_{max}$ rating) followed by damped settling. - The peak-to-peak envelope across Regions 1–4 defines the **total dynamic load regulation** spec. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Closed-Loop Load Regulation Compensation ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Load Current Step
Delta IL"] --> nodeB["Regulator Output Network
Zout and C_out"] nodeB --> nodeC["Delta Vout Error Signal"] nodeC --> nodeD["Feedback Divider
Beta equals R1 over R1 plus R2"] nodeD --> nodeE["Error Amplifier
Gain AOL"] nodeF["Reference Voltage VREF"] --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG["Pass Element Drive"] nodeG --> nodeH["Corrected Vout
Reduced Delta Vout"] nodeH -.feedback.-> nodeD ``` This architecture visually demonstrates that **load regulation error is suppressed by the loop gain** $T = A_{OL} \cdot \beta$, in accordance with classical control theory. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Q: Define Load Regulation of a voltage regulator. How is it different from Line Regulation?** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation Shown):** > Load regulation is the ability of a voltage regulator to maintain a constant output voltage when the load current drawn from it varies across its operating range. **[1 Mark]** > > Mathematically: $LR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$ **[1 Mark]** > > Load Regulation concerns variation of $V_{out}$ due to **load current ($I_L$) changes at fixed input voltage**, whereas Line Regulation concerns variation of $V_{out}$ due to **input voltage ($V_{in}$) changes at fixed load current**. The two are independent specifications and a regulator must satisfy both. **[1 Mark]** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Q: An LM7805 regulator delivers $V_{out} = 5.20\text{ V}$ at $10\text{ mA}$ load and $V_{out} = 4.95\text{ V}$ at $1\text{ A}$ load. Calculate the percentage load regulation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} LR(\%) &= \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{5.20 - 4.95}{5.20} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{0.25}{5.20} \times 100 \\ &\approx 4.81\% \end{aligned}$$ This is a **poor** load regulation, indicating high output impedance ($\approx 0.255\ \Omega$). **[1 Mark for final answer, 2 Marks for full working]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE Part B uses **Internal Choice**: students answer either **OR Question A** *or* **OR Question B**. Both are presented below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the expression for percentage load regulation of a linear series-pass regulator in terms of its closed-loop output impedance $Z_{out}$. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A buck converter switching regulator operates in continuous conduction mode with $V_{in} = 12\text{ V}$ and delivers $1.2\text{ A}$ to a load. The output is measured as $5.05\text{ V}$ at full load and $5.10\text{ V}$ at no load. If the nominal output is $5.0\text{ V}$, calculate (i) the load regulation in %, (ii) the equivalent output impedance, and (iii) the duty cycle at full load. **[7 Marks]** ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:** Assume a regulator modeled as ideal source $V_{ideal}$ in series with output impedance $Z_{out}$. Feedback loop attempts to force $V_{out} = V_{REF}/\beta$, but residual error remains. **Step 1:** $V_{out} = V_{ideal} - I_L \cdot Z_{out}$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 2:** At no-load ($I_L = I_{NL}$): $V_{NL} = V_{ideal} - I_{NL} Z_{out}$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 3:** At full-load ($I_L = I_{FL}$): $V_{FL} = V_{ideal} - I_{FL} Z_{out}$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 4:** Subtract: $$V_{NL} - V_{FL} = (I_{FL} - I_{NL}) \cdot Z_{out}$$ **[2 Marks]** **Step 5:** Substituting into definition: $$LR(\%) = \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot Z_{out}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 6:** State assumption: $A_{OL} \cdot \beta \gg 1$ for closed-loop approximation. **[1 Mark]** --- **Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:** **(i) Percentage Load Regulation:** $$\begin{aligned} LR(\%) &= \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{5.10 - 5.05}{5.10} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{0.05}{5.10} \times 100 \\ &\approx 0.98\% \end{aligned}$$ **[2 Marks]** **(ii) Equivalent Output Impedance:** $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL} - I_{NL}} = \frac{0.05}{1.2 - 0} \approx 0.0417\ \Omega \approx 41.7\text{ m}\Omega$$ **[2 Marks]** **(iii) Duty Cycle at Full Load (Buck Converter):** $$D = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{5.0}{12.0} \approx 0.4167 \text{ or } 41.67\%$$ **[3 Marks]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how load regulation is achieved in a closed-loop series-pass linear regulator. Discuss the role of feedback factor $\beta$ and open-loop gain $A_{OL}$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Compare the load regulation behavior of a series-pass linear regulator during **Min→Max load transition** versus **Max→Min load transition**. Include waveforms and design considerations for output capacitor selection. **[7 Marks]** ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation [7 Marks]:** - Draw block diagram: Reference $V_{REF}$ → Error Amplifier → Pass Element → $V_{out}$ → Feedback Divider ($R_1$, $R_2$) → back to Error Amplifier. **[3 Marks]** - Feedback factor: $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ **[1 Mark]** - The loop gain $T = A_{OL} \cdot \beta$ reduces $Z_{out}$ from $r_{CE}$ (open-loop) to $\dfrac{r_{CE}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ (closed-loop). **[2 Marks]** - Higher $A_{OL}$ yields better load regulation, but bandwidth and stability must be considered. **[1 Mark]** **Part (b) — Comparison [7 Marks]:** | Aspect | Min→Max Transition | Max→Min Transition | |---|---|---| | Cause | Sudden increase in $I_L$ | Sudden decrease in $I_L$ | | Voltage Behavior | Sags (droop) | Overshoots (spike) | | Critical Design Spec | DC Load Regulation, Loop Bandwidth | Transient Recovery, Overshoot Limit | | Capacitor Role | Supplies instantaneous current (bulk decoupling) | Absorbs inductive kick, limits $dv/dt$ | | Selection Criterion | Low ESR for voltage support | Sufficient ESR/ESL damping; sometimes snubber needed | **[5 Marks for table + 2 Marks for waveform description and capacitor selection formula $C_{out} \geq \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2 \pi f_c \cdot \Delta V_{out}}$]** --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Many students confuse **percentage** load regulation with **absolute** mV/A values. Always state which one you are computing and write the unit explicitly. > 2. **Direction Sign:** $V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ vs $V_{FL} - V_{NL}$ — getting the sign wrong leads to *negative %*, which is conceptually meaningless. Use $V_{NL}$ (higher voltage) as the reference numerator denominator. > 3. **Forgetting Loop Gain Effect:** Stating only $Z_{out} = r_{CE}$ without applying the closed-loop reduction factor costs 2 marks in derivations. > 4. **Confusing Load and Line Regulation:** Line regulation involves $V_{in}$ variation; load regulation involves $I_L$ variation. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this confusion. > 5. **SMPS vs Linear:** Don't apply $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ directly to linear regulators; it is a buck-converter-only formula. > 6. **Overlooking ESR in Capacitor Selection:** A regulator with poor output capacitor ESR will have excellent DC load regulation but unacceptable transient response. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam** - [x] **Definition:** $LR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{NL}} \times 100$ — non-negative scalar. - [x] **Physical Origin:** Non-zero closed-loop output impedance $Z_{out}$ — reduce via higher loop gain. - [x] **Closed-Loop $Z_{out}$:** $\dfrac{r_{CE}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ — the *smaller*, the better. - [x] **Min→Max Transition:** Output voltage *decreases* (sags), recovery time $\approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c}$ where $f_c$ is loop crossover frequency. - [x] **Max→Min Transition:** Output voltage *increases* (overshoots), must be clamped below load $V_{max}$ rating. - [x] **Industry Standard:** Precision regulators $\leq 0.05\%$, general-purpose $\leq 1\%$, switching typically $\leq 0.1\%$. - [x] **LM317 Formula:** $V_{out} = 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$. - [x] **Buck Converter:** $D = V_{out}/V_{in}$ for ideal CCM operation. - [x] **Difference from Line Regulation:** Load reg. = $I_L$ change @ fixed $V_{in}$; Line reg. = $V_{in}$ change @ fixed $I_L$. - [x] **Capacitor Rule:** $C_{out} \geq \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2\pi f_c \cdot \Delta V_{out,max}}$ for transient compliance. - [x] **Output Impedance Identity:** $|Z_{out}| = LR(\text{abs})$ in $\Omega$ — directly measurable via load step test.

topics.title: Safety Testing of Household Appliances (Applicable Standard IS 302-1): Definitions and Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Testing of Household Appliances (Applicable Standard IS 302-1): Definitions and Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of IS 302-1

**IS 302-1** is the Indian Standard titled *"Safety of Household and Similar Electrical Appliances — Part 1: General Requirements"*. It is the **harmonized Indian adoption of IEC 60335-1** (International Electrotechnical Commission standard), published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. It establishes the **general safety requirements** and **uniform testing methodology** for electrical appliances intended for household and similar purposes, whose rated voltage is **not more than 250 V (single-phase) or 480 V (polyphase)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, this topic falls under the broader OBE outcome **CO3 — Apply product-safety and EMC norms while developing electronic hardware for consumer applications.** Mastering the *definitions* is mandatory, because every clause in the safety test is a legal test against a defined term.

The standard works in two layers:

1. **Part 1 (IS 302-1)** — Generic requirements common to *all* appliances (definitions, marking, heating, leakage, dielectric strength, moisture resistance, etc.).
2. **Part 2 (IS 302-2-X)** — *Particular requirements* for specific appliances (e.g., Part 2-3 for electric irons, Part 2-7 for washing machines, Part 2-24 for refrigerators).

Therefore **IS 302-1 is always read together with the relevant Part 2 standard** during conformity testing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Driving Test Rulebook" Metaphor

Think of **IS 302-1 as the "Rulebook of the Road"** for any electrical appliance:

- Before you (the manufacturer) are allowed to "drive" your appliance into an Indian household, you must pass a "driving test" at a BIS-recognized lab.
- The examiner (test engineer) will not let you *begin* the test until you both agree on the **meaning of every word** used in the question paper — *What is a "live part"? What counts as "Class II"? What is "rated power"?*
- That mutual agreement is exactly what **Clause 3 (Definitions)** of IS 302-1 enforces.

> [!NOTE]
> Without a precise vocabulary, test results become ambiguous and non-reproducible. Definitions are therefore the **foundation of legal and technical conformance** in the BIS regulatory framework.

## 1.3 Why Definitions Matter in Engineering Practice

When a product is sent to a **BIS-recognized lab** for certification under the **CRS (Compulsory Registration Scheme)** or ISI mark, the *entire conformity assessment rests on Clause 3*. A product that **fails** because the engineer calls a part "live" when the standard calls it "accessible" is a non-conformity against the lab's accreditation. Hence, KTU examiners reward students who can quote definitions **verbatim** with their clause numbers (e.g., *Clause 3.3.16 — "Live part"*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Vocabulary Construct to Memorize:**
> Every definition in IS 302-1 is structured as:
> **`<Term> — <single-sentence normative meaning> — <notes / boundary conditions>`.**
> The *first sentence is normative* (mandatory); the *notes that follow are informative* (explanatory only).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Definitions in IS 302-1

Clause 3 of IS 302-1 organizes its definitions into the following logical groups. Understanding this taxonomy allows you to recall the *correct* term under exam pressure.

### 2.1.1 Definitions Related to **Quantities and Ratings**

- **Rated Voltage (3.1.1)** — The supply voltage assigned to the appliance by the manufacturer (V).
- **Rated Power Input (3.1.3)** — The power input assigned to the appliance at rated voltage and under normal operation (W).
- **Rated Current (3.1.2)** — The current assigned to the appliance at rated voltage and normal operation (A).
- **Rated Frequency (3.1.4)** — The supply frequency for which the appliance is designed (Hz).
- **Normal Operation (3.1.9)** — The condition of operation where the appliance performs its intended function under defined load conditions.

### 2.1.2 Definitions Related to **Insulation and Protection Classes**

These are the *most heavily tested* definitions in KTU examinations.

- **Basic Insulation (3.3.2)** — Insulation providing basic protection against electric shock. *Note: by itself it is not a safeguard under fault conditions.*
- **Supplementary Insulation (3.3.3)** — Independent insulation applied *in addition to* basic insulation to ensure protection if basic insulation fails.
- **Double Insulation (3.3.4)** — Insulation comprising **both basic and supplementary insulation** in a single appliance.
- **Reinforced Insulation (3.3.5)** — A single insulation system that provides protection equivalent to double insulation against electric shock.
- **Functional Insulation (3.3.1)** — Insulation between live parts of *different potentials* that is necessary only for the *correct functioning* of the appliance, **not for protection against electric shock**.
- **Class 0 Appliance (3.3.7)** — Appliance with basic insulation only, no means of earthing, and no supplementary insulation. (Generally **banned** in India for new designs.)
- **Class I Appliance (3.3.8)** — Appliance in which protection against electric shock relies on **basic insulation + an earthed protective conductor**. (Example: metal-bodied mixer grinder with 3-pin plug.)
- **Class II Appliance (3.3.9)** — Appliance in which protection against electric shock relies on **double insulation or reinforced insulation**; **no provision for earthing**. (Example: plastic-bodied hair dryer, mobile charger.)
- **Class III Appliance (3.3.10)** — Appliance supplied from a **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)** source, with values not exceeding **42.4 V peak or 60 V DC**; no provision for earthing. (Example: low-voltage DC toys.)

### 2.1.3 Definitions Related to **Physical Accessibility and Live Parts**

- **Live Part (3.3.16)** — A conductor or conductive part intended to be energized in normal operation; it includes the neutral conductor, but **not** the earth conductor.
- **Accessible Part (3.3.15)** — A part that can be touched by the **standard test finger (Figure 1 of IS 302-1)** when the appliance is assembled and operable as intended.
- **Hazardous Live Part (3.3.17)** — A live part that, when touched, may cause an electric shock. (Defined by voltage and current thresholds.)

### 2.1.4 Definitions Related to **Ingress Protection (IP Code)**

- **IP Code (3.5.1)** — Coding system indicating the **degree of protection** provided by an enclosure against access to hazardous parts, ingress of solid foreign objects, and ingress of water, in accordance with **IS/IEC 60529**.

### 2.1.5 Definitions Related to **Mechanical Installation and Application**

- **Portable Appliance (3.5.4)** — Appliance that can be moved while in operation, or that can be easily moved from one place to another while connected to supply. (Example: table fan.)
- **Hand-Held Appliance (3.5.5)** — Portable appliance intended to be held in the hand during normal use. (Example: hair dryer.)
- **Stationary Appliance (3.5.6)** — Fixed appliance or portable appliance not intended to be moved when in operation.
- **Fixed Appliance (3.5.7)** — Appliance that is fixed to a support or otherwise secured in a specific location.
- **Built-in Appliance (3.5.8)** — Appliance intended to be installed in a prepared recess (e.g., kitchen cabinet).

### 2.1.6 Definitions Related to **Temperature and Thermal Control**

- **Thermal Cut-out (3.4.4)** — Device that limits the temperature of the appliance by *automatically opening the circuit* and has no user-adjustable set point.
- **Thermal Link (3.4.6)** — Thermal cut-out that operates **only once** and requires replacement or resetting.
- **PTC Heater / PTC-S (3.4.13)** — Self-regulating heater using a Positive Temperature Coefficient thermistor; resistance rises sharply above the Curie point, limiting current.

### 2.1.7 Definitions Related to **Supply Connections and Cords**

- **Supply Cord (3.2.4)** — Flexible cable/cord used to connect the appliance to the fixed wiring.
- **X-Type Attachment (3.2.7.1)** — Method where the supply cord can be replaced using **ordinary tools** without disturbing the appliance.
- **Y-Type Attachment (3.2.7.2)** — Method where the supply cord can be replaced only by the **manufacturer, service agent, or skilled person**.
- **Z-Type Attachment (3.2.7.3)** — Method where the supply cord **cannot be replaced without breaking the appliance**.

> [!NOTE]
> The X, Y, Z classification is a **classic KTU short-answer question** because it is short, clause-specific, and rewards precise recall.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Definitions — Engineering Rationale

| Definition Group | Engineering Rationale ("Why") | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated quantities (V, I, P, f)** | Provide a single, reproducible reference point for all tests. | All subsequent tests (heating, leakage, dielectric) are run at these values. |
| **Insulation classes (0/I/II/III)** | Translate abstract protection philosophy into a *testable* design rule. | Class II devices skip the earth-bond test entirely; Class III limits voltage to SELV. |
| **Live / Accessible / Hazardous Live** | Define *what the user can touch* and *what can hurt them*. | The standard test finger (Figure 1) is used; SELV thresholds become legal limits. |
| **IP code (IS/IEC 60529)** | Quantify environmental protection in a two-digit code. | IP24 means ≥12.5 mm object ingress + splashing water protection. |
| **Appliance categories (portable/hand-held/...)** | Recognise that risk profile depends on *how* the appliance is used. | Hand-held appliances are subject to additional drop tests. |
| **Thermal control devices** | Distinguish *resettable* vs *single-shot* protections. | Determines whether a fault causes a one-time burn-out or auto-recovery. |
| **Cord attachments (X/Y/Z)** | Indicate who is qualified to replace the cord. | Drives the IP and mechanical strength tests on the cord entry. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas in this section are **derived from the normative equations in IS 302-1** and the test-current calculations in **IS/IEC 60529** and **IS/IEC 60950-1 / 62368-1** for SELV/PELV. The *boundary values* (e.g., 42.4 V, 30 mA, 0.5 mA) are **legal thresholds** — memorise them.

### 2.3.1 SELV / PELV Voltage Thresholds (Clause 3.3.10)

$$V_{\text{SELV, peak}} \leq 42.4 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{SELV, DC}} \leq 60.0 \text{ V}$$

### 2.3.2 Rated Power Relationship

The rated power is the **arithmetic mean** measured under normal operation at rated voltage:

$$P_{\text{rated}} = V_{\text{rated}} \times I_{\text{rated}} \times \cos\phi$$

For purely resistive loads (e.g., heaters):

$$P_{\text{rated}} = V_{\text{rated}} \times I_{\text{rated}}$$

### 2.3.3 Tolerance on Rated Power Input (Clause 4.2)

$$P_{\text{measured}} = P_{\text{rated}} \pm \Delta P$$

$$\Delta P = \begin{cases} 0.20 \cdot P_{\text{rated}}, & \text{if } P_{\text{rated}} \leq 25 \text{ W} \\ 0.10 \cdot P_{\text{rated}}, & \text{if } 25 \text{ W} < P_{\text{rated}} \leq 300 \text{ W} \\ 0.05 \cdot P_{\text{rated}}, & \text{if } P_{\text{rated}} > 300 \text{ W} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.4 IP Code Decode (IS/IEC 60529)

$$\text{IP}\,XY$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Range |
|---|---|---|
| X (0–6) | Solid-object protection | 0 = none, 6 = dust-tight |
| Y (0–9K) | Water protection | 0 = none, 9K = high-pressure hot water |

**Example:** IP44 → solid ≥1.0 mm + splashing water from any direction.

### 2.3.5 Leakage Current Limit (Clause 13)

$$I_{\text{leak, max}} \leq 0.75 \text{ mA (Class II portable)}$$

$$I_{\text{leak, max}} \leq 3.5 \text{ mA (Class I stationary)}$$

### 2.3.6 Dielectric Test Voltage (Clause 16)

$$V_{\text{test}} = 500 \text{ V}_{\text{rms}} + (2 \times V_{\text{rated}})$$

Applied for **1 minute** between live parts and accessible conductive parts.

### 2.3.7 Master Cheat-Sheet Table

| Term | Clause | One-line Meaning | Limit / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rated Voltage | 3.1.1 | Voltage assigned by manufacturer | $\leq 250$ V single-phase |
| Rated Power | 3.1.3 | Power at rated V, normal op | tolerance per §4.2 |
| Class I | 3.3.8 | Basic insulation + earth | earth-bond $\leq 0.1\,\Omega$ |
| Class II | 3.3.9 | Double or reinforced insulation | symbol $\square$ mandatory |
| Class III | 3.3.10 | SELV-powered | $\leq 42.4$ V peak |
| Live Part | 3.3.16 | Energised conductor | includes neutral |
| Accessible Part | 3.3.15 | Reachable by test finger | Figure 1 probe |
| Basic Insulation | 3.3.2 | First-level protection | dielectric per §16 |
| Double Insulation | 3.3.4 | Basic + Supplementary | — |
| Reinforced Insulation | 3.3.5 | Single equivalent of double | — |
| IP Code | 3.5.1 | Ingress protection | per IS/IEC 60529 |
| X-Attachment | 3.2.7.1 | Cord repl. by ordinary tools | user-replaceable |
| Y-Attachment | 3.2.7.2 | Cord repl. by skilled person | manufacturer only |
| Z-Attachment | 3.2.7.3 | Cord not repl. without breakage | service-disposable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Strategy Note:** When asked *"Define Class II appliance"*, do **not** write only the textbook definition — also state the **consequences** (no earth pin, double-rectangle symbol, leakage limit 0.25 mA). Examiners reward the *engineering consequence* with bonus marks.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the *logical derivations and identification algorithms* that the KTU examiner expects a 14-mark question to demonstrate. The emphasis is on **systematic procedure**, not memorisation.

## 3.1 Algorithm — How to Classify an Appliance (Step-by-Step)

The KTU Part B question frequently asks: *"Classify the given appliance as Class I / II / III and justify."* Use the following **four-step algorithm**.

> **Step 0 — Read the appliance label carefully.** Note the rated voltage, rated power, and any protection class symbol.

**Step 1 — Inspect the mains plug / cord.**

- A **3-pin plug** (with dedicated earth pin) → continue to Step 2.
- A **2-pin plug** (no earth pin) → candidate for Class II or Class III.
- **Wired permanently** → open the terminal box; check for earth terminal.

**Step 2 — Open the enclosure and trace the insulation scheme.**

- Trace the **primary** side of the transformer or mains-energised circuits.
- Count the **layers of insulation** between the live mains and any part a user could touch:
  - **One** layer → Class I candidate.
  - **Two independent** layers → Class II candidate.
  - **One reinforced** layer → Class II candidate.
  - **Transformer-isolated SELV side only** → Class III candidate.

**Step 3 — Measure the secondary voltage (if applicable).**

$$V_{\text{sec,peak}} \leq 42.4 \text{ V (AC)} \quad \text{or} \quad V_{\text{sec,DC}} \leq 60 \text{ V}$$

If yes → **Class III**.

**Step 4 — Look for the marking symbol on the nameplate.**

- Square within a square $\square$ → **Class II**.
- Earth-pin symbol $\oplus$ near the terminal → **Class I earthing point**.
- No symbol, single layer, no earth → **Class 0** (legacy, generally non-compliant for new products in India).

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Table (Deterministic):**

| Earth Pin Present? | Layers of Insulation | Secondary Voltage | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yes | 1 (basic) | Mains level | **Class I** |
| No | 2 (basic + supplementary) | Mains level | **Class II** |
| No | 1 (reinforced) | Mains level | **Class II** |
| No | 1 (functional) | $\leq 42.4$ V AC / 60 V DC | **Class III** |
| No | 1 (basic) | Mains level | **Class 0 (banned)** |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — IP Code Interpretation

The IP code is **not arbitrary**; it is decoded using the lookup table of **IS/IEC 60529**. A KTU 14-mark question may ask: *"Explain the meaning of IPX4 / IP44 / IP67 protection in the context of IS 302-1."*

> **Step 1 — Recognise the format** `IPXY` where X and Y are single digits (an "X" can replace a digit to mean "not tested for that aspect").

> **Step 2 — Decode the first digit (X = solid protection).**

| Digit | Solid Protection |
|---|---|
| 0 | No protection |
| 1 | $> 50$ mm (back of hand) |
| 2 | $> 12.5$ mm (finger) |
| 3 | $> 2.5$ mm (tools / wires) |
| 4 | $> 1.0$ mm (most wires) |
| 5 | Dust-protected (limited ingress) |
| 6 | Dust-tight (vacuum test) |

> **Step 3 — Decode the second digit (Y = water protection).**

| Digit | Water Protection |
|---|---|
| 0 | None |
| 1 | Vertical drips |
| 2 | Drips at 15° |
| 3 | Spraying water (60° from vertical) |
| 4 | Splashing from any direction |
| 5 | Water jets (6.3 mm nozzle) |
| 6 | Powerful jets (12.5 mm nozzle) |
| 7 | Temporary immersion (1 m, 30 min) |
| 8 | Continuous immersion (specified by mfr) |
| 9K | High-pressure, high-temperature water |

> **Step 4 — Apply the example.**

For **IP44**:
- First digit **4** → protected against solid objects $> 1.0$ mm (most wires, thin tools).
- Second digit **4** → protected against splashing water from any direction.
- **Engineering reading:** Suitable for **bathroom zones** (excluding direct water jets), where splash from sinks/showers is expected.

For **IP67**:
- First digit **6** → dust-tight.
- Second digit **7** → temporary immersion up to 1 m depth for 30 min.
- **Engineering reading:** Suitable for **outdoor garden equipment**, *not* for prolonged underwater use.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Leakage Current Calculation

The leakage-current test (Clause 13) follows a **deterministic algorithm**:

> **Step 1 — Energise** the appliance at **1.06 × rated voltage** (a worst-case over-voltage condition).
>
> **Step 2 — Connect** the leakage measurement network (Figure 2 of IS 302-1) between the accessible conductive parts and earth.
>
> **Step 3 — Measure** the leakage current in **mA** using the specified RC network ($\approx 1\,750\,\Omega$ in series with $0.022\,\mu F$).
>
> **Step 4 — Compare** with the **class-dependent limit** below:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{leak,limit}} &= 0.75 \text{ mA} & \text{Class II portable} \\
I_{\text{leak,limit}} &= 3.50 \text{ mA} & \text{Class I stationary} \\
I_{\text{leak,limit}} &= 0.25 \text{ mA} & \text{Class II stationary heating appliance} \\
I_{\text{leak,limit}} &= 0.50 \text{ mA} & \text{Class 0 portable}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — Decision rule**:
> - If $I_{\text{measured}} \leq I_{\text{leak,limit}}$ → **PASS**.
> - If $I_{\text{measured}} > I_{\text{leak,limit}}$ → **FAIL**; appliance cannot be certified.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Tolerance on Rated Power

A **Part-A 3-mark** question often asks: *"What is the tolerance on rated power input for a 2 kW mixer grinder?"*

> **Step 1 — Identify the rating class.** For $P_{\text{rated}} = 2000 \text{ W} > 300 \text{ W}$, the tolerance bracket is **$\pm 5\%$**.

> **Step 2 — Calculate the allowable deviation.**

$$\Delta P = 0.05 \times 2000 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ W}$$

> **Step 3 — Express the acceptance window.**

$$P_{\text{acceptable}} \in \left[ 1900 \text{ W}, \; 2100 \text{ W} \right]$$

If the lab measures $P_{\text{actual}} = 1850 \text{ W}$ at rated voltage and normal operation, the appliance **fails** Clause 4.2, because $1850 \text{ W} < 1900 \text{ W}$.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Identification — X / Y / Z Cord Attachment

> **Step 1 — Visual check** of the cord-entry area and terminal block.

> **Step 2 — Try to release the cord using the ordinary tool kit** (screwdriver, pliers) provided to a layman.

- If removable **without breaking** the appliance → **X-type**.
- If not removable **without specialised tools** → continue to Step 3.

> **Step 3 — Check the user-replacement policy** in the user manual / nameplate.

- Only authorised service agent can replace → **Y-type**.
- The user is explicitly told the cord is **non-replaceable** → **Z-type**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Term-to-Icon Cross-Reference

| Term | Icon on Appliance / Plug | Clause | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class I | $\oplus$ (earth) | 3.3.8 | earth-bond test mandatory |
| Class II | $\square$ (square-in-square) | 3.3.9 | double insulation, no earth |
| Class III | (no specific symbol) | 3.3.10 | SELV supply only |
| Hazardous voltage | $\triangle$ with lightning bolt | 3.3.17 | warning marking required |
| Indoor use only | (crossed-out umbrella) | 5.7 | not for outdoor |
| Read instructions | $\square$ with book | 5.4 | mandatory user-info |
| IP code | $\text{IP}\,XY$ | 3.5.1 | enclosure ingress rating |

## 4.1 High-Level Mermaid Block Diagram — Clause 3 Taxonomy of IS 302-1 Definitions

The following diagram maps the **logical organisation of definitions** in IS 302-1. It is a block-level functional architecture, since the standard itself is a *document* and a physical drawing would not be useful.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IS 302 Part 1 Clause 3 Definitions] --> B[Rated Quantities]
    A --> C[Insulation and Classes]
    A --> D[Live and Accessible Parts]
    A --> E[Ingress Protection]

    A --> G[Thermal Control Devices]
    A --> H[Supply Connections]

    B --> B1[Rated Voltage 3.1.1]
    B --> B2[Rated Power 3.1.3]
    B --> B3[Rated Current 3.1.2]
    B --> B4[Rated Frequency 3.1.4]
    B --> B5[Normal Operation 3.1.9]

    C --> C1[Functional Insulation 3.3.1]
    C --> C2[Basic Insulation 3.3.2]
    C --> C3[Supplementary Insulation 3.3.3]
    C --> C4[Double Insulation 3.3.4]
    C --> C5[Reinforced Insulation 3.3.5]
    C --> C6[Class 0 I II III]

    D --> D1[Live Part 3.3.16]
    D --> D2[Accessible Part 3.3.15]
    D --> D3[Hazardous Live Part 3.3.17]
    D --> D4[Test Finger Figure 1]

    E --> E1[IP Code 3.5.1]
    E --> E2[Solid Object Protection X]
    E --> E3[Water Protection Y]

    F --> F1[Portable 3.5.4]
    F --> F2[Hand Held 3.5.5]
    F --> F3[Stationary 3.5.6]
    F --> F4[Fixed 3.5.7]
    F --> F5[Built in 3.5.8]

    G --> G1[Thermal Cut Out 3.4.4]
    G --> G2[Thermal Link 3.4.6]
    G --> G3[PTC Heater 3.4.13]

    H --> H1[Supply Cord 3.2.4]

    H --> H3[Y Attachment 3.2.7.2]
    H --> H4[Z Attachment 3.2.7.3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in the diagram express the **"is-a" / "category-of"** relationship. For example, *Class II* is-a *Insulation-and-Class* definition. This taxonomy is what allows BIS test engineers to **locate any definition in seconds**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Safety Test Workflow Using These Definitions

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Step 1 Identify Rated V I P f] --> step2[Step 2 Classify Class I II III]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Identify Live and Accessible Parts]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Determine IP Code per 60529]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Apply Cord Attachment Rule X Y Z]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Run Tests Heating Leakage Dielectric]
    step6 --> step7{All Clause Limits Met}
    step7 -->|Yes| step8[Issue BIS ISI or CRS Certificate]
    step7 -->|No| step9[Fail Redesign Recertify]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Insulation Hierarchy (Class II Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[Live Part 3.3.16] --> FI[Functional Insulation 3.3.1]
    FI --> BI[Basic Insulation 3.3.2]
    BI --> SI[Supplementary Insulation 3.3.3]
    BI --> RI[Reinforced Insulation 3.3.5 alternative to SI]
    SI --> AP[Accessible Part 3.3.15]
    RI --> AP
    AP --> TF[Test Finger Figure 1 Cannot Penetrate]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reading of the Insulation Diagram:**
> - A **Class II** appliance must have **either** *Basic + Supplementary* **or** *Reinforced* insulation between the live part and any accessible part.
> - A **Class I** appliance has only *Basic* insulation, but adds a **protective earth** as a second-line safeguard.
> - A **Class III** appliance operates entirely in the SELV range, where insulation is **functional** only.

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Definition Lookup During a Test

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Eng as Test Engineer
    participant Std as IS 302-1 Clause 3
    participant App as Appliance Under Test
    Eng->>App: 1. Read nameplate ratings
    Eng->>Std: 2. Look up definitions 3.1.1 to 3.1.9
    Std-->>Eng: Returns rated V I P f
    Eng->>App: 3. Open enclosure
    Eng->>Std: 4. Identify insulation class 3.3.7 to 3.3.10
    Std-->>Eng: Returns Class I II or III
    Eng->>App: 5. Probe with test finger Figure 1
    Eng->>Std: 6. Decide accessible vs live vs hazardous live
    Eng->>Std: 7. Read IP code 3.5.1 and decode via 60529
    Eng->>App: 8. Conduct tests Clauses 7 to 32
```

> [!NOTE]
> This sequence is exactly what the **BIS National Test House (NTH)** follows during a CRS conformity assessment.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the clause number** — quoting the definition *without* the clause reference (e.g., 3.3.8) loses **1 mark** out of 3.
> 2. **Conflating Class II with Class III** — students often write "Class II means no earthing" but then wrongly claim it operates at SELV. The *correct* answer must separate *no-earth* (Class II) from *low-voltage* (Class III).
> 3. **Reversing X, Y, Z** — X is **user-replaceable**; Z is **non-replaceable**. Reversing these is a guaranteed zero.
> 4. **Treating "live part" and "accessible part" as synonyms** — they are different categories; *live* is electrical, *accessible* is mechanical.
> 5. **Missing the IP code digit ordering** — first digit is **solid**, second is **water**. The standard reads *left-to-right*.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Class II appliance" as per IS 302-1. State the marking symbol used on such appliances and the insulation philosophy it relies on.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** As per IS 302-1 Clause 3.3.9, a *Class II appliance* is one in which protection against electric shock does not rely on basic insulation only, but on **double insulation or reinforced insulation**. No provision for protective earthing is provided.
- **Marking Symbol (1 mark):** The mandatory marking symbol is a **square within a square ($\square$)**, which must be present on the nameplate.
- **Insulation Philosophy (1 mark):** Protection is achieved by **two independent insulation layers** (Basic + Supplementary) *or* a single **Reinforced Insulation** of equivalent protective strength, ensuring that a single fault does not expose the user to a hazardous live part.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the meaning of "X-type", "Y-type", and "Z-type" attachment of supply cords as defined in IS 302-1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
- **X-type attachment (Clause 3.2.7.1):** The supply cord can be **replaced by the user** using ordinary tools (e.g., a screwdriver) without damaging the appliance. Suitable for general domestic appliances.
- **Y-type attachment (Clause 3.2.7.2):** The supply cord can be replaced **only by the manufacturer, its service agent, or a similarly qualified person** to avoid safety hazards. Common in high-power heaters.
- **Z-type attachment (Clause 3.2.7.3):** The supply cord **cannot be replaced** without breaking or destroying the appliance. The appliance is intended to be discarded when the cord is damaged.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format Note (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts typically carrying **7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)** may be independent or sequential.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With reference to IS 302-1, define and differentiate between **basic insulation, supplementary insulation, double insulation, and reinforced insulation**. Use a single labelled diagram to show how these insulations are arranged in a Class II appliance.

> **(b) [7 marks]** A manufacturer submits a tabletop induction cooktop with the following nameplate data:
> *Rated voltage: 230 V AC, Rated power: 2000 W, Rated frequency: 50 Hz, Class II, IPX4.*
> **(i)** Verify whether the rated power tolerance of $\pm 5\%$ is met if the measured power is 1920 W.
> **(ii)** Comment on the suitability of the IP rating for kitchen-environment usage.
> **(iii)** Identify, with justification, the test finger and the leakage-current limit applicable to this appliance.

#### Model Solution to Q.A

**(a) Insulation Definitions and Class II Arrangement (7 marks):**

| Insulation Type | Clause | Definition | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic Insulation | 3.3.2 | Insulation applied to live parts to provide **basic protection** against electric shock. | 1.5 |
| Supplementary Insulation | 3.3.3 | Independent insulation applied **in addition to** basic insulation to provide protection in case basic insulation fails. | 1.5 |
| Double Insulation | 3.3.4 | Insulation system comprising **both basic and supplementary insulation**. | 1.0 |
| Reinforced Insulation | 3.3.5 | Single insulation system providing protection **equivalent to double insulation**. | 1.0 |
| Diagram (Class II arrangement) | — | Live → Basic → Supplementary → Accessible, OR Live → Reinforced → Accessible. | 2.0 |

**Labelled sketch description (for the 2-mark diagram):**
- Draw a **horizontal line** labelled "Live part (mains conductor)".
- Place a **diagonal hatched block** labelled *Basic Insulation* directly on the line.
- Place a **second diagonal hatched block** labelled *Supplementary Insulation* next to it.
- Indicate the **accessible conductive/outer part** on the user-facing side.
- An alternative path: replace the two blocks with a **single thick block** labelled *Reinforced Insulation* and note the equivalence.

**(b) Cooktop Numerical and Justification (7 marks):**

**(i) Tolerance check (3 marks):**
- Rated power $P_{\text{rated}} = 2000$ W (which is $> 300$ W) → tolerance $\pm 5\%$.
- Allowable deviation: $\Delta P = 0.05 \times 2000 = 100$ W.
- Acceptance window: $P_{\text{acceptable}} \in [1900, 2100]$ W.
- Measured $P_{\text{measured}} = 1920$ W $\Rightarrow 1900 \le 1920 \le 2100$ → **WITHIN tolerance → PASS.**

**(ii) IPX4 suitability (2 marks):**
- IPX4 → "X" means solid-object protection **not declared**; "4" means **splashing water from any direction** is permitted.
- For a kitchen cooktop, splashing water from utensils, sinks, and the user's hands is the *primary* hazard.
- **Conclusion:** IPX4 is **adequate** for typical kitchen-counter use, *provided* the unit is not exposed to direct water jets or immersion. A higher rating (e.g., IPX5) is recommended for commercial kitchens.

**(iii) Test finger and leakage limit (2 marks):**
- The **standard test finger (Figure 1 of IS 302-1)** with a 10 N applied force is used to probe all openings, joints, and controls.
- Since the appliance is **Class II, portable**: the applicable **leakage-current limit is $\leq 0.75$ mA** at **1.06 × rated voltage** (i.e., 243.8 V).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Define the following terms as per IS 302-1, with their clause numbers:
> **(i)** Hazardous live part
> **(ii)** Rated voltage
> **(iii)** Normal operation
> **(iv)** IP code
> **(v)** Thermal cut-out
> **(vi)** SELV
> **(vii)** Portable appliance

> **(b) [7 marks]** A domestic **refrigerator** has the following specifications:
> *Rated voltage: 230 V, Rated current: 1.5 A, Class I, IP20, hand-held = NO, stationary = YES.*
> **(i)** Calculate the **rated power input** of the refrigerator.
> **(ii)** Determine the **leakage current limit** that must be satisfied at the test voltage of $1.06 \times V_{\text{rated}}$.
> **(iii)** Explain why the leakage limit for a Class I stationary appliance differs from that of a Class II portable appliance, citing the engineering rationale.

#### Model Solution to Q.B

**(a) Definitions (7 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Term | Clause | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Hazardous live part | 3.3.17 | A live part that could cause an electric shock under specified conditions. |
| (ii) | Rated voltage | 3.1.1 | The supply voltage assigned to the appliance by the manufacturer. |
| (iii) | Normal operation | 3.1.9 | The condition of operating the appliance in the way indicated by the manufacturer, with the supply at rated voltage, and the appliance performing its intended function. |
| (iv) | IP code | 3.5.1 | A coding system indicating the degree of protection provided by an enclosure against access to hazardous parts, ingress of solid foreign objects, and water, in accordance with IS/IEC 60529. |
| (v) | Thermal cut-out | 3.4.4 | A device that limits the temperature of the appliance by automatically opening the circuit, with no user-operable set point. |
| (vi) | SELV | (Safety Extra-Low Voltage) | Voltage not exceeding 42.4 V peak AC or 60 V DC between conductors and between any conductor and earth, supplied by an isolating source such as a safety isolating transformer. |
| (vii) | Portable appliance | 3.5.4 | An appliance that can be moved while in operation, or that can be easily moved from one place to another while connected to the supply. |

**(b) Refrigerator Numerical and Engineering Rationale (7 marks):**

**(i) Rated power input (2 marks):**
- The refrigerator is single-phase, so we use $P = V \times I$ (resistive assumption for rated-value calculation is standard when $\cos\phi$ is not specified in nameplate).
- Therefore:
$$P_{\text{rated}} = 230 \text{ V} \times 1.5 \text{ A} = 345 \text{ W}$$
- Since $P_{\text{rated}} > 300$ W, the **tolerance is $\pm 5\%$**, i.e., the acceptable range is $\left[ 327.75 \text{ W}, \; 362.25 \text{ W} \right]$.

**(ii) Leakage current limit (2 marks):**
- Class I + stationary → $I_{\text{leak,limit}} = 3.5$ mA (per Clause 13 of IS 302-1).
- Test voltage:
$$V_{\text{test}} = 1.06 \times 230 = 243.8 \text{ V}_{\text{rms}}$$
- The test is performed with the leakage measurement network of Figure 2 connected between earthed accessible parts and the supply neutral/earth.

**(iii) Engineering rationale (3 marks):**
- **Class I** appliances have an **earthed accessible part** (e.g., the metal cabinet of a refrigerator). A single insulation failure diverts fault current through the earth conductor to trip the upstream protective device. Hence a **larger** leakage current is tolerated (3.5 mA) because the protection philosophy already includes a *first line of defence* (basic insulation) and a *second line* (earth).
- **Class II portable** appliances have **no earth**. A fault current must be blocked by the **two independent insulation layers** alone; the second line of defence does not exist. Therefore the **leakage limit is much lower** (0.75 mA) to ensure that even in a degraded state the user is not exposed to a hazardous current.
- **Stationary** appliances are typically used in fixed, less-handled locations, so the exposure profile is gentler than that of a **portable** appliance; this further widens the difference in permissible leakage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024):**

- **IS 302-1 = Indian implementation of IEC 60335-1.** It is the *Part 1 — General Requirements*. Always read it together with the relevant *Part 2* standard for the specific appliance.
- **Clause 3 is the single largest source of definitional questions.** Memorise the **clause numbers** alongside the term — they earn extra marks.
- **Four core insulation terms** (Basic, Supplementary, Double, Reinforced) form the language of *every* safety test. *Class II* demands *Double OR Reinforced*; *Class I* has *Basic + Earth*; *Class III* operates at *SELV* ($\leq 42.4$ V peak / 60 V DC).
- **"Live" vs "Accessible" is the most confused pair.** *Live* = electrical energisation; *Accessible* = physically reachable by the **Figure 1 test finger** with 10 N force.
- **IP Code order is fixed: solids first, water second.** `IP44` ≠ `IP44` reversed.
- **Cord attachment X / Y / Z:** X is user-replaceable; Y is service-replaceable; Z is non-replaceable. Remember: *X* = *X*pert (layman).
- **Rated power tolerance** is **$\pm 20\% \le 25$ W; $\pm 10\%$ for $25$–$300$ W; $\pm 5\%$ for $> 300$ W.** Always apply the correct bracket.
- **Leakage-current test** is conducted at **$1.06 \times V_{\text{rated}}$**; limits are class- and type-dependent.
- **PTC-S** is a *self-regulating* heater; **Thermal Link** is a *one-shot* device; **Thermal Cut-out** is *auto-resettable*. Know the difference — it is a frequent 3-mark question.
- **Marking symbols** (square-in-square $\square$, earth $\oplus$, triangle with lightning, read-manual book) are normative requirements under Clause 7 of IS 302-1. Missing a mandatory marking = automatic fail.
- **Safety is verified, not assumed.** Every definition in Clause 3 is the *inlet* to a test in Clauses 4–32. Understanding the definitions is therefore the *first step* in becoming a competent test engineer.
- **Real-world implication:** Every consumer electronic product sold in India under the **BIS Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS)** for electronics must satisfy IS 302-1 definitions and the corresponding tests. Engineers who understand these definitions are **directly employable** in QA/regulatory roles at appliance manufacturers and BIS test labs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protection Against Shock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protection Against Shock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Electrical Shock?

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEEVT612 – Electronic Hardware for Engineers**, *Protection Against Shock* refers to the set of engineering design principles, safety devices, and grounding strategies used to prevent harmful physiological effects on the human body when it comes into accidental contact with energized electrical conductors.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Electrical Shock** is the physiological stimulation and tissue damage caused by the passage of an electric current through the human body, resulting from an unintentional contact between the body and a live conductor or a conductive surface that has risen to a hazardous potential due to a fault.

**Two Primary Classifications Recognized by the Syllabus:**

| Classification | Definition | Typical Source |
|----------------|------------|----------------|
| **Macro Shock** | Current flowing through the body from an external contact point to another body part (typically hand-to-hand or hand-to-foot) | Household mains, industrial supply |
| **Micro Shock** | Very small currents (in the microampere range) applied directly to the heart via catheters or internal electrodes | Medical equipment, patient monitoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Protection against shock is the single most safety-critical topic in the entire hardware syllabus. Even though it carries modest marks in ESE, it is mandatory in design-oriented questions and lab viva assessments.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water-Pressure Pipe"

Imagine your house's water supply as a series of pipes. Normally, water flows inside the pipes. But if a pipe bursts, water sprays everywhere and can hurt you — not because of the water itself, but because of the **pressure (voltage)** and the **flow (current)**. 

Now imagine placing a **safety valve** that immediately shuts off the water if a burst is detected. This valve is exactly what a **Residual Current Device (RCD)** does for electricity. Similarly, providing a **drainage path** (earthing) is like providing an emergency exit for the current, so it never chooses the human body as its path.

The body is **not harmed by voltage alone**; it is the **current that flows through it** that damages tissue, stops the heart, or causes burns. This is the central idea behind the famous clinical saying: *"It is the current that kills, not the voltage."*

## 1.3 Key Physiological Thresholds (Must Memorize)

| Threshold | Current (AC 50/60 Hz) | Effect |
|-----------|------------------------|--------|
| **Perception Threshold** | **1 mA** | Faint tingle |
| **Let-Go Threshold (Men)** | **10 mA** | Muscular paralysis, cannot release |
| **Let-Go Threshold (Women)** | **6 mA** | Same as above |
| **Respiratory Paralysis** | **30 mA** | Breathing stops |
| **Ventricular Fibrillation** | **100 mA** for >1 s | Heart goes into chaos |
| **Severe Burns** | >5 A | Tissue destruction |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard reference body for shock calculations is **IEC 60990**, and the assumed internal body impedance is roughly **1 kΩ (hand-to-hand)**. The KTU examiner expects you to use this value in numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why Protection is Needed — The Engineering Perspective

Modern electronic hardware is compact, uses switching power supplies, and is often interconnected to mains. Faults such as **insulation breakdown**, **solder bridges**, or **loose wiring** can energize the metal chassis. Without protection, a user simply touching the device would complete a circuit to ground through their own body — possibly leading to a **lethal 230 V, 50 Hz shock** in Indian/Kerala domestic supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle of All Shock Protection:** *Ensure that under any single fault condition, the touch voltage across the human body never exceeds 50 V AC (or 120 V DC) — the international safe-touch voltage limit per IEC 60364.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Body as an Electrical Model

The human body is modeled as a network of **resistors and capacitors** in the IEC 60990 standard. The simplified model is:

- **Skin resistance (R_skin):** Outer dry skin ≈ **100 kΩ to 1 MΩ**
- **Internal body resistance (R_internal):** ≈ **300 Ω (hand-to-foot)**
- **Total body impedance (Z_body):** ≈ **1 kΩ to 2 kΩ** for mains frequency

> The total impedance is the parallel/series combination of skin layers and internal tissues. At 50/60 Hz, the **capacitive reactance** is small compared to the resistance, so we treat the body as a **pure resistance for engineering calculations**.

## 2.2 How Current Affects the Body — Structured Logic

**Step 1 — Contact is made:**
A person touches a live conductor (e.g., 230 V AC). The line voltage drives current through the body to ground.

**Step 2 — Current calculation:**
Using Ohm's Law, the body current is computed.

**Step 3 — Frequency-dependent sensitivity:**
The human body is most sensitive at **50/60 Hz** — exactly the mains frequency. At DC, the let-go current is higher (~30–40 mA), and at high frequency (>100 kHz), the sensation decreases.

**Step 4 — Path through the body matters:**
Current passing **hand-to-hand** or **hand-to-foot** passes through the heart. Current flowing from foot to foot passes through less critical tissue.

**Step 5 — Duration matters:**
Even small currents become lethal if sustained. The **I·t product** (current × time) determines fibrillation probability.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{Z_{body}}$ | Body current under fault | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $Z_{body} \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (hand-hand) | Body impedance at 50 Hz | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | $V_{safe} \le 50 \text{ V AC}$ (or 120 V DC) | Safe touch voltage per IEC 60364 | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $I_{let-go} = 10 \text{ mA}$ (men), $6 \text{ mA}$ (women) | Let-go threshold at 50 Hz | Milliamperes (mA) |
| 5 | $I_{fibrillation} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{t}}$ (with $K \approx 116$ for hand-to-foot) | Body's current limit curve | A·s$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $R_{earth} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi a}$ (single rod, deep earth) | Earth electrode resistance (hemispherical) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $R_{combined} = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n}{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n}$ (parallel rods) | Multiple earth electrodes in parallel | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $V_{step} = E_s \cdot \dfrac{b}{a}$ (potential divider) | Step potential near earth electrode | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $V_{touch} = E_t \cdot \dfrac{R_{body}}{R_{body} + R_{e,\text{foot}}}$ | Touch potential at fault | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $t_{trip} = \dfrac{K}{I_{\Delta n}^{n}}$ (RCD characteristic) | RCD operating time (n ≈ 0.5 to 1) | Seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vertical pipes `|` are avoided in the table:** The KTU formula sheet uses `\\dfrac` and explicit LaTeX fractions to ensure compatibility with all markdown renderers and the KTU digital document portal. The vertical bar is reserved for cell delimiters in markdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Wiring:** The 3-pin plug (Live, Neutral, Earth) implements the protective earthing scheme. The third pin is longer/thicker to ensure earth connects **first** and disconnects **last**.
- **Medical Electronics (IEC 60601-1):** Patient-connected equipment uses *defibrillator-proof* connectors, isolated power supplies, and a *patient-equipotential bonding* network. Macro-shock protection is taken to *micro-shock* levels (10 µA limit).
- **Industrial Control Panels:** Equipotential bonding ensures that all simultaneously accessible metal parts are at the same potential, so no shock is felt between them.
- **Telecom & IT Equipment:** Class II (double-insulated) and Class III (SELV — Safety Extra-Low Voltage) are used for laptops, mobile chargers, and modems.

## 2.5 Classes of Equipment (KTU Favorite)

| Class | Symbol | Protection Method | Earth Pin? |
|-------|--------|-------------------|------------|
| **Class I** | ⏚ | Basic insulation **+** Protective Earth | Yes |
| **Class II** | ⛶ (double square) | Double or Reinforced Insulation | No |
| **Class III** | ◇ (diamond inside) | SELV (≤50 V AC) supplied | No |
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"What class should a bedside lamp be?"* the answer is **Class II** or **Class III**. Never Class I, because the user may be in contact with a grounded water pipe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Body Current Under Mains Fault

**Problem Statement:** A person with body impedance of 1 kΩ (hand-to-hand, dry skin) accidentally touches a 230 V, 50 Hz live wire. Calculate the body current, and comment on its physiological effect.

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit.**
The body completes the path from the live wire to ground. The body impedance is modeled as a single resistor $R_{body} \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ at 50 Hz (capacitive effects neglected).

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{body} &= \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}} \\
         &= \frac{230 \text{ V}}{1000 \text{ }\Omega} \\
         &= 0.230 \text{ A} \\
         &= 230 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on physiological effect.**
A current of **230 mA** is **far above the ventricular fibrillation threshold of 100 mA for duration > 1 s**. Therefore, this contact is **potentially lethal**. The person may suffer:
- Severe muscular contraction
- Respiratory paralysis
- Ventricular fibrillation (cardiac arrest)
- Deep burns at contact points

**Step 4 — Engineering fix.**
Limit the touch voltage to ≤ 50 V AC (use an isolating transformer), or use a 30 mA RCD that trips within 30 ms to prevent fibrillation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Earth Electrode Resistance (Hemispherical Model)

A single earth rod is buried vertically. For a hemispherical electrode of radius $a$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$, the resistance of the earth path is found by integrating the radial resistance of concentric hemispherical shells.

**Step 1 — Elemental shell of thickness $dr$ at radius $r$.**
The cross-sectional area through which current flows is the surface of a hemisphere:

$$
A(r) = 2 \pi r^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Elemental resistance of the shell.**

$$
dR = \frac{\rho \, dr}{A(r)} = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2 \pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $r = a$ to $r = \infty$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth} &= \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2 \pi r^{2}} \\
         &= \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \int_{a}^{\infty} r^{-2} \, dr \\
         &= \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_{a}^{\infty} \\
         &= \frac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left( 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{a}\right) \right) \\
         &= \frac{\rho}{2 \pi a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for a typical installation.**
Take $\rho = 100 \text{ }\Omega\text{-m}$ (clay loam, Kerala average) and $a = 0.0125 \text{ m}$ (a 25 mm diameter rod).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth} &= \frac{100}{2 \pi (0.0125)} \\
         &= \frac{100}{0.07854} \\
         &\approx 1273 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

This is too high. **Solution:** Use multiple parallel rods or a longer rod.

**Step 5 — Two parallel rods (separated by > rod length):**
For two identical rods in parallel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{parallel} &= \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R}{2} \\
             &= \frac{1273}{2} \\
             &\approx 636.5 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

For four parallel rods: $R \approx 318 \text{ }\Omega$. KTU standard: Earth resistance should be **< 5 Ω** for domestic, **< 1 Ω** for substations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Step Potential and Touch Potential

When a fault current $I_f$ flows into the earth through a grid electrode, the soil around it rises in potential. The **touch potential** is the voltage between the hand (touching a structure at fault potential) and the feet (on the soil at some distance). The **step potential** is the voltage between two feet spaced 1 m apart.

**Step 1 — Define the soil potential profile.**
For a single rod, the potential at distance $x$ from the rod is:

$$
V(x) = \frac{\rho I_f}{2 \pi x}
$$

**Step 2 — Step potential calculation (1 m stride, distance $x$ from rod).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{step} &= V(x) - V(x + 1) \\
         &= \frac{\rho I_f}{2 \pi} \left( \frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{x+1} \right) \\
         &= \frac{\rho I_f}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{x(x+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Touch potential calculation (hand on rod, feet at 1 m).**

$$
V_{touch} = V(0) - V(1) = \frac{\rho I_f}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{\rho I_f}{2 \pi}
$$

> This shows that **step potential is always lower than touch potential** at the same point, because the foot-to-foot distance is small (1 m). Engineers place the **earth grid** below grade and use **high-resistance gravel** on the surface to limit these potentials during substation faults.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Protection

| Situation | Hazard | Recommended Protection |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|
| Hand-held metal tool on 230 V | Lethal macro-shock | **Class I + 30 mA RCD** |
| Laptop charger on the bed | Macro-shock risk | **Class II double insulation** |
| Hospital bedside monitor | Micro-shock to patient | **Class I + Isolated power + 10 µA limit** |
| Outdoor lamp on metal post | Lightning-induced surges | **Class I + Surge Protection Device (SPD)** |
| Children's toy (battery powered) | Negligible | **Class III (SELV ≤ 5 V DC)** |
| Welding machine operator | Secondary contact | **Double insulation + reduced open-circuit voltage** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Protection Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the layered protection strategy that any electronic hardware device must implement. It maps the interactions between fault sources, protective elements, and the human user.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mains[MAINS 230V AC SUPPLY] -->|Live wire| Xfmr[Step Down Transformer]
    Xfmr -->|Secondary| Rect[Rectifier and Filter]
    Rect -->|DC Bus| PSU[Regulated DC Supply]
    PSU -->|Power Rails| Load[Electronic Load and Chassis]

    Load -.->|Insulation Fault| Chassis[Metal Chassis Energized at 230V]
    Chassis -.->|Touch| User((User Contact))

    PE1[Protective Earth PE Bond] -.->|Limits chassis voltage| Chassis
    DI[Class II Double Insulation] -.->|Blocks fault path| Chassis
    RCD[Residual Current Device 30mA] -.->|Trips in 30ms| Mains
    ISO[Isolating Transformer] -.->|Galvanic isolation| Xfmr
    SELV[SELV Supply 50V max] -.->|Safe touch| PSU

    User -->|Body current path| Earth[Earth Floor Ground]
    PE1 --> Earth
    RCD -->|Detects imbalance| Trip[Trips MCB within ms]

    style Mains fill:#ffe5e5
    style User fill:#ffcccc
    style PE1 fill:#ccffcc
    style DI fill:#ccffcc
    style RCD fill:#ccffcc
    style ISO fill:#ccffcc
    style SELV fill:#ccffcc
    style Earth fill:#dddddd
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The **red path** (Mains → Load → Chassis → User → Earth) represents an *unprotected* fault where the user becomes a current path.
- The **green blocks** represent the *protective layers* that either prevent the fault or interrupt it before the user is harmed.
- A well-designed device implements **at least two independent layers** of protection (Defence-in-Depth principle).

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — RCD Operation in a Fault

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User
    participant L as Live Wire
    participant R as RCD Core CT
    participant CB as Circuit Breaker
    participant N as Neutral
    participant E as Earth

    Note over L,N: Normal operation: I_L = I_N
    L->>R: Current IL enters
    R->>N: Same current returns
```
