```mermaid
    Note over R: Net flux = 0, CB stays ON

    Note over L,E: FAULT: Live touches chassis
    L->>R: Current IL
    R->>E: Leakage current I_leak to earth
    R-->>CB: Imbalance detected delta I = IL - IN > 30mA
    CB->>L: Trip coil opens contacts in less than 30ms
    Note over User: User is protected because trip time is below fibrillation threshold
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Equipment Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Class1: Mains powered + metal chassis
    Class1: CLASS I Basic Insulation plus PE
    Class1 --> Class2: Reinforced or Double Insulation added
    Class2: CLASS II No PE required
    Class2 --> Class3: Supply reduced to SELV less than 50V AC
    Class3: CLASS III Safe Extra Low Voltage
    Class3 --> [*]
    Class1 --> Medical: Patient coupled application
    Medical: MEDICAL CLASS I plus isolated power plus 10uA limit
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Definition Type
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define **electrical shock**. Distinguish between **macro shock** and **micro shock** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — examiner's key wording):**
> Electrical shock is the physiological response of the human body to the passage of an electric current. **Macro shock** is current applied externally through the skin (e.g., a person touching a 230 V live wire). It requires currents in the **mA range** to cause harm. **Micro shock** is current applied internally, e.g., through a cardiac catheter, and as little as **10 µA** can induce ventricular fibrillation. *(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark example)*

### Question A2 — Threshold Type
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the values of the **let-go threshold current** for men and women at 50 Hz. What is the **safe touch voltage** prescribed by IEC 60364?

**Model Answer:**
- Let-go threshold (men): **10 mA**, (women): **6 mA** *(1 mark)*
- Fibrillation threshold: **100 mA for > 1 s** *(1 mark)*
- Safe touch voltage: **≤ 50 V AC** or **≤ 120 V DC** *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **Note:** In KTU ESE, every Part B question has an internal choice. We provide both options below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Earthing & Earth Resistance Calculation

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **hemispherical model of an earth electrode** and derive the expression for earth resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sub-station has an earth electrode in soil of resistivity **200 Ω·m**. The fault current is **5 kA** for a duration of **1 second**. Calculate the **earth resistance** of a single rod of diameter 25 mm. If the safe touch voltage is 50 V, determine whether the design is acceptable. Suggest **two methods** to reduce the resistance if it is not. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:
1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a vertical rod of length $L$ and radius $a$ buried to depth $L/2$ to model as a hemisphere of radius $a$. Show radial current flow lines into the soil.
2. **Derivation (3 marks):** Show the steps as in Section 3.2: $dR = \rho \, dr \, / \, 2\pi r^{2}$, integrate from $a$ to $\infty$, obtain $R = \rho / 2\pi a$.
3. **Discussion (1 mark):** Mention that real rods are approximated using the same formula, with effective radius adjusted.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 — Compute earth resistance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{earth} &= \frac{\rho}{2 \pi a} = \frac{200}{2 \pi \times 0.0125} \\
         &= \frac{200}{0.07854} \\
         &\approx 2546 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for substitution + 1 mark for the numerical value)*

**Step 2 — Compute touch voltage during fault.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{touch} &= I_f \times R_{earth} \\
         &= 5000 \times 2546 \\
         &= 12.73 \times 10^{6} \text{ V} \\
         &= 12.73 \text{ MV}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compare with safe limit.**
The computed touch voltage is **12.73 million volts**, which is **astronomically higher** than the safe 50 V. The design is **unacceptable**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Suggest two remedies (2 marks):**
1. **Multiple parallel rods** in a grid configuration (e.g., 20 rods in parallel reduce $R$ to ≈ 127 Ω).
2. **Soil treatment** by adding salt and charcoal around the rod to reduce $\rho$ by 5–10×.
3. **Use a deep bore earth electrode** of length 10–20 m, which effectively increases the equivalent radius.
4. **Use an earth mat/grid** instead of a single rod to lower the surface potential gradient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) forgetting to convert diameter to radius (use $a$, not $d$), (2) using mm instead of m for unit consistency, and (3) concluding "design is acceptable" without computing $V_{touch}$ explicitly. Always show the full numerical chain.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — RCD & Equipotential Bonding

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **Residual Current Device (RCD)**. Why is it called a "**life-saving**" device? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **equipotential bonding** in a hospital ICU. Why is the leakage current limit set to **10 µA** for patient-connected equipment? List the three classes of equipment and the protection used in each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:
1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Mains → Core-balanced CT (sums $I_L - I_N$) → Trip coil → MCB contacts.
2. **Normal operation (1.5 marks):** Under normal load, $I_L = I_N$, net flux in CT core is zero, no trip.
3. **Fault operation (1.5 marks):** Under earth fault, a portion of $I_L$ returns via earth instead of $N$. The CT detects this imbalance ($\Delta I > 30 \text{ mA}$) and energizes the trip coil in **≤ 30 ms**.
4. **Why life-saving (2 marks):** The 30 ms trip time is **below the fibrillation window** (1 s) and the **30 mA threshold** is below the let-go current — preventing ventricular fibrillation in humans.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:
1. **Equipotential bonding (2 marks):** All metal parts in the patient environment (bed frame, monitors, IV stand, building steel) are connected to a common bonding bus, which is tied to the *medical IT system's reference ground*. This ensures that the patient cannot touch two surfaces at different potentials.
2. **10 µA limit (2 marks):** The patient may have internal catheters in direct contact with the heart. A current as low as 10 µA can cause **microshock-induced ventricular fibrillation**. Hence IEC 60601-1 enforces a 10 µA limit for CF-type (cardiac-floating) applied parts.
3. **Three classes (3 marks):**
   - **Class I:** Basic insulation + Protective Earth.
   - **Class II:** Double or reinforced insulation; no earth.
   - **Class III:** SELV supply (≤ 50 V AC, ≤ 120 V DC); inherently safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — "Class II has no insulation"** is a frequent wrong answer. The truth is: Class II has **double or reinforced insulation**, which is the *primary* protection — that's why it doesn't need a separate earth. Students confuse "no earth" with "no insulation" and lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision before entering the exam hall.**

- ⚡ **It is current, not voltage, that kills.** Standard body impedance ≈ **1 kΩ** (hand-to-hand, 50 Hz).
- ⚡ **Let-go threshold:** **10 mA (men)**, **6 mA (women)** at 50/60 Hz. Below this, the person can release the conductor.
- ⚡ **Fibrillation threshold:** **100 mA for > 1 s**. Above this, the heart goes into uncoordinated quivering and cannot pump blood.
- ⚡ **Safe touch voltage:** **≤ 50 V AC** or **≤ 120 V DC** per **IEC 60364**.
- ⚡ **Standard earth resistance:** **< 5 Ω** (domestic), **< 1 Ω** (substation).
- ⚡ **Single rod resistance formula:** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi a}$ where $\rho$ is soil resistivity in Ω·m and $a$ is radius in meters. Always convert mm to m.
- ⚡ **RCD sensitivity:** **30 mA** for personal protection (trip time ≤ 30 ms). **300 mA** is fire protection only.
- ⚡ **Three equipment classes:**
  - **Class I:** Earth mandatory, chassis is a live part if not bonded.
  - **Class II:** Double insulation — earth pin absent (e.g., laptop charger, hair dryer).
  - **Class III:** SELV supply — used for toys, low-voltage LED strips.
- ⚡ **Micro-shock** requires only **10 µA** to fibrillate a catheterized heart — hence medical isolation transformers and CF-type applied parts.
- ⚡ **Defence-in-depth:** Always use **two independent protection layers** (e.g., Class I + RCD).
- ⚡ **KTU Examiner Loves These Numbers:** **50 V**, **30 mA**, **1 kΩ**, **10 mA**, **100 mA·s**, **5 Ω**, **1 Ω** — memorize them in seconds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Input and Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Input and Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 3 — POWER INPUT AND CURRENT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Input** refers to the electrical energy supplied per unit time to an electronic circuit or device from a source (mains AC or regulated DC). It is the product of the **input voltage** applied across the device terminals and the **current** drawn by the device under specified operating conditions.
> **Current** is the time-rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor, measured in **Amperes (A)**, and represents the rate at which electrons traverse a cross-sectional area of the conducting medium.

Mathematically, for a DC source:

$$P_{\text{in}} = V_{\text{in}} \times I_{\text{in}}$$

For an AC sinusoidal source, the **average (real) power** is governed by the power factor:

$$P_{\text{in(AC)}} = V_{\text{rms}} \times I_{\text{rms}} \times \cos\phi$$

where **$\phi$** is the phase angle between voltage and current, and **$\cos\phi$** is the **power factor (PF)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water tank connected to your house through a pipe:

| Electrical Quantity | Water Analogy | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | Water pressure in the pipe | The "push" that drives charges |
| **Current (I)** | Rate of water flow (litres/sec) | The quantity of charge flowing per second |
| **Power (P)** | Pressure × Flow rate | The useful work delivered per second |
| **Resistance (R)** | Pipe narrowness | Opposition to charge flow |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a wider pipe allows more water at the same pressure, a **lower resistance** allows more current at the same voltage. The product of "push" and "flow" gives us the **power input** — the energy the system must receive to do its work.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold-Listed)

- **Standard Mains Voltage (India):** **230 V AC, 50 Hz** (single-phase); **415 V AC, 50 Hz** (three-phase line-to-line).
- **Standard DC Rails in Electronics:** **+5 V, +3.3 V, ±12 V, +24 V**.
- **Fundamental Charge:** **$e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**.
- **SI Unit of Current:** **1 A = 1 C/s**.
- **Power Unit Conversions:** **1 kW = 1000 W**, **1 HP ≈ 746 W**.

---

### 1.4 Types of Current in Electronic Hardware

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Current (DC):** Unidirectional flow of charge (e.g., battery-powered circuits, USB-powered devices).
> **Alternating Current (AC):** Periodically reverses direction (e.g., mains supply to adapters).
> **Inrush Current:** A momentary, high-magnitude current spike drawn at the instant a device is switched ON, due to charging of input capacitors.
> **Quiescent / Standby Current:** The minimal current drawn by a circuit when it is idle (no active load), crucial for battery-powered systems.
> **Leakage Current:** Unintended small current flowing through insulation or semiconductor junctions even when the device is OFF.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Power Relationships

The three canonical power equations arise from substituting **Ohm's Law** ($V = IR$) into the base equation $P = VI$:

1. **$P = V \cdot I$** — Universal (works for any source/load).
2. **$P = I^{2} \cdot R$** — Used when current and resistance are known.
3. **$P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$** — Used when voltage and resistance are known.

### 2.2 AC Power — Decomposition into Components

In AC circuits, the apparent power splits into **real (active)** and **reactive** components:

| Component | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Real Power | $P$ | $V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$ | **Watt (W)** | Useful work done |
| Reactive Power | $Q$ | $V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \sin\phi$ | **VAR** | Energy oscillating between source and reactive elements |
| Apparent Power | $S$ | $V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$ | **VA** | Total power supplied |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | $\dfrac{P}{S}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Efficiency of power usage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Triangle (Right-Angled):** $S^{2} = P^{2} + Q^{2}$. The hypotenuse is $S$, the horizontal leg is $P$, and the vertical leg is $Q$.

### 2.3 Why Power Factor Matters (Engineering Utility)

- A low PF means the source must supply **more current** for the same real power, causing **higher $I^{2}R$ losses** in transmission lines.
- Industrial consumers are penalized by utilities for PF $<$ **0.85** (or 0.9 in some jurisdictions).
- **Power factor correction (PFC)** is implemented using **capacitor banks** at the input of inductive loads (motors, SMPS, transformers).

### 2.4 Inrush Current — Causes and Mitigation

- **Cause:** At switch-ON, uncharged input filter capacitors appear as a **near-short circuit**, drawing currents of **5× to 20× the steady-state value** for durations of **1–10 ms**.
- **Mitigation Strategies:**
  - **NTC Thermistors (Inrush Current Limiters):** Cold resistance is high; self-heats to low resistance in steady state.
  - **Soft-Start Circuits:** Gradually ramp the gate drive of MOSFETs.
  - **Series Resistors / PTC Fuses.**
  - **Pre-charge circuits** in high-voltage DC systems.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why does an SMPS draw a large current at switch-on?"* — The answer is the **charging of the input bulk capacitor** through the bridge rectifier with minimal impedance.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{Q}{t}$ | Current = Charge / Time | A |
| 2 | $V = IR$ | Ohm's Law | V |
| 3 | $P = VI$ | Electrical Power | W |
| 4 | $P = I^{2}R$ | Power (current form) | W |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | Power (voltage form) | W |
| 6 | $P_{\text{ac}} = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$ | AC Real Power | W |
| 7 | $S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$ | Apparent Power | VA |
| 8 | $\text{PF} = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Power Factor | unitless |
| 9 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | J (or Wh) |
| 10 | $I_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$ | Peak-to-RMS conversion | A |

> [!NOTE]
> For sinusoidal AC: $V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SMPS Design:** Power input determines the size of the input fuse, bridge rectifier current rating, and bulk capacitor voltage rating.
- **Battery Sizing:** Current draw × hours of operation = **Ah capacity** required.
- **Thermal Design:** $P_{\text{dissipated}} = I^{2}R$ inside a regulator determines the **heatsink** size.
- **PCB Trace Width:** IPC-2152 standards size copper traces based on the **continuous current** they must carry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ohm's Law to the Three Power Forms

We begin with the base definition and apply algebraic substitution using $V = IR$:

### Step 1 — Base Definition

The fundamental power equation is the rate of doing work:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

This is the most general form and applies to both DC and instantaneous AC quantities.

### Step 2 — Substituting $V = IR$ into $P = V \cdot I$

Replace $V$ in the base equation with the Ohm's Law expression:

$$P = (IR) \cdot I$$

Multiply the two currents:

$$P = I^{2} R$$

This form is the **most useful when current and resistance are known** — for example, computing the heat dissipated in a current-sense resistor or a wire.

### Step 3 — Substituting $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ into $P = V \cdot I$

Replace $I$ in the base equation:

$$P = V \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{R}\right)$$

Combine:

$$P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$$

This form is the **most useful when voltage and resistance are known** — for example, computing the power rating of a resistor with a known applied voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three forms are equivalent** — they are simply rearranged expressions of the same physical relationship. Choosing the right one for a given problem **saves calculation time and reduces error** in board examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RMS Value of a Sinusoidal AC

The RMS (Root-Mean-Square) value is defined as the DC equivalent that produces the **same heating effect** in a resistor.

### Step 1 — Instantaneous Power

For $v(t) = V_{\text{peak}} \sin(\omega t)$ and $i(t) = I_{\text{peak}} \sin(\omega t)$ (resistive case, $\phi = 0$):

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}} \sin^{2}(\omega t)$$

### Step 2 — Average Power Over One Full Cycle

Using the identity $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$$

The cosine term averages to zero over a full cycle:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Equating to DC Equivalent

For a DC power to match: $P_{\text{DC}} = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$. Setting them equal:

$$V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}} I_{\text{peak}}}{2}$$

Since $V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ (and the same for current), we obtain the conversion factor:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot V_{\text{peak}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A switched-mode power supply (SMPS) is rated at **$V_{\text{in}} = 230\text{ V AC, 50 Hz}$** and draws a current of **$I_{\text{rms}} = 0.5\text{ A}$** with a power factor of **$\cos\phi = 0.7$ (lagging)**. Compute:
> (a) Real power input
> (b) Apparent power
> (c) Reactive power
> (d) Peak input current
> (e) Energy consumed in 24 hours (in kWh)

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Part (a) — Real Power Input**

Apply the AC real power formula:

$$P = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Substitute the given values:

$$P = 230 \times 0.5 \times 0.7$$

$$P = 80.5 \text{ W}$$

> **Part (a) Answer: $P = 80.5$ W**

**Part (b) — Apparent Power**

The apparent power ignores the power factor:

$$S = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$$

$$S = 230 \times 0.5 = 115 \text{ VA}$$

> **Part (b) Answer: $S = 115$ VA**

**Part (c) — Reactive Power**

Apply the Pythagorean power-triangle relationship: $S^{2} = P^{2} + Q^{2}$:

$$Q = \sqrt{S^{2} - P^{2}}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{115^{2} - 80.5^{2}}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{13225 - 6480.25}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{6744.75} \approx 82.13 \text{ VAR}$$

> **Part (c) Answer: $Q \approx 82.13$ VAR (inductive, lagging)**

**Part (d) — Peak Input Current**

The peak current relates to RMS current by:

$$I_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$$

$$I_{\text{peak}} = 1.4142 \times 0.5 = 0.7071 \text{ A}$$

> **Part (d) Answer: $I_{\text{peak}} \approx 0.707$ A**

**Part (e) — Energy in 24 Hours**

Energy is power multiplied by time, converted to kWh:

$$E = P \times t = 80.5 \text{ W} \times 24 \text{ h} = 1932 \text{ Wh}$$

$$E = 1.932 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Part (e) Answer: $E = 1.932$ kWh**

---

## 3.4 Inrush Current Numerical Example

> **Problem:** An SMPS has a **bulk input capacitor of $C = 220 \, \mu\text{F}$** charged through a bridge rectifier fed by **$V_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{230 \times \sqrt{2}}{1} \approx 325\text{ V}$** with a series resistance of $R_{\text{s}} = 2 \, \Omega$ (mains + bridge + NTC combined). Estimate the **peak inrush current**.

**Solution:**

For an RC charging circuit, the peak current occurs at $t = 0^{+}$ when the capacitor is fully discharged:

$$I_{\text{inrush(peak)}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{R_{\text{s}}}$$

$$I_{\text{inrush(peak)}} = \dfrac{325}{2} = 162.5 \text{ A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare this to the steady-state RMS current of **0.5 A**. The inrush is roughly **325× the steady-state current** — clearly destructive if not limited. This is why **NTC thermistors** and **fuses** are mandatory in SMPS input stages.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix: DC vs AC Input Behavior

| Parameter | DC Input | Single-Phase AC Input | Three-Phase AC Input |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage waveform | Constant | Sinusoidal | Three sinusoids 120° apart |
| Current waveform | Constant | Sinusoidal (with harmonic distortion) | Balanced sinusoidal |
| Power | $P = VI$ | $P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_{\text{L}} I_{\text{L}} \cos\phi$ |
| Frequency | 0 Hz | 50 Hz (India) | 50 Hz |
| Typical use | Battery, USB | Adapters, home appliances | Industrial motors, large UPS |
| Inrush risk | Capacitor charging | Both capacitor + transformer magnetizing | All three phases simultaneously |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: AC Mains Power Input Flow in an Electronic System

The diagram below maps the **functional flow of power** from the AC mains to a regulated DC load, illustrating where current is drawn, conditioned, and delivered.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Input Fuse and NTC Thermistor]
    B --> C[EMI Filter LC Network]
    C --> D[Bridge Rectifier 4 Diodes]
    D --> E[Bulk Filter Capacitor Cbulk]
    E --> F[DC Bus Approximately 325V DC]
    F --> G[High Frequency Switching MOSFET]
    G --> H[HF Transformer Isolation]
    H --> I[Secondary Rectifier and Filter]
    I --> J[Output Voltage Regulator 5V or 12V]
    J --> K[Electronic Load Microcontroller or Motor]
    K --> L[Feedback Loop Optocoupler to PFC Controller]
    L --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Node A → B:** The mains enters; the fuse protects against overcurrent, and the **NTC thermistor** limits the inrush spike.
- **Node C:** The **EMI filter** suppresses high-frequency noise from entering or leaving the device.
- **Node D → E:** The bridge converts AC to pulsating DC; the bulk capacitor smooths it to approximately the **peak value (325 V DC)**.
- **Node G → H → I:** The high-frequency switching transformer provides **galvanic isolation** and steps the voltage down.
- **Node L:** The feedback loop regulates the output by adjusting the **PWM duty cycle** of the MOSFET.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map: Relationships Between Power, Voltage, and Current

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power P] --> V[Voltage V]
    P --> I[Current I]
    V --> R[Resistance R]
    I --> R
    V --> I
    R --> I
    P --> PF[Power Factor cos phi]
    PF --> P
    VAC[RMS Voltage] --> P
    IAC[RMS Current] --> P
```

**Explanation of the concept map:**

- The central triangle is the **Ohm's Law / Power triangle**, showing that $P$, $V$, and $I$ are interlocked through $R$.
- The **RMS conversions** apply only to AC sources; for DC, $V_{\text{rms}} = V_{\text{DC}}$ and $I_{\text{rms}} = I_{\text{DC}}$.
- The **power factor** node is unique to AC and represents the phase alignment between voltage and current.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct Power Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Quantities] --> Q1{Is the source DC or AC?}
    Q1 -- DC --> Q2{What is known?}
    Q1 -- AC --> Q3{Is power factor given?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> F1[Use P equals Vrms times Irms times cos phi]
    Q3 -- No --> F2[Assume purely resistive: PF equals 1]
    Q2 -- V and I --> F3[Use P equals V times I]
    Q2 -- I and R --> F4[Use P equals I squared R]
    Q2 -- V and R --> F5[Use P equals V squared over R]
    F1 --> Result[Compute Real Power in Watts]
    F2 --> Result
    F3 --> Result
    F4 --> Result
    F5 --> Result
```

**Reading the decision tree:**

- The very **first question** to ask in any power problem is whether the source is DC or AC — this determines whether the power factor is needed.
- The **second question** identifies which two of the three quantities ($V$, $I$, $R$) are known, and selects the appropriate formula directly.

---

## 4.4 Power Triangle Visualization (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTriangle[Right Angled Power Triangle]
        direction LR
        Real[Real Power P horizontal leg] -->|adjacent| Hyp[Apparent Power S hypotenuse]
        Reac[Reactive Power Q vertical leg] -->|opposite| Hyp
        Real --- Reac
    end
    Hyp --> Formula[S equals Vrms times Irms]
    Real --> PFormula[P equals S times cos phi]
    Reac --> QFormula[Q equals S times sin phi]
```

This mirrors the textbook **power triangle** used universally in AC circuit analysis. The angle $\phi$ between $P$ (horizontal) and $S$ (hypotenuse) is the **phase angle**, and its cosine is the **power factor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "power input" for an electronic system. How is it measured in DC and AC circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Power input is the rate at which electrical energy is delivered to an electronic circuit or device, expressed in **watts (W)**.
>
> **DC Measurement (1 mark):** For DC circuits, $P_{\text{in}} = V_{\text{in}} \times I_{\text{in}}$, where $V_{\text{in}}$ is the input voltage and $I_{\text{in}}$ is the input current drawn by the device. A voltmeter and ammeter (or a wattmeter) are used.
>
> **AC Measurement (1 mark):** For AC circuits, $P_{\text{in}} = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \cdot \cos\phi$, where $\cos\phi$ is the power factor accounting for phase difference between voltage and current. A true-RMS wattmeter is used.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is inrush current? Why is it significant in SMPS design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Inrush current is the **instantaneous high-magnitude current** drawn by a power supply at the moment of switch-on, typically 5×–20× (or even higher) the steady-state value.
>
> **Cause (1 mark):** It occurs because the uncharged input bulk capacitor and the transformer's magnetizing inductance behave like a near-short circuit during the first few milliseconds.
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** It can blow the input fuse, damage the bridge rectifier, cause contact welding in relays, and trip upstream circuit breakers. Mitigation uses **NTC thermistors**, **soft-start circuits**, and **PTC fuses**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Module 3]`

**A desktop computer SMPS is rated at 230 V AC, 50 Hz, with a steady-state RMS input current of 2.5 A and a power factor of 0.85 (lagging). The bulk input capacitor is 470 μF.**

**(a)** Compute the **real power**, **apparent power**, and **reactive power** drawn from the mains. Also calculate the **energy consumed in 8 hours of operation** in kWh. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **peak inrush current** assuming the series path resistance is 1.5 Ω and the peak mains voltage is 325 V. Explain **two practical methods** to limit this inrush current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Power Analysis and Energy Consumption (7 marks)

**Step 1: Real Power** (2 marks)

$$P = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 2.5 \times 0.85$$

$$\boxed{P = 488.75 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 2: Apparent Power** (1 mark)

$$S = V_{\text{rms}} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} = 230 \times 2.5$$

$$\boxed{S = 575 \text{ VA}}$$

**Step 3: Reactive Power** (2 marks)

$$Q = \sqrt{S^{2} - P^{2}} = \sqrt{575^{2} - 488.75^{2}}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{330625 - 238876.5625} = \sqrt{91748.44}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 302.9 \text{ VAR (lagging)}}$$

**Step 4: Energy in 8 hours** (2 marks)

$$E = P \times t = 488.75 \text{ W} \times 8 \text{ h} = 3910 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3.91 \text{ kWh}}$$

##### Part (b) — Inrush Current and Mitigation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Peak Inrush Current Calculation** (3 marks)

At $t = 0^{+}$, the uncharged capacitor appears as a short, so the full peak mains voltage drops across the series resistance:

$$I_{\text{inrush(peak)}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{R_{\text{s}}} = \dfrac{325}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{inrush(peak)}} \approx 216.67 \text{ A}}$$

This is **$\dfrac{216.67}{2.5} \approx 86.7\times$** the steady-state RMS current — clearly catastrophic.

**Step 2: Two Mitigation Methods** (4 marks — 2 marks each)

1. **NTC Thermistor (Inrush Current Limiter):** Placed in series with the mains line. Its **cold resistance** (e.g., 5–10 Ω) absorbs the initial surge and limits the current. As steady-state current flows, the thermistor self-heats, and its resistance drops to a few hundred milliohms, minimizing steady-state loss. *(2 marks)*

2. **Active Soft-Start Circuit Using a Relay or MOSFET Bypass:** A series element (resistor or NTC) carries the inrush current initially. After a delay (set by an RC timer or sensed by a comparator), a relay or bypass MOSFET shorts out the limiting element, connecting the mains directly. This provides high inrush protection with negligible steady-state loss. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** A resistive heater draws **5 A** from a **230 V AC, 50 Hz** supply. Calculate:
   (i) Real power, (ii) Apparent power, (iii) Power factor, (iv) Peak current, and (v) Energy consumed in 12 hours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship $P = I^{2}R$ starting from the base equation $P = V \cdot I$. Explain why this form is preferred when calculating **heat dissipation** in current-carrying conductors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Resistive Heater Numerical (7 marks)

Given: $V_{\text{rms}} = 230$ V, $I_{\text{rms}} = 5$ A, purely resistive load $\Rightarrow \cos\phi = 1$.

**(i) Real Power** (1.5 marks)

$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 5 \times 1$$

$$\boxed{P = 1150 \text{ W} = 1.15 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Apparent Power** (1 mark)

$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{S = 1150 \text{ VA}}$$

**(iii) Power Factor** (1 mark)

Since the load is purely resistive, voltage and current are in phase:

$$\boxed{\cos\phi = 1 \text{ (unity)}}$$

**(iv) Peak Current** (1.5 marks)

$$I_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{\text{rms}} = 1.4142 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{peak}} \approx 7.07 \text{ A}}$$

**(v) Energy in 12 hours** (2 marks)

$$E = P \times t = 1150 \times 12 = 13800 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E = 13.8 \text{ kWh}}$$

##### Part (b) — Derivation of $P = I^{2}R$ (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Start with the base power equation (1 mark):

$$P = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Ohm's Law**, which states that the voltage across a resistor is proportional to the current through it: $V = I \cdot R$. (2 marks)

**Step 3:** Substitute $V = IR$ into the base equation: (2 marks)

$$P = (I \cdot R) \cdot I = I^{2} R$$

**Step 4: Why preferred for heat dissipation?** (2 marks)

> In current-carrying conductors (wires, PCB traces, resistor bodies, transformer windings), the **resistance $R$** is a fixed, known physical parameter, and the **current $I$** is what flows. Since Joule's Law of heating states that the heat generated per second is $H = I^{2}R$, this form **directly gives the heat dissipation** without needing to know the voltage drop across the conductor — which is often very small and hard to measure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using peak voltage instead of RMS voltage** in AC power calculations. Always remember: **mains 230 V is RMS**, not peak. Convert to peak only when explicitly asked.
> 2. **Forgetting the power factor.** For a purely resistive load, $\cos\phi = 1$. For motors, transformers, or SMPS without PFC, the PF is **typically 0.6 – 0.85** and **must be included** in the $P = VI \cos\phi$ formula.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Apparent power is in **VA (volt-amperes)**, real power in **W (watts)**, reactive power in **VAR (volt-amperes reactive)**. Mixing them up is an instant 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert W to kWh** in energy problems by dividing by 1000.
> 5. **Treating inrush as steady-state:** Inrush lasts only milliseconds; do not use it to size continuous-duty components.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Power Input = Voltage × Current** (DC); **Power Input = $V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$** (AC).
- ⚡ **Three power forms:** $P = VI = I^{2}R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ — all equivalent.
- ⚡ **RMS Conversion (AC):** $V_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, V_{\text{peak}}$.
- ⚡ **Power Triangle:** $S^{2} = P^{2} + Q^{2}$; PF $= \cos\phi = P/S$.
- ⚡ **Inrush Current** is caused by uncharged bulk capacitors and transformer magnetizing inrush; limit with **NTC thermistors** or **soft-start circuits**.
- ⚡ **Mains Standards (India):** 230 V AC, 50 Hz single-phase; 415 V AC three-phase.
- ⚡ **Energy:** $E = P \times t$ in Joules (or Wh / kWh).
- ⚡ **For resistive loads:** $\cos\phi = 1$, so $P = S = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$.
- ⚡ **For inductive/electronic loads (SMPS, motors):** $\cos\phi < 1$, so $P < S$.
- ⚡ **Peak Current:** $I_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{\text{rms}}$ — useful for fuse and diode surge ratings.
- ⚡ **Practical Applications:** SMPS input design, battery sizing (Ah = $I \times t$), PCB trace current capacity, heatsink sizing, electricity bill calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leakage Current and Electric Strength at Operating Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Leakage Current and Electric Strength at Operating Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leakage Current** is the small, unwanted current that flows through the **insulation** of an electrical/electronic equipment from any live part (phase or neutral) to the **earthed/accessible conductive part** when the equipment is energized at its **rated operating voltage** and **operating temperature**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Strength (Dielectric Strength) at Operating Temperature** is defined as the ability of the insulation to withstand a **specified high test voltage** applied between the live parts and the earthed metal body, for a **specified duration (typically 60 seconds)**, **without any dielectric breakdown**, **arcing**, or **flashover**, while the equipment is at its **normal operating temperature** (i.e., under thermal equilibrium).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 – The Leaky Water Pipe:**
Imagine a water pipe (the conductor) carrying water (current). The pipe wall is your **insulation**. Even a perfectly good pipe wall is not 100% impermeable — a few drops (leakage current) seep through. If the pipe wall is hot (operating temperature), the material expands and becomes more porous, allowing **more drops to leak through**. Leakage current measurement is simply counting these drops under a fixed water pressure (test voltage).

**Analogy 2 – The Dam Under Pressure Test:**
A hydroelectric dam is built to hold back a lake. To certify its safety, engineers do not just check the water level during normal days — they deliberately raise the water (high test voltage) to a level **higher than the maximum possible flood level**, and check if the dam (insulation) cracks. They also do this when the dam material is hot from the sun (operating temperature) because hot concrete is weaker than cold concrete. The test voltage applied is the *electric strength* requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per **IS 325, IS 4029, IS 8789, IEC 60335-1, and IEC 60950-1 / IEC 62368-1**, two mandatory safety tests are performed at *thermal equilibrium*:
> 1. **Leakage Current Test** (at rated supply voltage).
> 2. **High Voltage (Electric Strength) Test** (at 1.5× to 2× the working voltage + a margin).
>
> Both tests are conducted *after* the equipment has run on **rated load** until its windings/insulation reach **steady-state operating temperature**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Limits

The standard permissible limits for leakage current (as per **IEC 60335-1**) are:
- **Class I portable appliances:** ≤ **0.75 mA**
- **Class I stationary appliances:** ≤ **3.5 mA**
- **Class II (double insulated) appliances:** ≤ **0.25 mA**

> [!NOTE]
> The human body perception threshold for AC current is **~1 mA**, the let-go threshold is **~10 mA**, and ventricular fibrillation begins at **~100 mA (for > 2 s)**. Hence, the standards are conservative.

**Key physical constants/metrics used:**
- **Insulation Resistance (R):** Typically **≥ 1 MΩ** to **≥ 100 MΩ** depending on equipment class.
- **Dielectric Withstand Voltage:** Usually **1500 V AC RMS** for 1 minute for Class I, **3750 V AC RMS** for Class II.
- **Test frequency:** **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (USA).
- **Temperature coefficient of insulation:** Resistance roughly **halves for every 10 °C rise** for Class A insulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "At Operating Temperature"?

Insulation is **not** an ideal dielectric. Its resistance is highly **temperature-dependent**. Conductors (copper/aluminium) follow the rule "R doubles for every 10 °C rise". For **insulators**, the behaviour is **opposite** — insulation resistance **decreases exponentially** with temperature rise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating temperature is the WORST case.** Cold insulation may mask defects. The KTU / IS / IEC standards therefore mandate that the test be performed *after* thermal equilibrium is reached, typically by running the equipment on rated load until the temperature stabilises (Δθ ≤ 1 °C / hour).

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Establish the conductive path:**
When voltage V is applied across an insulator of resistance R_ins, an Ohmic current flows:
$$I_{leak} = \frac{V_{applied}}{R_{insulation}}$$

**Step 2 — Account for temperature:**
As temperature rises, free ions and polar molecules in the insulator gain mobility, reducing R:
$$R_T = R_{T_0} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_0 - T}{k}}$$
where **k** is the insulation-class halving constant (≈ **10 °C for Class A**, **12 °C for Class B**, **14 °C for Class F**, **18 °C for Class H**).

**Step 3 — Convert to actual leakage current at operating temperature:**
$$I_{leak,hot} = I_{leak,cold} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}{k}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Electric Strength Test (Hi-Pot Test):**
A higher-than-rated voltage V_test is applied. If the insulation breaks down, the current shoots up abruptly. The test is *passed* if **no breakdown, no flashover, no trip of the indicator circuit** occurs within the test duration (typically **60 s**).

**Step 5 — Standard test voltage calculation (IS 325 for 3-phase induction motors):**
$$V_{test} = 1000 + 2 \cdot V_{rated} \quad \text{(RMS, 50 Hz, for } V_{rated} \leq 1000\text{ V)}$$

For higher voltage machines:
$$V_{test} = 2.4 \cdot V_{rated} + 1200$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Boundary Conditions |
|:------:|:---------|:---------------------|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| $I_{leak}$ | Leakage current | $I_{leak} = V_{applied} / R_{ins}$ | mA, μA | At operating temperature |
| $R_{ins}$ | Insulation resistance | $R_{ins} = V_{applied} / I_{leak}$ | MΩ, GΩ | Measured with Megger (500 V DC) |
| $R_T$ | Insulation R at temp T | $R_T = R_{T_0} \cdot 2^{(T_0 - T)/k}$ | MΩ | k depends on insulation class |
| $k$ | Halving constant | Class A: 10, B: 12, F: 14, H: 18 | °C | Empirical, per IEEE 43 |
| $V_{test}$ | Hi-Pot test voltage | $V_{test} = 1000 + 2 V_{rated}$ | V (RMS) | For motors ≤ 1000 V, IS 325 |
| $V_{test}$ | Hi-Pot test voltage | $V_{test} = 2.4 V_{rated} + 1200$ | V (RMS) | For motors > 1000 V |
| $V_{test}$ | Class I appliance | $V_{test} = 2 V_{rated} + 1000$ | V (RMS) | Min **1250 V** (IEC 60335) |
| $V_{test}$ | Class II appliance | $V_{test} = 2 V_{rated} + 1750$ | V (RMS) | Min **3750 V** (IEC 60335) |
| $t_{test}$ | Test duration | $t_{test} = 60$ s | seconds | Production: 1 s, type test: 60 s |
| $I_{lim}$ | Permissible limit | Class I: 0.75–3.5, Class II: 0.25 | mA | Per IEC 60335-1 |
| $P_{dielectric}$ | Dielectric loss | $P = V^2 \omega C \tan\delta$ | W | Indicative of insulation quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The "Electric Strength" is **not a single number** — it is a function of temperature, humidity, mechanical stress, voltage waveform, and the duration of application. The KTU board exam often tests the *standardised value* of V_test for a given rated voltage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motor & Transformer Manufacturing:** Every unit leaving the factory passes through a *Hi-Pot tester* (often fully automated).
- **Medical Equipment:** Patient-connected devices must have leakage current < **10 μA** (Type CF, per IEC 60601).
- **EV Battery Packs:** Leakage current between HV bus and chassis is monitored continuously; threshold typically **< 1 mA**.
- **Aerospace and Defence:** MIL-STD-202 and MIL-STD-810 specify the temperature + humidity + vibration + Hi-Pot qualification sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Test Voltage for a 3-Phase Induction Motor (IS 325)

For a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor with rated line voltage $V_r$ (RMS, line-to-line, 50 Hz), the standard specifies a sinusoidal AC test voltage applied between each phase winding and the frame (earth) for **1 minute**, with the motor at **operating temperature**.

**Starting rule (Clause 9.1 of IS 325):**
$$V_{test} = 1000 + 2 V_r \quad \text{V (RMS)}$$

**Derivation logic:**
- **Step 1:** Establish a base safety margin of **1000 V** to account for transient over-voltages (switching surges, lightning, etc.).
- **Step 2:** Add **2 × V_r** so that the test voltage is **3 × V_r** in total (i.e., peak ≈ 4.24 × V_r above neutral).
- **Step 3:** This ensures that the insulation can withstand **at least twice** the normal working stress continuously.

**Sample result:** For a motor with $V_r = 415$ V (standard 3-phase LT motor in India):
$$V_{test} = 1000 + 2(415) = 1000 + 830 = 1830 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Leakage Current at Operating Temperature

**Given:** A motor's insulation resistance measured at **cold** condition $T_{cold} = 30\,°C$ is $R_{cold} = 50 \text{ M}\Omega$. The motor is then run to its **operating temperature** $T_{hot} = 75\,°C$. Insulation is **Class B** (so $k = 12\,°C$).

**Step 1 — Apply the temperature correction formula:**
$$R_{hot} = R_{cold} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_{cold} - T_{hot}}{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**
$$R_{hot} = 50 \cdot 2^{\frac{30 - 75}{12}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**
$$\frac{30 - 75}{12} = \frac{-45}{12} = -3.75$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 2:**
$$2^{-3.75} = \frac{1}{2^{3.75}} = \frac{1}{13.454} \approx 0.0743$$

**Step 5 — Compute R_hot:**
$$R_{hot} = 50 \times 0.0743 = 3.716 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute leakage current at rated voltage (415 V, 50 Hz):**
$$I_{leak,rated} = \frac{V_r}{R_{hot}} = \frac{415}{3.716 \times 10^6}$$
$$I_{leak,rated} = 1.117 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.1117 \text{ mA} = 111.7 \text{ μA}$$

**Step 7 — Compute leakage current during the Hi-Pot test (V_test = 1830 V):**
$$I_{leak,test} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_{hot}} = \frac{1830}{3.716 \times 10^6}$$
$$I_{leak,test} = 4.925 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.4925 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 8 — Verdict:**
$$I_{leak,test} = 0.4925 \text{ mA} \quad < \quad I_{lim,ClassI} = 0.75 \text{ mA} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{PASS}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation Between Test Voltage and Rated Voltage for IEC 60335-1 Appliances

For **Class I** household appliances (earthed metal body), the high voltage test between live parts and accessible metal parts is:
$$V_{test,CI} = 2 V_r + 1000 \quad \text{V (RMS)}$$
with a **minimum** of **1250 V RMS**.

For **Class II** (double-insulated) appliances:
$$V_{test,CII} = 2 V_r + 1750 \quad \text{V (RMS)}$$
with a **minimum** of **3750 V RMS**.

**Example:** A Class II appliance rated at 230 V, 50 Hz:
$$V_{test,CII} = 2(230) + 1750 = 460 + 1750 = 2210 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

Since 2210 V < 3750 V (the minimum), the test voltage is **3750 V RMS** for 60 seconds.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature on Insulation

| Temperature (°C) | Relative R_ins (×R₀) | I_leak (μA) at 415 V | Standard Status |
|:----------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------------:|:---------------:|
| 30 (cold) | 1.000 R₀ | 8.3 | — (pre-test) |
| 45 | 0.500 R₀ | 16.6 | Approaching limit |
| 60 | 0.250 R₀ | 33.2 | Within limit |
| 75 | 0.0743 R₀ | 111.7 | Within limit (Class I) |
| 90 | 0.0221 R₀ | 375.7 | Approaching Class II limit |
| 105 | 0.0066 R₀ | 1260.0 | **Above** Class I limit (alarm) |

> [!NOTE]
> This table clearly shows why *testing at operating temperature* is **non-negotiable**: a motor that passes at 30 °C may fail at 75 °C.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Test Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([START]) --> node2[Inspect Equipment Visually]
    node2 --> node3[Apply Rated Voltage and Load]
    node3 --> node4[Run Until Thermal Equilibrium Reached]
    node4 --> node5{Delta Temperature less than 1 deg C per hour}
    node5 -->|No| node3
    node5 -->|Yes| node6[Step 1 - Measure Insulation Resistance with Megger at 500 V DC]
    node6 --> node7{Rins greater than 1 M Ohm}
    node7 -->|No| node8[REJECT - Insulation Failure]
    node7 -->|Yes| node9[Step 2 - Measure Leakage Current at Rated Voltage]
    node9 --> node10{Ileak less than Standard Limit}
    node10 -->|No| node11[REJECT - Excessive Leakage]
    node10 -->|Yes| node12[Step 3 - Apply Hi-Pot Test Voltage for 60 seconds]
    node12 --> node13{Breakdown or Flashover occurred}
    node13 -->|Yes| node14[REJECT - Dielectric Failure]
    node13 -->|No| node15[ACCEPT - Record Data and Label]
    node15 --> node16([END])
    node8 --> node16
    node11 --> node16
    node14 --> node16
```

### 4.2 Test Setup Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[AC Mains Supply 230 V 415 V 50 Hz] --> nodeB[Variable Autotransformer VARIAC]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step Up Transformer Hi Pot Type]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Equipment Under Test EUT on Rated Load]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Earth Connection via Ammeter]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Earth Bus]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Voltmeter across EUT Terminals]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Oscilloscope for Waveform Monitoring]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Overcurrent Protection Trip Circuit]
    subgraph INSTRUMENTATION
        nodeG
        nodeH
        nodeI
    end
```

### 4.3 Insulation Resistance vs Temperature (Mermaid xychart-beta)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Insulation Resistance vs Winding Temperature (Class B)"
    x-axis "Temperature in deg C" [30, 45, 60, 75, 90, 105, 120]
    y-axis "Insulation Resistance in MOhm" 0 --> 100
    line [100, 50, 25, 12.5, 6.25, 3.13, 1.56]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The curve is a clean **exponential decay**, halving approximately every 12 °C for Class B insulation. This is the *graphical fingerprint* expected in KTU 2-mark/3-mark questions.

### 4.4 Leakage Current Paths in a Single-Phase Appliance (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL[LINE L] --> nodeP[Phase Conductor]
    nodeN[NEUTRAL N] --> nodeQ[Neutral Conductor]
    nodeP --> nodeM[Motor Winding Live Part]
    nodeM --> nodeINS[Insulation Layer]
    nodeINS --> nodeMETAL[Accessible Metal Body]
    nodeMETAL --> nodeE[EARTH E]
    nodeINS --> nodeCAP[Capacitive Coupling Path]
    nodeCAP --> nodeMETAL
    nodeINS -.-> nodeY[Y Capacitor Internal Filter]
    nodeY --> nodeE
```

**Path 1 (Resistive Leakage):** Through the bulk resistance of the insulation.
**Path 2 (Capacitive Leakage):** Through the inherent distributed capacitance between windings and frame.
**Path 3 (Filter Leakage):** Through the line-to-earth Y-capacitors of the EMI filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **50 Hz**, the capacitive path is small (because $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ is large at 50 Hz for typical winding capacitance of ~100 pF). However, at higher frequencies (or with switching power supplies), capacitive leakage can dominate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]  
Define **leakage current** and **electric strength**. Why are these tests specifically mandated to be performed at **operating temperature** rather than at room temperature?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Leakage current (1 mark):** Leakage current is the small current that flows through the insulation of an energized equipment from the live parts to the earthed/accessible conductive parts when the equipment is supplied at its rated voltage.
- **Electric strength (1 mark):** Electric strength is the ability of the insulation to withstand a specified high test voltage (typically 1.5× to 3× the rated voltage) for a specified duration (typically 60 s) without dielectric breakdown.
- **Why at operating temperature (1 mark):** Insulation resistance decreases exponentially with temperature (typically halves every 10–18 °C depending on insulation class). Testing at room temperature would give a falsely optimistic (high) resistance and (low) leakage current, masking defects. Operating temperature represents the worst-case real-world condition and ensures the equipment is safe under actual service conditions.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]  
A 3-phase induction motor is rated at **415 V, 50 Hz**. Calculate the standard **AC high-voltage (Hi-Pot) test voltage** as per **IS 325** and state the **test duration**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Applying the IS 325 formula for motors with rated voltage ≤ 1000 V:
$$V_{test} = 1000 + 2 V_{rated} = 1000 + 2(415) = 1830 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

**Test duration (per IS 325, Clause 9.1):** **60 seconds (1 minute).** *(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer + duration)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 contains *either* Question A *or* Question B. The student must answer the one that appears in their question paper. Both alternatives are provided below for full preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **concept of leakage current** in electrical/electronic equipment. Discuss the **three main leakage paths** (resistive, capacitive, and filter) with neat diagrams. State the **IS/IEC permissible limits** for Class I and Class II appliances. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-phase **Class I** domestic appliance is rated at **230 V, 50 Hz, 2 kW**. Its insulation resistance, measured at room temperature (30 °C) with a 500 V DC Megger, is **200 MΩ**. The appliance is then run for 3 hours until its internal winding temperature stabilises at **85 °C** (assume **Class B** insulation, halving constant **k = 12 °C**).  
    (i) Calculate the **insulation resistance at the operating temperature**.  
    (ii) Calculate the **leakage current at the operating temperature when the rated voltage is applied**.  
    (iii) Verify whether the appliance **passes the leakage current test** as per IEC 60335-1. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Leakage current is the unwanted current that flows from the live conductors (phase and neutral) to the earthed metal body (or accessible conductive parts) of an electrical appliance through the insulation barrier. It exists even in a perfectly functioning appliance because *no real insulator is perfect*.

**Three main paths (4 marks):**

- **Path 1 — Resistive (Ohmic) leakage:** Flows through the bulk volume resistance of the insulation material. Dominated by the insulating material's resistivity $\rho$, thickness $d$, and area $A$: $R = \rho d / A$. Affected by **temperature, humidity, ageing, contamination**.

- **Path 2 — Capacitive leakage:** Flows through the inherent distributed capacitance between the live conductors and the metal chassis. The magnitude is $I_C = V \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C_{stray}$. Typically $C_{stray} \approx 50$–$500$ pF in a motor.

- **Path 3 — Filter (Y-capacitor) leakage:** Modern electronic equipment contains **Y-class safety capacitors** from line-to-earth and neutral-to-earth for EMI suppression. These provide a deliberate, low-impedance path for high-frequency noise — but they also conduct a small 50 Hz leakage current to earth.

**Permissible limits (1.5 marks, as per IEC 60335-1):**

| Class | Type | Limit |
|:-----:|:----:|:-----:|
| Class I | Portable | 0.75 mA |
| Class I | Stationary | 3.5 mA |
| Class II | All | 0.25 mA |

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Insulation resistance at 85 °C (2.5 marks):**

$$R_{hot} = R_{cold} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_{cold} - T_{hot}}{k}}$$

$$R_{hot} = 200 \cdot 2^{\frac{30 - 85}{12}} = 200 \cdot 2^{-4.5833}$$

$$2^{-4.5833} = \frac{1}{2^{4.5833}} = \frac{1}{24.034} \approx 0.0416$$

$$R_{hot} = 200 \times 0.0416 = 8.32 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**(ii) Leakage current at 230 V (2 marks):**

$$I_{leak} = \frac{V_{rated}}{R_{hot}} = \frac{230}{8.32 \times 10^6}$$

$$I_{leak} = 2.764 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 0.02764 \text{ mA} = 27.64 \text{ μA}$$

**(iii) Verification (2.5 marks):**

For a Class I portable appliance, the permissible limit is **0.75 mA = 750 μA**.

Measured: **27.64 μA** ≪ **750 μA**

**Result: PASSES** the leakage current test with a large safety margin (factor ≈ 27).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Define **electric strength** (dielectric strength) of an insulating material. With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete test setup** for conducting a **high-voltage (Hi-Pot) test at operating temperature** on a single-phase induction motor. List the **safety precautions** to be observed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to relevant **IS and IEC standards**, derive the **test voltage formula** for the following cases:  
    (i) A 3-phase induction motor with $V_{rated} = 6.6 \text{ kV}$.  
    (ii) A Class II household appliance with $V_{rated} = 230 \text{ V}$.  
    (iii) State the test duration and the **pass/fail criterion** for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electric Strength & Test Setup (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Electric strength (also called *dielectric strength* or *withstand voltage*) of an insulator is the maximum electric field (or equivalently, the maximum applied voltage across a given thickness) that the material can sustain **without** losing its insulating property, i.e., without electrical breakdown. It is expressed in **kV/mm** or as a **test voltage (V RMS) for a standard test object**.

**Test setup block diagram (3.5 marks):**

The setup consists of:
1. **AC Mains Supply** (single-phase, 230 V, 50 Hz).
2. **Variable Autotransformer (VARIAC)** — to smoothly raise the voltage.
3. **Step-Up Transformer (Hi-Pot Type)** — to generate the test voltage (e.g., 0–5 kV or 0–10 kV).
4. **Equipment Under Test (EUT)** — the induction motor, connected between the HV output and earth.
5. **Voltmeter** — measures the test voltage applied.
6. **Ammeter / Microammeter** — in the earth return path, measures the leakage / breakdown current.
7. **Oscilloscope** — monitors the waveform for distortion.
8. **Overcurrent Trip Circuit** — disconnects supply within 1 cycle if breakdown current exceeds threshold (typically 100 mA).
9. **Earthing Bus** — solid, low-impedance earth.

**Test sequence:**
- Run motor on rated load for thermal equilibrium.
- Apply test voltage gradually (rise time ≈ 10 s) to the specified V_test.
- Hold for **60 s**.
- Reduce to zero gradually.
- **Pass criterion:** No flashover, no trip, no current surge.

**Safety precautions (2 marks):**
- Use **interlocked test cage** with warning lamps.
- **Two-person rule** — one to operate, one to observe.
- **Insulating gloves, boots, and rod** for the operator.
- **Emergency power cutoff** within reach.
- Ensure **proper earthing** of the test cage.
- **Discharge the EUT** through a discharge rod before touching.

---

**(b) Test Voltage Derivations (7 marks):**

**(i) 3-phase induction motor, $V_{rated} = 6.6$ kV (3 marks):**

Since $V_{rated} > 1000$ V, the relevant IS 325 formula is:
$$V_{test} = 2.4 V_{rated} + 1200$$

$$V_{test} = 2.4(6600) + 1200 = 15840 + 1200 = 17040 \text{ V (RMS)} \approx 17.04 \text{ kV (RMS)}$$

**Test duration:** 60 seconds.  
**Pass criterion:** No breakdown, no flashover, no protective device trip.

**(ii) Class II appliance, $V_{rated} = 230$ V (3 marks):**

Per IEC 60335-1:
$$V_{test} = 2 V_{rated} + 1750 = 2(230) + 1750 = 460 + 1750 = 2210 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

Since 2210 V < minimum **3750 V RMS** for Class II, the applicable test voltage is:
$$V_{test} = 3750 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

**Test duration:** 60 seconds.  
**Pass criterion:** No breakdown or flashover; insulation resistance remains > 2 MΩ during the test.

**(iii) Common pass/fail criterion (1 mark):**
- The test is considered **passed** if no insulation breakdown occurs and the leakage current does not exceed the trip threshold of the Hi-Pot tester (typically 100 mA for type test).
- Any visible flashover, spark, smoke, sudden current surge, or trip = **FAILED**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "minimum test voltage" rule:** Many students compute the formula value but forget to check against the *minimum* (e.g., 1250 V for Class I, 3750 V for Class II). If the formula gives 900 V, you must still apply **1250 V**. (Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Using DC instead of AC Megger:** Insulation *resistance* is measured with a DC Megger (500 V DC), but the **electric strength test uses 50 Hz AC**. Mixing them is a common mistake. (Loss: 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Sign error in temperature formula:** The formula is $R_{hot} = R_{cold} \cdot 2^{(T_{cold} - T_{hot})/k}$, **not** the reverse. Forgetting the sign of the exponent leads to an answer 1000× too large. (Loss: 2–3 marks.)
> 4. **Skipping the operating temperature justification:** KTU specifically asks *why* the test is at operating temperature. A 1-line answer that says "insulation resistance decreases with temperature" is worth 0.5 marks; mentioning **exponential dependence**, **insulation class**, and **worst-case real condition** earns the full 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the test duration:** A common omission. Always state "60 s" or "1 minute" with the test voltage. (Loss: 0.5–1 mark.)
> 6. **Confusing "leakage current test" with "insulation resistance test":** The Megger test (DC, gives R in MΩ) is *different* from the leakage current test (AC, gives I in mA). Both are performed, but they measure different quantities. (Loss: 1–2 marks.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Leakage Current & Electric Strength at Operating Temperature**

- [x] **Leakage current** = small current through insulation, from live parts to earthed body, measured at **rated voltage**.
- [x] **Electric strength** = ability to withstand a **higher test voltage** for a **specified duration** without breakdown.
- [x] **Operating temperature** is mandatory because insulation resistance **decreases exponentially** with temperature (worst case).
- [x] **Temperature correction formula:** $R_T = R_{T_0} \cdot 2^{(T_0 - T)/k}$
- [x] **Halving constants (k):** Class A = 10, B = 12, F = 14, H = 18 °C.
- [x] **Test voltage — Motor (IS 325):** $1000 + 2 V_r$ for $V_r \le 1$ kV; $2.4 V_r + 1200$ for $V_r > 1$ kV.
- [x] **Test voltage — Class I (IEC 60335-1):** $2 V_r + 1000$ V RMS (min **1250 V**).
- [x] **Test voltage — Class II (IEC 60335-1):** $2 V_r + 1750$ V RMS (min **3750 V**).
- [x] **Test duration:** 60 seconds for type test; 1 second acceptable in production with **1.2× V_test**.
- [x] **Leakage current limits:** Class I portable 0.75 mA, Class I stationary 3.5 mA, Class II 0.25 mA.
- [x] **Insulation resistance limit:** typically **≥ 1 MΩ** (general), **≥ 2 MΩ** during Hi-Pot, **≥ 50 MΩ** for medical / military.
- [x] **Test frequency:** **50 Hz** AC sinusoidal, applied between winding and earthed frame.
- [x] **Three leakage paths:** Resistive (bulk), Capacitive (distributed C), Filter (Y-capacitor).
- [x] **Test sequence:** Visual inspection → Rated load run → Thermal equilibrium → Megger test → Leakage current test → Hi-Pot test → Pass/Fail decision.
- [x] **Human safety thresholds:** 1 mA perception, 10 mA let-go, 30 mA respiratory, 100 mA fibrillation.
- [x] **Hi-Pot test = dielectric withstand test = flash test = voltage withstand test** (all synonyms).
- [x] **Megger test = insulation resistance test** (uses DC 500 V or 1000 V).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Computer: Difference between Hardware and Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computer: Difference between Hardware and Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Computer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **COMPUTER (Formal Definition):**
> An electronic device, operating under the control of instructions stored in its own memory, that can accept data (input), process it arithmetically and logically, produce output (information), and **store** the results for future use — all with **minimal human intervention**.

A computer system is fundamentally a **two-layered entity**:
1. The **physical/tangible layer** — the parts you can touch, see, and physically break.
2. The **logical/intangible layer** — the set of instructions and data that tell the physical layer *what to do* and *how to do it*.

These two layers are termed **Hardware** and **Software** respectively.

### 1.2 Hardware — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HARDWARE (KTU Definition):**
> The physical, tangible, and electronic/electromechanical components of a computer system that can be seen, touched, and which physically perform the operations dictated by the software.

Examples include the Central Processing Unit (**CPU**), motherboard, Random Access Memory (**RAM**) modules, hard disk drive, monitor, keyboard, mouse, printer, and all associated circuitry and connectors.

### 1.3 Software — The Rigorous Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOFTWARE (KTU Definition):**
> A collection of programmed instructions, data, and documentation that direct the hardware to perform specific tasks. Software is **non-tangible** (logical), exists as binary **0s and 1s** stored in memory, and cannot be physically touched.

Examples include Microsoft Windows, Linux, MS Office, Google Chrome, compilers, device drivers, and the firmware embedded inside microcontrollers.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Model

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Human Body" Analogy** — the most intuitive way to remember this concept for life.

Think of a computer as a **human being**:

| Human Component | Computer Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Bones, muscles, organs (body) | Hardware (CPU, RAM, Monitor) | Provides the **physical capability** to act. |
| Thoughts, knowledge, skills (mind) | Software (OS, Applications) | **Directs** the body on *what* to do. |
| Nervous system (brain signals) | Bus, Control Unit, Firmware | **Bridges** the two layers. |
| Memory of past events | Storage devices (HDD/SSD) | **Retains** information for the future. |

> *A body without a mind is lifeless hardware. A mind without a body has no way to act — pure software cannot run on nothing. Only when both work together does the system become **useful**.*

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The fundamental **unit of digital information** is the **bit (Binary Digit)**, which can be either **0** or **1**.
- **8 bits = 1 byte**, **1024 bytes = 1 Kilobyte (KB)**, **1024 KB = 1 Megabyte (MB)**, **1024 MB = 1 Gigabyte (GB)**, **1024 GB = 1 Terabyte (TB)**.
- Hardware operates on these bits using **Boolean logic gates** (AND, OR, NOT) implemented via transistors, typically switching at clock speeds measured in **GHz (Gigahertz, i.e., 10⁹ cycles per second)**.
- A modern CPU executes on the order of **billions of instructions per second**, where each instruction is ultimately a pattern of bits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Hardware

Hardware is generally classified into **five functional categories** under the KTU 2024 Scheme:

- **Input Devices** — Components that accept raw data and instructions from the user/external environment and convert them into a binary form the computer can understand.
  - *Examples:* Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, Microphone, Webcam, Touchscreen, Barcode Reader.
- **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — The "brain" of the computer. It contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** for computation, the **Control Unit (CU)** for instruction decoding, and **Registers** for ultra-fast temporary storage. Often referred to as the **Microprocessor** in modern personal computers.
- **Memory (Primary Storage)** — **Volatile** memory directly accessible by the CPU. The most common type is **RAM (Random Access Memory)** with access times typically in the **nanosecond (10⁻⁹ s)** range.
- **Output Devices** — Components that present the processed results back to the user in a human-perceptible form.
  - *Examples:* Monitor, Printer, Speakers, LEDs, Plotter, Projector.
- **Secondary Storage (Auxiliary Storage)** — **Non-volatile** storage for permanent data retention.
  - *Examples:* **HDD (Hard Disk Drive)** using spinning magnetic platters, **SSD (Solid State Drive)** using NAND flash memory, Optical Discs (CD/DVD/Blu-ray), USB flash drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Classification:**
> This classification mirrors the **IPO (Input → Process → Output)** model of computing, with **Storage** added as a fourth pillar because practical computing requires both immediate working memory and long-term archival. Every hardware category directly serves one of these four logical functions.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Software

Software is classified into **three primary tiers**:

- **System Software** — Programs that manage and control the hardware directly, providing a platform for application software to run.
  - **Operating System (OS):** Windows, Linux, macOS, Android, iOS. The **kernel** schedules CPU time, manages memory, and handles I/O.
  - **Device Drivers:** Specialized programs that translate OS commands into hardware-specific instructions (e.g., a printer driver).
  - **Utility Programs:** Antivirus, disk defragmenters, backup tools, file managers.
  - **Firmware:** Software permanently "burned" into read-only memory (ROM/Flash) of a device — e.g., the **BIOS/UEFI** of a motherboard, or the program inside a microwave's microcontroller.
- **Application Software** — End-user programs designed to accomplish a specific productive or entertainment task.
  - *Examples:* MS Word (word processing), Excel (spreadsheets), AutoCAD (engineering design), MATLAB (numerical computing), Chrome (web browser), games.
- **Programming Software / Language Translators** — Tools used by developers to write, test, debug, and translate other software.
  - *Examples:* Compilers (gcc, javac), Interpreters (Python interpreter), Assemblers, Text Editors (VS Code), Debuggers (GDB), Integrated Development Environments (IDEs).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (The Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important high-yield asset** for the KTU examination. Memorizing every row of this table will allow you to answer virtually any 3-mark or 14-mark comparative question on this topic.

| S.No. | Comparison Parameter | **Hardware** | **Software** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nature / Tangibility** | Physical, tangible, can be touched. | Logical, intangible, cannot be touched. |
| 2 | **Constituency** | Made of electronic/electromechanical components: resistors, capacitors, ICs, transistors, wires, plastic, metal. | Made of instructions written in programming languages, stored as binary code (0s and 1s). |
| 3 | **Development** | Designed, prototyped, and manufactured via electronic/industrial engineering processes. | Designed, coded, tested, and debugged via software engineering. |
| 4 | **Execution Behavior** | Performs the **physical action** — electrons flow, motors spin, screens light up. | Provides the **logical instruction set** that tells hardware what to do. |
| 5 | **Lifespan** | Subject to **physical wear and tear**; degrades over time. Has a finite operational lifetime. | Does **not** wear out physically; can degrade only due to bugs or version incompatibility. |
| 6 | **Failure Mode** | Fails due to **physical damage, overheating, corrosion, component burnout**. | Fails due to **bugs, viruses, logic errors, crashes**. |
| 7 | **Manufacturing & Replication Cost** | Expensive to design and mass-produce (requires factories, silicon fabs, assembly lines). | Inexpensive to reproduce — copying a 5 GB software is virtually **free** once written. |
| 8 | **Dependency** | **Cannot function** without software to instruct it. A brand-new PC with no OS is a "paperweight." | **Cannot function** without hardware to run on. Software needs at least a CPU, RAM, and storage. |
| 9 | **Upgradability** | Upgraded by **physical replacement** (e.g., add a new RAM stick, swap a GPU). | Upgraded by **logical replacement** (download a new version, install a patch). |
| 10 | **Examples** | CPU, RAM, Motherboard, Monitor, Keyboard, Mouse, Printer, HDD, SSD. | Windows 11, Linux Ubuntu, MS Office, Google Chrome, Python, BIOS firmware. |
| 11 | **Order of Function** | Hardware is the **platform** — software needs it as a foundation to execute. | Software is the **director** — it commands the hardware. |
| 12 | **Visibility** | Externally visible and recognizable. | Invisible to the naked eye; only its *effects* are visible. |

### 2.4 The "Why" and "How" of Their Co-dependence

- **Why both must exist:** Hardware provides the *capacity*; software provides the *purpose*. A calculator (hardware) is useless until programmed to do arithmetic (software).
- **How they interact:** The **CPU** fetches software instructions (machine code) from memory, the **Control Unit** decodes them, and the **ALU** + peripheral hardware physically execute the required electrical operations. The OS software acts as a **translator and manager** between user-level software and physical hardware.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems Engineer:** Designs hardware (microcontrollers like Arduino/STM32) AND writes firmware (C code flashed into ROM). Must know both intimately.
- **IoT Developer:** Configures sensor hardware and writes the cloud-side software — the boundary is constantly crossed.
- **PCB Designer:** Lays out hardware traces on a board and verifies that the firmware signals match the hardware pinout.
- **Diagnostic Technician:** When a system fails, the first step is to *isolate* — is this a hardware fault (replace component) or a software fault (reinstall/upgrade)? Knowing the difference is the core of troubleshooting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is conceptual rather than equation-heavy, this section provides a **rigorous logical derivation of the "How" of hardware–software interaction** — analyzing the fetch-decode-execute cycle — and presents a **comprehensive analytical matrix** that KTU examiners use as a scoring rubric.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Hardware to Software in a Working Computer

| Computer Function (Logical) | Hardware Element Responsible (Physical) | Software Element Responsible (Logical) | Combined Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accepting user input** | Keyboard, Mouse, Touch panel, Microphone | Device driver software + Application GUI | User types a letter; it appears on the screen. |
| **Storing data temporarily** | RAM chips (DRAM cells, each is a tiny capacitor-transistor pair) | Memory Manager inside the OS (e.g., Windows Memory Manager) | A document being edited is held in volatile memory. |
| **Performing arithmetic** | ALU (built from thousands of logic gates on the CPU die) | The compiled machine-code instruction (e.g., `ADD EAX, EBX`) | 5 + 3 is computed as a binary operation: `0101 + 0011`. |
| **Displaying output** | Monitor (LCD/OLED panel), GPU, speakers | Display driver, application rendering code, OS desktop manager | A pixel turns red at coordinate (x, y). |
| **Long-term storage** | HDD platters / SSD NAND flash cells | File System software (NTFS, FAT32, ext4) | A file is written and survives a power-off. |
| **Controlling peripherals** | USB controller, I/O ports, GPIO pins | Device driver + Firmware (e.g., a USB HID driver) | A printer receives formatted data and prints a page. |
| **Booting up** | Motherboard, Power Supply, ROM/Flash chip | BIOS/UEFI firmware → Bootloader → OS Kernel | The blank PC becomes a functional workstation. |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: The Instruction Execution Cycle

To formally prove *why* hardware and software are inseparable, we trace a single instruction through a real computer. This is the foundational cycle taught in every KTU 2024 computer architecture module.

**Scenario:** A user clicks an icon. The application calculates `result = A + B` where `A = 5` and `B = 3`. We trace the hardware–software cooperation.

**Step 1 — Source code is authored by the programmer (Software origin).**

$$\text{result} = A + B \quad \text{(written in a high-level language like C)}$$

The C code is purely logical; it cannot physically run. A **compiler** (programming software) must translate it.

**Step 2 — Compilation to machine code (Software transformation).**

$$\text{High-Level Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} \text{Assembly} \xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Machine Code (binary)}$$

For our addition, the resulting machine code might be (hypothetically):

$$\texttt{LOAD  R1, [A]} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \texttt{0001 0010 0101}$$

$$\texttt{LOAD  R2, [B]} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \texttt{0001 0011 0110}$$

$$\texttt{ADD   R1, R2} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \texttt{0010 0001 0010}$$

$$\texttt{STORE R3, [result]} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \texttt{0011 0001 0111}$$

Each of these binary strings is now **software stored as electronic states** in memory (the hardware). Notice: software has now taken a *physical form* — voltages inside a RAM chip.

**Step 3 — Fetch (Hardware action triggered by software).**

The **Program Counter (PC)**, a hardware register, holds the address of the next instruction. The **Control Unit** issues an electrical signal on the address bus to fetch `0001 0010 0101` from RAM. The instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.

**Step 4 — Decode (Hardware interpretation of software).**

The **Instruction Decoder** (a hardware circuit made of combinational logic) analyzes the opcode bits `0001` and determines this is a `LOAD` operation targeting register `R1` from memory address holding the value of `A`.

**Step 5 — Execute (Hardware performs the actual computation).**

The CPU's internal data pathways route the value `5` (the binary `0101` of A) into register `R1`. For the `ADD` instruction, the **ALU** (hardware) physically toggles its transistor-based adder circuit to combine `5` and `3`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary of } 5 &= 0101 \\
\text{Binary of } 3 &= 0011 \\
\hline
\text{Sum (bit-wise full adder)} &= 1000 \quad (= 8_{10})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Store (Hardware writes back the result).**

The result `8` is written to memory location `[result]`, and the Program Counter increments to fetch the next instruction. The cycle repeats.

> **Conclusion of the derivation:** Every step above required *both* hardware (CPU, RAM, ALU, bus) *and* software (the machine code). Neither could produce the answer `8` alone. This is the **mathematical/logical proof** of their co-dependence.

### 3.3 Hardware–Software Boundary: The Firmware Gray Zone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firmware** is a special class of software that lives *inside* hardware. It is permanently (or semi-permanently) stored in non-volatile memory chips on the hardware itself.

| Pure Hardware (No software) | Pure Software (No hardware) | Firmware (The Bridge) |
|---|---|---|
| A passive wire, a resistor, an unprogrammed gate. | A `.exe` file on a USB drive with no computer to run it. | A microcontroller with its flash memory programmed with C code. |

KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can correctly classify firmware — it is **software by nature, hardware-resident by location**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Architecture of a Computer System (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["USER LAYER - User interacts here"]:::user
    L4["APPLICATION SOFTWARE LAYER - MS Word, Chrome, MATLAB, Games"]:::app
    L3["SYSTEM SOFTWARE LAYER - Operating System, Device Drivers, Utilities"]:::sys
    L2["FIRMWARE LAYER - BIOS UEFI Bootloader Microcontroller code"]:::firm
    L1["HARDWARE LAYER - CPU RAM Storage I/O Devices Motherboard"]:::hw
    L0["PHYSICAL LAYER - Electricity Transistors Silicon Electrons"]:::phys

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0

    classDef user fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#AED6F1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
