The dielectric withstand (hipot) test confirms that no breakdown occurs at $1.5 \times V_{rated}$ for 60 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Connector Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application Layer] --> B[Connector Selection Block]
    B --> C{Decision Matrix}
    C -- Signal --> D[Signal Connector Block]
    C -- Power --> E[Power Connector Block]
    C -- RF --> F[RF Coaxial Connector Block]
    D --> G[Pin Contact and Socket Contact]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Dielectric Housing]
    H --> I[Locking and Latching Mechanism]
    I --> J[Shielding and EMI Suppression]
    J --> K[Strain Relief and Cable Boot]
    K --> L[Final Mated Interface]
    L --> M[Load and Application]
    stepA[Current Flow Path] --> G
    stepB[Normal Force Spring] --> G
    stepC[Plating Layer] --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Connector Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Identify Signal Type] --> P2{Analog or Digital or RF or Power}
    P2 -- Analog --> Q1[Audio Jack and RCA and XLR]
    P2 -- Digital --> Q2[USB and HDMI and RJ45 and D-sub]
    P2 -- RF --> Q3[SMA and BNC and UFL]
    P2 -- Power --> Q4[Anderson and XT60 and DC Jack]
    Q1 --> R1[Verify Voltage Current Bandwidth]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Check Mating Cycle Endurance]
    S1 --> T1[Validate Temperature Rating]
    T1 --> U1[Confirm IP and EMI Requirements]
    U1 --> V1[Final Connector Selection]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional View of a Coaxial Connector (RF SMA Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Outer Metal Shell and Shield] --> R2[Outer Conductor D]
    R2 --> R3[PTFE Dielectric Filler eps_r 2.1]
    R3 --> R4[Center Conductor Pin d]
    R4 --> R5[Gold Plating Layer]
    R5 --> R6[Contact a-spot Interface]
    R6 --> R7[Mating Socket Contact]
    R7 --> R8[Characteristic Impedance Z0 = 50 ohm]
    stepR1[Current and Signal Flow] --> R4
    stepR2[Return Path on Outer Shell] --> R2
    stepR3[EMI Containment] --> R1
```

## 4.4 Pin Header Pitch Reference Diagram (ASCII)

```
   2.54 mm pitch              1.27 mm pitch           0.50 mm pitch
   (Standard Header)          (Shrouded)              (BTB mezzanine)

   o   o   o   o   o         o o o o o o o         o o o o o o o o
   |   |   |   |   |         | | | | | | |         | | | | | | | |
   |   |   |   |   |         | | | | | | |         | | | | | | | |
```

## 4.5 USB Connector Pinout Reference

```
USB Type-A (Pinout, looking at receptacle):
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │ 1  2  3  4      │
    │ VBUS  D−  D+  GND│
    └─────────────────┘
    
Pin 1: VBUS  (+5 V)
Pin 2: D−     (Data minus)
Pin 3: D+     (Data plus)
Pin 4: GND    (Ground)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]
**Define the term "electrical connector." List the four essential components of any connector assembly.**

**Model Answer:**
An electrical connector is a separable electromechanical device that provides a detachable electrical and mechanical interface between two or more conductors, sub-systems, or PCBs without permanent bonding.

Four essential components:
1. **Contact (Pin/Socket)** — Current-carrying metallic element.
2. **Insulator (Housing)** — Dielectric body that positions and isolates contacts.
3. **Locking Mechanism** — Maintains mated condition.
4. **Shielding** — Optional EMI/RFI suppression enclosure.

*(Examiner Note: Naming all four is required for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]
**Differentiate between crimp and IDC (Insulation Displacement) termination methods in connectors.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Crimp | IDC |
|---|---|---|
| Tool required | Yes (crimping tool) | No |
| Wire prep | Strip insulation required | Insulation piercing |
| Cycle time | Slower | Faster |
| Reliability | Very high | Moderate (depends on plating) |
| Application | Power, harsh environment | Ribbon cable, mass termination |
| Cost | Higher (per wire) | Lower (per wire) |

*(Examiner Note: Any three valid distinguishing points fetch 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the Holm's contact resistance equation. Discuss the role of plating materials (Au, Sn, Ag) on contact resistance with reference to the equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A connector is rated at 8 A current at 20 °C ambient with $T_{max} = 90\,°C$. Calculate the derated current at 65 °C ambient. Comment on the engineering implication. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A:

**(a) Holm's Contact Resistance Equation**

The constriction resistance at a single a-spot is given by:

$$ R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi H}{F_n}} $$

Where:
- $\rho$ = bulk resistivity (Ω·m)
- $H$ = material hardness (Pa)
- $F_n$ = normal force (N)

**Role of plating:**
- **Gold (Au):** $\rho = 2.2 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$, no oxide layer → lowest $R_c$.
- **Silver (Ag):** $\rho = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$, lowest of all, but sulfides.
- **Tin (Sn):** $\rho = 11.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$, prone to whiskers and oxidation.

**Implication:** Au gives ~5× lower $R_c$ than Sn, justifying its use in low-voltage signal contacts where **< 0.5 V** signals cannot break through Sn-oxide films (fretting corrosion). Sn is acceptable for **> 3 V** signals.

**(b) Derating Calculation**

$$ I_{allowed}(T) = I_{rated}\left(1 - \dfrac{T_{amb} - 20}{T_{max} - 20}\right) $$

$$ I_{allowed}(65) = 8 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{65 - 20}{90 - 20}\right) $$

$$ I_{allowed}(65) = 8 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{45}{70}\right) = 8 \cdot (1 - 0.643) $$

$$ I_{allowed}(65) = 8 \cdot 0.357 = 2.86\,\text{A} $$

**Engineering Implication:** At 65 °C ambient (typical enclosed industrial environment), the connector loses **64% of its current rating**. The designer must either:
1. Select a higher-rated connector (≥ 14 A).
2. Add forced-air cooling.
3. Reduce ambient temperature via enclosure design.

This demonstrates the **non-negotiable nature** of thermal derating in connector specification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **coaxial RF connector**. Define characteristic impedance and derive the equation for $Z_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coaxial connector has outer-inner diameter ratio $D/d = 3.5$ and uses PTFE dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 2.1$). Calculate $Z_0$. Is this connector suitable for standard RF instrumentation? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B:

**(a) Construction and Working of Coaxial RF Connector**

A coaxial connector (e.g., SMA) consists of:
1. **Center Pin** — Inner conductor carrying the signal.
2. **Dielectric Bead** — Maintains concentric geometry and defines $Z_0$.
3. **Outer Shell (Body)** — Returns ground path and provides shielding.
4. **Coupling Nut** — Threaded locking mechanism.
5. **Gold-plated Contacts** — Minimize contact resistance.

**Working:** The center conductor carries the RF signal; the outer shell simultaneously serves as the ground return and as a Faraday cage to prevent external EMI coupling. The **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ is the ratio of voltage to current of a propagating wave, defined by geometry and dielectric, independent of length:

$$ Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right) $$

This geometry ensures **no reflection** when mated with a 50 Ω transmission line (cable).

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

Given $D/d = 3.5$, $\varepsilon_r = 2.1$:

$$ Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{2.1}} \cdot \ln(3.5) $$

$$ Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{1.449} \cdot 1.2528 $$

$$ Z_0 = 41.41 \cdot 1.2528 \approx 51.9\,\Omega $$

**Justification:** $Z_0 = 51.9\,\Omega$ is within the **±5% tolerance band** allowed for 50 Ω systems (i.e., 47.5–52.5 Ω). Therefore, this connector **is suitable for standard RF instrumentation**, though at the upper limit. For tighter applications (e.g., vector network analyzer calibration), a connector with $D/d \approx 3.5$ adjusted to compensate for the dielectric bead ($\varepsilon_{bead} \neq \varepsilon_{PTFE}$ at the joint) is preferred.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always state **mΩ, A, °C, dB, V** explicitly.
> 2. **Skipping intermediate steps** in derating — Show full formula substitution.
> 3. **Mixing 50 Ω and 75 Ω** coaxial systems — This is a frequent error in RF connector questions.
> 4. **Not mentioning plating role** — In any contact resistance question, **plating** must be discussed.
> 5. **Ignoring derating** — Always apply temperature derating unless the question says "at rated temperature."
> 6. **Confusing mating cycles with operating cycles** — Mating = insertion/removal, not active use.
> 7. **Misnaming connector standards** — E.g., calling RJ45 an "Ethernet plug" is vague; "8P8C modular connector per IEC 60603-7" is precise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A connector is a *separable electromechanical* device — separable is the keyword.
- **Key formula:** $R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi H}{F_n}}$ (Holm's contact resistance).
- **Derating:** Current rating drops **linearly** above 20 °C reference ambient.
- **RF rule:** $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$ for instrumentation, $75\,\Omega$ for video/broadcast.
- **Plating hierarchy:** Au < Ag < Sn in resistivity; Au best for low-voltage signals.
- **Mating cycles:** ≥ 100 commercial, ≥ 500 industrial, ≥ 10,000 military/aerospace.
- **IP rating:** IP67 (dust-tight + 1 m immersion) is the **industrial benchmark**.
- **Voltage drop:** Connector voltage drop should be **< 1% of supply** for power applications.
- **Failure modes:** The "Big Six" — corrosion, fretting, force loss, contamination, plating wear, fatigue.
- **Pinout memorization:** USB-A (VBUS, D−, D+, GND); RJ45 (8P8C with T568A/B color codes).
- **Standards:** MIL-DTL-38999 (military), USCAR (auto), IEC 61076 (industrial), IEC 60603-7 (RJ45).
- **Selection steps:** Signal type → Voltage/Current → Bandwidth → Mating cycles → Temperature → IP/EMI → Final selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-liner for KTU Viva:** *"Connectors are not just plugs; they are engineered electromechanical systems whose reliability depends on normal force, plating metallurgy, thermal derating, and impedance matching."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Motherboard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT612 - ELECTRONIC HARDWARE FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Motherboard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **motherboard** (also known as a **mainboard**, **system board**, or **logic board**) is the principal printed circuit board (PCB) that establishes the central communication backbone of a computer system. It mechanically mounts and electrically interconnects critical subsystems including the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Random Access Memory (RAM)**, **chipset**, **Basic Input/Output System (BIOS/UEFI)**, storage controllers, expansion slots, and peripheral interfaces through a network of conductive **copper traces**, **bus architectures**, and **power planes**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEEVT612 Module 4** syllabus, motherboards are broadly classified by their **form factor**, which defines the standardized geometric dimensions, mounting hole patterns, connector placements, and power delivery specifications defined by industry consortiums such as the **Intel and industry form factor working groups** and the **SSI (Server System Infrastructure) forum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEEVT612, Module 4):**
> Students must be able to **identify, compare, and justify** the selection of motherboard form factors based on **physical dimensions, supported processors, expansion capability, power architecture, and target application (desktop, workstation, server, embedded, or portable)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a computer system as a **modern city**:
- The **motherboard** is the **roadway network and central square** of the city.
- The **CPU** is the **City Hall** (the decision-making authority).
- The **RAM** represents **short-term meeting halls** (fast, but volatile).
- The **Hard Disk/SSD** is the **city archives** (long-term storage).
- **I/O ports (USB, HDMI, LAN)** are the **city gates and airports** (entry/exit points).
- The **chipset** acts as the **traffic control system** (coordinating flow).

The **"type" of motherboard** is therefore analogous to the **city plan** — a sprawling suburban layout (ATX), a compact studio apartment (Mini-ITX), or a high-density industrial complex (server board). Each layout is optimized for a different use-case, population density (component count), and traffic pattern (data bandwidth).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Metrics Used in Motherboard Classification:**
> - **Form Factor Dimensions:** Measured in **millimeters (mm)** — standardized by industry body.
> - **Power Delivery:** **Wattage (W)** of VRM (Voltage Regulator Module) stages.
> - **Bus Width:** **bits** (commonly 8, 16, 32, 64 bit) for internal data paths.
> - **Clock Frequency of System Bus:** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — typically **MHz** or **GHz**.
> - **Expansion Slot Standard:** **PCIe Gen 4.0/5.0** at **x1, x4, x8, x16** lane widths.
> - **Maximum RAM Capacity:** Measured in **Gigabytes (GB)** — modern boards support up to **128 GB / 192 GB / 256 GB**.
> - **SATA Transfer Rate:** **6 Gbps (SATA III)** as the modern baseline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Matrix

## 2.1 Classification Logic of Motherboard Types

Motherboards are categorized based on five key engineering parameters:
1. **Physical Form Factor** (Size & Layout)
2. **Power Connector Architecture** (AT, ATX12V, EPS12V)
3. **Chipset Compatibility** (Intel Z-series, B-series, AMD X-series, etc.)
4. **Target Application Class** (Consumer, Workstation, Server, Embedded)
5. **I/O & Expansion Capability** (Number of PCIe lanes, USB ports, M.2 slots)

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Motherboard Types

### 2.2.1 AT (Advanced Technology) Motherboard
- **Introduced:** IBM in **1984**.
- **Dimensions:** **12" × 13.8"** (305 mm × 350 mm).
- **Key Drawback:** Power connector (P8 + P9) was difficult to insert, causing **reverse polarity damage**.
- **Status:** **Obsolete** — no longer manufactured for modern systems.

### 2.2.2 ATX (Advanced Technology Extended) Motherboard
- **Introduced:** Intel in **1995**.
- **Dimensions:** **12" × 9.6"** (305 mm × 244 mm).
- **Key Innovations:**
  - Single **20-pin (later 24-pin) ATX power connector** with keying.
  - Rear **I/O shield** with standardized port cluster.
  - **Soft power** support via ACPI (Advanced Configuration and Power Interface).
- **Modern Use:** Mainstream desktop PCs and gaming rigs.

### 2.2.3 Micro-ATX (µATX) Motherboard
- **Dimensions:** **9.6" × 9.6"** (244 mm × 244 mm).
- **Advantage:** Backward compatible with **ATX chassis and power supplies**.
- **Trade-off:** Fewer expansion slots (typically **3 PCIe slots** instead of 7).
- **Application:** Budget desktops, office PCs, and small-form-factor builds.

### 2.2.4 Mini-ITX Motherboard
- **Origin:** VIA Technologies, **2001**.
- **Dimensions:** **6.7" × 6.7"** (170 mm × 170 mm).
- **Cooling Profile:** Designed for **fanless / low-noise** operation.
- **Application:** HTPC (Home Theatre PC), embedded systems, compact desktops.
- **Expansion:** Only **1 PCIe slot** (usually x16).

### 2.2.5 E-ATX (Extended ATX) Motherboard
- **Dimensions:** **12" × 13"** (305 mm × 330 mm).
- **Feature:** Supports **dual CPUs**, **8 RAM slots**, and **multiple PCIe x16 slots**.
- **Use Case:** High-end workstations and enthusiast gaming.

### 2.2.6 BTX (Balanced Technology Extended) Motherboard
- **Introduced:** Intel, **2004**.
- **Dimensions:** **12.8" × 10.5"** (325 mm × 267 mm).
- **Innovation:** Inverted airflow path (intake at front → exhaust at rear).
- **Status:** **Discontinued (2006)** — failed in market adoption.

### 2.2.7 NLX (New Low-profile Extended) Motherboard
- **Dimensions:** **13.6" × 9.0"** (max) / **8.0" × 10.0"** (min).
- **Design:** Used a **riser card** to allow vertical expansion in slim-line cases.
- **Status:** Obsolete — replaced by proprietary slim form factors.

### 2.2.8 Server Motherboards
- **Socket Support:** Single, dual, or **quad-socket LGA configurations** (e.g., **Intel LGA 4189, AMD SP3**).
- **Memory Channels:** **6-channel, 8-channel** DDR4/DDR5 ECC (Error Correcting Code) registered memory.
- **Management:** Integrated **IPMI (Intelligent Platform Management Interface)**, **BMC (Baseboard Management Controller)**, and **iLO/iDRAC** remote management.
- **Examples:** SSI CEB (12" × 10.5"), SSI EEB (12" × 13").

### 2.2.9 Laptop / Mobile Motherboards
- **Form:** Proprietary, non-standardized dimensions.
- **Components Soldered:** CPU, GPU, and sometimes RAM are **BGA (Ball Grid Array) soldered** to the board.
- **Cooling:** Integrated heat pipes, single shared exhaust path.
- **I/O:** Limited (typically 1–2 USB-C, 1 HDMI/DP, headphone jack).

### 2.2.10 Embedded & Industrial Motherboards
- **Examples:** **COM Express, Qseven, SMARC** modules.
- **Characteristics:** Wide temperature range (**−40°C to +85°C**), long lifecycle (7–15 years), fanless operation.
- **Application:** Industrial automation, medical devices, IoT gateways.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Cheat Sheet)

| Form Factor | Dimensions (mm) | Typical Expansion Slots | Power Connector | Target Application | Market Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AT** | 305 × 350 | 8 (ISA) | P8 + P8/P9 | Legacy PCs | **Obsolete** |
| **ATX** | 305 × 244 | 7 PCIe | 24-pin ATX12V | Mainstream desktop | **Active (Standard)** |
| **Micro-ATX** | 244 × 244 | 3 PCIe | 24-pin ATX12V | Budget/SFF desktop | **Active** |
| **Mini-ITX** | 170 × 170 | 1 PCIe | 24-pin ATX12V | HTPC/Embedded | **Active** |
| **E-ATX** | 305 × 330 | 4–6 PCIe | 24+8 pin | Workstation/Enthusiast | **Active** |
| **BTX** | 325 × 267 | 7 | 24-pin | Designed for cooling | **Discontinued** |
| **NLX** | varies | Riser-based | AT/ATX | Slim desktops | **Obsolete** |
| **SSI EEB** | 305 × 330 | 6–7 PCIe | 24+8 pin | Server | **Active** |
| **WTX** | 356 × 425 | Variable | WTX-specific | Workstation | **Discontinued** |
| **Laptop** | Proprietary | None (soldered) | Internal battery | Mobile | **Active** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Selecting the correct motherboard form factor is critical in:
> - **Embedded Product Design:** Mini-ITX or COM Express for space-constrained systems.
> - **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** E-ATX or SSI EEB for multi-GPU and large RAM capacity.
> - **Industrial Automation:** Wide-temp embedded boards with long lifecycle support.
> - **OEM Manufacturing:** Micro-ATX offers the best **price-to-functionality ratio** for mass production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Selection Logic

## 3.1 Motherboard Selection Decision Algorithm (Step-by-Step Derivation)

Since this is a hardware classification topic, the "derivation" is presented as an **analytical decision matrix** with deterministic logic flow.

### Step 1 — Determine the Target System Class
The first engineering question is:
$$ \text{System Class} = \begin{cases} \text{Desktop} & \text{if portability not required} \\ \text{Laptop} & \text{if battery-powered mobile use} \\ \text{Server} & \text{if 24/7 reliability \& ECC RAM required} \\ \text{Embedded} & \text{if industrial/IoT application} \end{cases} $$

> **Logic:** This single variable eliminates 60–70% of all candidate motherboards. *Why?* Each system class has its own standards body, certification (e.g., UL, CE for embedded), and ecosystem (proprietary BIOS/UEFI).

### Step 2 — Define the Physical Constraint Vector
The physical constraints form a vector:
$$ \vec{C}_{physical} = [L_{max}, W_{max}, H_{max}, M_{available}] $$
where $L_{max}, W_{max}, H_{max}$ are maximum allowed length, width, and height in **mm**, and $M_{available}$ is the available mounting hole pattern.

The candidate motherboard must satisfy:
$$ L_{board} \le L_{max} \quad \text{AND} \quad W_{board} \le W_{max} \quad \text{AND} \quad H_{component} \le H_{max} $$

> **Logic:** For example, a Mini-ITX board (170 × 170 mm) fits inside any ATX chassis, but an E-ATX (305 × 330 mm) does **not** fit a Mini-ITX chassis. This is a hard geometric constraint.

### Step 3 — Define the Performance Requirement Vector
$$ \vec{P}_{perf} = [N_{PCIe}, N_{SATA}, N_{M.2}, C_{RAM_{max}}, F_{FSB}] $$
where:
- $N_{PCIe}$ = number of PCIe expansion slots required
- $N_{SATA}$ = number of storage devices
- $N_{M.2}$ = number of NVMe SSDs
- $C_{RAM_{max}}$ = maximum RAM capacity in **GB**
- $F_{FSB}$ = supported front-side bus frequency in **MHz / GHz**

### Step 4 — Compute Form Factor Score
A weighted score is computed for each candidate board:
$$ S_{candidate} = w_1 \cdot N_{PCIe} + w_2 \cdot N_{M.2} + w_3 \cdot \frac{C_{RAM_{max}}}{64} - w_4 \cdot \frac{A_{board}}{A_{max}} $$

where $A_{board}$ is the board area in **mm²** and $A_{max}$ is the maximum allowable area.

> **Logic:** This score rewards boards with **more expansion per unit area** (Mini-ITX is penalized, E-ATX rewarded for high expansion density).

### Step 5 — Power Architecture Verification
Verify the Power Supply Unit (PSU) compatibility:
$$ V_{PSU} \in V_{compatible} = \{ 24\text{-pin ATX12V}, 24+8\text{-pin EPS12V}, 24+4+8\text{-pin dual-CPU} \} $$

If $V_{PSU} \notin V_{compatible}$, a **power adapter cable** may be required, or the system is rejected.

### Step 6 — Final Selection
The optimal motherboard is:
$$ M_{selected} = \arg\max_{M \in Candidates} \left( S_{candidate} - \lambda \cdot \text{Cost}(M) \right) $$

where $\lambda$ is the cost-sensitivity weight chosen by the designer.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Analytical Selection

> **Problem:** Design a workstation for a CAD engineer requiring 64 GB RAM, 1 GPU, 2 NVMe SSDs, fitting within a 250 × 250 mm chassis.

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1:** System Class = Desktop Workstation
- **Step 2:** Physical constraint: $L_{max} = 250$ mm, $W_{max} = 250$ mm
  - ATX (305 mm) ❌ Fails
  - Micro-ATX (244 mm) ✅ Passes
  - Mini-ITX (170 mm) ✅ Passes
  - E-ATX (305 mm) ❌ Fails
- **Step 3:** Performance needs: $N_{PCIe} \ge 1$, $C_{RAM_{max}} \ge 64$ GB, $N_{M.2} \ge 2$
- **Step 4:** Score evaluation
  - Micro-ATX: $w_1(3) + w_2(2) + w_3(1) - w_4(0.96)$ → **High score**
  - Mini-ITX: $w_1(1) + w_2(1) + w_3(1) - w_4(0.45)$ → **Lower score due to fewer slots**
- **Step 5:** Power: 24-pin ATX + 8-pin EPS → both supported
- **Step 6:** **Selected: Micro-ATX** ✅

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical workflow is directly tested in KTU ESE questions where students are asked to **"justify the choice of motherboard form factor for a given application."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motherboard Form Factor Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Computer Motherboard Classification] --> B[Legacy Form Factors]
    A --> C[Modern Desktop Form Factors]
    A --> D[Server Form Factors]

    B --> B1[AT - 305x350 mm]
    B --> B2[Baby AT - 216x254 mm]

    C --> C3[Mini ITX - 170x170 mm]
    C --> C4[E ATX - 305x330 mm]

    D --> D1[SSI EEB - 305x330 mm]
    D --> D2[SSI CEB - 305x267 mm]
    D --> D3[Proprietary Dual Socket]

    E --> E1[COM Express]
    E --> E2[Qseven Module]

    F --> F1[Laptop Proprietary]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Motherboard Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    PWR[Power Supply Unit] -->|24-pin ATX12V| MB[Motherboard PCB]
    PWR -->|8-pin EPS12V| MB
    CPU[CPU Socket LGA AM5] -->|DMI 4.0 16 Gbps| CHIP[PCH Chipset]
    RAM[DDR4 DDR5 DIMM Slots] -->|Dual Channel 128-bit| CPU
    STORAGE[SATA M.2 NVMe Ports] -->|PCIe Gen4 64 Gbps| CHIP
    PCIE[PCIe x16 x8 x4 x1 Slots] -->|PEG Bus| CPU
    PCIE -->|General Purpose| CHIP
    USB[USB 3.2 Gen2x2 Type C] -->|10 Gbps x2| CHIP
    LAN[2.5G 10G Ethernet Controller] -->|PCIe x4| CHIP
    AUDIO[Realtek ALC4080 HD Audio] -->|HDA Bus| CHIP
    WIFI[WiFi 6E 7 Module] -->|CNVi PCIe| CHIP
    BIOS[UEFI BIOS Flash Chip] -->|SPI Bus| CHIP
    IO[Rear I/O Shield Ports] --> CHIP
    MB --> FORM[Form Factor ATX mATX ITX EATX]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — ATX vs Micro-ATX vs Mini-ITX

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ATX_BLOCK[ATX Motherboard]
        A1[Dimensions 305x244 mm]
        A2[7 PCIe Slots]
        A3[4 RAM Slots]
```
